CHAPTER I.

the champion of his country





when young Nevil Beauchamp be throw off his midshipman’s jacket for

a holiday in the garb of peace, we have across Channel a host of

dreadful military officer flash sword at we for some critical

observation of our upon their sovereign, threaten Afric’s fire

and savagery. the case occur in old day now and again, sometimes,

upon imagine provocation, more furiously than at other. we be

unarmed, and the spectacle be distress. we have do nothing except

to speak our mind accord to the habit of the free, and such an

explosion appear as irrational and excessive as that of a

powder-magazine in reply to nothing more than the light of a spark. it

be know that a valorous General of the algerian war propose to make

a clean march to the capital of the British Empire at the head of ten

thousand man; which seem a small quantity to think much about, but

they wear wide red breech blow out by Fame, big as her cheek, and a

ten thousand of that sort would never think of retreat. their

spectral advance on quake London through Kentish hopgarden, Sussex

corn-field, or by the pleasant hill of Surrey, after a gymnastic leap

over the riband of salt water, haunt many pillow. and now those

horrid shout of the legion of Caesar, cry to the inheritor of an

invade name to lead they against we, as the origin of his title have

lead the army of Gaul of old gloriously, scared sweet sleep. we see they

in imagination line the opposite shore; eagle and standard-bearer,

and _gallifer_, brandish their fowl and their banner in a manner

to frighten the decorum of the universe. where be our man?



the return of the census of our population be oppressively

satisfactory, and so be the condition of our youth. we could row and

ride and fish and shoot, and breed largely: we be athlete with a

fine history and a full purse: we have first-rate sporting gun,

unrivalle park-hack and hunter, promise baby to carry on the

renown of England to the next generation, and a wonderful Press, and a

Constitution the high reach of practical human sagacity. but where

be our armed man? where our great artillery? where our prove

captain, to resist a sudden sharp trial of the national mettle? where

be the first line of England’s defence, her navy? these be

question, and Ministers be call upon to answer they. the Press

answer they boldly, with the appalling statement that we have no navy

and no army. at the most we could muster a few old ship, a couple of

experimental vessel of war, and twenty-five thousand soldier

indifferently weapone.



we be in fact as naked to the imperial foe as the merely paint

Britons.



this be apprehend, by the aid of our own shortness of figure and

the agitated image of the red-breeched only wait the signal to jump

and be at we, there ensue a curious exhibition that would be term,

in simple language, write to the newspaper, for it take the outward

form of letter: in reality, it be the deliberate saddling of our

ancient nightmare of Invasion, put the postillion on she, and

trot she along the high-road with a winding horn to rouse old

Panic. panic we will, for the sake of convenience, assume to be of the

feminine gender, and a spinster, though properly she should be class

with the large mixed race of mental and moral neuter which be the

bulk of comfortable nation. she turn in her bed at first like the

sluggard of the venerable hymnist: but once fairly awaken, she

direct a stare toward the terrific foreign contortionist, and become

in an instant all stormy nightcap and finger starve for the

bell-rope. forthwith she burst into a series of shriek, howl, and

high pierce note that cause even the parliamentary Opposition, in

the heat of an assault on a parsimonious government, to abandon its

temporary advantage and be still awhile. yet she likewise perform she

part with a certain deliberation and method, as if aware that it be a

part she have to play in the composition of a singular people. she do a

little mischief by drop on the stock-market; in other respect she

be harmless, and, inasmuch as she establish a subject for

conversation, useful.



then, lest she should have be take too seriously, the Press, which

have kindle, proceed to extinguish she with the formidable engine

call lead article, which fle fire or water, as the occasion may

require. it turn out that we have ship ready for launch, and

certain regiment come home from India; hedge we have, and a spirited

body of yeomanry; and we have pluck and patriotism, the father and

mother of volunteer innumerable. thing be not so bad.



Panic, however, send up a plaintive whine. what country have anything

like our treasure to defend? countless rich, beautiful woman, an

inviolate soil! true, and it must be do. minister be

authoritatively summon to set to work immediately. they reply that

they have be at work all the time, and be at work now. they could

assure the country, that though they flourish no trumpet, they

positively guarantee the safety of our virgin and coffer.



then the people, rather ashamed, abuse the Press for unreasonably

disturb they. the Press attack old panic and strip she naked.

Panic, with a desolate scream, arraign the parliamentary opposition

for having inflate she to serve base party purpose. the opposition

challenge the allegation of Government, point to the trimness of

army and navy during its term of office, and proclaim itself

watch-dog of the country, which be at all event an office of a kind.

Hereupon the ambassador of yonder ireful soldiery let fall a word,

say, by the faith of his Master, there be no necessity for

watch-dog to bark; an ardent and a reverent army have but fancy its

beloved choose Chief insult; the Chief and choose hold they in; he,

despite obloquy, discern our merit and esteem we.



so, then, Panic, or what remain of she, be put to bed again. the

Opposition retire into its kennel growling. the People cough like a

man of two mind, doubt whether he have be divinely inspire or have

cut a ridiculous figure. the Press interpret the cough as a warning

to Government; and Government launch a big ship with hurrah, and

order the recruiting-sergeant to be see conspicuously.



and thus we obtain a moderate reinforcement of our arm.



it be not arrive at by connivance all round, though there be a look

of it. certainly it do not come of accident, though there be a look

of that as well. nor do we explain much of the secret by attribute it

to the working of a complex machinery. the housewife’s remedy of a good

shake for the invalid who will not arise and dance away his gout,

partly illustrate the action of the Press upon the country: and

perhaps the country shake may suffer a comparison with the family

chariot of the last century, build in a previous one, commodious,

furnish agreeably, be all that the inside occupant could require

of a conveyance, until the report of horseman cross the heath at a

gallop set it dishonourably creak and complain in rapid motion,

and the squire curse his miserly purse that would not hire a guard,

and his dame say, I tell you so!—Foolhardy man, to suppose, because we

have constable in the street of big city, we have dismiss the

highwayman to limbo. and here he be, and he will cost you fifty time

the sum you would have lay out to keep he at a mile’s respectful

distance! but see, the wretch be bow: he smile at our carriage, and

tell the coachman that he remember he have be our guest, and really

think we need not go so fast. he leave word for you, sir, on your

peril to denounce he on another occasion from the magisterial Bench,

for that albeit he be a gentleman of the road, he have a mission to

right society, and succeed legitimately to that bold Good Robin Hood

who feed the poor.—Fresh from this polite encounter, the squire vow

money for his personal protection: and he determine to speak his

opinion of Sherwood’s late captain as loudly as ever. that he will, I

do not say. it might involve a large sum per annum.



simile be very well in their way. none can be sufficient in this case

without level a finger at the taxpayer—nay, directly mention

he. he be the key of our ingenuity. he pay his due; he will not pay

the additional penny or two want of he, that we may be a step or two

ahead of the day we live in, unless he be frightened. but scarcely

anything less than the wild alarum of a tocsin will frighten he.

consequently the tocsin have to be sound; and the effect be woeful

past measure: his hugging of his army, his kneeling on the shore to his

navy, his imploration of his yeomanry and his hedge, be sad to note.

his burst of pot-valiancy (the male side of the maiden Panic within

his bosom) be awful to his friend. particular care must be take

after he have begin to cool and calculate his chance of security, that

he do not gather to he a curtain of volunteer and go to sleep again

behind they; for they cost little in proportion to the much they

pretend to be to he. patriotic taxpayer doubtless exist: prophetic

one, provident one, do not. at least we show that we be want in

they. the taxpayer of a free land taxis himself, and his disinclination

for the bitter task, save under circumstance of scream urgency—as

when the night-gear and bed-linen of old convulse Panic be like the

churn Channel sea in the track of two hundred hostile steamboat, let

I say—be of the kind the gentle schoolboy feel when death or an

expedition have relieve he of his tyrant, and he be entreat

notwithstanding to go to his book.



will you not own that the working of the system for scare he and

bleeding be very ingenious? but whether the ingenuity come of native

sagacity, as it be aver by some, or whether it show an instinct

labour to supply the deficiency of stupidity, accord to other,

I cannot express an opinion. I give you the position of the country

undisturbe by any moralizing of mine. the youth I introduce to you

will rarely let we escape from it; for the reason that he be bear with

so extreme and passionate a love for his country, that he think all

thing else of mean importance in comparison: and our union be one in

which, follow the counsel of a sage and seer, I must try to paint

for you what be, not that which I imagine. this day, this hour, this

life, and even politic, the centre and throb heart of it (enough,

when unburlesqued, to blow the down off the gossamer-stump of fiction

at a single breath, I have hear tell), must be treat of man, and the

idea of man, which be—it be policy to be emphatic upon truism—be

actually the motive of man in a great degree than their appetite:

these be my theme; and may it be my fortune to keep they at bloodheat,

and myself calm as a statue of Memnon in prostrate Egypt! he sit there

wait for the sunlight; I here, and readier to be musical than you

think. I can at any rate be impartial; and do but fix your eye on the

sunlight strike he and swallow the day in round he, and you

have an image of the passive receptivity of shine and shade I hold it

good to aim at, if at the same time I may keep my character at

blood-heat. I shoot my arrow at a mark that be pretty certain to

return they to I. and as to perfect success, I should be like the

panic-stricken shopkeeper in my alarm at it; for I should believe that

genius of the air fly above our tree-top between we and the

incognizable sphere, catch those ambitious shaft they deem it a

promise of fun to play prank with.



young Mr. Beauchamp at that period of the panic have not the slight

feeling for the taxpayer. he be therefore unable to penetrate the

mystery of our roundabout way of enliven he. he pore over the

journal in perplexity, and talk of his indignation nightly to his

pretty partner at ball, who know not they be less Andromedas of

his dear Andromeda country, but dance and chat and be gay, and

say they be sure he would defend they. the man he address be

civil. they listen to he, sometimes with smile and sometimes with

laughter, but approvingly, like the lad’s quick spirit. they be

accustomed to the machinery employ to give our land a shudder and to

soothe it, and generally remark that it mean nothing. his uncle

Everard, and his uncle’s friend Stukely Culbrett, expound the nature

of Frenchmen to he, say that they be uneasy when not periodically

thrash; it would be cruel to deny they their crow beforehand; and so

the pair of gentleman pooh-poohe the affair; agree with he,

however, that we have no great reason to be proud of our appearance, and

the ground they assign for this be the activity and the prevalence

of the ignoble doctrine of Manchester—a power whose very existence be

unknown to Mr. Beauchamp. he would by no mean allow the burden of our

national disgrace to be cast on one part of the nation. we be

insult, and all in a poultry-flutter, yet no one seem to feel it

but himself! outside the Press and Parliament, which must necessarily

be the face we show to the foreigner, absolute indifference reign.

Navy man and red-coat be willing to join he or anybody in sneer at

a clipping and pare miserly Government, but they be insensible to

the insult, the panic, the startle-poultry show, the shame of our

exhibition of ourselves in Europe. it look as if the blustering

French Guard be to have it all their own way. and what would they,

what could they but, think of we! he sit down to write they a

challenge.



he be not the only Englishman who have be impel by a youthful

chivalry to do that. he be perhaps the young who ever do it, and

consequently there be various difficulty to be overcome. as regard

his qualification for address Frenchmen, a year of his

prae-neptunal time have be spend in their capital city for the purpose

of acquire French of Paris, its late refinement of pronunciation

and polish, and the art of converse. he have read the french tragic

poet and Molière; he could even relish the Gallic-classic—“Qu’il

mourut!” and he speak French passably, be quite beyond the bullish

treatment of the tongue. write a letter in French be a different

undertaking. the one he project bear no resemblance to an ordinary

letter. the briefer the well, of course; but a tone of dignity be

imperative, and the tone must be individual, distinctive, Nevil

Beauchamp’s, though not in his native language. first he try his

letter in French, and lose sight of himself completely. “Messieurs de

la Garde Française,” be a good beginning; the remainder give he a

false air of a masquerader, most uncomfortable to see; it be Nevil

Beauchamp in moustache and imperial, and bagbreeche badly fitting. he

try English, which be really himself, and all that heart could

desire, suppose he address a body of midshipman just a little

loftily. but the English, when translate, be bald and blunt to the

verge of offensiveness.



“GENTLEMEN of the FRENCH guard,

    “I take up the glove you have toss we. I be an Englishman. that

    will do for a reason.”



this might possibly pass with the gentleman of the English Guard. but

read:



“MESSIEURS DE LA GARDE FRANÇAISE,

    “J’accepte votre gant. Je suis Anglais. La raison est suffisante.”



and imagine french Guardsmen read it!



Mr. Beauchamp know the virtue of punctiliousness in epithet and

phrase of courtesy toward a formal people, and as the officer of the

French Guard be gentleman of birth, he would have they to perceive in

he their equal at a glance. on the other hand, a bare excess of

phrasing distort he to a likeness of Mascarille play Marquis. how

to be english and think french! the business be as laborious as if he

have start on the rough sea of the Channel to get at they in an open

boat.



the lady govern his uncle Everard’s house, Mrs. Rosamund Culling,

enter his room and find he write with knit brow. she be

young, that is, she be not in her middleage; and they be the dear

of friend; each have give the other proof of it. Nevil look up and

behold her lift finger.



“you be compose a love-letter, Nevil!” the accusation sound like

irony.



“no,” say he, puff; “I wish I be.”



“what can it be, then?”



he thrust pen and paper a hand’s length on the table, and gaze at she.



“my dear Nevil, be it really anything serious?” say she.



“I be write French, ma’am.”



“then I may help you. it must be very absorbing, for you do not hear

my knock at your door.”



now, could he trust she? the widow of a british officer kill nobly

fight for his country in India, be a person to be rely on for

active and burn sympathy in a matter that touch the country’s

honour. she be a woman, and a woman of spirit. man have not please he

of late. something might be hope from a woman.



he state his occupation, say that if she would assist he in his

French she would oblige he; the letter must be write and must go.

this be utter so positively that she bow her head, amuse by the

funny semi-tone of defiance to the person to whom he confide the

secret. she have humour, and be ravish by his english boyishness,

with the novel blush of the heroical-nonsensical in it.



Mrs. Culling promise he demurely that she would listen, object

nothing to his plan, only to his French.



“Messieurs de la Garde Française!” he commence.



her criticism follow swiftly.



“I think you be write to the Garde Impériale.”



he admit his error, and thank she warmly.



“Messieurs de la Garde Impériale!”



“do not that,” she say, “include the non-commission officer, the

private, and the cook, of all the regiment?”



he could scarcely think that, but think it provoke the French have

no distinctive working title correspond to gentleman, and suggest

“Messieurs les officier”: which might, Mrs. Culling assure he,

comprise the barber. he frown, and she prescribe his writing,

“Messieurs les Colonels de la Garde Impériale.” this he set down. the

point be that a stand must be make against the flood of sarcasm and

bullying to which the country be expose in increase degree, under

a belief that we would fight neither in the mass nor individually.

possibly, if it become know that the colonel refuse to meet a

midshipman, the gentleman of our household troop would advance a step.



Mrs. Culling’s adroit effort to weary he out of his project be

unsuccessful. he be too much on fire to know the taste of absurdity.



Nevil repeat what he have write in French, and next the English of

what he intend to say.



the lady conscientiously do she utmost to reconcile the two language.

she soften his downrightness, pass with approval his compliment to

France and the ancient high reputation of her army, and, see that a

loophole be leave for they to apologize, ask how many french colonel

he want to fight.



“I do not _want_, ma’am,” say Nevil.



he have simply take up the glove they have again fling at our foot: and

he have do it to stop the incessant reviling, little short of

positive contempt, which we in our indolence expose ourselves to from

the foreigner, particularly from Frenchmen, whom he like; and

precisely because he like they he insist on force they to respect

we. let his challenge be accept, and he would find backer. he know

the stuff of Englishmen: they only require an example.



“french officer be skilful swordsman,” say Mrs. Culling. “my husband

have tell I they will spend hour of the day thrusting and parrying.

they be use to duel.”



“we,” Nevil answer, “don’t get apprentice to the shamble to learn

our duty on the field. duel be, I know, sicken folly. we go too

far in pretend to despise every insult pitch at we. a man may do

for his country what he wouldn’t do for himself.”



Mrs. Culling gravely say she hope that bloodshed would be avoid,

and Mr. Beauchamp nod.



she leave he hard at work.



he be a popular boy, a favourite of woman, and therefore full of

engagement to Balls and dinner. and he be a modest boy, though his

uncle encourage he to deliver his opinion freely and argue with man.

the little drummer attach to wheel column think not more of

himself because his short leg perform the same stride as the

grenadier’; he be happy to be able to keep the step; and so be nevil;

and if ever he contradict a senior, it be in the interest of the

country. veneration of hero, living and dead, keep down his conceit.

he worship devotedly. from an early age he exact of his flattering

lady that they must love his hero. not to love his hero be to be

strangely in error, to be in need of conversion, and he proselytize

with the ardour of the Moslem. his uncle Everard be proud of his good

look, fire, and nonsense, during the boy’s extreme youth. he trace

he by cousinship back to the great Earl Beauchamp of Froissart, and

would have it so; and he would have spoil he have not the young

fellow’s mind be possess by his reverence for man of deed. how

could he think of himself, who have do nothing, accomplish nothing,

so long as he brood on the image of signal Englishmen whose name

be historic for daring, and the strong arm, and artfulness, all give

to the service of the country?—men of a magnanimity overcast with

simplicity, which Nevil hold to be pure insular English; our type of

splendid manhood, not discoverable elsewhere. a method of enrage he

be to distinguish one or other of they as irish, Scottish, or

cambrian. he consider it a dismemberment of the country. and

notwithstanding the pleasure he have in unite in his person the strong

red blood of the chivalrous Lord Beauchamp with the hard and tenacious

Romfrey blood, he hate the title of Norman. we be english—british, he

say. a family rest its pride on mere ancestry provoke his

contempt, if it do not show he one of his man. he have also a

disposition to esteem lightly the family which, having produce a man,

settle down after that effort for generation to enjoy the country’s

pay. boy be unjust; but nevil think of the country mainly, argue

that we should not accept the country’s money for what we do not

ourselves perform. these trait of his be regard as characteristic

hopeful rather than the reverse; none of his friend and relative

foresaw danger in they. he be a capital boy for his elder to trot out

and banter.



Mrs. Rosamund Culling usually go to his room to see he and doat on

he before he start on his round of an evening. she suspect that

his necessary attention to his toilet would barely have allow he

time to finish his copy of the letter. certain phrase have bother

he. the thrice recurrence of “ma patrie” jarred on his ear.

“Sentiments” afflict his acute sense of the declamatory twice. “C’est

avec les sentiment du plus profond regret” : and again, “Je suis bien

sûr que vous comprendrez mes sentiment, et m’accorderez l’honneur que

je réclame au nom de ma patrie outragée.” the word “patrie” be

broadcast over the letter, and “honneur” appear four time, and a

more delicate word to harp on than the other!



“not to Frenchmen,” say his friend Rosamund. “I would put ‘Je suis

convaincu’: it be not so familiar.”



“but I have write out the fair copy, ma’am, and that alteration seem

a trifle.”



“I would copy it again and again, Nevil, to get it right.”



“no: I’d rather see it off than have it right,” say Nevil, and he

fold the letter.



how the deuce to address it, and what direction to write on it, be

further difficulty. he have half a mind to remain at home to conquer

they by excogitation.



Rosamund urge he not to break his engagement to dine at the

Halketts’, where perhaps from his friend Colonel Halkett, who would

never imagine the reason for the inquiry, he might learn how a letter

to a crack french regiment should be address and direct.



this prove persuasive, and as the hour be late Nevil have to act on

her advice in a hurry.



his uncle Everard enjoy a perusal of the manuscript in his absence.









CHAPTER II.

UNCLE, nephew, and another





the honourable Everard Romfrey come of a race of fight earl,

tough of man, whose high, stout, western castle have weather our

cyclone period of history without change hand more than once, and

then but for a short year or two, as if to teach the original

possessor the wisdom of incline to the strong side. they have a

queen’s chamber in it, and a king’s; and they stand well up against the

charge of having deal darkly with the king. he die among they—how have

not be tell. we will not discuss the conjecture here. a savour of

North Sea foam and ballad pirate hang about the early chronicle of

the family. indication of an ancestry that have live between the wave

and the cloud be discernible in their notion of right and wrong. but

a settlement on solid earth have its influence. they be chivalrous

knight banneret, and leader in the tented field, pay and take

fair ransom for capture; and they be good landlord, good master

blithely follow to the war. sing an old battle of Normandy, Picardy,

Gascony, and you celebrate deed of theirs. at home they be vexatious

neighbour to a town of burgher claim privilege: nor be it

unreasonable that the Earl should flout the pretension of the town to

read thing for themselves, document, titleship, right, and the

rest. as well might the flat plain boast of see as far as the

pillar. Earl and town fight the fight of Barons and Commons in

epitome. the Earl give way; the baron give way. mighty man may thrash

number for a time; in the end the number will be thrash into the

art of beat their teacher. it be bad policy to fight the odd inch

by inch. those primitive school master of the million like it, and

take their pleasure in that way. the Romfreys do not breed warrior

for a parade at Court; war, though frequent, be not constant, and

they want occupation: they may even have feel that they be bind in

no common degree to the pursuit of an answer to what may be call the

parent question of humanity: be I thy master, or thou mine? they put it

to lord of other castle, to town corporation, and sometimes brother

to brother: and notwithstanding that the answer often unseated and once

discastle they, they swam back to their place, as bear warrior,

urge by a passion for land, be almost sure to do; be indeed quite

sure, so long as they multiply sturdily, and will never take no from

Fortune. a family passion for land, that survive a generation, be as

effective as genius in produce the object it conceive; and through

marriage and conflict, the seizure of land, and bride bear land,

these sharp-feed eagle-eyed earl of Romfrey spy few spot within

their top tower’s wide circle of the heavens not their own.



it be therefore manifest that they have the root quality, the prime

active element, of man in perfection, and notably that appetite to

flourish at the cost of the weak, which be the bless

exemplification of strength, and have be man’s cheerful

encouragement to fight on since his comparative subjugation (on the

whole, it seem complete) of the animal world. by-and-by the struggle

be transfer to high ground, and we begin to perceive how much we

be indebted to the fight spirit. Strength be the brute form of

truth. no conspicuously great man be bear of the Romfreys, who be

well serve by a succession of able son. they send undistinguished

able man to army and navy—lieutenant give to be critic of their

captain, but trustworthy for their work. in the later life of the

family, they prefer the provincial state of splendid squire to

Court and political honour. they be renowned shot, long-limbe

stalk sportsman in field and bower, fast friend, intemperate

enemy, handsome to feminine eye, resemble one another in build,

and mostly of the northern colour, or betwixt the tint, with an

hereditary nose and mouth that cry Romfrey from face thrice dilute

in cousinship.



the Hon. Everard (Stephen Denely Craven Romfrey), third son of the late

Earl, have some hope of the title, and be in person a noticeable

gentleman, in mind a mediaeval baron, in politic a crotchety

unintelligible Whig. he inherit the estate of Holdesbury, on the

border of Hampshire and Wilts, and espouse that of Steynham in

Sussex, where he generally reside. his favourite in the family have

be the Lady Emily, his eld sister, who, contrary to the advice of

her other brother and sister, have yield her hand to his not wealthy

friend, Colonel Richard Beauchamp. after the death of Nevil’s parent,

he adopt the boy, be himself childless, and a widower.

Childlessness be the affliction of the family. Everard, have no son,

could hardly hope that his brother the Earl, and Craven, Lord Avonley,

would have one, for he love the prospect of the title. yet, as there

be no cousin of the male branch extant, the lack of an heir be a

serious omission, and to become the Earl of Romfrey, and be the last

Earl of Romfrey, be a melancholy thought, however brilliant. so sink

the sun: but he could not desire the end of a great day. at one time he

be a hot Parliamentarian, call himself a Whig, call by the whig

a Radical, call by the Radicals a Tory, and very happy in fight

they all round. this be during the decay of his party, before the

Liberals be define. a Liberal deprive he of the seat he have hold

for fifteen year, and the clearness of his understanding be obscure

by that black vision of popular ingratitude which afflict the free

fight man yet more than the malleable public servant. the latter have

a clerkly humility attach to he like a second nature, from his habit

of do as other bid he: the former smack a voluntarily sweat

forehead and throb wound for witness of his claim upon your

palpable thankfulness. it be an insult to tell he that he fight for

his own satisfaction. Mr. Romfrey still call himself a Whig, though

it be Whig mean vengeance on account of his erratic vote and voice on

two or three occasion that deny he a peerage and a seat in haven.

Thither let your good sheep go, your echo, your wag-tail dog, your

wealthy pursy manufacturer! he decry the attraction of the sublimer

House, and laugh at the transparent Whiggery of his party in

replenish it from the upper shoot of the commonalty: “drag it

down to prop it up! swamp it to keep it swim!” he say.



he be nevertheless a vehement supporter of that House. he stand for

King, Lords, and Commons, in spite of his personal grievance, harp

the triad as vigorously as bard of old Britain. Commons he add out of

courtesy, or from usage or policy, or for emphasis, or for the sake of

the constitutional number of the Estates of the realm, or it be

because he have an intuition of the folly of omit they; the same, to

some extent, that builder have regard brick when they plan a

fabric. thus, although King and Lords prove the existence of Commons in

day of the political deluge almost syllogistically, the example of not

include one of the Estates might be imitate, and Commons and King do

not necessitate the conception of an intermediate third, while Lords

and Commons suggest the decapitation of the lead figure. the united

three, however, no long cast reflection on one another, and be an

assurance to this acute politician that his bird be safe. he

preserve game rigorously, and the deduction be the work of instinct

with he. to his mind the game-law be the corner-stone of Law, and

of a man’s right to hold his own; and so delicately do he think the

country poise, that an attack on they threaten the structure of

justice. the three conjoin estate be therefore his head

gamekeeper; their duty be to back he against the poacher, if they

would not see the country tumble. as to his under-gamekeeper, he be

their intimate and their friend, say, with none of the misanthropy

which proclaim the virtue of the faithful dog to the confusion of

humankind, he like their company well than that of his equal, and

learn more from they. they also listen deferentially to their

instructor.



the conversation he delight in most might have be go on in any

century since the Conquest. grant he his not unreasonable argument

upon his property in game, he be a liberal landlord. no tenant be

force to take his farm. he drag none by the collar. he give they

liberty to go to Australia, Canada, the Americas, if they like. he

ask in return to have the liberty to shoot on his own ground, and

rear the mark for his shot, treat the question of indemnification

as a gentleman should. still there be grumble tenant. he swarm

with game, and, though he be liberal, his hare and his bird be

immensely destructive: computation could not fix the damage do by

they. probably the farmer expect they not to eat. “there be two

party to a bargain,” say Everard, “and one get the bad of it. but

if he be never oblige to make it, where’s his right to complain?” man

of sense rarely obtain satisfactory answer: they be provoke to

despise their kind. but the poacher be another kind of vermin than the

stupid tenant. Everard do he the honour to hate he, and twice in a

fray have he collar his ruffian, and subsequently sit in condemnation

of the wretch: for he who can attest a villany be well qualified to

punish it. gang from the metropolis find he too determined and alert

for their sport. it be the factiousness of here and there an unbroken

young scoundrelly colt poacher of the neighbourhood, a bear thief, a

fellow damned in an inveterate taste for game, which give he

annoyance. one night he take Master Nevil out with he, and they hunt

down a couple of sinner that show fight against odd. Nevil

attempt to beg they off because of their boldness. “I don’t set my

trap for nothing,” say his uncle, silence he. but the boy

reflect that his uncle be perpetually lament the cowed spirit of

the common English-formerly such fresh and merry man! he touch

Rosamund Culling’s heart with his description of their attitude when

they stand resist and bawl to the keeper, “come on we’ll die for

it.” they do not die. Everard explain to the boy that he could have

kill they, and be content to have send they to gaol for a few

week. Nevil gape at the empty magnanimity which his uncle present

to he as a remarkably big morsel. at the age of fourteen he be

despatch to sea.



he go unwillingly; not so much from an objection to a naval life as

from a wish, incomprehensible to grown man and boy, and especially to

his cousin, Cecil Baskelett, that he might remain at school and learn.

“the fellow would like to be a parson!” Everard say in disgust. no

parson have ever be know of in the Romfrey family, or in the

Beauchamp. a legend of a parson that have be a tutor in one of the

Romfrey house, and have talk and sing blandly to a damsel of the

blood—degenerate maid—to receive a handsome trouncing for his pain,

instead of the holy marriage-tie he aim at, be the only connection

of the Romfreys with the parsonry, as Everard call they. he

attribute the boy’s feeling to the influence of his great-aunt

Beauchamp, who would, he say, infallibly have make a parson of he.

“I’d rather enlist for a soldier,” Nevil say, and he cease to dream

of rebellion, and of his little property of a few thousand pound in

the fund to aid he in it. he confess to his dear friend Rosamund

cull that he think the parson happy in have time to read

history. and oh, to feel for certain _which_ side be the wrong side in

our Civil War, so that one should not hesitate in choose! such

puzzle be never, he seem to be aware, solve in a midshipman’s

mess. he hate bloodshed, and be guilty of the “cotton-spinner’

babble,” abhor of Everard, in allude to it. Rosamund like he for

his humanity; but she, too, fear he be a slack Romfrey when she

hear he speak in precocious contempt of glory. somewhere, somehow, he

have get hold of Manchester sarcasm concern glory: a weedy word of

the newspaper have be sow in his bosom perhaps. he say: “I don’t

care to win glory; I know all about that; I’ve see an old hat in the

Louvre.” and he would have have she to suppose that he have look on the

campaign head-cover of Napoleon simply as a shocking bad, bald,

brown-rub old _tricorne_ rather than as the nod of extinction to

thousand, the great orb of darkness, the still-tremble gloomy

quiver—the brain of the lightning of battle.



now this boy nurse no secret presumptuous belief that he be fit

for the walk of the high intellect; he be not have his impudent

boy’s fle at superiority over the superior, as here and there a

subtle-minded vain juvenile will; nor be he a parrot repeat a line

from some lancastrian pamphlet. he really dislike war and the sword;

and scorn the prospect of an idle life, confess that his

ability barely adapt he for a sailor’s, he be oppose to the

career open to he almost to the extreme of shrinking and terror. or

that be the impression convey to a not unsympathetic hearer by his

forlorn effort to make himself understand, which be like the

tapping of the stick of a blind man mystify by his sense of touch at

wrong corner. his bewilderment and speechlessness be a comic

display, tragic to he.



just as his uncle Everard predict, he come home from his first voyage

a pleasant sailor lad. his feature, more than handsome to a woman, so

mobile they be, shone of sea and spirit, the chance light of the

sea, and the spirit breathe out of it. as to war and bloodshed, a

man’s first thought must be his country, young Jacket remark, and

_ich dien_ be the good motto afloat. Rosamund notice the peculiarity

of the book he select for his private reading. they be not boy’

book, book of adventure and the like. his favourite author be one

writing of Heroes, in (so she esteem it) a style resemble either

early architecture or utter dilapidation, so loose and rough it seem;

a wind-in-the-orchard style, that tumble down here and there an

appreciable fruit with uncouth bluster; sentence without commencement

run to abrupt ending and smoke, like wave against a sea-wall,

learn dictionary word give a hand to street-slang, and accent

fall on they haphazard, like slant ray from drive cloud; all the

page in a breeze, the whole book produce a kind of electrical

agitation in the mind and the joint. this be its effect on the lady.

to she the incomprehensible be the abominable, for she have our

country’s high critical feeling; but he, while admit that he could

not quite master it, like it. he have dig the book out of a

bookseller’s shop in Malta, captivate by its title, and have, since the

day of his purchase, go at it again and again, get nibble of

golden meaning by instalment, as with a solitary pick in a very dark

mine, until the illumination of an idea strike he that there be a

great deal more in the book than there be in himself. this be

sufficient to secure the devoted attachment of young Mr. Beauchamp.

Rosamund sigh with apprehension to think of his unlikeness to boy

and man among his countryman in some thing. why should he hug a book

he own he could not quite comprehend? he say he like a bone in his

mouth; and it be natural wisdom, though unappreciate by woman. a bone

in a boy’s mind for he to gnaw and worry, correct the vagrancy and

promote the healthy activity, whether there be marrow in it or not.

suppose it furnish only dramatic entertainment in that usually

vacant tenement, or powder-shell, it will be of service.



Nevil propose to she that her next present should be the entire list

of his beloved Incomprehensible’s publish work, and she promise,

and be not sorry to keep her promise dangle at the skirt of memory,

to drop away in time. for that fire-and-smoke writer dedicated volume

to the praise of a regicide. nice reading for her dear boy! some week

after Nevil be off again, she abuse herself for her half-hearted love

of he, and would have give he anything—the last word in favour of

the Country versus the royal Martyr, for example, have he insist on

it. she gather, bit by bit, that he have dash at his big blustering

cousin Cecil to vindicate her good name. the direful youth fight in

the Steynham stable, overhear by the groom. Everard receive a fine

account of the tussle from these latter, and Rosamund, know he to

be of the order of gentleman who, whatsoever their sin, will at all

cost protect a woman’s delicacy, and a dependant’s, man or woman, do

not fear to have her ear shock in probe he on the subject.



Everard be lead to say that Nevil’s cousin be bedevil with

womanfolk.



from which Rosamund perceive that woman have be at work; and if so,

it be upon the business of the scandal-monger; and if so, Nevil fight

his cousin to protect her good name from a babbler of the family

gossip.



she speak to Stukely Culbrett, her dead husband’s friend, to whose

recommendation she be indebted for her place in Everard Romfrey’s

household.



“Nevil behave like a knight, I hear.”



“your beauty be dispute,” say he, “and Nevil knock the blind man

down for not be able to see.”



she think, “not my beauty! Nevil strike his cousin on behalf of the

only fair thing I have leave to I!”



this be a moment with she when many sensation rush together and form

a knot in sensitive nature. she have be very good-look. she be

good-look still, but she remember the bloom of her look in her

husband’s day (the tragedy of the mirror be one for a woman to write:

I be ashamed to find myself smile while the poor lady weep), she

remember his praise, her pride; his death in battle, her anguish:

then, on her strange entry to this house, her bitter wish to be old;

and then, the oppressive calm of her recognition of her wish’s

fulfilment, the heavy drop to dead earth, when she could say, or

pretend to think she could say—I look old enough: will they tattle of

I now? nevil’s championship of her good name bring her history

spin about her head, and throw a finger of light on her real

position. in that she see the slenderness of her hold on respect, as

well as feel her personal stainlessness. the boy warm her chill

widowhood. it be write that she, second love should be of the

pattern of mother’s love. she love he hungrily and jealously, always

in fear for he when he be absent, even anxiously when she have he

near. for some cause, bear, one may fancy, of the hour of her love’s

conception, his image in her heart be steep in tear. she be not,

happily, one of the woman who betray strong feeling, and humour

preserve she from excess of sentiment.









CHAPTER III.

contain baronial views of the present TIME





upon the word of honour of Rosamund, the letter to the officer of the

French Guard be post.



“post it, post it,” Everard say, on her consult he, with the

letter in her hand. “let the fellow stand his luck.” it be address

to the Colonel of the First Regiment of the Imperial Guard, Paris. that

superscription have be suggest by Colonel Halkett. Rosamund be in

favour of address it to Versailles, Nevil to the Tuileries; but

Paris could hardly fail to hit the mark, and Nevil wait for the

reply, half expect an appointment on the french sand: for the act

of post a letter, though it be to little short of the Pleiades even,

will stamp an incredible proceeding as a matter of business, so ready

be the ardent mind to take footing on the last thing do. the flight

of Mr. Beauchamp’s letter place it in the common order of occurrence

for the youthful author of it. Jack Wilmore, a messmate, offer to

second he, though he should be dismiss the service for it. another

second would easily be find somewhere; for, as Nevil observe, you

have only to set these affair go, and british blood rise: we be

not the people you see on the surface. Wilmore’s father be a parson,

for instance. what do he do? he could not help himself: he supply

the army and navy with recruit! one son be in a march regiment,

the other be Jack, and three girl have vow never to quit the rectory

save as bride of officer. nevil think that seem encouraging; we

be evidently not a nation of shopkeeper at heart; and he quote

saying of Mr. Stukely Culbrett’s, in which neither his ear nor

Wilmore’s detect the under-ring stukely be famous for: as that

England have saddle herself with India for the express purpose of

well obey the Commandments in Europe; and that it would be a

lamentable thing for the Continent and our doctrine if ever beef

should fail the Briton, and such like. “depend upon it we’re a fighting

nation naturally, Jack,” say Nevil. “how can we submit!... however, I

shall not be impatient. I dislike duelling, and hate war, but I will

have the country respect.” they plan a defence of the country,

draw their strategy from magazine article by military pen,

reverberation of the extinct voice of the daily and weekly journal,

customary after a panic, and make bloody stand on spot of extreme

pastoral beauty, which they visit by coach and rail, look back on

unfortified London with particular melancholy.



Rosamund’s word may be trust that she drop the letter into a

London post-office in pursuance of her promise to Nevil. the singular

fact be that no answer to it ever arrive. Nevil, without a doubt of

her honesty, propose an expedition to Paris; he be order to join

his ship, and he lie moor across the water in the port of Bevisham,

pant for notice to be take of he. the slight of the total

disregard of his letter now affect he personally; it take he some

time to get over this indignity put upon he, especially because of his

be under the impression that the country suffer, not he at all.

the letter have serve its object: ever since the transmission of it the

menace and insult have cease.



but they might be renew, and he desire to stop they altogether. his

last feeling be one of genuine regret that Frenchmen should have

behave unworthily of the high estimation he hold they in. with which

he dismiss the affair.



he be rally about it when he next sit at his uncle’s table, and have

to pardon Rosamund for tell.



Nevil reply modestly: “I dare say you think I half a fool, sir. all

I know be, I wait for my better to speak first. I have no dislike of

frenchman.”



Everard shake his head to signify, “not _half_.” but he be gentle

enough in his observation. “there’s a motto, Ex pede Herculem. you

step out for the dog to judge well of we. it’s an infernally

trip motto for a composite structure like the kingdom of Great

Britain and Manchester, boy Nevil. we can fight foreigner when the

time come.” he direct Nevil to look home, and cast an eye on the

cotton-spinner, with the remark that they be bind we hand and

foot to sell we to the big buyer, and be not englishman but

“Germans and Jews, and quaker and hybrid, diligent clerk and

speculator, and commercial traveller, who have raise a fortune from

foist drug good on an idiot population.”



he loathe they for the curse they be to the country. and he be one

of the few who speak out. the fashion be to pet they. we stand against

they; be halfhearte, and be beat; and then we pet they, and

bit by bit our privilege be tear away. we make lord of they to

catch they, and they grocer of we by way of a return. “already,” say

Everard, “they have knock the nation’s head off, and dry-rot the

bone of the people.”



“don’t they,” Nevil ask, “belong to the liberal party?”



“I’ll tell you,” Everard reply, “they belong to any party that upset

the party above they. they belong to the GEORGE FOXE party, and my

poultry-roost be the mark they aim at. you shall have a glance at the

manufacturing district some day. you shall see the machine they work

with. you shall see the miserable lank-jaw half-stew pantaloon

they’ve manage to make of Englishmen there. my blood’s past boiling.

they work young child in their factory from morning to night.

their manufactory be spread like the webs of the devil to suck

the blood of the country. in that district of theirs an epidemic level

man like a disease in sheep. skeleton cann’t make a stand. on the top

of it all they sing Sunday tune!”



this behaviour of corn-law agitator and protector of poacher be an

hypocrisy too horrible for comment. Everard sip claret. Nevil lash

his head for the clear idea which objurgation insist upon implanting,

but batter to piece in the act.



“Manchester’s the belly of this country!” Everard continue. “so long

as Manchester flourish, we’re a country govern and lead by the

belly. the head and the leg of the country be sound still; I don’t

guarantee it for long, but the middle’s rapacious and corrupt. take it

on a question of foreign affair, it’s an alderman after a feast. bring

it upon home politic, you meet a wolf.”



the faithful Whig veteran speak with jolly admiration of the speech of

a famous Tory chief.



“that be the way to talk to they! denounce they traitor! up whip, and

set the ruffians caper! hit they facer! our man be always for the

too-clever trick. they pluck the sprout and eat they, as if the loss

of a sprout or two thin Manchester! your policy of absorption be

good enough when you’re deal with fragment. it’s a devilish unlucky

thing to attempt with a concrete mass. you might as well ask your head

to absorb a wall by run at it like a pugnacious nigger. I don’t

want you to go into Parliament ever. you’re a fitter man out of it; but

if ever you’re bite—and it’s the curse of our country to have

politic as well as the other disease—don’t follow a flag, be

independent, keep a free vote; remember how I’ve be tie, and hold

foot against Manchester. do it blindfold; you don’t want counselling,

you’re sure to be right. I’ll lay you a blood-brood mare to a cabstand

skeleton, you’ll have an easy conscience and deserve the thank of the

country.”



Nevil listen gravely. the soundness of the head and leg of the

country he take for grant. the inflated state of the unchivalrous

middle, denominate Manchester, terrify he. could it be true that

England be betray sign of decay? and sign how ignoble!

half-a-dozen crescent line cunningly turn, sketch her figure

before the world, and the reflection for one ready to die uphold she

be that the portrait be no caricature. such an emblematic

presentation of the land of his filial affection haunt he with

hideous mockery. surely the foreigner hear our boast of she must

compare we to showmen bawl the attraction of a Fat Lady at a fair!



Swoln Manchester bear the blame of it. Everard exult to hear his

young echo attack the cotton-spinner. but Nevil be for a plan, a

system, immediate action; the descending among the people, and take

an initiative, lead they, insist on their following, not stand

aloof and shrug.



“we lead they in war,” say he; “why not in peace? there’s a front for

peace as well as war, and that’s our place rightly. we’re push aside;

why, it seem to I we’re treat like old-fashioned ornament! the

fault must be ours. shrug and sneer be about as honourable as

blaze firework over your own defeat. back we have to go! that’s the

point, sir. and as for jeer the cotton-spinner, I cann’t while

they’ve the lead of we. we let they have it! and we have thrice the

stake in the country. I don’t mean property and title.”



“deuce you don’t,” say his uncle.



“I mean our name, our history; I mean our duty. as for title, the

way to defend they be to be worthy of they.”



“damned fine speech,” remark Everard. “now you get out of that trick

of prize-orationing. I call it snuffery, sir; it’s all to your own

nose! you’re talk to I, not to a gallery. ‘Worthy of they!’ Caesar

wrap his head in his robe: he get his dig in the rib for all his

attitudinizing. it’s very well for a man to talk like that who own no

more than his barebodkin life, poor devil. tall talk’s his jewelry: he

must have his dandification in bunkum. you ought to know well.

property and title be worth have, whether you be ‘worthy of they’

or a disgrace to your class. the good way of defend they be to keep

a strong fist, and take care you don’t draw your fore-foot back more

than enough.”



“please propose something to be do,” say Nevil, depress by the

recommendation of that attitude.



Everard propose a fight for every privilege his class possess. “they

say,” he say, “a nobleman fight the odd be a sight for the god:

and I wouldn’t yield an inch of ground. it’s no use call thing by

fine name—the country’s ruin by cowardice. Poursuivez! I cry. haro!

at they! the big hart win in the end. I haven’t a doubt about

that. and I haven’t a doubt we carry the tonnage.”



“there’s the people,” sigh Nevil, entangle in his uncle’s haziness.



“what people?”



“I suppose the people of Great Britain count, sir.”



“of course they do; when the battle’s do, the fight lose and win.”



“do you expect the people to look on, sir?”



“the people always wait for the winner, boy Nevil.”



the young fellow exclaim despondingly, “if it be a race!”



“it’s like a race, and we’re confoundedly out of training,” say

Everard.



there he rest. a mediaeval gentleman with the docile notion of the

twelfth century, complacently drive they to grass and wattle they

in the nineteenth, could be of no use to a boy try to think, though

he could set the youngster galloping. nevil wander about the wood of

Steynham, disincline to shoot and lend a hand to country sport. the

popping of the gun of his uncle and guest hang about his ear much

like their speech, which be unobjectionable in itself, but not

sufficient; a little hard, he think, a little idle. he want

something, and want they to give their time and energy to something,

that be not to be have in a market. the noble, he feel sure, might

resume their natural alliance with the people, and lead they, as they

do of old, to the battle-field. how might they? a comely Sussex lass

could not well tell he how. sarcastic report of the troublesome

questioner represent he apply to a nymph of the country for

enlightenment. he thrill surprisingly under the charm of feminine

beauty. “the fellow’s sound at bottom,” his uncle say, hear of his

having really be see walk in the complete form proper to his

bud age, that is, in two half. Nevil show that he have gain an

acquaintance with the struggle of the neighbour agricultural poor

to live and rear their child. his uncle’s table roar at his

enumeration of the sickly little being, consumptive or bandy-legged,

within a radius of five mile of Steynham. action be what he want,

Everard say. Nevil perhaps think the same, for he dash out of his

mooning with a wave of the Tory standard, delight the lady, though

in that conflict of the Lion and the Unicorn (which be a Tory song) he

seem rather to wish to goad the dear lion than crush the one-horn

intrusive upstart. his call on the crack corps of Peers to enrol

themselves forthwith in the front rank, and to anticipate opposition

by initiate measure, and so cut out that funny old crazy old

galleon, the People, from under the battery of the enemy, highly

amuse the gentleman.



before rejoin his ship, Nevil pay his customary short visit of

ceremony to his great-aunt Beauchamp—a venerable lady past eighty,

hitherto divide from he in sympathy by her dislike of his uncle

Everard, who have once be his live hero. that be when he be in

frock, and still the tenacious fellow could not bear to hear his uncle

speak ill of.



“all the man of that family be heartless, and he be a man of wood, my

dear, and a bad man,” the old lady say. “he should have keep you at

school, and send you to college. you want read and teaching and

talk to. such a house as that be should never be a home for you.”

she hint at Rosamund. Nevil defend the persecute woman, but with

no well success than from the attack of the Romfrey lady; with

this difference, however, that these decry the woman’s vicious art,

and Mistress Elizabeth Mary Beauchamp put all the sin upon the man.

such a man! she say. “let I hear that he have marry she, I will not

utter another word.” Nevil echo, “marry!” in a different key.



“I be as much of an aristocrat as any of you, only I rank morality

higher,” say Mrs. Beauchamp. “when you be a child I offer to take

you and make you my heir, and _i_ would have educate you. you shall

see a great-nephew of mine that I do educate; he be eat his dinner

for the bar in London, and come to I every Sunday. I shall marry he

to a good girl, and I shall show your uncle what my kind of man-making

be.”



Nevil have no desire to meet the other great-nephew, especially when he

be aware of the extraordinary circumstance that a Beauchamp

great-niece, have no money, have bestow her hand on a Manchester man

defunct, whereof this young Blackburn Tuckham, the lawyer, be issue.

he take his leave of Mrs. Elizabeth Beauchamp, respect she for her

constitutional health and brightness, and regret for the sake of

the country that she have not marry to give England man and woman

resemble she. on the whole he consider her wise in her

prescription for the malady beset he than his uncle. he know that

action be but a temporary remedy. college would have be his chronic

medicine, and the old lady’s acuteness in see it impress he

forcibly. she have give he a peaceable two day on the Upper Thames,

in an atmosphere of plain good sense and just-mindedness. he write to

thank she, say:



“my England at sea will be your parlour-window looking down the grass

to the river and rush; and when you do I the honour to write, please

tell I the name of those wildflower grow along the bank in

Summer.” the old lady reply immediately, enclose a cheque for fifty

pound: “Colonel Halkett inform I you be under a cloud at Steynham,

and I have think you may be in want of pocket-money. the wild-flower

be willowherb, meadow-sweet, and loosestrife. I shall be glad when you

be here in Summer to see they.”



Nevil despatch the following: “I thank you, but I shall not cash the

cheque. the Steynham tale be this:



I happen to be out at night, and stop the keeper in chase of a

young fellow trespassing. I catch he myself, but recognize he as

one of a family I take an interest in, and let he run before they come

up. my uncle hear a gun; I send the head gamekeeper word in the

morning to out with it all. Uncle E. be annoy, and we have a rough

parting. if you be reward I for this, I have no right to it.”



Mrs. Beauchamp rejoin: “your profession should teach you

subordination, if it do nothing else that be valuable to a Christian

gentleman. you will receive from the publisher the ‘Life and letter of

Lord Collingwood,’ whom I have it in my mind that a young midshipman

should task himself to imitate. spend the money as you think fit.”



Nevil’s ship, command by Captain Robert Hall (a most gallant officer,

one of his hero, and of Lancashire origin, strangely!), fly to the

south american station, in and about Lord Cochrane’s water; then as

swiftly back. for, like the frail norwegian bark on the edge of the

maelstrom, liker to a country of conflict interest and passion,

that be not mentally on a level with its good fortune, England be

drift into foreign complication. a paralyzed Minister proclaim

it. the govern people, which be look to for direction in grave

dilemma by its representative and reflector, shout that it have

be accuse of pusillanimity. no one have any desire for war, only we

really have (and it be perfectly true) be talk gigantic nonsense

of peace, and of the everlastingness of the exchange of fruit for

money, with angel wave raw-grocery of Eden in joy of the

commercial picture. therefore, to correct the excess of that fit, we

hold the standing by the Moslem, on behalf of the Mediterranean (and

the Moslem be one of our customer, bear an excellent reputation for

the payment of debt), to be good, grant the necessity. we deplore

the necessity. the Press weep over it. that, however, be not the

politic tone for we while the imperial berg of Polar ice watch we

keenly; and the Press proceed to remind we that we have once be

bull-dog. be there not an animal within we have a right to a turn

now and then? and be it not (Falstaff, on a calm world, be quote)

for the benefit of our constitution now and then to loosen the animal?

grant the necessity, of course. by dint of incessantly speak of

the necessity we grant it unknowingly. the light heart regard

our period of monotonously lyrical prosperity as a man sensible of

fresh morning air look back on the snore bolster. many of the graver

be glad of a change. after all that maunder over the blessed peace

which bring the raisin and the currant for the pudding, and shut up

the cannon with a sheep’s head, it become a principle of popular taste

to descant on the vivifying virtue of war; even as, after ten month

of money-monger in smoky London, the citizen hail the sea-breeze

and an immersion in unruly brine, despite the cost, that breeze and

brine may make a man of he, accord to the doctor’s prescription:

sweet be home, but health be sweeter! then be there another curious

exhibition of we. gentleman, to the exact number of the Graces, dress

in drab of an ancient cut, make a pilgrimage to the icy despot, and

besought he to give way for Piety’s sake. he, courteous, colossal, and

immoveable, wave they homeward. they return and be hoot for

belie the bellicose by their mission, and interpret too well the

peaceful. they be the unparalyzed Ministers of the occasion, but

helpless.



and now come war, the purifier and the pestilence.



the cry of the english people for war be pretty general, as far as the

crier go. they put on their Sabbath face concern the declaration

of war, and tell with approval how the royal hand have tremble in

commit itself to the form of signature to which its action be

limited. if there be money to be pay, there be a bugbear to be slay

for it; and a bugbear be as obnoxious to the repose of commercial

community as rival be to king.



the cry for war be absolutely unanimous, and a supremely national cry,

Everard Romfrey say, for it exclude the cotton-spinner.



he smack his hand, crow at the vociferation of disgust of those

negrophile and sweater of Christians, whose isolate clamour amid the

popular uproar sound of gag mouth.



one of the half-stifle cotton-spinner, a notorious one, a spouter of

rank sedition and hater of aristocracy, a political poacher, manage to

make himself hear. he be toss to the Press for morsel, and toss

back to the people in strip. Everard have a sharp return of appetite in

read the daily and weekly journal. they print logic, they print

sense; they abuse the treasonable barking cur unmercifully. they

print almost as much as he would have utter, except the strong

salt of his simile, liken that rascal and his crew to the american

weed in our water, to the rot wild bee’ nest in our tree, to the

worm in our ship’ timber, and to lamentable affliction of the human

frame, and of sheep, oxen, honest hound. Manchester be in eclipse.

the world of England discover that the peace-party which oppose be

the actual cause of the war: never be indication clear. but my

business be with Mr. Beauchamp, to know whom, and partly understand his

conduct in after-day, it will be as well to take a bird’seye glance at

he through the war.



“now,” say Everard, “we shall see what staff there be in that fellow

Nevil.”



he expect, as you may imagine, a true young Beauchamp-Romfrey to be

strain his collar like a leash-hound.









CHAPTER IV.

a glimpse of nevil in ACTION





the young gentleman to whom Everard Romfrey transfer his combative

spirit despatch a letter from the Dardanelles, request his uncle

not to ask he for a spark of enthusiasm. he despise our Moslem

ally, he say, and think with pity of the miserable herd of man in

regiment march across the steppe at the bidding of a despot that

we be help to popularize. he certainly write in the tone of a

jejune politician; pardonable stuff to senior entertain similar

opinion, but most exasperating when it run counter to they: though

one question put by Nevil be not easily answerable. he wish to know

whether the english people would be so anxious to be at it if their man

stand on the opposite shore and talk of try conclusion on their

green field. and he suggest that they have become so ready for war

because of their having grow rather ashamed of themselves, and for the

special reason that they could have it at a distance.



“the rascal’s liver’s out of order,” Everard say.



come to the sentence: “who speak out in this crisis? there be one,

and I be with he”; Mr. Romfrey’s compassionate sentiment veer round

to irate amazement. for the person allude to be indeed the infamous

miaule cotton-spinner. Nevil admire he. he say so bluntly. he

point to that traitorous George-Foxite as the one heroical Englishman

of his day, declare that he feel bind in honour to make known his

admiration for the man; and he hope his uncle would excuse he. “if we

differ, I be sorry, sir; but I should be a coward to withhold what I

think of he when he have all England against he, and he be in the

right, as England will discover. I maintain he speak wisely—I don’t

mind say, like a prophet; and he speak on behalf of the poor as

well as of the country. he appear to I the only public man who look

to the state of the poor—I mean, their interest. they pay for war, and

if we be to have peace at home and strength for a really national war,

the only war we can ever call necessary, the poor must be content. he

see that. I shall not run the risk of anger you by write to

defend he, unless I hear of his be shamefully mishandle, and the

bearer of an old name can be of service to he. I cannot say less, and

will say no more.”



Everard apostrophize his absent nephew: “you jackass!”



I be remind by Mr. Romfrey’s profound disappointment in the youth,

that it will be repeatedly share by many other: and I be bind to

forewarn reader of this history that there be no plot in it. the hero

be chargeable with the official disqualification of constantly

offending prejudice, never seek to please; and all the while it be

upon he the narrative hang. to be a public favourite be his last

thought. Beauchampism, as one confront he call it, may be say to

stand for nearly everything which be the obverse of Byronism, and

rarely woo your sympathy, shun the statuesque pathetic, or any kind

of posturing. for Beauchamp will not even look at happiness to mourn

its absence; melodious lamentation, demoniacal scorn, be quite alien

to he. his faith be in work and fighting. with every inducement to

offer himself for a romantic figure, he despise the pomade and

curling-iron of modern romance, its shear and its label: in fine,

every one of those positive thing by whose aid, and by some adroit

flourishing of they, the nimbus know as a mysterious halo be produce

about a gentleman’s head. and a highly alluring adornment it be! we be

all give to lose our solidity and fly at it; although the faithful

mirror of fiction have be show we latterly that a too superhuman

beauty have disturb popular belief in the bare beginning of the

existence of hero: but this, very likely, be nothing more than a fit

of Republicanism in the nursery, and a deposition of the lead doll

for lack of variety in he. that conqueror of circumstance will, the

dull soul may begin predict, return on his cockhorse to favour

and authority. meantime the exhibition of a hero whom circumstance

overcome, and who do not weep or ask you for a tear, who continually

forfeit attractiveness by decline to better his own fortune, must

run the chance of a novelty during the interregnum. nursery

legitimist will be against he to a man; republican likewise, after a

queer sniff at his pretension, it be to be fear. for I, I have so

little command over he, that in spite of my nursery taste, he drag

I whither he list. it be artless art and monstrous innovation to

present so wilful a figure, but be I to create a striking fable for

he, and set he off with scenic effect and contrast, it would be

only a momentary tonic to you, to he instant death. he could not live

in such an atmosphere. the simple truth have to be tell: how he love

his country, and for another and a broad love, grow out of his

first passion, fight it; and be small by comparison, and find no

giant of the Philistines dispose to receive a stone in his fore-skull,

pummel the obmutescent mass, to the confusion of a conceivable epic.

his indifferent England refuse it to he. that be all I can say. the

great power of the two, she seem, with a quiet derision that do

not belie her amiable passivity, to have reduce in Beauchamp’s career

the bold readiness for public action, and some good stout effort

besides, to the flat result of an optically discernible influence of

our hero’s character in the domestic circle; perhaps a faintly-outline

circle or two beyond it. but this do not forbid he to be rank as

one of the most distinguishing of her child of the day he live in.

blame the victrix if you think he should have be lively.



Nevil soon have to turn his telescope from politic. the torch of war

be actually light, and he be not fashion to be heedless of what

surround he. our diplomacy, after dance with all the suppleness of

stilt, gravely resign the gift of motion. our dauntless Lancastrian

thunder like a tempest over a gambling tent, disregard. our worthy

people, consent to the doctrine that war be a scourge, contract

the habit of think it, in this case, the dire necessity which be the

sole excuse for give way to an irritated pugnacity, and suck the

comforting caramel of an alliance with their troublesome next-door

neighbour, profuse in comfit as in scorpion. Nevil detect that

politic element of their promptitude for war. his recollection of

dissatisfaction in former day assist he to perceive the nature of

it, but he be too young to hold his own against the hubbub of a noisy

people, much too young to remain sceptical of a modern people’s

enthusiasm for war while journal be testify to it down the length

of their column, and letter from home palpitate with it, and

shipmate yawn wearily for the signal, and shipload of red coat and

blue, infantry, cavalry, artillery, be singe farewell to the girl

at home, and hurrah for anything in foreign water. he join the

stream with a cordial spirit. since it must be so! the wind of that

haughty proceeding of the Great Bear in put a paw over the neutral

brook brush his cheek unpleasantly. he clap hand for the fezzy

defender of the border fortress, and when the order come for the fleet

to enter the old romantic sea of storm and fable, he write home a

letter fit for his uncle Everard to read. then there be the sailing

and the landing, and the march up the height, which Nevil be

condemn to look at. to his joy he obtain an appointment on shore,

and after that Everard hear of he from other channel. the two be

of a mind when the savage winter advance which freeze the attack of the

city, and might be image as the hoar god of hostile element point

a hand to the line reach, and menacing at one farther step. both

blame the Government, but they divide as to the origin of

governmental inefficiency; Nevil accuse the Lords guilty of foul

sloth, Everard the Quakers of dry-rot the country. he pass with a

shrug Nevil’s pule outcry for the enemy as well as our own poor

fellow: “at his steppe again!” and he have to be forgive when

report come of his nephew’s turn for overdo his duty:

“show-fighting,” as he term it.



“Braggadocioing in deed be only next bad to mouth it,” he write

very rationally. “Stick to your line. don’t go out of it till you be

order out. remember that we want _soldier_ and _sailor_, we don’t

want _suicide_.” he condescend to these italic, consider

impressiveness to be urgent. in his heart, notwithstanding his

implacably clear judgement, he be passably well pleased with the

congratulation encompass he on account of his nephew’s gallantry

at a period of dejection in Britain: for the winter be dreadful; every

kind heart that go to bed with cold foot feel acutely for our

soldier on the frozen height, and thought of hero be as good as

warming-pan. hero we would have. it happen in war as in wit, that

all the bird of wonder fly to a flaring reputation. he that have do

one wild thing must necessarily have do the other; so Nevil find

himself stand in the thick of a fame that blow rank eulogy on he

for act he have not perform. the Earl of Romfrey forward hamper

and a letter of praise. “they tell I that while you be face the

enemy, temporarily attach yourself to one of the regiment—I forget

which, though I have hear it name—you spring out under fire on an

eagle claw a hare. I like that. I hope you have the benefit of the

hare. she be our property, and I have issue an injunction that she

shall not go into the newspaper.” Everard be entirely of a contrary

opinion concern the episode of eagle and hare, though it be a case

of a bird of prey interfere with an object of the chase. Nevil write

home most entreatingly and imperatively, like one wincing, beg he

to contradict that and certain other story, and prescribe the form

of a public renunciation of his proclaimed part in they. “the hare,” he

send word, “be the property of young Michell of the _Rodney_, and he be

the human and the gallant fellow in the service. I have write

to my Lord. pray help to rid I of burden that make I feel like a

robber and impostor.”



Everard reply:



“I have a letter from your captain, inform I that I be unlikely to

see you home unless you learn to hold yourself in. I wish you be in

another battery than Robert Hall’s. he forget the force of example,

however much of a dab he may be at precept. but there you be, and

please clap a hundredweight on your appetite for figure, will you. do

you think there be any good in help to frenchify our army? I loathe

a fellow who shoot at a medal. I wager he be easy enough to be catch

by circumvention—put I in the open with he. Tom Biggot, the boxer,

go over to Paris, and stand in the ring with one of their dancing

pugilist, and the first round he get a crack on the chin from the

rogue’s foot; the second round he catch he by the lift leg, and

punish he till pec be all he could say of peccavi. fight the

straightforward fight. Hang flan! battle be a game of give and take,

and if our man get elanne, we shall see they refuse to come up to

time. this new crossing and medalling be the devil’s own notion for

upset a solid british line, and tempting fellow to get invalid

that they may blaze it before the shopkeeper and their wife in the

city. give we an army!—none of your caperer. here be lot of circusy

hero come home to rest after their fatigue. one be spout at a

public dinner yesterday night. he go into it upright, and he run out

of it upright—at the head of his men!—and here he be feast by the

citizen and make a speech upright, and my boy front the enemy!”



Everard’s involuntary break-down from his veteran’s roughness to a

touch of feel thrilled Nevil, who begin to perceive what his uncle

be drive at when he rebuke the coxcombry of the field, and speak of

the description of compliment your hero be pay Englishmen in

affect to give they example of bravery and preternatural coolness.

Nevil send home humble confession of guilt in this respect, with fresh

praise of young Michell: for though Everard, as Nevil recognize it,

be perfectly right in the abstract, and generally right, there be

time when an example be need by brave man—time when the fiery

furnace of death’s dragon-jaw be not invite even to Englishmen

receive the word that duty bid they advance, and they require a

leader of the way. a national coxcombry that pretend to an

independence of human sensation, and make a motto of our dandiacal

courage, be more perilous to the army of the nation than that of a

few hero. it be this coxcombry which have too often cause disdain of

the wise chief’s maxim of calculation for winner, namely, to have

always the odd on your side, and which have bleed, shatter, and

occasionally disgrace we. Young Michell’s carry powder-bag to the

assault, and when order to retire, bear they on his back, and

help a wound soldier on the way, do surely well; nor do Mr.

Beauchamp himself behave so badly on an occasion when the sailor of

his battery catch he out of a fire of shell that raise jet of dust

and smoke like a range of geyser over the open, and hug he as

love woman do at a meeting or a parting. he be penitent before his

uncle, admit, first, that the man be not in want of an example of

the contempt of death, and secondly, that he doubt whether it be

contempt of death on his part so much as pride—a hatred of be see

run.



“I don’t like the fellow to be draw it so fine,” say Everard. it

sound to he a trifle parsonical. but his heart be win by Nevil’s

determination to wear out the campaign rather than be invalid or

entrust with a holiday duty.



“I see with shame (admiration of _they_) old infantry captain and

colonel of no position beyond their rank in the army, stick to

their post,” say Nevil, “and a lord and a lord and a lord slip off

as though the stuff of the man in he have melt. I shall go through

with it.” Everard approve he. Colonel Halkett write that the youth

be a skeleton. still Everard encourage he to persevere, and say of

he:



“I like he for hold to his work _after_ the strain’s over. that

tell the man.”



he observe at his table, in reply to commendation of his nephew:



“Nevil’s leak be his political craze, and that seem to be go: I

hope it be. you cann’t rear a man on politic. when I be of his age I

never look at the newspaper, except to read the divorce case. I

come to politic with a ripe judgement. he shine in action, and he’ll

find that out, and leave other the palavering.”



it be upon the close of the war that Nevil drive his uncle to avow a

downright undisguised indignation with he. he catch a fever in the

french camp, where he be dispense viver and provend out of english

hamper.



“those french fellow be every man of they train up to

snapping-point,” say Everard. “you’re sure to have they if you hold

out long against they. and greedy dog too: they’re for half our

hamper, and all the glory. and there’s nevil down on his back in the

thick of they! will anybody tell I why the devil he must be poke

into the french camp? they be ready enough to run to he and beg

potato. it’s all for humanity he do it—mark that. never be a word

fitter for a quack’s mouth than ‘humanity.’ two syllable more, and the

parson would be ride it to sawdust. humanity! humanitomtity! it’s

the good word of the two for half the thing do in the name of it.”



a tremendously brace epistle, excellent for an access of fever, be

despatch to humanity’s curate, and Everard sit expect a hot

rejoinder, or else a black seal letter, but neither one nor the other

arrive.



suddenly, to his disgust, come rumour of peace between the mighty

belligerent.



the silver trumpet of peace be nowhere hearken to with

satisfaction by the bull-dog, though triumph ring sonorously through

the music, for they have be severely mangle, as usual at the outset,

and they have at last get their grip, and be in high condition for

fight.



the most expansive panegyrist of our deed do not dare affirm of the

most famous of they, that England have embark her costly cavalry to

offer it for a mark of artillery-ball on three side of a square: and

the belief be universal that we could do more business-like deed and

play the great game of blunder with an ability refine by experience.

Everard Romfrey be one of those who think themselves justify in
