infancy, for _he_ have to do all the humouring before he’s many week

old at it; only there’s the fact!—he soon find out he have to pay for

his first fling, like the son of a family sow his oats to reap his

Jews. credit I, sir, I think it prudent to counteract a bit of an

apothecary’s shop odour in the junior liberal candidate’s address. I

find the town sniff, they scent shrapnel in the composition.”



“every line of it be mine,” say Beauchamp.



“of course it be, and the address be admirably word, sir, I make

bold to say it to your face; but most indubitably it threaten

powerful drug for weak stomach, and it blow cold on vote, which be

sensitive plant like nothing else in botany.”



“if they be only to be get by abandon principle, and by anything

but honesty in state they, they may go,” say Beauchamp.



“I repeat, my dear sir, I repeat, the infant candidate delight in his

honesty, like the babe in its nakedness, the beautiful virgin in her

innocence. so he do; but he discover it’s time for he to wear

clothe in a contested election. and what’s that but to preserve the

outline pretty correctly, whilst he don’t shock and horrify the

optic? a dash of conventionalism make the whole civilized world kin,

ye know. that’s the truth. you must appear to be one of they, for they

to choose you. after all, there’s no harm in a dyer’s hand; and, sir, a

candidate look at his own, when he have win the Election...”



“ah, well,” say Beauchamp, swinge on his heel, “and now I’ll take my

leave of you, and I apologize for bring you down here so early.

please attend to what I have say; it’s peremptory. you will give I

great pleasure by dine with I to-night, at the hotel opposite. will

you? I don’t know what kind of wine I shall be able to offer you.

perhaps you know the cellar, and may help I in that.”



Timothy grasp his hand, “with pleasure, Commander Beauchamp. they

have a bucella over there that’s old, and a tolerable claret, and a

Port to be inquire for under the breath, in a mysteriously intimate

tone of voice, as one say, ‘I know of your treasure, and the corner

under ground where it lie.’ avoid the champagne: ’tis the banquet

wine. ditto the sherry. one can drink they, one can drink they.”



“at a quarter to eight this evening, then,” say Nevil.



“I’ll be there at the stroke of the clock, sure as the date of a bill,”

say Timothy.



and it’s early to guess whether you’ll catch Bevisham or you willn’t, he

reflect, as he gaze at the young gentleman cross the road; but

female Bevisham’s with you, if that count for much. Timothy confess,

that without the employment of any weapon save arrogance and a look of

candour, the commander have go some way toward catch the feminine

side of himself.









CHAPTER XV.

CECILIA HALKETT





Beauchamp walk down to the pier, where he take a boat for H.M.S.

_isis_, to see Jack Wilmore, whom he have not meet since his return from

his last cruise, and first he try the efficacy of a dive in salt

water, as a specific for irritation. it give the edge to a fine

appetite that he continue to satisfy while Wilmore talk of those

famous dog to which the navy have ever be go.



“we want another panic, Beauchamp,” say Lieutenant Wilmore. “no one

know well than you what a naval man have to complain of, so I hope

you’ll get your Election, if only that we may reckon on a good look-out

for the interest of the service. a regular Board with a permanent Lord

High Admiral, and a regular vote of money to keep it up to the mark.

Stick to that. hardist have a vote in Bevisham. I think I can get one or

two more. why ben’t you a tory? no Whigs nor Liberals look after we

half so well as the Tories. it’s enough to break a man’s heart to see

the troop of dockyard workman march out as soon as ever a Liberal

Government march in. then it’s one of our infernal panic again, and

patch here, patch there; every inch of it make-believe! I’ll prove to

you from example that the humbug of Government cause exactly the same

humbug workmanship. it seem as if it be a game of ‘rascal all.’

let they sink we! but, by heaven! one cann’t help feel for the

country. and I do say it’s the doing of those Liberals. skilled

workman, mind you, not to be net again so easily. America reap the

benefit of our folly .... that be a lucky run of yours up the Niger;

the admiral be friendly, but you deserve your luck. for God’s sake,

don’t forget the state of our service when you’re one of our cherub up

aloft, Beauchamp. this I’ll say, I’ve never hear a man talk about it

as you use to in old midshipmite day, whole watch through—don’t you

remember? on the north american station, and in the Black Sea, and the

Mediterranean. and that girl at Malta! I wonder what have become of she?

what a beauty she be! I dare say she ben’t so fine a girl as the

Armenian you unearth on the Bosphorus, but she have something about

she a fellow cann’t forget. that be a lovely creature come down the

hill over Granada on her mule. ay, we’ve see handsome woman, Nevil

Beauchamp. but you always be lucky, invariably, and I should bet on

you for the Election.”



“canvass for I, Jack,” say Beauchamp, smile at his friend’s

unconscious double-skeining of subject. “if I turn out as good a

politician as you be a seaman, I shall do. pounce on Hardist’s vote

without lose a day. I would go to he, but I’ve miss the Halketts

twice. they’re on the Otley river, at a place call Mount Laurels, and

I particularly want to see the colonel. can you give I a boat there,

and come?”



“certainly,” say Wilmore. “I’ve dance there with the lady, the

handsome girl, english style, of her time. and come, come, our

english style’s the good. it wear well, it look well. foreign

woman... they’re capital to flirt with. but a girl like Cecilia

Halkett—one cann’t call she a girl, and it willn’t do to say Goddess, and

queen and charmer be out of the question, though she’s both, and angel

into the bargain; but, by George! what a woman to call wife, you say;

and a man attach to a woman like that never can let himself look

small. no such luck for I; only I swear if I stand between a good and

a bad action, the thought of that girl would keep I straight, and I’ve

only dance with she once!”



not long after sketch this rough presentation of the lady, with a

masculine hand, Wilmore be able to point to she in person on the deck

of her father’s yacht, the _Esperanza_, stand out of Otley river.

there be a gallant splendour in the vessel that throw a touch of glory

on its mistress in the mind of the two young naval officer, as they

pull for she in the ship’s gig.



Wilmore sing out, “give way, man!”



the sailor bend to their oar, and presently the schooner’s head be

put to the wind.



“she see we’re give chase,” Wilmore say. “she cann’t be expect

_I_, so it must be you. no, the colonel don’t race she. they’ve only

be back from Italy six month: I mean the schooner. I remember she

talk of you when I have she for a partner. yes, now I mean Miss

Halkett. Blest if I think she talk of anything else. she see we.

I’ll tell you what she like: she like yacht, she like Italy, she

like painting, like thing old English, awfully fond of hero. I

tell she a tale of one of our man save life. ‘oh!’ say she, ‘don’t

your friend Nevil Beauchamp save a man from drown, off the

guardship, in exactly the same place?’ and next day she send I a

cheque for three pound for the fellow. steady, man! I keep she

letter.”



the boat go smoothly alongside the schooner. Miss Halkett have come to

the side. the oar swing fore and aft, and Beauchamp spring on deck.



Wilmore have to decline Miss Halkett’s invitation to he as well as his

friend, and return in his boat. he leave the pair with a ruffle

breeze, and a sky all sail, prepare, it seem to he, to enjoy the

most delicious you-and-I on salt water that a sailor could dream of;

and placidly envying, devoid of jealousy, there be just enough of

fancy quicken in Lieutenant Wilmore to give he picture of they

without disturbance of his feeling—one of the condition of the

singular visitation we call happiness, if he could have know it.



for a time his visionary eye follow they pretty correctly. so long

since they have part last! such change in the interval! and great

animation in Beauchamp’s gaze, and a blush on Miss Halkett’s cheek.



she say once, “Captain Beauchamp.” he retort with a solemn

formality. they smile, and immediately take footing on their previous

intimacy.



“how good it be of you to come twice to Mount Laurels,” say she. “I

have not miss you to-day. no address be on your card. where be you

stay in the neighbourhood? at Mr. Lespel’s?”



“I’m stay at a Bevisham hotel,” say Beauchamp.



“you have not be to Steynham yet? papa come home from Steynham

to-night.”



“do he? well, the _Ariadne_ be only just pay off, and I cann’t well

go to Steynham yet. I—” Beauchamp be astonish at the hesitation he

find in himself to name it: “I have business in Bevisham.”



“naval business?” she remark.



“no,” say he.



the sensitive prescience we have of a critical distaste of our

proceeding be, the world be aware, keener than our intuition of

contrary opinion; and for the sake of preserve the sweet outward

form of friendliness, Beauchamp be anxious not to speak of the

business in Bevisham just then, but she look and he have hesitate, so

he say flatly, “I be one of the candidate for the borough.”



“indeed!”



“and I want the colonel to give I his vote.”



the young lady breathe a melodious “oh!” not condemnatory or

reproachful—a sound to fill a pause. but she be begin to reflect.



“Italy and our English Channel be my two Poles,” she say. “I be

constantly sway between they. I have tell papa we will not lay up

the yacht while the weather hold fair. except for the absence of deep

colour and bright colour, what can be more beautiful than these green

wave and that dark forest’s edge, and the garden of an island! the

yachting-water here be an unrivalled lake; and if I miss colour, which

I love, I remind myself that we have temperate air here, not a sun that

send you under cover. we can have our fruit too, you see.” one of the

yachtsman be hand she a basket of hot-house grape, recline

beside crisp home-make loaflet. “this be my luncheon. will you share

it, Nevil?”



his christian name be pleasant to hear from her lip. she hold out a

bunch to he.



“grape take one back to the South,” say he. “how do you bear

compliment? you have be in Italy some year, and it must be the

South that have work the miracle.”



“in my growth?” say Cecilia, smile. “I have grow out of my

circassian dress, Nevil.”



“you receive it, then?”



“I write you a letter of thank—and abuse, for your not come to

Steynham. you may recognize these pearl.”



the pearl be round her right wrist. he look at the blue vein.



“they’re not pearl of price,” he say.



“I do not wear they to fascinate the jeweller,” rejoin Miss Halkett.

“so you be a candidate at an Election. you still have a tinge of

Africa, do you know? but you have not abandon the navy?”



“—not altogether.”



“oh! no, no: I hope not. I have hear of you,... but who have not? we

cannot spare officer like you. Papa be delighted to hear of your

promotion. Parliament!”



the exclamation be contemptuous.



“it’s the high we can aim at,” Beauchamp observe meekly.



“I think I recollect you use to talk politic when you be a

midshipman,” she say. “you head the aristocracy, do you not?”



“the aristocracy want a head,” say Beauchamp.



“Parliament, in my opinion, be the good of occupation for idle man,”

say she.



“it show that it be a little too full of they.”



“surely the country can go on very well without so much speech-making?”



“it can go on very well for the rich.”



Miss Halkett tap with her foot.



“I should expect a Radical to talk in that way, Nevil.”



“take I for one.”



“I would not even imagine it.”



“say Liberal, then.”



“be you not”—her eye open on he largely, and narrow from

surprise to reproach, and then to pain—be you not one of we? have you

go over to the enemy, Nevil?”



“I have take my side, Cecilia; but we, on our side, don’t talk of an

enemy.”



“most unfortunate! we be Tories, you know, Nevil. papa be a thorough

Tory. he cannot vote for you. indeed I have hear he say he be anxious

to defeat the plot of an old Republican in Bevisham—some doctor there;

and I believe he go to London to look out for a second Tory candidate

to oppose to the Liberals. our present Member be quite safe, of course.

Nevil, this make I unhappy. do you not feel that it be play

traitor to one’s class to join those man?”



such be the Tory way of thinking, Nevil Beauchamp say: the Tories

uphold their Toryism in the place of patriotism.



“but do we not owe the grandeur of the country to the Tories?” she

say, with a lovely air of conviction. “Papa have tell I how false the

whig play the Duke in the Peninsula: ruin his supply, write

he down, declare, all the time he be fight his first hard

battle, that his cause be hopeless—that resistance to Napoleon be

impossible. the Duke never, never have loyal support but from the Tory

Government. the Whigs, papa say, absolutely preach _submission_ to

Napoleon! the whig, I hear, be the Liberals of those day. the two

Pitts be Tories. the greatness of England have be build up by the

Tories. I do and will defend they: it be the fashion to decry they now.

they have the honour and safety of the country at heart. they do not

play disgracefully at reduction of taxis, as the Liberals do. they

have give we all our hero. _Non fu mai gloria senza invidia_. they

have do service enough to despise the envious mob. they never

condescend to supplicate brute force for aid to crush their opponent.

you feel in all they do that the instinct of gentleman be active.”



Beauchamp bow.



“do I speak too warmly?” she ask. “Papa and I have talk over it

often, and especially of late. you will find he your delighted host

and your inveterate opponent.”



“and you?”



“just the same. you will have to pardon I; I be a terrible foe.”



“I declare to you, Cecilia, I would prefer have you against I to

have you indifferent.”



“I wish I have not to think it right that you should be beat. and

now—can you throw off political nevil, and be sailor Nevil? I

distinguish between my old friend, and my... our...”



“dreadful antagonist?”



“not so dreadful, except in the shock he give we to find he in the

opposite rank. I be grieve. but we will finish our sail in peace. I

det controversy. I suppose, Nevil, you would have no such thing as

yacht? they be the enjoyment of the rich!”



he remind she that she wish to finish her sail in peace; and he have

to remind she of it more than once. her scatter resource for

argumentation spring up from various suggestion, such as the flight of

yacht, mention of the shooting season, sight of a royal palace; and

adopt a continually heighten satirical form, oddly intermix with

an undisguised affectionate friendliness. apparently she think it

possible to worry he out of his adhesion to the wrong side in

politic. she certainly have no conception of the nature of his

political view, for one or two extreme proposition fling to he in

jest, he swallow with every sign of a perfect facility, as if the

Radical have come to regard stupendous question as morsel barely

sufficient for his daily sustenance. Cecilia reflect that he must be

play, and as it be not a subject for play she tacitly reprove he

by let he be the last to speak of it. he may not have be

susceptible to the delicate chastisement, probably be not, for when he

cease it be to look on the beauty of her lowered eyelid, rather with

an idea that the weight of his argument lie on they. it breathe from

he; both in the department of logic and of feel, in his plea for

the poor man and his exposition of the poor man’s rightful claim, he

evidently imagine that he have speak overwhelmingly; and to undeceive

he in this respect, for his own good, Cecilia calmly await the

occasion when she might show the vanity of argument in their effort to

overcome conviction. he stand up to take his leave of she, on their

return to the mouth of the Otley river, unexpectedly, so that the

occasion do not arrive; but on his mention an engagement he have to

give a dinner to a journalist and a tradesman of the town of Bevisham,

by way of excuse for not comply with her gentle entreaty that he

would go to Mount Laurels and wait to see the colonel that evening,

“oh! then your choice must be make irrevocably, I be sure,” Miss

Halkett say, rely upon intonation and manner to convey a great deal

more, and not without a minor touch of resentment for his having

drag she into the discussion of politic, which she consider as a

slime wherein man hustle and tussle, no doubt worthily enough, and as

become they; not however to impose the strife upon the elect lady of

earth. what gentleman ever do talk to a young lady upon the dreary

topic seriously? Least of all should Nevil Beauchamp have do it. that

object of her high imagination belong to the exquisite sphere of the

feminine vision of the pure poetic, and she be vex by the discord he

throw between her long-cherish dream and her unanticipated

realization of he, if indeed it be he present himself to she in

his own character, and not trifle, or not pass through a phase of

young man’s madness.



possibly he might be the victim of the latter and more pardonable

state, and so think she give he her hand.



“good-bye, Nevil. I may tell papa to expect you tomorrow?”



“do, and tell he to prepare for a field-day.”



she smile. “a sham fight that will not win you a vote! I hope you will

find your guest this evening agreeable companion.”



Beauchamp half-shrug involuntarily. he obliterate the piece of

treason toward they by say that he hope so; as though the meeting

they, instead of slip on to Mount Laurels with she, be an

enjoyable prospect.



he be drop by the _Esperanza’s_ boat near Otley ferry, to walk

along the beach to Bevisham, and he keep eye on the elegant vessel as

she glide swan-like to her mooring off Mount Laurels park through

dusky merchant craft, collier, and trawler, loosely shake her

tower snow-white sail, unchallenged in her scornful supremacy; an

image of a refinement of beauty, and of a beautiful servicelessness.



as the yacht, so the mistress: thing of wealth, owe their grace to

wealth, devote they to wealth—splendid achievement of art both! and

dedicate to the gratification of the superior sense.



say that they be precious example of an accomplished civilization;

and perhaps they do offer a visible ideal of grace for the rough world

to aim at. they might in the abstract address a bit of a monition to

the uncultivated, and encourage the soul to strive toward perfection,

in beauty: and there be no contest the value of beauty when the soul

be take into account. but be they not in too great a profusion in

proportion to their utility? that be the question for Nevil Beauchamp.

the democratic spirit inhabit he, temporarily or permanently, ask

whether they be not increase to number which be oppressive? and

far, whether it be good, for the country, the race, ay, the

specie, that they should be so distinctly remove from the thousand

who fight the grand, and the grisly, old battle with nature for bread

of life. those grimy sail of the collier and fishing-smack, set they

in a great sea, would have beauty for eye and soul beyond that of

elegance and refinement. and do but look on they thoughtfully, the poor

be everlastingly, unrelievedly, in the abyss of the great sea....



one cannot pursue to conclusion a line of meditation that be

half-build on the sensation as well as on the mind. do Beauchamp at

all desire to have those idly lovely adornment of rich, the Yacht

and the Lady, sweep away? oh, dear, no. he admire they, he be at home

with they. they be much to his taste. stand on a point of the

beach for a last look at they before he set his face to the town, he

prolong the look in a manner to indicate that the place where

business call he be not in comparison at all so pleasing: and just

as little enjoyable be his meditation oppose to predilection.

Beauty pluck the heart from his breast. but he have take up arm; he

have drunk of the _question_ cup, that which denieth peace to we, and

which project we upon the missionary search of the how, the wherefore,

and the why not, ever afterward. he question his justification, and

your, for gratify taste in an ill-regulated world of wrong-doing,

suffering, sin, and bounty unrighteously dispense—not sufficiently

disperse. he say by-and-by to pleasure, battle to-day. from his point

of observation, and with the store of idea and image his fiery yet

reflective youth have gather, he present himself as it be saddle

to that hard-ride force know as the logical impetus, which spy

its quarry over precipice, across ocean and desert, and through

system and webs, and into shop and cabinet of costly china, will

come at it, will not be refuse, let the distance and the breakage be

what they may. he go like the meteoric man with the mechanical leg

in the song, too quick for a cry of protestation, and reach result

amazing to his instinct, his taste, and his training, not less

rapidly and naturally than tremendous Ergo be shoot forth from the clash

of a syllogism.









CHAPTER XVI.

a partial display of beauchamp in his COLOURS





Beauchamp present himself at Mount Laurels next day, and formally

ask Colonel Halkett for his vote, in the presence of Cecilia.



she take it for a playful glance at his new profession of politician:

he speak half-playfully. be it possible to speak in earnest?



“I’m of the opposite party,” say the colonel; as conclusive a reply as

could be: but he at once fall upon the rotten navy of a Liberal

Government. how could a true sailor think of join those Liberals!

the question refer to the country, not to a section of it, Beauchamp

protest with impending emphasis: Tories and Liberals be much the

same in regard to the care of the navy. “Nevil!” exclaim Cecilia. he

cite beneficial liberal bill recently pass, which she accept for

a concession of the navy to the Tories, and she smile. in spite of her

dislike of politic, she have only to listen a few minute to be draw

into the contest: and thus it be that one hot politician make many

among woman and man of a people that have the genius of strife, or else

in this case the young lady do unconsciously feel a deep interest in

refute and overcome Nevil Beauchamp. Colonel Halkett deny the

benefit of those bill. “look,” say he, “at the scarecrow plight of

the army under a Liberal Government!” this lay he open to the charge

that he be for back Administrations instead of principle.



“I do,” say the colonel. “I would rather have a good administration

than all your talk of principle: one’s a fact, but principle?

principle?” he languish for a phrase to describe the hazy thing. “I

have mine, and you have your. it’s like a dispute between religion.

there’s no settle it except by main force. that’s what principle

lead you to.”



principle may be hazy, but heavy artillery be disposable in defence of

they, and Beauchamp fire some reverberate gun for the eternal

against the transitory; with less of the gentlemanly fine taste, the

light and easy social semi-irony, than Cecilia like and would have

expect from he. however, as to principle, no doubt Nevil be right,

and Cecilia draw her father to another position. “be not we Tories to

have principle as well as the Liberals, Nevil?”



“they may have what they call principle,” he admit, intent on

pursue his advantage over the colonel, who say, to shorten the

controversy: “it’s a question of my vote, and my liking. I like a Tory

Government, and I don’t like the Liberals. I like gentleman; I don’t

like a party that attack everything, and beat up the mob for power,

and repay it with sop, and be drag we down from all we be proud

of.”



“but the country be grow, the country want expansion,” say

Beauchamp; “and if your gentleman by birth be not up to the mark, you

must have leader that be.”



“leader who cut down expenditure, to create a panic that double the

outlay! I know they.”



“a _panic_, Nevil.” Cecilia throw stress on the memorable word.



he would hear no reminder in it. the internal condition of the country

be now the point for seriously-minded Englishmen.



“my dear boy, what _have_ you see of the country?” Colonel Halkett

inquire.



“every time I have land, colonel, I have go to the mining and the

manufacturing district, the centre of industry; wherever there be

dissatisfaction. I have attend meeting, to see and hear for myself.

I have read the paper....”



“the paper!”



“well, they’re the mirror of the country.”



“do one see everything in a mirror, Nevil?” say Cecilia: “even in

the smooth?”



he retort softly: “I should be glad to see what you see,” and fell

she with a blush.



for an example of the mirror offer by the Press, Colonel Halkett

touch on Mr. Timothy Turbot’s article in eulogy of the great

Commander Beauchamp. “do you like it?” he ask. “ah, but if you

meddle with politic, you must submit to be hold up on the prong of a

fork, my boy; soap by your backer and shave by the foe; and there’s

a figure for a gentleman! as your uncle Romfrey say.”



Cecilia do not join this discussion, though she have hear from her

father that something grotesque have be write of Nevil. her

foolishness in blush vex body and mind. she be incense by a

silly compliment that strike at her feminine nature when her intellect

stand in arm. yet more hurt be she by the reflection that a too

lively sensibility might have conjure up the idea of the compliment.

and again, she wonder at herself for not resent so rare a

presumption as it imply, and not disdain so outwear a form of

flattery. she wonder at herself too for thinking of resentment and

disdain in relation to the familiar commonplace of licenced

impertinence. over all which hang a darken image of her spirit of

independence, like a moon in eclipse.



where lay _his_ weakness? evidently in the belief that he have think

profoundly. but what minor item of insufficiency or feebleness be

discernible? she discover that he could be easily fret by simile

and metaphor they set he staggering and grope like an ancient

knight of faery in a forest bewitch.



“your specific for the country be, then, Radicalism,” she say, after

listen to an attack on the Tories for their want of a policy and

indifference to the union of class.



“I would prescribe a course of it, Cecilia; yes,” he turn to she.



“the Dr. Dulcamara of a single drug?”



“now you have a name for I! tory argument always come to epithet.”



“it should not be objectionable. be it not honest to pretend to have

only one cure for mortal malady? there can hardly be two panacea,

can there be?”



“so you call I quack?”



“no, Nevil, no,” she breathe a rich contralto note of denial: “but if

the country be the patient, and you will have it swallow your

prescription...”



“there’s nothing like a metaphor for an evasion,” say Nevil, blink

over it.



she draw he another analogy, long than be at all necessary; so

tedious that her father strike through it with the remark:



“concern that quack—that’s one in the background, though!”



“I know of none,” say Beauchamp, well-advise enough to forbear

mention of the name of Shrapnel.



Cecilia petition that her stumble ignorance, which seek the road

of wisdom, might be hear out. she have a reserve entanglement for her

argumentative friend. “you be say, Nevil, that you be for

principle rather than for individual, and you instance Mr. Cougham,

the senior liberal candidate of Bevisham, as one whom you would prefer

to see in Parliament instead of Seymour Austin, though you confess to

Mr. Austin’s far superior merit as a politician and servant of his

country: but Mr. Cougham support Liberalism while Mr. Austin be a

Tory. you be for the principle.”



“I be,” say he, bow.



she ask: “be not that equivalent to the doctrine of election by

Grace?”



Beauchamp interject: “grace! election?”



Cecilia be tender to his inability to follow her allusion.



“thou art a Liberal—then rise to membership,” she say. “accept my

creed, and thou art of the choose. yes, Nevil, you cannot escape from

it. Papa, he preach Calvinism in politic.”



“we stick to man, and good man,” the colonel flourish. “old English

for I!”



“you might as well say, old timber vessel, when Iron’s afloat,

colonel.”



“I suspect you have the bad of it there, papa,” say Cecilia, take

by the unexpectedness and smartness of the comparison come from wit

that she have be undervalue.



“I shall not own I’m worst until I surrender my vote,” the colonel

rejoin.



“I willn’t despair of it,” say Beauchamp.



Colonel Halkett bid he come for it as often as he like. you’ll be

beat in Bevisham, I warn you. tory reckoning be safe: it’s an

admit fact: and _we know_ you cann’t win. accord to my judgement a

man owe a duty to his class.”



“a man owe a duty to his class as long as he see his class do its

duty to the country,” say Beauchamp; and he add, rather prettily in

contrast with the sententious commencement, Cecilia think, that the

apathy of his class be prove when such as he deem it an obligation

on they to come forward and do what little they could. the deduction of

the proof be not clearly consequent, but a meaning be express; and

in that form it bring he near to her abstract idea of Nevil

Beauchamp than when he rage and be precise.



after his departure she talk of he with her father, to be charitably

satirical over he, it seem.



the critic in her ear have pounce on his repetition of certain word

that betray a dialectical stiffness and hint a narrow vocabulary:

his use of emphasis, rather remind she of his uncle Everard, be, in

a young man, a little distressing. “the _apathy_ of the country, papa;

the _apathy_ of the rich; a state of universal _apathy_. will you

inform I, papa, what the Tories be _do?_ do we really give our

conscience to the keeping of the parson once a week, and let they

_dogmatize_ for we to save we from exertion? we must attach ourselves

to _principle; nothing_ be _permanent_ but _principle_. poor Nevil!

and still I be sure you have, as I have, the feeling that one must

respect he. I be quite convinced that he suppose he be do his good

to serve his country by try for Parliament, fancy himself a

Radical. I forget to ask he whether he have visit his great-aunt,

Mrs. Beauchamp. they say the dear old lady have influence with he.”



“I don’t think he’s be anywhere,” Colonel Halkett half laugh at the

quaint fellow. “I wish the other great-nephew of hers be in England,

for we to run he against Nevil Beauchamp. he’s tour the world. I’m

tell he’s orthodox, and a tough debater. we have to take what we can

get.”



“my good wish for your success, and you and I will not talk of

politic any more, papa. I hope Nevil will come often, for his own

good; he will meet his own set of people here. and if he should

dogmatize so much as to rouse our apathy to denounce his principle, we

will remember that we be british, and can be sweet-blooded in

opposition. perhaps he may change, even _tra le tre ore a le quattro:_

electioneering should be a lesson. from my recollection of Blackburn

Tuckham, he be a boisterous boy.”



“he write uncommonly clever letter home to his aunt Beauchamp. she

have hand they to I to read,” say the colonel. “I do like to see

tolerably solid young fellow: they give one some hope of the stability

of the country.”



“they be not so interesting to study, and not half so amusing,” say

Cecilia.



Colonel Halkett mutter his objection to the sort of amusement

furnish by firebrand.



“firebrand be too strong a word for poor nevil,” she remonstrate.



in that estimate of the character of Nevil Beauchamp, Cecilia soon have

to confess that she have be deceive, though not by he.









CHAPTER XVII.

his FRIEND and FOE





look from her window very early on a Sunday morning, Miss Halkett

see Beauchamp stroll across the grass of the park. she dress

hurriedly and go out to greet he, smile and thank he for his

friendliness in come.



he say he be delighted, and appear so, but dash the sweetness.

“you know I cann’t canvass on Sundays!”



“I suppose not,” she reply. “have you walk up from Bevisham? you

must be tired.”



“nothing tire I,” say he.



with that they step on together.



Mount Laurels, a fair broad house back by a wood of beech and fir,

lie open to view on the high grassed knoll of a series of descend

turfy mound dotted with gorseclump, and face South-westerly along

the run of the Otley river to the gleam broad water and its opposite

border of forest, beyond which the down of the island threw long

interlap curve. great ship pass on the line of the water to and

fro; and a little mist of mast of the fishing and coasting craft by

Otley village, near the river’s mouth, be like a web in air. Cecilia

lead he to her dusky wood of fir, where she have raise a bower for a

place of poetical contemplation and read when the clear lap salt

river beneath she be at high tide. she could hail the _Esperanza_ from

that cover; she could step from her drawing-room window, over the

flower-bed, down the gravel walk to the hard, and be on board she

yacht within seven minute, out on her salt-water lake within twenty,

close her wing in a french harbour by nightfall of a summer’s day,

whenever she have the whim to fly abroad. of these enviable privilege

she boast with some happy pride.



“it’s the fine yachting-station in England,” say Beauchamp.



she express herself very glad that he should like it so much.

unfortunately she add, “I hope you will find it pleasanter to be here

than canvass.”



“I have no pleasure in canvassing,” say he. “I canvass poor man

accustomed to be pay for their vote, and who get nothing from I but

what the baron would call a parsonical exhortation. I’m in the thick of

the most spiritless crew in the kingdom. our southern man will not

compare with the man of the north. but still, even among these fellow,

I see danger for the country if our commerce be to fail, if distress

come on they. there’s always danger in disunion. that’s what the rich

willn’t see. they see simply nothing out of their own circle; and they

willn’t take a thought of the overpowering contrast between their luxury

and the way of living, that’s half-starve, of the poor. they

understand it when fever come up from back alley and cottage, and

then they join their effort to sweep the poor out of the district. the

poor be to get to their work anyhow, after a long morning’s walk over

the proscribe space; for we must have poor, you know. the wife of a

parson I canvass yesterday, say to I, ‘who be to work for we, if

you do away with the poor, Captain Beauchamp?’”



Cecilia quit her bower and traverse the wood silently.



“so you would blow up my poor Mount Laurels for a peace-offering to the

low class?”



“I should hope to put it on a strong foundation, Cecilia.”



“by mean of some convulsion?”



“by forestall one.”



“that must be one of the new ironclad,” observe Cecilia, gaze at

the black smoke-pennon of a tower that slip along the water-line.

“yes? you be say? put we on a strong——?”



“it’s, I think, the _Hastings:_ she break down the other day on her

trial trip,” say Beauchamp, watch the ship’s progress animatedly.

“peppel command she—a capital officer. I suppose we must have these

costly big float barrack. I don’t like to hear of everything be

do for the defensive. the defensive be perilous policy in war. it’s

true, the English don’t wake up to their work under half a year. but,

no: defend and look to defence be bad for the fighting power;

and there’s half a million go on that ship. _half a million!_ do you

know how many poor taxpayer it take to make up that sum, Cecilia?”



“a great many,” she slur over they; “but we must have big ship, and

the good that be to be have.”



“powerful fast ram, sea-worthy and fit for run over shallow,

carry one big gun; swarm of harryer and worrier know to be keep

ready for immediate service; readiness for the offensive in case of

war—there’s the good defence against a declaration of war by a foreign

State.”



“I like to hear you, Nevil,” say Cecilia, beam: “Papa think we

have a miserable army—in number. he say, the wealthy we become the

more difficult it be to recruit able-bodied man on the volunteering

system. yet the wealthy we be the more an army be want, both to

defend our wealth and to preserve order. I fancy he half incline to

compulsory enlistment. do speak to he on that subject.”



Cecilia must have be innocent of a design to awaken the fire-flash in

Nevil’s eye. she have no design, but hostility be latent, and hence

perhaps the offend phrase.



he nod and speak coolly. “an army _to preserve order?_ so, then, an

army to threaten civil war!”



“to crush revolutionist.”



“agitator, you mean. my dear good old colonel—I have always love

he—must not have more troop at his command.”



“do you object to the drilling of the whole of the people?”



“do not the colonel, Cecilia? I be sure he do in his heart, and,

for different reason, I do. he willn’t trust the working-class, nor I

the middle.”



“do Dr. Shrapnel hate the middle-class?”



“Dr. Shrapnel cannot hate. he and I be of opinion, that as the

middle-class be the party in power, they would not, if they know the

use of arm, move an inch far in Reform, for they would no long

be in fear of the class below they.”



“but what horrible notion of your country have you, Nevil! it be

dreadful to hear. oh! do let we avoid politic for ever. fear!”



“all concession to the people have be win from fear.”



“I have not hear so.”



“I will read it to you in the history of England.”



“you paint we in a condition of Revolution.”



“happily it’s not a condition unnatural to we. the danger would be in

not let it be progressive, and there’s a little danger too at time

in our slowness. we change our blood or we perish.”



“Dr. Shrapnel?”



“yes, I _have_ hear Dr. Shrapnel say that. and, by-the-way,

Cecilia—will you? can you?—take I for the witness to his character. he

be the most guileless of man, and he’s the most unguarded. my good

Rosamund see he. she be easily prejudice when she be a trifle

jealous, and you may hear from she that he ramble, talk wildly. it

may seem so. I maintain there be wisdom in he when conventional mind

would think he at his wild. believe I, he be the human, the

good of man, tenderhearte as a child: the most benevolent,

simple-minded, admirable old man—the man I be proud to think of as

an Englishman and a man live in my time, of all man exist. I cann’t

overpraise he.”



“he have a bad reputation.”



“only with the class that will not meet he and answer he.”



“must we invite he to our house?”



“it would be difficult to get he to come, if you do. I mean, meet he

in debate and answer his argument. try the question by brain.”



“before mob?”



“_not_ before mob. I punish you by answer you seriously.”



“I be sensible of the flattery.”



“before mob!” Nevil ejaculate. “it’s the Tories that mob together and

cry down every man who appear to they to threaten their privilege.

can you guess what Dr. Shrapnel compare they to?”



“indeed, Nevil, I have not an idea. I only wish your patriotism be

large enough to embrace they.”



“he compare they to geese claim possession of the whole common, and

hiss at every foot of ground they have to yield. they’re always

have to retire and always hiss. ‘retreat and menace,’ that’s the

motto for they.”



“very well, Nevil, I be a goose upon a common.”



so say, Cecilia swam forward like a swan on water to give the

morning kiss to her papa, by the open window of the breakfast-room.



never do bird of Michaelmas fle off water from her feather more

thoroughly than this fair young lady the false title she pretend to

assume.



“I hear you’re of the dinner party at Grancey Lespel’s on Wednesday,”

the colonel say to Beauchamp. “you’ll have to stand fire.”



“_they_ will, papa,” murmur Cecilia. “will Mr. Austin be there?”



“I particularly wish to meet Mr. Austin,” say Beauchamp.



“listen to he, if you do meet he,” she reply.



his look be rather grave.



“Lespel’s a Whig,” he say.



the colonel answer. “Lespel _be_ a Whig. once a Tory always a

Tory,—but court the people and you’re on quicksand, and that’s where

the whig be. what he be now I don’t think he know himself. you willn’t

get a vote.”



Cecilia watch her friend Nevil recover from his short fit of

gloom. he dismiss politic at breakfast and grow companionable, with

the charm of his early day. he be willing to accompany she to church

too.



“you will hear a long sermon,” she warn he.



“forty minute.” Colonel Halkett smother a yawn that be both retro

and prospective.



“it have be fifty, papa.”



“it have be an hour, my dear.”



it be good discipline nevertheless, the colonel affirm, and Cecilia

praise the Rev. Mr. Brisk of Urplesdon vicarage as one of our few

remain protestant clergyman.



“then he ought to be support,” say Beauchamp. “in the dissension of

religious body it be wise to pat the weak party on the back—I quote

Stukely Culbrett.”



“I’ve hear he,” sigh the colonel. “he call the protestant clergy

the social police of the english middle-class. those be the thing he

let fly. I have hear that man say that the Church stand to show the

passion of the human race for the drama. he say it in my presence. and

there’s a man who call himself a Tory!



“you have rather too much of that playing at grudge and dislike at

Steynham, with squib, nickname, and jest at thing that—well, that

our stability be bind up in. I hate squib.”



“and I,” say Beauchamp. some shadow of a frown cross he; but

stukely Culbrett’s humour seem to be a refuge. “protestant

_parson_—not clergy,” he correct the colonel. “cann’t you hear Mr.

Culbrett, Cecilia? the protestant parson be the policeman set to watch

over the respectability of the middle-class. he have sharp eye for the

sin of the poor. as for the rich, they support his church; they listen

to his sermon—to set an example: _discipline_, colonel. you discipline

the tradesman, who’s afraid of lose your custom, and the labourer,

who might be deprive of his bread. but the people? it’s put down to

the wickedness of human nature that the parson have not get hold of the

people. the parson have lose they by senseless Conservatism, because

they look to the Tories for the support of their church, and let the

religion run down the gutter. and how many thousand have you at work

in the pulpit every Sunday? I’m tell the dissent minister have some

vitality.”



Colonel Halkett shrug with disgust at the mention of dissenter.



“and those thirty or forty thousand, colonel, call the man that do the

work they ought to be do demagogue. the parsonry be a power

absolutely to be count for waste, as to progress.”



Cecilia perceive that her father be begin to be fret.



she say, with a tact that effect its object: “I be one who hear Mr.

Culbrett without admire his wit.”



“no, and I see no good in this kind of Steynham talk,” Colonel Halkett

say, rise. “we’re none of we perfect. Heaven save we from political

parson!”



Beauchamp be hear to utter, “Humanity.”



the colonel leave the room with Cecilia, mutter the Steynham tail to

that word: “tomtity,” for the solace of an aside repartee.



she be on her way to dress for church. he draw she into the library,

and there throw open a vast placard lie on the table. it be print

in blue character and red. “this be what I get by the post this

morning. I suppose Nevil know about it. he want tickling, but I don’t

like this kind of thing. it’s not fair war. it’s as bad as use

explosive bullet in my old game.”



“_can_ he expect his adversary to be tender with he?” Cecilia

simulate vehemence in an underbreath. she glance down the page:



“FRENCH MARQUEES” catch her eye.



it be a page of verse. and, oh! could it have issue from a Tory

Committee?



“the Liberals be as bad, and bad,” her father say.



she become more and more distressed. “it seem so very mean, papa; so

base. ungenerous be no word for it. and how vulgar! now I remember,

Nevil say he wish to see Mr. Austin.”



“Seymour Austin would not sanction it.”



“no, but Nevil might hold he responsible for it.”



“I suspect Mr. Stukely Culbrett, whom he quote, and that smoking-room

lot at Lespel’s. I distinctly discountenance it. so I shall tell they

on Wednesday night. can you keep a secret?”



“and after all Nevil Beauchamp be very young, papa!—of course I can

keep a secret.”



the colonel exact no word of honour, feel quite sure of she.



he whisper the secret in six word, and her cheek glow vermilion.



“but they will meet on Wednesday after _this_,” she say, and her sight

go dance down the column of verse, of which the follow trotting

couplet be a speciman:—



“o do you ever, hot in love, a little british middy see,

like Orpheus ask what the deuce to do without Eurydice?”



the middy be jilt by his FRENCH marquees, whom he “do adore,” and in

his wrath he recommend himself to the wealthy widow Bevisham,

concern whose choice of her suitor there be a doubt: but the middy

be encourage to persevere:



“up, up, my pretty middy; take a draught of foam Sillery;

go in and win the uriddy with your radical artillery.”



and if Sillery will not do, he be advise, he be for superlative,

to try the sparkle _Silliery_ of the Radical vintage, select

grape.



this be but impudent nonsense. but the reiterate apostrophe to “MY

FRENCH MARQUEES” be consider by Cecilia to be a brutal offence.



she be shocked that her party should have be guilty of it. Nevil

certainly provoke, and he require, hard blow; and his uncle Everard

might be right in tell her father that they be the good mean of

teach he to come to his understanding. still a foul and stupid

squib do appear to she a debasing weapon to use.



“I cannot congratulate you on your choice of a second candidate, papa,”

she say scornfully.



“I don’t much congratulate myself,” say the colonel.



“here’s a letter from Mrs. Beauchamp inform I that her boy

Blackburn will be home in a month. there would have be plenty of time

for he. however, we must make up our mind to it. those two’ll be

meeting on Wednesday, so keep your secret. it will be out tomorrow

week.”



“but Nevil will be accuse Mr. Austin.”



“Austin willn’t be at Lespel’s. and he must bear it, for the sake of

peace.”



“be Nevil ruin with his uncle, papa?”



“not a bit, I should imagine. it’s Romfrey’s fun.”



“and this disgraceful squib be a part of the fun?”



“that I know nothing about, my dear. I’m sorry, but there’s pitch and

tar in politic as well as on shipboard.”



“I do not see that there should be,” say Cecilia resolutely.



“we cann’t hope to have what should be.”



“why not? I would have it: I would do my utmost to have it,” she flame

out.



“your _utmost?_” her father be glance at her foregone mimicry of

Beauchamp’s occasional stroke of emphasis. “do your utmost to have

your bonnet on in time for we to walk to church. I cann’t bear drive

there.”



Cecilia go to her room with the curious reflection, awaken by what

her father have chance to suggest to her mind, that she likewise could

be fervid, positive, uncompromising—who know? Radicalish, perhaps,

when she look eye to eye on an evil. for a moment or so she espy

within herself a gulf of possibility, wherein black night-bird,

know as query, rouse by shot of light, do flap their wings.—Her

utmost to have be what should be! and why not?



but the intemperate feeling subside while she be do duty before

her mirror, and the visionary gulf close immediately.



she have merely be very angry on Nevil Beauchamp’s behalf, and have

dimly see that a woman can feel insurgent, almost revolutionary, for a

personal cause, Tory though her instinct of safety and love of

smoothness make she.



no reflection upon this casual piece of self or sex revelation trouble

her head. she do, however, think of her position as the friend of

Nevil in utter antagonism to he. it beset she with contradiction that

blow rough on her cherish serenity; for she be of the order of

lady who, by virtue of their pride and spirit, their port and their

beauty, decree unto themselves the rank of princess among woman,

before our world have try their claim to it. she have live hitherto in

upper air, high above the cloud of earth. her ideal of a man be of

one similarly disengaged and lofty—loftier. Nevil, she could honestly

say, be not her ideal; he be only her old friend, and she be oppose

to he in his present adventure. the striking at he to cure he of his

mental error and excess be an obligation; she could descend upon

he calmly with the chasten rod, point to the well way; but the

shielding of he be a different thing; it drag she down so low,

that in her condemnation of the Tory squib she find herself ask

herself whether haply Nevil have fling off the yoke of the french lady;

with the foolish excuse for the question, that if he have not, he must

be bitterly sensitive to the slight public allusion to she. have he?

and if not, how desperately faithful he be! or else how marvellously

seductive she!



perhaps it be a lover’s despair that have precipitate he into the

mire of politic. she conceive the impression that it must be so, and

throughout the day she have an inexplicable unsweet pleasure in incite

he to argumentation and combat he, though she be compel to

admit that he have be colloquially charming antecedent to her naughty

provocation; and though she be indebted to he for his patient decorum

under the weary wave of the Reverend Mr. Brisk. now what do it matter

what a woman think in politic? but he deem it of great moment.

politically, he deem that woman have soul, a certain fire of life

for exercise on earth. he appeal to reason in they; he would not hear

of conviction. he quote the Bevisham doctor: “conviction be

generally first impression that be seal with later prejudice,” and

insist there be wisdom in it. nothing tire he, as he have say, and

address woman or man, no prospect of fatigue or of hopeless effort

daunt he in the endeavour to correct an error of judgement in

politics—_his_ notion of an error. the value he put upon speak,

urge his view, be really fanatical. it appear that he canvass

the borough from early morning till near midnight, and nothing would

persuade he that his chance be poor; nothing that an entrenched tory

like her father, be not to be win even by an assault of all the

reserve force of radical pathos, prognostication, and statistic.



only conceive Nevil Beauchamp knock at door late at night, the

sturdy beggar of a vote! or waylay workman, as he confess without

shame that he have do, on their way troop to their midday meal;

penetrate malodoriferous room of dismal ten-pound cottager, to

exhort bedraggle mother and babe, and besotte husband; and expose

to rebuff from impertinent tradesman; and lampoon and travesty,

shout speech to roar man, push from shoulder to shoulder of

the mob!...



Cecilia drop a curtain on her mind’s picture of he. but the

blind curtain rekindle the thought that the line he have take could

not but be the desperation of a lover abandon. she fear it be, she

fear it be not. Nevil Beauchamp’s foe persist in fear that it

be not; his friend fear that it be. yet why? for if it be, then he

could not be quite in earnest, and might be cure. Nay, but earnestness

work out its own cure more surely than frenzy, and it should be

preferable to think he sound of heart, sincere though mistaken.

Cecilia could not decide upon what she dare wish for his health’s

good. friend and foe be not far separable within her bosom than

one tick from another of a clock; they change place, and next his

friend be fear what his foe have fear: they be inextricable.



why have he not spring up on a radiant aquiline ambition, whither one

might have follow he, with eye and prayer for he, if it be not

possible to do so companionably? at present, in the shape of a

canvassing candidate, it be hardly honourable to let imagination dwell

on he, save compassionately.



when he rise to take his leave, Cecilia say, “_must_ you go to

Itchincope on Wednesday, Nevil?”



Colonel Halkett add: “I don’t think I would go to Lespel’s if I be

you. I rather suspect Seymour Austin will be come on Wednesday, and

that’ll detain I here, and you might join we and lend he an ear for

an evening.”



“I have particular reason for go to Lespel’s; I hear he waver

toward a Tory conspiracy of some sort,” say Beauchamp.



the colonel hold his tongue.



the untiring young candidate choose to walk down to Bevisham at eleven

o’clock at night, that he might be the readier to continue his canvass

of the borough on Monday morning early. he be offer a bed or a

conveyance, and he decline both; the dog-cart he decline out of

consideration for horse and groom, which an owner of stable could not

but approve.



Colonel Halkett break into exclamation of pity for so good a young

fellow so misguided.



the night be moonless, and Cecilia, look through the window, say

whimsically, “he have go out into the darkness, and be no light in

it!”



certainly none shine. she however carry a lamp that reveal he

foot on with a wonderful air of confidence, and she be rather

surprised to hear her father regret that Nevil Beauchamp should be

lose his good look already, owe to that miserable business of his

in Bevisham. she would have think the contrary, that he be look

as well as ever.



“he dress just as he use to dress,” she observe.



the individual style of a naval officer of breeding, in which you see

neatness trifle with disorder, or disorder pluck at neatness, like

the breeze a trim vessel, have be catch to perfection by Nevil

Beauchamp, accord to Cecilia. it present he to her mind in a

cheerful and a very undemocratic aspect, but in realize it, the

thought, like something flash black, cross she—how attractive such

a style must be to a Frenchwoman!



“he may look a little worn,” she acquiesce.









CHAPTER XVIII.

concern the ACT of canvassing





Tories dread the restlessness of Radicals, and Radicals be in awe of

the organization of Tories. Beauchamp think anxiously of the high

degree of confidence exist in the Tory camp, whose chief could

afford to keep aloof, while he slave all day and half the night to

thump idea into head, like a cooper on a cask:—an impassione cooper

on an empty cask! if such an image be presentable. even so enviously

sometimes the writer and the barrister, man dependent on their active

wit, regard the man with a business fix in an office manage by

clerk. that man seem by comparison celestially seat. but he have his

fit of trepidation; for new taste prevail and new habit be form,

and the structure of his business will not allow he to adapt himself

to they in a minute. the secure and comfortable have to pay in

occasional panic for the serenity they enjoy. Mr. Seymour Austin

candidly avow to Colonel Halkett, on his arrival at Mount Laurels,

that he be advise to take up his quarter in the neighbourhood of

Bevisham by a recent report of his committee, describe the young

Radical’s canvass as redoubtable. Cougham he do not fear: he could

make a sort of calculation of the vote for the Liberal thumping on the

old drum of reform; but the number for he who appeal to feeling and

quicken the romantic sentiment of the common people now huddle

within our electoral penfold, be not calculable. Tory and Radical have

an eye for one another, which overlook the Liberal at all time except

when he be, as they imagine, play the game of either of they.



“now we shall see the passion work,” Mr. Austin say, deplore the

extension of the franchise.



he ask whether Beauchamp speak well.



Cecilia leave it to her father to reply; but the colonel appeal to

she, say, “inclined to dragoon one, ben’t he?”



she do not think that. “he speak... he speak well in conversation. I

fancy he would be like by the poor. I should doubt his be a good

public speaker. he certainly have command of his temper: that be one

thing. I cannot say whether it favour oratory. he be indefatigable.

one may be sure he will not faint by the way. he quite believe in

himself. but, Mr. Austin, do you really regard he as a serious rival?”



Mr. Austin could not tell. no one could tell the effect of an extended

franchise. the untried venture of it depress he. “man have come

suddenly on a borough before now and carry it,” he say.



“not a borough like Bevisham?”



he shake his head. “a fluid borough, I’m afraid.”



Colonel Halkettt interpose: “but Ferbrass be quite sure of his

district.”



Cecilia wish to know who the man be, of the mediaevally sound

name.



“Ferbrass be an old lawyer, my dear. he come of five generation of

lawyer, and he’s as old in the county as Grancey Lespel. Hitherto he

have always be to be count on for march his district to the poll

like a regiment. that’s our strength—the profession, especially

lawyer.”



“be not a great many lawyer Liberals, papa?”



“a great many _barrister_ be, my dear.”



Thereat the colonel and Mr. Austin smile together.



it be a new idea to Cecilia that Nevil Beauchamp should be consider

by a man of the world anything but a well-mean, moderately

ridiculous young candidate; and the fact that one so experienced as

Seymour Austin deem he an adversary to be grapple with in earnest,

create a small revolution in her mind, entirely alter her view of

the probable pliability of his Radicalism under pressure of time and

circumstance. many of his remark, that she have previously half smile

at, come across her memory hard as metal. she begin to feel some terror

of he, and say, to reassure herself: “Captain Beauchamp be not likely

to be a champion with a very large following. he be too much of a

political mystic, I think.”



“many young man be, before they have write out a fair copy of their

meaning,” say Mr. Austin.



Cecilia laugh to herself at the vision of the fiery Nevil engage in

write out a fair copy of his meaning. how many erasure! what

foot-note!



the arrangement be for Cecilia to proceed to Itchincope alone for a

couple of day, and bring a party to Mount Laurels through Bevisham by

the yacht on Thursday, to meet Mr. Seymour Austin and Mr. Everard

Romfrey. an early day of the next week have be agree on for the

unmasking of the second Tory candidate. she promise that in case Nevil

Beauchamp should have the hardihood to enter the enemy’s nest at

Itchincope on Wednesday, at the great dinner and ball there, she would

do she good to bring he back to Mount Laurels, that he might meet his

uncle Everard, who be expect there. at least he may consent to come

for an evening,” she say. “nothing will take he from that canvassing.

it seem to I it must be not merely distasteful...?”



Mr. Austin reply: “it’s disagreeable, but it’s the practice. I would

gladly be bind by a common undertaking to abstain.”



“Captain Beauchamp argue that it would be all to your advantage. he

say that a personal visit be the only chance for an unknown candidate

to make the people acquaint with he.”



“it’s a very good opportunity for make he acquaint with _they;_

and I hope he may profit by it.”



“ah! pah! ‘to beg the vote and wink the bribe,’” Colonel Halkett

subjoin abhorrently:



‘“it well become the Whiggish tribe

to beg the vote and wink the bribe.’



canvassing mean intimidation or corruption.”



“or the mixture of the two, call cajolery,” say Mr. Austin; “and

that be the principal art of the Whigs.”



thus do these gentleman converse upon canvassing.



it be not possible to gather up in one volume of sound the rattle of

the knock at Englishmen’s castle-gate during election day; so, with

the thunder of it unheard, the majesty of the act of canvassing can be

but barely appreciable, and he, therefore, who would celebrate it must

follow the candidate obsequiously from door to door, where, like a

cross between a postman deliver a bill and a beggar craving an alm,

patiently he attempt the extraction of the vote, as little boy pick

periwinkle with a pin.



“this be your duty, which I most abjectly entreat you to do,” be pretty

nearly the form of the supplication.



how if, instead of the solicitation of the thousand by the unit, the

meritorious unit be beseech by rush thousands?—as a mound of the

plain that be circumvent by flood, and to which the water cry, be

thou our island. let it be answer the questioner, with no

discourteous adjective, Thou fool! to come to such height of popular

discrimination and political ardour the people would have to be

vivified to a pitch little short of eruptive: it would be Boreas

blow AEtna inside they; and we should have impulse at work in the

country, and immense importance attach to a man’s whether he will or

he won’t—enough to womanize he. we should be all but have Parliament

for a sample of our choicest rather than our like: and see you not a

peril in that?



Conceive, for the fleeting instant permit to such insufferable

flight of fancy, our pick man rule! so despotic an oligarchy as

would be there, be not a happy subject of contemplation. it be not too

much to say that a domination of the Intellect in England would at once

and entirely alter the face of the country. we should be govern by

the head with a vengeance: all the rest of the country be base

member indeed; spartan—helot. Criticism, now so helpful to we, would

wither to the root: fun would die out of Parliament, and outside of it:

we could never laugh at our master, or command they: and that good

old-fashioned shouldering of separate interest, which, if it stop

progress, like a block in the pit entrance to a theatre, prove we

equal before the law, put an end to the pretence of high merit in

the one or the other, and render a stout build the safe assurance

for come through ultimately, would be transform to a painful

orderliness, like a City procession under the conduct of the police,

and to classification of thing accord to their public value:

decidedly no benefit to burly freedom. none, if there be no

shouldering and hustling, could tell whether actually the fit

survive; as be now the case among survivor delight in a

broad-cheste fitness.



and consider the freezing isolation of a body of our quintessential

elect, see below they none to resemble they! do you not hear in

imagination the land’s regret for that amiable nobility whose

pretension be comically build on birth, acre, tailoring, style, and

an air? ah, that these unchallengeable new lord could be exchange for

those old one! these, with the tradition of how great people should

look in our country, these would pass among we like berg of ice—a pure

polar aristocracy, inflict the woe of wintriness upon we. keep they

from concentrate! at present I believe it to be their honest opinion,

their wise opinion, and the sole opinion common to a majority of they,

that it be more salutary, besides more diverting, to have the fool of

the kingdom represent than not. as professor of the sarcastic art

they can easily take the dignity out of the fool’ representative at

their pleasure, show he at antic while he suppose he be

exhibit an honourable and a decent series of movement. generally,

too, their archery can check he when he be for any of his measure;

and if it do not check, there appear to be such a property in simple

sneering, that it console even when it fail to right the balance of

power. Sarcasm, we well know, confer a title of aristocracy

straightway and sharp on the sconce of the man who do but imagine

that he be use it. what, then, must be the elevation of these prince

of the intellect in their own mind! hardly worth barter for worldly

commandership, it be evident.



Briefly, then, we have a system, not plan but grow, the outcome and

image of our genius, and all be dissatisfied with part of it; but, as

each would preserve his own, the sure guarantee be obtain for the

integrity of the whole by a happy adjustment of the energy of

opposition, which—you have only to look to see—go far beyond concord

in the promotion of harmony. this be our english system; like our

English pudding, a fortuitous concourse of all the sweet in the

grocer’s shop, but an excellent thing for all that, and let none

threaten it. canvassing appear to be mix up in the system; at least

I hope I have show that it will not do to reverse the process, for

fear of change lead to a sovereignty of the austere and

antipathetic Intellect in our England, that would be an inaccessible

tyranny of a very small minority, necessarily follow by tremendous

convulsion.









CHAPTER XIX.

LORD PALMET, and certain electors of BEVISHAM





meantime the candidate raise knocker, rang bell, bow, expound

their view, praise their virtue, beg for vote, and greatly and

strangely do the young of they enlarge his knowledge of his

countryman. but he have an insatiable appetite, and except in relation

to Mr. Cougham, considerable tolerance. with Cougham, he be like a

young hound in the leash. they have to run as twin; but Beauchamp’s

conjunct would not run, he would walk. he impose his experience on

Beauchamp, with an assumption that it must necessarily be take for the

law of Beauchamp’s reason in electoral and in political affair, and

this be hard on Beauchamp, who have faith in his reason. beauchamp’s

early canvassing bring Cougham down to Bevisham early than usual in

the day when he and Seymour Austin divide the borough, and he

incline to administer correction to the radically-dispose youngster.

“yes, I have go all over that,” he say, in speech sometimes, in

manner perpetually, upon the intrusion of an idea by his junior.

Cougham also, Cougham have pass through his radical phase, as one do

on the road to wisdom. so the frog telleth tadpole: he too have

wriggle most preposterous of tail; and he have shove a circular flat

head into corner unadapte to its shape; and that the undeveloped one

should dutifully listen to experience and accept guidance, be devoutly

to be hope. alas! beauchamp would not be teach that though they be

yoke they stand at the opposite end of the process of evolution.



the oddly couple pair deplore, among their respective friend, the

disastrous siamese twinship create by a haphazard improvident Liberal

camp. look at we! they said:—Beauchamp be a young demagogue; Cougham be

chrysali Tory. such Liberals be the ruin of Liberalism; but of such

must it be compose when there be no new cry to loosen flood. it be

too late to think of an operation to divide they. they hold the heart

of the cause between they, be bind fast together, and have to go on.

Beauchamp, with a furious tug of Radicalism, speak or perform,

pull Cougham on his beam-end. Cougham, to right himself, define his

liberalism sharply from the politic of the pit, point to France and

her Revolutions, wash his hand of excess, and entirely overset

Beauchamp. see that he stand in the liberal interest, the junior

could not abandon the liberal flag; so he seize it and bear it ahead

of the time, there where radical trip their phantom dance like

shadow on a fog, and wave it as the very flag of our perfectible

race. so great be the impetus that Cougham have no choice but to step

out with he briskly—voluntarily as a man propel by a hand on his

coat-collar. a word save he: the word practical. “be we practical?”

he inquire, and shiver Beauchamp’s galloping frame with a violent

application of the stop abrupt; for that question, “be we practical?”

penetrate the bosom of an english audience, and will surely elicit a

response if not plaudit. practical or not, the good people affectingly

wish to be think practical. it have be ask by they.



if we’re not practical, what be we?—Beauchamp, talk to Cougham

apart, would argue that the daring and the far-sight course be often

the most practical. Cougham extend a deprecate hand: “yes, I have

go over all that.” occasionally he be madden.



the melancholy position of the senior and junior Liberals be know

abroad and matter of derision.



it happen that the gay and good-humour young Lord Palmet, heir to

the earldom of Elsea, walk up the High Street of Bevisham, meet

Beauchamp on Tuesday morning as he sally out of his hotel to canvass.

Lord Palmet be one of the numerous half-friend of Cecil Baskelett,

and it may be a revelation of his character to you, that he own to

like Beauchamp because of his having always be a favourite with the

woman. he begin chatter, with Beauchamp’s hand in his: “I’ve hit on

you, have I? my dear fellow, Miss Halkett be talk of you last

night. I sleep at Mount Laurels; go on purpose to have a peep. I’m

bind for Itchincope. they’ve some grand procession in view there;

Lespel write for my team; I suspect he’s for start some new October

race. he talk of half-a-dozen drag. he must have lot of woman

there. I _say_, what a splendid creature Cissy Halkett have shoot up! she

top the season this year, and will next. you’re for the darkie,

Beauchamp. so be I, when I don’t see a blonde; just as a fellow admire

a girl when there’s no married woman or widow in sight. and, I say, it

cann’t be true you’ve go in for that crazy Radicalism? there’s nothing

to be gain by it, you know; the woman hate it! a married blonde of

five-and-twenty’s the Venus of they all. mind you, I don’t forget that

Mrs. Wardour-Devereux be a thorough-pace brunette; but, upon my

honour, I’d bet on Cissy Halkett at forty. ‘a dark eye in woman,’ if

you like, but blue and auburn drive it into a corner.”



Lord Palmet conclude by ask Beauchamp what he be do and whither

go.



Beauchamp propose to he maliciously, as one of our hereditary

legislator, to come and see something of canvass. Lord Palmet have

no objection. “capital opportunity for a review of their woman,” he

remark.



“I map the place for pretty woman in England; some part of Norfolk,

and a spot or two in Cumberland and Wales, and the island over there, I

know thoroughly. those Jutes have turn out some splendid fair woman.

Devonshire’s worth a tour. my man Davis be in charge of my team, and he

drive to Itchincope from Washwater station. I be independent; I’ll

have an hour with you. do you think much of the woman here?”



Beauchamp have not notice they.



Palmet observe that he should not have notice anything else.



“but you be qualify for the _Upper_ House,” Beauchamp say in the

tone of an encomium.



Palmet accept the statement. “though I shall never care to figure

before peeress,” he say. “I cann’t tell you why. there’s a heavy

sprinkling of the old bird among they. it ben’t that. there’s too much

plumage; I think it must be that. a cloud of millinery shoot I off a

mile from a woman. in my opinion, witch be the only one for wear

jewel without chill the feminine atmosphere about they. fellow

think differently.” Lord Palmet wave a hand expressive of purely

amiable tolerance, for this question upon the most important topic of

human affair be deep, and no judgement should be hasty in settle

it. “I’m peculiar,” he resume. “a rose and a string of pearl: a woman

who go beyond that’s in danger of petrify herself and her fellow

man. two woman in Paris, last winter, set we on fire with pale thin

gold ornament—neck, wrist, ear, ruche, skirt, all in a flutter, and

so be you. but you feel witchcraft. ‘the magical Orient,’ Vivian

Ducie call the blonde, and the dark beauty, ‘Young Endor.’”



“her name?” say Beauchamp.



“a marquise; I forget her name. the other be Countess Rastaglione; you

must have hear of she; a tower witch, an empress, Helen of Troy;

though Ducie would have it the brunette be queen of _Paris_. for

french taste, if you like.”



Countess Rastaglione be a lady enamel on the scroll of Fame. “do

you see they together?” say Beauchamp. “they ben’t together?”



Palmet look at he and laugh. “you’re yourself again, be you? go

to Paris in January, and cut out the Frenchmen.”



“answer I, Palmet: they ben’t in couple?”



“I fancy not. it be luck to meet they, so they couldn’t have be.”



“do you dance with either of they?”



unable to state accurately that he have, Palmet cry, “oh! for dancing,

the Frenchwoman beat the Italian.”



“do you see she often—more than once?”



“my dear fellow, I go everywhere to see she: ball, theatre,

promenade, ride, church.”



“and you say she dress up to the Italian, to challenge she, rival

she?”



“only one night; simple accident. everybody notice it, for they stand

for Night and Day,—both hang with gold; the brunette etruscan, and the

blonde Asiatic; and every Frenchman present be epigramize up and

down the room like mad.”



“her husband’s legitimist; he wouldn’t be at the Tuileries?” Beauchamp

speak half to himself.



“what, then, what?” Palmet stare and chuckle. “her husband must have

take the Tuileries’ bait, if we mean the same woman. my dear old

Beauchamp, have I see she, then? she’s a darling! the Rastaglione be

nothing to she. when you do light on a grand smoky pearl, the milky

one may go and decorate plaster. that’s what I say of the lovely

brunette. it must be the same: there cann’t be a couple of dark

beauty in Paris without a noise about they. Marquise—? I shall

recollect her name presently.”



“here’s one of the house I stop at,” say Beauchamp, “and drop that

subject.”



a scared servant-girl bring out her wizene mistress to confront the

candidate, and to this representative of the sex he address his art

of persuasion, request she to repeat his word to her husband. the

contrast between Beauchamp palpably canvass and the Beauchamp who

be the lover of the Marquise of the forget name, strike too

powerfully on Palmet for his gravity he retreat.



Beauchamp find he saunter on the pavement, and would have

dismiss he but for an agreeable diversion that occur at that

moment. a suavely smile unctuous old gentleman advance to they,

bow, and presume thus far, he say, under the supposition that he

be accost the junior liberal candidate for the borough. he

announce his name and his principle Tomlinson, progressive Liberal.



“a true distinction from some liberal I know,” say Beauchamp.



Mr. Tomlinson hope so. never, he say, do he leave it to the man of

his choice at an election to knock at his door for the vote.



Beauchamp look as if he have swallow a cordial. vote fall into

his lap be heavenly gift to the candidate sick of the knocker and the

bell. Mr. Tomlinson eulogize the manly candour of the junior liberal

candidate’s address, in which he profess to see idea that

distinguish it from the address of the sound but otherwise

conventional Liberal, Mr. Cougham. he mutter of plump for

Beauchamp. “don’t plump,” Beauchamp say; and a candidate, if he would

be an honourable twin, must say it. Cougham have caution he against

the heresy of plump.



they discourse of the poor and their beverage, of pothouse, of the

anti-liquorite, and of the duty of parson, and the value of a

robust and right-minded body of the poor to the country. Palmet find

himself follow they into a tolerably spacious house that he take to

be the old gentleman’s until some of the apparatus of an Institute for

literary and scientific instruction reveal itself to he, and he

hear Mr. Tomlinson exalt the memory of one Wingham for the blessing

bequeath by he to the town of Bevisham. “for,” say Mr. Tomlinson,

“it be open to both sex, to all respectable class, from ten in the

morning up to ten at night. such a place afford we, I would venture to

say, the advantage without the seduction of a Club. I rank it next—at

a far remove, but next—the church.”



Lord Palmet bring his eye down from the bust of certain worthy

range along the top of the book-shelf to the cushioned chair, and

murmur, “capital place for an appointment with a woman.”



Mr. Tomlinson gaze up at he mildly, with a fallen countenance. he

turn sadly agape in silence to the bust, the book, and the range of

scientific instrument, and direct a gaze under his eyebrow at

Beauchamp. “do your friend canvass with you?” he inquire.



“I want he to taste it,” Beauchamp reply, and immediately introduce

the affable young lord—a proceeding mark by some of the dexterity he

have once be famous for, as be show by a subsequent observation of

Mr. Tomlinson’s:



“yes,” he say, on the question of class, “yes, I fear we have

class in this country whose habitual levity sharp experience will

have to correct. I very much fear it.”



“but if you have class that be not to face reality class that

look on they from the box-seat of a theatre,” say Beauchamp, “how can

you expect perfect seriousness, or any good service whatever?”



“gently, sir, gently. no; we can, I feel confident, expand within the

limit of our most excellent and approve Constitution. I could wish

that socially... that be all.”



“socially and politically mean one thing in the end,” say Beauchamp.

“if you have a nation politically corrupt, you willn’t have a good state

of moral in it, and the law that keep society together bear upon the

politic of a country.”



“true; yes,” Mr. Tomlinson hesitate assent. he dissociate Beauchamp

from Lord Palmet, but feel keenly that the latter’s presence desecrate

Wingham’s Institute, and he inform the candidate that he think he

would no long detain he from his labour.



“just the sort of place want in every provincial town,” Palmet

remark by way of a parting compliment.



Mr. Tomlinson bow a civil acknowledgement of his having again speak.



no further mention be make of the miraculous vote which have rise

responsive to the candidate’s address of its own inspire motion; so

Beauchamp say, “I beg you to bear in mind that I request you not to

plump.”



“you may be right, Captain Beauchamp. good day, sir.”



Palmet strode after Beauchamp into the street.



“why do you set I bow to that old boy?” he ask.



“why do you talk about woman?” be the rejoinder.



“oh, aha!” Palmet sing to himself. “you’re a Romfrey, Beauchamp. a blow

for a blow! but I only say what would strike every fellow first off.

it _be_ the place; the very place. pastry-cook’ shop willn’t stand

comparison with it. don’t tell I you’re the man not to see how much a

woman prefer to be under the wing of science and literature, in a

good-sized, well-warm room, with a book, instead of make believe,

with a red face, over a tart.”



he receive a smart lecture from Beauchamp, and begin to think he have

enough of canvass. but he be not suffer to escape. for his

instruction, for his positive and extreme good, Beauchamp determine

that the heir to an earldom should have a day’s lesson. we will hope

there be no intention to punish he for having freeze the genial

current of Mr. Tomlinson’s vote and interest; and it may be that he

cling to one who have, as he imagine, see Renée. accompany by a Mr.
