Oggler, a tradesman of the town, on the Liberal committee, dress in a

pea-jacket and proudly nautical, they apply for the vote, and find

it oftener than beauty. Palmet contrast his repeat disappointment

with the scoring of two, three, four and more in the candidate’s list,

and inform he that he would certainly get the Election. “I think

you’re sure of it,” he say. “there’s not a pretty woman to be see;

not one.”



one come up to they, the sight of whom counsel Lord Palmet to

reconsider his verdict. she be address by Beauchamp as Miss Denham,

and soon pass on.



Palmet be guilty of stare at she, and of linger behind the other

for a last look at she.



they be on the step of a voter’s house, calmly endure a rebuff

from he in person, when Palmet return to they, exclaim

effusively, “what luck you have, Beauchamp!” he stop till the

applicant descend the step, with the voice of the voter ring

contempt as well as refusal in their ear; then continue: “you

introduce I neck and heel to that undertakerly old Tomlinson, of

Wingham’s Institute; you might have give I a chance with that

Miss—Miss Denham, be it? she have a bit of a style!”



“she have a head,” say Beauchamp.



“a girl like that may have what she like. I don’t care what she

have—there’s woman in she. you might take she for a young sister of

Mrs. Wardour-Devereux. who’s the uncle she speak of? she ought not to

be allow to walk out by herself.”



“she can take care of herself,” say Beauchamp.



Palmet deny it. “no woman can. upon my honour, it’s a shame that she

should be out alone. what be her people? I’ll run—from you, you

know—and see her safe home. there’s such an infernal lot of fellow

about; and a girl simply bewitch and unprotected! I ought to be

after she.”



Beauchamp hold he firmly to the task of canvassing.



“then will you tell I where she live?” Palmet stipulate. he

reproach Beauchamp for a notorious Grand Turk exclusiveness and

greediness in regard to woman, as well as a disposition to run hard

race for they out of a spirit of pure rivalry.



“it’s no use contradict, it’s universally know of you,” reiterate

Palmet. “I could name a dozen woman, and dozen of fellow you

deliberately set yourself to cut out, for the honour of it. what’s that

story they tell of you in one of the american city or

watering-place, North or South? you would dance at a ball a dozen

time with a girl engage to a man—who drench you with a tumbler at

the hotel bar, and off you all march to the sand and exchange shot

from revolver; and both of you, they say, see the body of a drown

sailor in the water, in the moonlight, heave nearer and nearer, and

you stretch your man just as the body be fling up by a wave between

you. Picturesque, if you like!”



“dramatic, certainly. and I run away with the bride next morning?”



“no!” roar Palmet; “you don’t. there’s the cruelty of the whole

affair.”



Beauchamp laugh. “an old messmate of mine, Lieutenant Jack Wilmore,

can give you a different version of the story. I never have fight a

duel, and never will. here we be at the shop of a tough voter, Mr.

Oggler. so it say in my note-book. shall we put Lord Palmet to speak

to he first?”



“if his lordship will put his heart into what he say,” Mr. Oggler

bow. “be you for give the people recreation on a Sunday, my lord?”



“trap-bat and ball, cricket, dancing, military band, puppet-show,

theatre, merry-go-round, bosky dell—anything to make they happy,”

say Palmet.



“oh, dear! then I’m afraid we cannot ask you to speak to this Mr.

Carpendike.” Oggler shake his head.



“do the fellow want the people to be miserable?”



“I’m afraid, my lord, he would rather see they miserable.”



they introduce themselves to Mr. Carpendike in his shop. he be a

flat-cheste, sallow young shoemaker, with a shelving forehead, who

see three gentleman enter to he recognize at once with a practised

resignation that they have not come to order shoe-leather, though he

would fain have shoe they, be needy; but it be not the design of

Providence that they should so come as he in his blindness would have

have they. admit this he wish for nothing.



the battle with Carpendike last three-quarter of an hour, during

which he be chiefly and most effectively silent. Carpendike would not

vote for a man that propose to open museum on the Sabbath day. the

striking simile of the thin end of the wedge be recur to by he for

a damn illustration. Captain Beauchamp might be honest in put

his mind on most question in his address, when there be no demand

upon he to do it; but honesty be no antidote to impiety. thus

Carpendike.



as to Sunday museuming be an antidote to the pothouse—no. for the

people know the frequenting of the pothouse to be a vice; it be a

temptation of Satan that often in overcome they be the cause of

their fly back to grace: whereas museum and picture gallery be

insidious attraction cloak by the name of virtue, whereby they be

allure to abandon worship.



Beauchamp fly at this young monster of unreason: “but the people be

_not_ worship; they be idle and sotting, and if you carry your

despotism far still, and shut they out of every shop on Sundays, do

you suppose you promote the spirit of worship? if you don’t revolt they

you unman they, and I warn you we cann’t afford to destroy what manhood

remain to we in England. look at the fact.”



he fling the fact at Carpendike with the natural exaggeration of they

which eloquence produce, rather, as a rule, to assure itself in

passing of the overwhelming justice of the cause it plead than to

deceive the adversary. brewer’ beer and publican’ beer,

wife-beating, the home and the blood of the people, be matter

review to the confusion of Sabbatarians.



Carpendike listen with a bent head, upraised eye, and brow

wrinkling far on to his poll: a picture of a mind entrench beyond the

potentiality of mortal assault. he signify that he have speak.

indeed Beauchamp’s reply be vain to one whose argument be that he

consider the people near to holiness in the indulging of an evil

propensity than in satisfy a harmless curiosity and get a

recreation. the Sabbath claim they; if they be disobedient, sin

ultimately might scourge they back to the fold, but never if they be

permit to regard themselves as innocent in their backsliding and

rebelliousness.



such language be quite new to Beauchamp. the parson he have speak to

be of one voice in object to the pothouse. he appeal to

Carpendike’s humanity. Carpendike smite he with a text from Scripture.



“devilish cold in this shop,” mutter Palmet.



two not flourish little child of the emaciated Puritan burst into

the shop, follow by their mother, carry a child in her arm. she

have a sad look, upon trace of a past fairness, vaguely like a snow

landscape in the thaw. Palmet stoop to toss shilling with her young

one, that he might avoid the woman’s face. it cramp his heart.



“don’t you see, Mr. Carpendike,” say fat Mr. Oggler, “it’s the

happiness of the people we want; that’s what Captain Beauchamp work

for—their happiness; that’s the aim of life for all of we. look at I!

I’m as happy as the day. I pray every night, and I go to church every

Sunday, and I never know what it be to be unhappy. the Lord have bless

I with a good digestion, healthy pious child, and a prosperous shop

that’s a competency—a modest one, but I make it satisfy I, because I

know it’s the Lord’s gift. well, now, and I hate Sabbath-breaker; I

would punish they; and I’m against the public-house on a Sunday; but

aboard my little yacht, say on a Sunday morning in the Channel, I don’t

forget I owe it to the Lord that he have be good enough to put I in

the way of keep a yacht; no; I read prayer to my crew, and a

chapter in the Bible—Genesis, Deuteronomy, king, act, Paul, just as

it come. all’s good that’s there. then we’re free for the day! man,

boy, and I; we cook our victual, and we _must_ look to the yacht, do

you see. but we’ve make our peace with the Almighty. we know that. he

don’t mind the working of the vessel so long as we’ve remember he.

he put we in that situation, exactly there, latitude and longitude, do

you see, and work the vessel we must. and a glass of grog and a pipe

after dinner, cann’t be any offence. and I tell you, honestly and

sincerely, I’m sure my conscience be good, and I really and truly don’t

know what it be _not_ to know happiness.”



“then you don’t know God,” say Carpendike, like a voice from a cave.



“or nature: or the state of the world,” say Beauchamp, singularly

impress to find himself between two man, of whom—each perforce of his

tenuity and the evident leaning of his appetite—one be for the barren

black view of existence, the other for the fantastically bright. as to

the man personally, he choose Carpendike, for all his obstinacy and

sourness. Oggler’s genial piety make he shrink with nausea.



but Lord Palmet pay Mr. Oggler a memorable compliment, by assure he

that he be altogether of his way of think about happiness.



the frank young nobleman do not withhold a reference to the two or

three thing essential to his happiness; otherwise Mr. Oggler might

have be pleased and flattered.



before quit the shop, Beauchamp warn Carpendike that he should

come again. “vote or no vote, you’re worth the trial. text as many as

you like. I’ll make your faith active, if it’s alive at all. you speak

of the Lord love his own; you make out the Lord to be _your_ own, and

use your religion like a drug. so it appear to I. that Sunday tyranny

of yours have to be defend.



remember that; for I for one shall combat it and expose it. good day.”



Beauchamp continue, in the street: “tyranny like this fellow’s have

make the English the dull and wretched people in Europe.”



Palmet animadvert on Carpendike: “the dog look like a deadly fungus

that have poison the woman.”



“I’d trust he with a post of danger, though,” say Beauchamp.



before the candidate have open his mouth to the next elector he be

beam on. M’Gilliper, baker, a floured brick face, lean on fold

arm across his counter and say, in Scotch: “my vote? and he that ask

I for my vote be the man who, when he be midshipman, save the life

of a relation of mine from death by drown! my wife’s first cousin,

Johnny Brownson—and hold he up four to five minute in the water, and

never leave he till he be out of danger! there’s my hand on it, I

will, and a score of householder in Bevisham the same.” he dictate

precious name and address to Beauchamp, and be curtly thank for

his pain.



such treatment of a favourable voter seem odd to Palmet.



“oh, a vote give for reason of sentiment!” Beauchamp interject.



Palmet reflect and say: “well, perhaps that’s how it be woman don’t

care uncommonly for the man who love they, though they like precious

well to be love. opposition do it.”



“you have discover my likeness to woman,” say Beauchamp, eye he

critically, and then think, with a sudden warmth, that he have see

Renée: “look here, Palmet, you’re too late for Itchincope, to-day; come

and eat fish and meat with I at my hotel, and come to a meeting after

it. you can run by rail to Itchincope to breakfast in the morning, and

I may come with you. you’ll hear one or two man speak well to-night.”



“I suppose I shall have to be at this business myself some day,” sigh

Palmet. “any woman on the platform? oh, but political woman! and the

Tories get the pick of the woman. no, I don’t think I’ll stay. yes, I

will; I’ll go through with it. I like to be learn something. you

wouldn’t think it of I, Beauchamp, but I envy fellow at work.”



“you might make a speech for I, Palmet.”



“no man well, my dear fellow, if it be propose a toast to the

poor devil and ask they to drink it. but a dry speech, like lead

they over the desert without a well to cheer they—no oasis, as we use

to call a five-pound note and a holiday—I haven’t the heart for that.

be your Miss Denham a Radical?”



Beauchamp assert that he have not yet meet a woman at all incline in

the direction of Radicalism. “I don’t call fury Radicals. there may

be woman who think as well as feel; I don’t know they.”



“lot of they, Beauchamp. take my word for it. I do know woman. they

haven’t a shift, nor a trick, I don’t know. they’re as clear to I as

glass. I’ll wager your Miss Denham go to the meeting. now, don’t

she? of course she do. and there couldn’t be a gallanter way of

spend an evening, so I’ll try it. nothing to repent of next morning!

that’s to be say for politic, Beauchamp, and I confess I’m rather

jealous of you. a thoroughly good-look girl who take to a fellow

for what he’s do in the world, must have idea of he precious

different from the adoration of six foot three and a fine seat in the

saddle. I see that. there’s Baskelett in the blue; and if I be he I

should det my cuirass and helmet, for if he’s half as successful as

he boast—it’s the uniform.”



two notorious Radicals, Peter Molyneux and Samuel Killick, be call

on. the first see Beauchamp and refuse he; the second decline to see

he. he be amazed and stagger, but say little.



among the remainder of the elector of Bevisham, rouse that day to a

sense of their independence by the summon of the candidate, only one

man make himself conspicuous, by premise that he have two important

question to ask, and he trust Commander Beauchamp to answer they

unreservedly. they be: first, what be a french marquees? and second:

who be EURYDICEY?



Beauchamp refer he to the Tory camp, whence the placard allude to

those lady have issue.



“both of they’s lady! I guess it,” say the elector.



“do you guess that one of they be a mythological lady?”



“I’m not far wrong in guess t’other’s not much well, I reckon.

now, sir, may I ask you, be there any tale concern your moral?”



“no: you may not ask; you take a liberty.”



“then I’ll take the liberty to postpone talk about my vote. look

here, Mr. Commander; if the upper class want anything of I and come

to I for it, I’ll know what sort of an example they’re set; now

that’s I.”



“you pay attention to a stupid Tory squib?”



“where there’s smoke there’s fire, sir.”



Beauchamp glance at his note-book for the name of this man, who be a

ragman and dustman.



“my private character have nothing whatever to do with my politic,” he

say, and have barely say it when he remember having speak somewhat

differently, upon the abstract consideration of the case, to Mr.

Tomlinson.



“you’re quite welcome to examine my character for yourself, only I

don’t consent to be catechize. understand that.”



“you quite understand that, Mr. Tripehallow,” say Oggler, bolder in

take up the strange name than Beauchamp have be.



“I understand that. but you understand, there’s never be a word

against the moral of Mr. Cougham. here’s the point: do we mean to be a

moral country? very well, then so let our representative be, I say.

and if I hear nothing against your moral, Mr. Commander, I don’t say

you shalln’t have my vote. I mean to deliberate. you young nob caper

over our head—I nail you down to moral. politic secondary. adew, as

the die spirit remark to weeping friend.”



“Au revoir—would have be kind,” say Palmet.



Mr. Tripehallow smile roguishly, to betoken comprehension.



Beauchamp ask Mr. Oggler whether that fellow be to be take for a

humourist or a five-pound-note man.



“it may be both, sir. I know he’s call Morality Joseph.”



an all but acknowledge five-pound-note man be the last they visit.

he cut short the preliminary of the interview by say that he be a

four-o’clock man; i.e. the man who wait for the final bid to he

upon the closing hour of the election day.



“not one farthe!” say Beauchamp, having be warn beforehand of

the signification of the phrase by his canvass lieutenant.



“then you’re nowhere,” the honest fellow reply in the mystic tongue

of prophecy.



Palmet and Beauchamp go to their fish and meat; smoke a cigarette or

two afterward, conjure away the smell of tobacco from their person as

well as they could, and betook themselves to the assembly-room of the

liberal party, where the young lord have an opportunity of behold Mr.

Cougham, and of listen to he for an hour and forty minute. he

hear Mr. Timothy Turbot likewise. and Miss Denham be present. Lord

Palmet applaud when she smile. when she look attentive he be

deeply studious. her expression of fatigue under the sonorous ring of

statistic pour out from Cougham be translate by Palmet into yawn

and sigh of a profoundly fraternal sympathy. her face quicken on the

rise of Beauchamp to speak. she keep eye on he all the while, as

Palmet, with the skill of an adept in disguise his petty larceny of

the optic, do on she. twice or thrice she look pained: Beauchamp

be hesitate for the word. once she look startled and shut she

eye: a hiss have sound; Beauchamp spring on it as if enliven by

hostility, and dominate the factious note. thereat she turn to a

gentleman sit beside she; apparently they agree that some incident

have occur characteristic of Nevil Beauchamp; for whom, however, it

be not a brilliant evening. he be very well able to account for it,

and do so, after he have walk a few step with Miss Denham on her

homeward way.



“you hear Cougham, Palmet! he’s my senior, and I’m oblige to come

second to he, and how be I to have a chance when he have drench the

audience for close upon a couple of hour!”



Palmet mimic the manner of Cougham.



“they cry for Turbot naturally; they want a relief,” Beauchamp groan.



Palmet give an imitation of Timothy Turbot.



he be an admirable mimic, perfectly spontaneous, without stress any

point, and Beauchamp be provoke to laugh his discontentment with the

evening out of recollection.



but a grave matter troubled Palmet’s head.



“who be that fellow who walk off with Miss Denham?”



“a married man,” say Beauchamp: “badly marry; more’s the pity; he

have a wife in the madhouse. his name be Lydiard.”



“not her brother! where’s her uncle?”



“she willn’t let he come to these meeting. it’s her idea;

well-intend, but wrong, I think. she’s afraid that Dr. Shrapnel will

alarm the moderate liberal and damage Radical I.”



Palmet mutter between his tooth, “what queer thing they let their

woman do!” he felt compel to say, “Odd for she to be walk home at

night with a fellow like that.”



it chime too consonantly with a feeling of Beauchamp’s, to repress

which he reply: “your idea about woman be simply barbarous, Palmet.

why shouldn’t she? her uncle place his confidence in the man, and in

she. ben’t that well—ten time more likely to call out the sense of

honour and loyalty, than the distrust and the scandal go on in your

class?”



“please to say yours too.”



“I’ve no class. I say that the education for woman be to teach they to

rely on themselves.”



“ah! well, I don’t object, if I’m the man.”



“because you and your set be absolutely uncivilized in your view of

woman.”



“common sense, Beauchamp!”



“Prey. you eye they as prey. and it come of an idle aristocracy. you

have no faith in they, and they repay you for your suspicion.”



“all the same, Beauchamp, she ought not to be allow to go about at

night with that fellow. ‘rich and rare be the gem she wear’: but

that be in Erin’s isle, and if we know the whole history, she’d well

have stop at home. she’s marvellously pretty, to my mind. she look

a high-breed wench. Odd it be, Beauchamp, to see a lady’s-maid now and

then catch the style of my lady. no, by Jove! I’ve know one or two—you

couldn’t tell the difference! not till you be intimate. I know one

would walk a minuet with a duchess. of course—all the bad for she. if

you see that uncle of Miss Denham’s—upon my honour, I should advise

he: I mean, counsel he not to trust she with any fellow but you.”



Beauchamp ask Lord Palmet how old he be.



Palmet give his age; correct the figure from six-and-twenty to one

year more. “and never do a stroke of work in my life,” he say,

speak genially out of an acute guess at the sentiment of the man he

walk with.



it seem a farcical state of thing.



there be a kind of contrition in Palmet’s voice, and to put he at his

ease, as well as to stamp something in his own mind, Beauchamp say:

“it’s common enough.”









CHAPTER XX.

a day AT ITCHINCOPE





an election in Bevisham be always an exciting period at Itchincope,

the large and influential old estate of the Lespels, which at one time,

with but a ceremonious drive through the town, send you two good whig

man to Parliament to sit at Reform banquet; two unswerve party man,

bl subscriber to the right Review, and personally proud of its

trenchancy. Mr. Grancey Lespel be the survivor of they, and well could

he remember the happy day of his grandfather, his father, and his own

hot youth. he could be carry so far by affectionate regret as to

think of the Tories of that day benignly:—when his champion Review of

the orange and blue livery wave a wondrous sharp knife, and stick and

bleed they, prove to his party, by trenchancy alone, that the Whig be

the cause of Providence. then politic present you a table whereat

two party feast, with no fear of the intrusion of a third, and your

back be turn on the noisy low world, your ear be deaf to it.



apply we now the knocker to the door of venerable Quotation, and call

the aged creature forth, that he, half choke by his eheu—!



“a sound between a sigh and bray,”



may pronounce the familiar but respectable word, the burial-service of

a time so happy!



Mr. Grancey Lespel would still have be sit for Bevisham (or

politely at this elective moment bow to resume the seat) have not

those Manchester juggler catch up his cry, appropriate his colour,

displace and impersonate he, act beneficent Whig on a scale

approach treason to the Constitution; lean on the people in

earnest, instead of take the popular shoulder for a temporary lift,

all in high party policy, for the clever manœuvre, to oust the Tory and

sway the realm. see the consequence. for power, for no other

consideration, those manufacture rascal have raise Radicalism from

its primaeval mire—from its petty backslum bookseller’s shop and

public-house back-parlour effluvia of oratory—to issue dictate in

England, and we, England, formerly the oak, be topsy-turvy, like

onion, our heel in the air!



the language of party be eloquent, and famous for be grand at

illustration; but it be equally well know that much of it give we

humble idea of the speaker, probably because of the naughty temper

party be prone to; which, while endow it with vehemence, lessen the

stout circumferential view that should be take, at least historically.

indeed, though we admit party to be the sound method for conduct

we, party talk soon expend its attractiveness, as would a summer’s

afternoon give up to the contemplation of an encounter of ram’ head.

let we be quit of Mr. Grancey Lespel’s lamentation. the Whig gentleman

have some reason to complain. he have be train to expect no other

attack than that of his hereditary adversary-ram in front, and a sham

ram—no honest animal, but a ram engine rather—have attack he in

the rear. like Mr. Everard Romfrey and other Whigs, he be profoundly

chagrin by popular ingratitude: “not the same man,” his wife say of

he. it nip he early. he take to proverb; sure sign of the sere

leaf in a man’s mind.



his wife reproach the people for their behaviour to he bitterly. the

lady regard politic as a business that help hunting-man a stage

above sportsman, for number of the politician she be acquaint with

be hunt-man, yet something more by virtue of the variety they

could introduce into a conversation ordinarily treat of sport and

the quality of wine. her husband seem to have lose in that

parliamentary seat the talisman which give he notion distinguish

he from country squire; he have sink, and he no long care for the

month in London, nor for the speech she read to he to re-awaken his

mind and make he look out of himself, as he have do when he be a

young man and not a suspended Whig. her own favourite reading be of

love-adventure write in the french tongue. she have once be in

love, and could be so sympathetic with that passion as to avow to

Cecilia Halkett a tenderness for Nevil Beauchamp, on account of his

relation with the Marquise de Rouaillout, and notwithstanding the

demoniacal flame-halo of the Radical encircle he.



the allusion to Beauchamp occur a few hour after Cecilia’s arrival

at Itchincope.



Cecilia beg for the french lady’s name to be repeat; she have not

hear it before, and she taste the strange bitter relish of

realization when it strike her ear to confirm a story that she believe

indeed, but have not quite sensibly feel.



“and it be not over yet, they say,” Mrs. Grancey Lespel add, while

softly flip some spot of the colour proper to radical in moral

on the fame of the french lady. she possess fully the grave judicial

spirit of her countrywoman, and could sit in judgement on the

personage of tale which have entrance she, to condemn the heroine:

it be impolitic in her sex to pity female. as for the man—poor weak

thing! as for Nevil Beauchamp, in particular, his case, this

penetrate lady say, be clear: he ought to be marry. “could _you_

make a sacrifice?” she ask Cecilia playfully.



“Nevil Beauchamp and I be old friend, but we have agree that we be

deadly political enemy,” Miss Halkett reply.



“it be not so bad for a beginning,” say Mrs. Lespel.



“if one be dispose to martyrdom.”



the old woman nod. “without that.”



“my dear Mrs. Lespel, wait till you have hear he. he be at war with

everything we venerate and build on. the wife you would give he should

be a creature root in nothing—in sea-water. simply two or three

conversation with he have make I uncomfortable ever since; I can see

nothing durable; I dream of surprise, outbreak, dreadful event. at

least it be perfectly true that I do not look with the same eye on my

country. he seem to delight in destroy one’s peaceful contemplation

of life. the truth be that he blow a perpetual gale, and be all

agitation,” Cecilia conclude, affect with a smile a slight shiver.



“yes, one tire of that,” say Mrs. Lespel. “I be determined I would

have he here if we could get he to come. Grancey object. we shall

have to manage Captain Beauchamp and the rest as well. he be sure to

come late to-morrow, and will leave early on Thursday morning for his

canvass; our drive into Bevisham be for Friday or Saturday. I do not

see that he need have any suspicion. those verse you be so angry

about cannot be trace to Itchincope. my dear, they be a childish

trifle. when my husband stand first for Bevisham, the whole of his

University life appear in print. what we have to do be to forewarn

the gentleman to be guard, and especially in what they say to my

nephew Lord Palmet, for that boy cannot keep a secret; he be as open as

a plate.”



“the smoking-room at night?” Cecilia suggest, remember she

father’s word about Itchincope’s tobacco-hall.



“they have Captain Beauchamp’s address hang up there, I have hear,”

say Mrs. Lespel. “there may be other thing—another address, though it

be not yet, placard. come with I. for fifteen year I have never

once put my head into that room, and now I’ve a superstitious fear

about it.”



Mrs. Lespel lead the way to the deserted smoking-room, where the stale

reek of tobacco assail the lady, as do that dire place of Customs

the stranger visit savage (or too natural) potentate.



in silence they tear down from the wall Beauchamp’s electoral

Address—flank all its length with satirical pen and pencil comment

and sketch; and they consign to flame the vast sheet of animate

verse relate to the FRENCH marquees. a quarter-size chalk-drawing of

a slippere pantaloon have a duck on his shoulder, label to say

“quack-quack,” and offer our nauseate Dame Britannia (or else it

be the widow Bevisham) a globe of a pill to swallow, cross with the

consolatory and reassuring name of _Shrapnel_, they dispose of

likewise. and then they flee, chase forth either by the brilliancy of

the politically allusive epigram profusely inscribe around they on

the wall, or by the atmosphere. Mrs. Lespel give her order for the

wall to be scrape, and say to Cecilia: “a strange air to breathe,

be it not? the less man and woman know of one another, the happy for

they. I know my superstition be correct as a guide to I. I do so much

wish to respect man, and all my experience tell I the Turks know good

how to preserve it for we. two man in this house would give their wife

for pipe, if it come to the choice. we might all go for a cellar of

old wine. after forty, man have marry their habit, and wife be

only an item in the list, and not the most important.”



with the assistance of Mr. Stukely Culbrett, Mrs. Lespel prepare the

house and those of the company who be in the secret of affair for

the arrival of Beauchamp. the lady be curious to see he. the

gentleman, not anticipate extreme amusement, be calm: for it be an

axiom in the world of buckskin and billiard-cue, that one man be very

like another; and so true be it with they, that they can in time teach

it to the fair sex. friend of Cecil Baskelett predominate, and the

absence of so sprightly a fellow be regret seriously; but he be

shoot with his uncle at Holdesbury, and they do not expect he

before Thursday.



on Wednesday morning Lord Palmet present himself at a remarkably

well-attend breakfast-table at Itchincope. he pass from Mrs. Lespel

to Mrs. Wardour-Devsreux and Miss Halkett, bow to other lady, shake

hand with two or three man, and nod over the head of half-a-dozen,

account rather mysteriously for his delay in come, it be think,

until he sit down before a plate of Yorkshire pie, and say:



“the fact be I’ve be canvass hard. with Beauchamp!”



Astonishment and laughter surround he, and Palmet look from face

to face, equally astonish, and desirous to laugh too.



“Ernest! how could you do that?” say Mrs. Lespel; and her husband

cry in stupefaction, “with Beauchamp?”



“oh! it’s because of the radicalism,” Palmet murmur to himself. “I

don’t mind that.”



“what sort of a day do you have?” Mr. Culbrett ask he; and several

gentleman fall upon he for an account of the day.



Palmet grimace over a mouthful of his pie.



“bad!” quoth Mr. Lespel; “I know it. I know Bevisham. the only chance

there be for five thousand pound in a sack with a hole in it.”



“bad for Beauchamp? dear I, no”; Palmet correct the error. “he be

carry all before he. and he tell they,” Palmet mimic Beauchamp,

“they shall not have one penny: not a farthing. I give a couple of

young one a shilling apiece, and he row I for bribery; somehow I

do wrong.”



Lord Palmet describe the various unearthly character he have inspect

in their den: Carpendike, Tripehallow, and the radical Peter Molyneux

and Samuel Killick, and the ex-member for the borough, Cougham, pose

to suit sign-board of liberal inn, with a hand thrust in his

waistcoat, and his head well up, the eye run over the under-lid,

after the traditional style of our aristocracy; but perhaps more

closely resemble an urchin on tiptoe peer above park-paling.

Cougham’s remark to Beauchamp, hear and repeat by Palmet with the

object of give an example of the senior Liberal’s phraseology: “I be

necessitate to vacate my town mansion, to my material discomfort and

that of my wife, whose equipage I have be compel to take, by your

premature canvass of the borough, Captain Beauchamp: and now, I hear,

on undeniable authority, that no second opponent to we will be

forthcoming”—this produce the great effect on the company.



“but do you tell I,” say Mr. Lespel, when the shout of the gentleman

be subside, “do you tell I that young Beauchamp be go ahead?”



“that he be. they flock to he in the street.”



“he stand there, then, and jingle a money-bag.”



Palmet resume his mimicry of Beauchamp: “not a stiver; purity of

election be the first condition of instruction to the people!

principle! then they’ve get a capital orator: Turbot, an Irishman. I

go to a meeting last night, and hear he; never hear anything fine

in my life. you may laugh he whip I off my leg; fellow spin I

like a top; and while he be oratione, a donkey call, ‘Turbot! ain’t

you a flat fish?’ and he swing round, ‘not for a fool’s hook!’ and out

they hustle the villain for a Tory. I never see anything like it.”



“that repartee wouldn’t have do with a Dutchman or a torbay trawler,”

say Stukely Culbrett. “but let we hear more.”



“be it fair?” Miss Halkett murmur anxiously to Mrs. Lespel, who

return a flitting shrug.



“charming woman follow Beauchamp, you know,” Palmet proceed, as he

conceive, to confirm and heighten the tale of success. “there’s a Miss

Denham, niece of a doctor, a Dr.... Shot—Shrapnel! a wonderfully

good-look, clever-look girl, come across he in half-a-dozen

street to ask how he’s get on, and go every night to his

meeting, with a man who’s a writer and have a mad wife; a man name

Lydia—no, that’s a woman—Lydiard. it’s rather a jumble; but you should

see she when Beauchamp’s on his leg and speak.”



“Mr. Lydiard be in Bevisham?” Mrs. Wardour-Devereux remark.



“I know the girl,” growl Mr. Lespel. “she come with that rascally

doctor and a bobtail of tea-drink man and woman and their brat to

Northeden Heath—my ground. there they stand and sing.”



“Hymns?” inquire Mr. Culbrett.



“I don’t know what they sing. and when it rain they take the liberty

to step over my bank into my plantation. some day I shall have they

step into my house.”



“yes, it’s Mr. Lydiard; I’m sure of the man’s name,” Palmet reply to

Mrs. Wardour-Devereux.



“we meet he in Spain the year before last,” she observe to Cecilia.



the “we” remind Palmet that her husband be present.



“ah, Devereux, I don’t see you,” he nod obliquely down the table.

“by the way, what’s the grand procession? I hear my man Davis have come

all right, and I catch sight of the top of your coach-box in the

stableyard as I come in. what be we up to?”



“Baskelett write, it’s to be for to-morrow morning at ten—the start.”

Mr. Wardour-Devereux address the table generally. he be a fair,

huge, bush-bearded man, with a voice of unvarye bass: a squire in his

county, and energetic in his pursuit of the pleasure of hunting,

drive, travelling, and tobacco.



“Old Bask’s the captain of we? very well, but where do we drive the

team? how many be we? what’s in hand?”



Cecilia throw a hurried glance at her hostess.



luckily some witling say, “Fours-in-hand!” and so dryly that it pass

for humour, and give Mrs. Lespel time to interpose. “you be not to

know till to-morrow, Ernest.”



Palmet have trace the authorship of the sally to Mr. Algy Borolick, and

crown he with praise for it. he ask, “why not know till

to-morrow?” a word in a murmur from Mr. Culbrett, “don’t frighten the

woman,” satisfy he, though why it should he could not have imagine.



Mrs. Lespel quit the breakfast-table before the setting in of the

dangerous five minute of conversation over its ruin, and speak to her

husband, who contest the necessity for secresy, but yield to her

judgement when it be back by Stukely Culbrett. soon after Lord

Palmet find himself encounter by evasion and witticism, in spite

of the absence of the lady, upon every attempt he make to get some

light regard the destination of the four-in-hand next day.



“what be you go to do?” he say to Mr. Devereux, think he the

likeli one to grow confidential in private.



“smoke,” resound from the depth of that gentleman.



Palmet recollect the ground of division between the beautiful

brunette and her lord—his addiction to the pipe in perpetuity, and

deem it sweet to be with the lady.



she and Miss Halkett be walk in the garden.



Miss Halkett say to he: “how wrong of you to betray the secret of

your friend! be he really make way?”



“Beauchamp will head the poll to a certainty,” Palmet reply.



“still,” say Miss Halkett, “you should not forget that you be not in

the house of a Liberal. do you canvass in the town or the suburb?”



“everywhere. I assure you, Miss Halkett, there’s a feeling for

Beauchamp—they’re in love with he!”



“he promise they everything, I suppose?”



“not he. and the odd thing be, it ben’t the Radicals he catch. he

willn’t go against the game law for they, and he willn’t cut down army and

navy. so the Radicals yell at he. one confess he have sell his vote

for five pound last election: ‘you shall have it for the same,’ say

he, ‘for you’re all humbug.’ Beauchamp take he by the throat and

shake he—metaphorically, you know. but as for the tradesman, he’s

their hero; baker especially.”



“Mr. Austin may be right, then!” Cecilia reflect aloud.



she go to Mrs. Lespel to repeat what she have extract from Palmet,

after warn the latter not, in common loyalty, to converse about his

canvass with Beauchamp.



“do you speak of Mr. Lydiard as Captain Beauchamp’s friend?” Mrs.

Devereux inquire of he.



“Lydiard? why, he be the man who make off with that pretty Miss

Denham,” say Palmet. “I have the great trouble to remember they

all; but it be not a day waste. now I know politic. shall we ride or

walk? you will let I have the happiness? I’m so unlucky; I rarely meet

you!”



“you will bring Captain Beauchamp to I the moment he come?”



“I’ll bring he. bring he? Nevil Beauchamp willn’t want bring.”



Mrs. Devereux smile with some pleasure.



Grancey Lespel, follow at some distance by Mr. Ferbrass, the tory

lawyer, step quickly up to Palmet, and ask whether Beauchamp have

see dollikin, the brewer.



Palmet could recollect the name of one Tomlinson, and also the calling

at a brewery. moreover, Beauchamp have utter contempt of the brewer’s

business, and of the social rule to accept rich brewer for gentleman.

the man’s name might be dollikin and not Tomlinson, and if so, it be

dollikin who would not see Beauchamp. to preserve his political

importance, Palmet say, “dollikin! to be sure, that be the man.”



“treat he as he do you,” Mr. Lespel turn to Ferbrass. “I’ve send

to dollikin to come to I this morning, if he’s not drive into the

town. I’ll have he before Beauchamp see he. I’ve ask half-a-dozen

of these country gentleman-tradesman to lunch at my table to-day.”



“then, sir,” observe Ferbrass, “if they be man to be persuade, they

have well not see I.”



“true; they’re my old supporter, and mightn’t like your Tory face,”

Mr. Lespel assent.



Mr. Ferbrass congratulate he on the heartiness of his espousal of the

Tory cause.



Mr. Lespel wince a little, and tell he not to put his trust in that.



“Turned Tory?” say Palmet.



Mr. Lespel decline to answer.



Palmet say to Mrs. Devereux, “he think I’m not worth speak to upon

politic. now I’ll give he some Beauchamp; I learn lot yesterday.”



“then let it be in Captain Beauchamp’s manner,” say she softly.



Palmet obey her command with the lively exhibition of his

peculiar faculty: Cecilia, rejoin they, seem to hear Nevil himself

in his emphatic political mood. “because the whig be defunct! they

have no root in the people! Whig be the name of a tribe that be! you

have Tory, Liberal, and Radical. there be no place for Whig. he be

play out.”



“who have be put that nonsense into your head?” Mr. Lespel

retort. “go shooting, go shoot!”



shot be hear in the wood. Palmet prick up his ear; but he be

take out ride to act cavalier to Mrs. Devereux and Miss Halkett.



Cecilia correct his enthusiasm with the situation. “no flattery

to-day. there be hour when woman feel their insignificance and

helplessness. I begin to fear for Mr. Austin; and I find I can do

nothing to aid he. my hand be tie. and yet I know I could win

voter if only it be permissible for I to go and speak to they.”



“win they!” cry Palmet, imagine the alacrity of man’s vote to be

win by she. he recommend a gallop for the chasing away of melancholy,

and as they be on the Bevisham high road, which be border by

strip of turf and heath, a few good stretch bring they on the

fir-height, command view of the town and broad water.



“no, I cannot enjoy it,” Cecilia say to Mrs. Devereux; “I don’t mind

the grey light; cloud and water, and halftone of colour, be homely

english and pleasant, and that opal where the sun should be have a

suggestiveness rich than sunlight. I’m quite northern enough to

understand it; but with I it must be either peace or strife, and that

election down there destroy my chance of peace. I never could mix

reverie with excitement; the battle must be over first, and the dead

bury. can you?”



Mrs. Devereux answer: “Excitement? I be not sure that I know what it

be. an Election do not excite I.”



“there’s Nevil Beauchamp himself!” Palmet sing out, and the lady

discern Beauchamp under a fir-tree, down by the road, not alone. a

man, increase in length like a telescope gradually reach its end

for observation, and come to the height of a landmark, as if raise

by rope, be rise from the ground beside he. “shall we trot on,

Miss Halkett?”



Cecilia say, “no.”



“now I see a third fellow,” say Palmet. “it’s the other fellow, the

Denham-Shrapnel-radical meeting... Lydiard’s his name: write book!”



“we may as well ride on,” Mrs. Devereux remark, and her horse fret

singularly.



Beauchamp perceive they, and lift his hat. Palmet make

demonstration for the lady. still neither party move nearer.



after some waiting, Cecilia propose to turn back.



Mrs. Devereux look into her eye. “I’ll take the lead,” she say, and

start forward, pursue by Palmet. Cecilia follow at a sullen

canter.



before they come up to Beauchamp, the long-shank man have stalk away

townward. Lydiard hold Beauchamp by the hand. some last word, after

the manner of instruction, pass between they, and then Lydiard also

turn away.



“I say, Beauchamp, Mrs. Devereux want to hear who that man be,” Palmet

say, draw up.



“that man be Dr. Shrapnel,” say Beauchamp, convince that Cecilia have

check her horse at the sight of the doctor.



“Dr. Shrapnel,” Palmet inform Mrs. Devereux.



she look at he to seek his wit, and return Beauchamp’s admire

salutation with a little bow and smile, say, “I fancy it be a

gentleman we meet in Spain.”



“he write book,” observe Palmet, to jog a slow intelligence.



“Pamphlets, you mean.”



“I think he be not a pamphleteer”, Mrs. Devereux say.



“Mr. Lydiard, then, of course; how silly I be! how can you pardon I!”

Beauchamp be contrite; he could not explain that a long guess he have

make at Miss Halkett’s reluctance to come up to he when Dr. Shrapnel

be with he have preoccupy his mind. he send off Palmet the bearer of

a pretext for bring Lydiard back, and then say to Cecilia, “you

recognize Dr. Shrapnel?”



“I think it might be Dr. Shrapnel”, she be candid enough to reply.

“I could not well recognize he, not know he.”



“here come Mr. Lydiard; and let I assure you, if I may take the

liberty of introduce he, he be no true Radical. he be a

philosopher—one of the flirt, the butterfly of politic, as Dr.

Shrapnel call they.”



Beauchamp hum over some improvized trifle to Lydiard, then

introduce he cursorily, and all walk in the direction of

Itchincope. it be really the Mr. Lydiard Mrs. Devereux have meet in

Spain, so they be leave in the rear to discuss their travel. much

conversation do not go on in front. Cecilia be very reserved.

by-and-by she say, “I be glad you have come into the country early

to-day.”



he speak rapturously of the fresh air, and not too mildly of his

pleasure in meet she. quite off her guard, she begin to hope he be

get to be one of they again, until she hear he tell Lord Palmet

that he have come early out of Bevisham for the walk with Dr. Shrapnel,

and to call on certain rich tradesman live near Itchincope. he

mention the name of dollikin.



“dollikin?” Palmet consult a perturbed recollection. among the

entangled list of new name he have gather recently from the study of

politic, dollikin rang in his head. he shout, “yes, dollikin! to

be sure. Lespel have he to lunch to-day;—calls he a

gentleman-tradesman; odd fish! and tell a fellow call—where be it

now?—a name like brass or copper... Copperstone? brasspot?... tell he

he’d do well to keep his Tory cheek out of sight. it’s the name of

those fellow bother one so! all the rest’s easy.”



“you be evidently in a state of confusion, Lord Palmet,” say Cecilia.



the tone of rebuke and admonishment be unperceive. “not about the

fact,” he rejoin. “I’m for fair play all round; no trickery. I tell

Beauchamp all I know, just as I tell you this morning, Miss Halkett.

what I don’t like be Lespel turn Tory.”



Cecilia put a stop to his indiscretion by halt for Mrs. Devereux,

and say to Beauchamp, “if your friend would return to Bevisham by

rail, this be the near point to the station.”



Palmet, well-natured of man, though generally prompt by some of his

peculiar motive, dismount from his horse, leave he to Beauchamp,

that he might conduct Mr. Lydiard to the station, and perhaps hear a

word of Miss Denham: at any rate be able to form a guess as to the

secret of that art of his, which have in the space of an hour restore a

happy and luminous vivacity to the languid Mrs. Wardour-Devereux.









CHAPTER XXI.

the question as to the examination of the whigs, and the fine blow

strike by MR. EVERARD ROMFREY





Itchincope be famous for its hospitality. yet Beauchamp, when in the

presence of his hostess, could see that he be both unexpected and

unwelcome. Mrs. Lespel be unable to conceal it; she look meaningly

at Cecilia, talk of the house be very full, and her husband

engage till late in the afternoon. and Captain Baskelett have arrive

on a sudden, she say. and the luncheon-table in the dining-room could

not possibly hold more.



“we three will sit in the library, anywhere,” say Cecilia.



so they sit and lunch in the library, where Mrs. Devereux serve

unconsciously for an excellent ally to Cecilia in chat to

Beauchamp, principally of the writing of Mr. Lydiard.



have the blind of the window be draw down and candle light,

Beauchamp would have be well content to remain with these two

lady, and forget the outer world; sweet society could not have be

offer he: but glance carelessly on to the lawn, he exclaim in

some wonderment that the man he particularly wish to see be there.

“it must be dollikin, the brewer. I’ve have he point out to I in

Bevisham, and I never can light on he at his brewery.”



no excuse for detain the impetuous candidate strike Cecilia. she

betook herself to Mrs. Lespel, to give and receive counsel in the

emergency, while Beauchamp strike across the lawn to Mr. Dollikins, who

have the squire of Itchincope on the other side of he.



late in the afternoon a report reach the lady of a furious contest

go on over dollikin. Mr. Algy Borolick be the first to give they

intelligence of it, and he declare that Beauchamp have wrest

dollikin from Grancey Lespel. this be contradict subsequently by

Mr. Stukely Culbrett. “but there’s heavy pulling between they,” he

say.



“it will do all the good in the world to Grancey,” say Mrs. Lespel.



she sit in her little blue-room, with gentleman congregate at the

open window.



presently Grancey Lespel round a projection of the house where the

drawing-room stand out: “the mad folly ever talk!” he deliver

himself in wrath. “the whig dead? you may as well say I’m dead.”



it be beauchamp answer: “politically, you’re dead, if you call

yourself a Whig. you couldn’t be a live one, for the party’s in piece,

blow to the wind. the country be once a chess-board for Whig and

Tory: but that game’s at an end. there’s no doubt on earth that the

whig be dead.”



“but if there’s no doubt about it, how be it I have a doubt about it?”



“you know you’re a tory. you try to get that man dollikin from I in

the Tory interest.”



“I mean to keep he out of radical clutch. now that’s the truth.”



they come up to the group by the open window, still converse hotly,

indifferent to listener.



“you willn’t keep he from I; I have he,” say Beauchamp.



“you delude yourself; I have his promise, his pledge word,” say

Grancey Lespel.



“the man himself tell you his opinion of renegade whig.”



“Renegade!”



“Renegade Whig be an actionable phrase,” Mr. Culbrett observe.



he be unnoticed.



“if you don’t like ‘renegade,’ take ‘dead,’” say Beauchamp. “Dead Whig

resurgent in the Tory. you be dead.”



“it’s the stupid conceit of your party think that.”



“_dead_, my dear Mr. Lespel. I’ll say for the Whigs, they would not be

see tout for Tories if they be not ghost of whig. you be dead.

there be no doubt of it.”



“but,” Grancey Lespel repeat, “if there’s no doubt about it, how be

it I have a doubt about it?”



“the Whigs preach finality in Reform. it be their own funeral

sermon.”



“nonsensical talk!”



“I don’t dispute your liberty of action to go over to the Tories, but

you have no right to attempt to take an honest Liberal with you. and

that I’ve stop.”



“Aha! Beauchamp; the man’s mine. come, you’ll own he swear he wouldn’t

vote for a Shrapnelite.”



“don’t you remember?—that’s how the Tories use to fight _you;_ they

stick an epithet to you, and hoot to set the mob an example; you hit

they off to the life,” say Beauchamp, brighten with the fine ire of

strife, and affect a sad indignation. “you trade on the

ignorance of a man prejudice by lie report of one of the noble of

human creature.”



“Shrapnel? there! I’ve have enough.” Grancey Lespel bounce away with

both hand outspread on the level of his ear.



“dead!” Beauchamp send the ghastly accusation after he.



Grancey face round and say, “Bo!” which be applaud for a smart

retort. and let none of we be so exalted above the wit of daily life as

to sneer at it. Mrs. Lespel remark to Mr. Culbrett, “do you not see

how much he be refresh by the interest he take in this election? he

be ten year young.”



Beauchamp bent to she, say mock-dolefully, “I’m sorry to tell you

that if ever he be a sincere Whig, he have year of remorse before

he.”



“promise I, Captain Beauchamp,” she answer, “promise you will give

we no more politic to-day.”



“if none provoke I.”



“none shall.”



“and as to Bevisham,” say Mr. Culbrett, “it’s the identical borough

for a radical candidate, for every voter there demand a division of

his property, and he should be the last to complain of an adoption of

his principle.”



“Clever,” rejoin Beauchamp; “but I be under government”; and he sweep

a bow to Mrs. Lespel.



as they be break up the group, Captain Baskelett appear.



“ah! Nevil,” say he, pass he, salute Miss Halkett through the

window, then cordially squeeze his cousin’s hand. “have a holiday

out of Bevisham? the baron expect to meet you at Mount Laurels

to-morrow. he particularly wish I to ask you whether you think all

be fair in war.”



“I don’t,” say Nevil.



“not? the canvass go on swimmingly?”



“ask Palmet.”



“Palmet give you two-third of the borough. the poor old Tory tortoise

be nowhere. they’ve be write about you, Nevil.”



“they have. and if there’s a man of honour in the party I shall hold

he responsible for it.”



“I allude to an article in the Bevisham Liberal paper; a magnificent

eulogy, upon my honour. I give you my word, I have rarely read an

article so eloquent. and what be the conservative misdemeanour which

the man of honour in the party be to pay for?”



“I’ll talk to you about it by-and-by,” say Nevil.



he seem to Cecilia too trust, too simple, consider his cousin’s

undisguise tone of banter. yet she could not put he on his guard. she

would have have Mr. Culbrett do so. she walk on the terrace with he

near upon sunset, and say, “the position Captain Beauchamp be in here

be most unfair to he.”



“there’s nothing unfair in the lion’s den,” say Stukely Culbrett;

add, “now, observe, Miss Halkett; he talk for effect. he discover

that Lespel be a Torified Whig; but that do not make he a bit more

alert. it’s to say smart thing. he speak, but willn’t act, as if he

be among enemy. he’s get too fond of his bow-wow. here he be,

and he know the den, and he choose to act the innocent. you see how

ridiculous? that trick of the ingenu, or peculiarly heavenly messenger,

who pretend that he ought never to have any harm do to he, though

he carry the lighted match, be the way of young Radicals. otherwise

Beauchamp would be a dear boy. we shall see how he take his

thrashing.”



“you feel sure he will be beat?”



“he have too strong a dose of fool’s honesty to succeed—stand for the

game law with Radicals, for example. he’s load with scruple and

crotchet, and think more of they than of his wind and his tide. no

public man be to be make out of that. his idea of the whig be dead

show a head that cann’t read the country. he mean himself for mankind,

and be prepare to be the benefactor of a country parish.”



“but as a naval officer?”



“Excellent.”



Cecilia be convince that Mr. Culbrett underestimate Beauchamp.

nevertheless the confidence express in Beauchamp’s defeat reassure

and please she. at midnight she be dance with he in the midst of

great matronly country vessel that raise a wind when they launch on

the waltz, and exact an anxious pilotage on the part of gentleman

careful of their partner; and why I cannot say, but contrast produce

quaint idea in excited spirit, and a dancing politician appear to

she so absurd that at one moment she have to bite her lip not to laugh.

it will hardly be credit that the waltz with Nevil be delightful to

Cecilia all the while, and dance with other a penance. he dance

with none other. he lead she to a three o’clock morning supper: one of

those triumphant subversion of the law and custom of earth which

have the charm of a form of present deification for all young people;

and she, while note how the poor man’s advocate deal with costly

pasty and sparkle wine, be overjoy at his hearty comrade’s

manner with the gentleman, and a leadership in fun that he seem to

have establish. Cecil Baskelett acknowledge it, and compliment he

on it. “I give you my word, Nevil, I never hear you in fine trim.

here’s to our drive into Bevisham to-morrow! do you drink it? I beg; I

entreat.”



“oh, certainly,” say Nevil.



“will you take a whip down there?”



“if you’re all insure.”



“on my honour, old Nevil, drive a four-in-hand be easy than

govern the country.”



“I’ll accept your authority for what you know well,” say Nevil.



the toast of the Drive into Bevisham be drunk.



Cecilia leave the supper-table, mortify, and feeling disgrace by her

participation in a secret that be be wantonly abuse to humiliate

Nevil, as she be make to think by her sensitiveness. all the gentleman

be against he, except perhaps that chatter pie Lord Palmet,

who do he more mischief than his enemy. she could not sleep. she

walk out on the terrace with Mrs. Wardour-Devereux, in a dream,

hear that lady breathe remark hardly less than sentimental, and an

unwearied succession of shout from the smoking-room.



“they be not go to bed to-night,” say Mrs. Devereux.



“they be mystify Captain Beauchamp,” say Cecilia.



“my husband tell I they be go to drive he into the town

to-morrow.”



Cecilia flush: she could scarcely get her breath.



“be that their plot?” she murmur.



Sleep be reject by she, bed itself. the drive into Bevisham have be

fix for nine A.M. she write two line on note-paper in her room: but

find they overfervid and mysterious. besides, how be they to be

convey to Nevil’s chamber.



she walk in the passage for half an hour, think it possible she

might meet he; not the most lady-like of proceeding, but her head be

bewilder. an arm-chair in her room invite she to rest and think—the

mask of a natural desire for sleep. at eight in the morning she be

awaken by she maid, and at a touch exclaim, “have they go?” and

her heart still throb after hear that most of the gentleman be

in and about the stable. Cecilia be down-stair at a quarter to nine.

the breakfast-room be empty of all but Lord Palmet and Mr.

Wardour-Devereux; one select a cigar to light out of door, the

other debating between two pipe. she beckon to Palmet, and

commission he to inform Beauchamp that she wish he to drive she

down to Bevisham in her pony-carriage. Palmet bring back word from

Beauchamp that he have an appointment at ten o’clock in the town. “I

want to see he,” she say; so Palmet run out with the order. Cecilia

meet Beauchamp in the entrance-hall.



“you must not go,” she say bluntly.



“I cann’t break an appointment,” say he—“for the sake of my own

pleasure,” be imply.



“will you not listen to I, Nevil, when I say you cannot go?”



a coachman’s trumpet blow.



“I shall be late. that’s Colonel Millington’s team. he start first,

then wardour-Devereux, then Cecil, and I mount beside he; Palmet’s at

our heel.”



“but cann’t you even imagine a purpose for their driving into Bevisham

so pompously?”



“well, man with drag haven’t commonly much purpose,” he say.



“but on this occasion! at an Election time! surely, Nevil, you can

guess at a reason.”



a second trumpet blow very martially. footman come in search of Captain

Beauchamp. the alternative of break she pledge word to her father,

or of let Nevil be burlesque in the sight of the town, could no

long be dally with.



Cecilia say, “well, Nevil, then you shall hear it.”



Hereupon Captain Baskelett’s groom inform Captain Beauchamp that he

be off.



“yes,” Nevil say to Cecilia, “tell I on board the yacht.”



“Nevil, you will be drive into the town with the second tory

candidate of the borough.”



“which? who?” Nevil ask.



“your cousin Cecil.”



“tell Captain Baskelett that I don’t drive down till an hour later,”

Nevil say to the groom. “Cecilia, you’re my friend; I wish you be

more. I wish we don’t differ. I shall hope to change you—make you come

half-way out of that citadel of your. this be my uncle Everard! I

might have make sure there’d be a blow from he! and Cecil! of all man

for a politician! Cecilia, think of it! cecil Baskelett! I beg Seymour

Austin’s pardon for having suspect he...”



now sound Captain Baskelett’s trumpet.



angry though he be, Beauchamp laugh. “ben’t it exactly like the

baron to spring a mine of this kind?”



there be decidedly humour in the plot, and it be a lusty quarterstaff

blow into the bargain. beauchamp’s head ring with it. he could not

conceal the stunning effect it have on he. gratitude and tenderness

toward Cecilia for save he, at the cost of a partial breach of faith

that he quite understand, from the scandal of the public entry into

Bevisham on the Tory coach-box, alternate with his interjection

regard his uncle Everard.



at eleven, Cecilia sit in her pony-carriage give final direction to

Mrs. Devereux where to look out for the _Esperanza_ and the schooner’s

boat. “then I drive down alone,” Mrs. Devereux say.



the gentleman be all off, and every available maid with they on the

coach-box, a brilliant sight that have be miss by nevil and

Cecilia.



“why, here’s Lydiard!” say Nevil, suppose that Lydiard must be

approach he with tiding of the second Tory candidate. but Lydiard

know nothing of it. he be the bearer of a letter on foreign

paper—mark urgent, in Rosamund’s hand—and similarly word in the

well-know hand which have inscribe the original address of the letter

to Steynham.



Beauchamp open it and read:



Château Tourdestelle

“(Eure).



“come. I give you three day—no more.



“RENÉE.”



the brevity be horrible. do it spring from childish imperiousness or

tragic peril?



Beauchamp could imagine it to be this or that. in moment of excited

speculation we do not dwell on the possibility that there may be a

mixture of motive.



“I fear I must cross over to France this evening,” he say to Cecilia.



she reply, “it be likely to be stormy to-night. the steamboat may not

run.”



“if there’s a doubt of it, I shall find a french lugger. you be tired,

from not sleep last night.”



“no,” she answer, and nod to Mrs. Devereux, beside whom Mr.

Lydiard stand: “you will not drive down alone, you see.”



for a young lady threaten with a tempest in her heart, as disturb

to she as the one gathering in the West for ship at sea, Miss Halkett

bear herself well.









CHAPTER XXII.

the drive into bevisham





Beauchamp be request by Cecilia to hold the rein. his fair

companion in the pony-carriage prefer to lean back musing, and he

have leisure to think over the blow deal he by his uncle Everard with

so sure an aim so ringingly on the head. and in the first place he make

no attempt to disdain it because it be nothing but artful and

heavy-handed, after the mediaeval pattern. of old he himself have

delight in artfulness as well as boldness and the unmistakeable hit.

highly to prize generalship be in his blood, though latterly the very

force propel he to his political warfare have forbid the use of

it to he. he see the patient veteran lay his gun for a long shot—to

give as good as he have receive; and in realize Everard Romfrey’s

perfectly placid bearing under provocation, such as he certainly would

have maintain while prepare his reply to it, the raw fighting

humour of the plot touch the sense of justice in Beauchamp enough to

make he own that he have be the first to offend.



he could reflect also on the likelihood that other offended man of his

uncle’s age and position would have sulk or storm, threaten the

parthian shot of the vindictive testator. if there be godlessness in

turn to politic for a weapon to strike a domestic blow, manfulness

in some degree signalize it. beauchamp could fancy his uncle cry

out, who set the example? and he be not at that instant incline to

dwell on the occult virtue of the example he have set. to be honest,

this elevation of a political puppet like Cecil Baskelett, and the

start he, out of the same family which Turbot, the journalist, have

magnify, into Bevisham with such pomp and flourish in opposition to

the serious young champion of popular right and the Puritan style, be

ludicrously effective. Conscienceless of course. but that be the way

of the Old School.



Beauchamp break the silence by thank Cecilia once more for save

he from the absurd exhibition of the radical candidate on the Tory

coach-box, and laugh at the grimmish slyness of his uncle Everard’s

conspiracy a something in it that be half-smile half-sneer; not

exactly malignant, and by no mean innocent; something make up of the

simplicity of a light match, and its proximity to powder, yet neither

deadly, in spite of a wicked twinkle, nor at all pretend to be

harmless: in short, a speciman of old english practical humour.



he labour to express these or corresponding view of it, with

tolerably natural laughter, and Cecilia rally her spirit at his

pleasant manner of take his blow.



“I shall compliment the baron when I meet he tonight,” he say. “what

can we compare he to?”



she suggest the Commander of the Faithful, the Lord Haroun, who

likewise have a turn for buffoonery to serve a purpose, and could

direct they loftily and sovereignty.



“no: Everard Romfrey’s a northerner from the foot up,” say Beauchamp.



Cecilia compliantly offer he a sketch of the scandinavian Troll:

much nearer the mark, he think, and exclaim: “Baron Troll! I’m

afraid, Cecilia, you have rob he of the good part of his fun. and

you will owe it entirely to he if you should be represent in

Parliament by my cousin Baskelett.”



“promise I, Nevil, that you will, when you meet Captain Baskelett, not

forget I do you some service, and that I wish, I shall be so glad if

you do not resent certain thing.... very objectionable, we all think.”



he release she from the embarrassing petition: “oh! now I know my man,

you may be sure I willn’t waste a word on he. the fact be, he would not

understand a word, and would require more—and that I don’t do. when I

fancy Mr. Austin be the responsible person, I mean to speak to

he.”



Cecilia smile gratefully.



the sweetness of a love-speech would not have be sweet to she than

this proof of civilized chivalry in Nevil.



they come to the fir-height overlook Bevisham. here the breezy

beginning of a south-western autumnal gale toss the pony’ mane and

make thread of Cecilia’s short lock of beautiful auburn by the

temple and the neck, bluster the curl that stream in a thick

involution from the silken band gather they off her uncovered

clear-sweep ear.



Beauchamp take an impression of her side face. it seem to offer he

everything the world could offer of cultivate purity, intelligent

beauty and attractiveness; and “Wilt thou?” say the wing minute.

Peace, a good repute in the mouth of man, home, and a trustworthy

woman for mate, an ideal english lady, the rare growth of our

country, and friend and fair esteem, be offer. last night he have

waltz with she, and the manner of this tall graceful girl in

submit to the union of the measure and reserve her individual

distinction, have exquisitely flatter his taste, give he an

auspicious image of she in partnership, through the use of life.



he look ahead at the low dead-blue cloud swinge from across

channel. what could be the riddle of Renée’s letter! it chain he

completely.



“at all event, I shall not be away long than three day,” he say;

paused, eyed Cecilia’s profile, and add, “do we differ so much?”



“it may not be so much as we think,” say she.



“but if we do!”



“then, Nevil, there be a difference between we.”



“but if we keep our lip close?”



“we should have to shut our eye as well!”



a lovely melt image of she stole over he; all the warm for her

unwittingness in produce it: and it awaken a tenderness toward the

simple speaker.



Cecilia’s delicate breeding save she from run on figuratively. she

continue: “intellectual difference do not cause wound, except when

very unintellectual sentiment be behind them:—my conceit, or your

impatience, Nevil? ‘_Noi veggiam come quei, che ha mala luce._’... I

can confess my sight to be imperfect: but will you ever do so?”



her musical voice in Italian charm his hearing.



“what poet be that you quote?”



“the wise: Dante.”



“Dr. Shrapnel’s favourite! I must try to read he.”



“he read Dante?” Cecilia throw a stress on the august name; and it be

manifest that she care not for the answer.



contemptuous exclusiveness could not go far.



“he be a man of cultivation,” Beauchamp say cursorily, try to avoid

dissension, but in vain. “I wish I be half as well instruct, and

the world half as charitable as he!—You ask I if I shall admit my

sight to be imperfect. yes; when you prove to I that priest and

landlord be willing to do their duty by the people in preference to

their church and their property: but will you ever shake off

prejudice?”



here be opposition sound again. Cecilia mentally reproach Dr.

Shrapnel for it.



“indeed, Nevil, really, must not—may I not ask you this?—must not every

one feel the evil spell of some association? and Dante and Dr.

Shrapnel!”



“you don’t know he, Cecilia.”



“I see he yesterday.”



“you think he too tall?”



“I think of his character.”



“how angry I should be with you if you be not so beautiful!”



“I be immensely indebted to my unconscious advocate.”



“you be clothe in steel; you flash back; you willn’t answer I out of the

heart. I’m convince it be pure wilfulness that make you oppose I.”



“I fancy you must be convince because you cannot imagine woman to have

any share of public spirit, Nevil.”



a grain of truth in that remark set Nevil reflect.



“I want they to have it,” he remark, and glance at a Tory placard,

probably the puppet’s fresh-print address to the elector, on one of

the wayside fir-tree. “Bevisham look well from here. we might make a

north-western Venice of it, if we like.”



“Papa tell you it would be money sink in mud.”



“do I mention it to him?—Thoroughly Conservative!—So he would leave

the mud as it be. they insist on our not venture anything—those

Tories! exactly as though we have gain the good of human condition,

instead of count crop of rogue, malefactor, egoist, noxious and

lumbersome creature that deaden the country. your town down there be

one of the ugly and dirty in the kingdom: it might be the

fairest.”



“I have often think that of Bevisham, Nevil.”



he draw a visionary sketch of quay, embankment, bridged island,

public building, magical emanation of patriotic architecture, with a

practical air, an absence of that enthusiasm which strike she with

suspicion when it be not apply to landscape or the Arts; and she

accept it, and warm, and even allow herself to appear hesitate

when he return to the similarity of the state of mud-begirt Bevisham

and our great sluggish England.



be he not perhaps to be pity in his bondage to the Frenchwoman, who

could have no idea in common with he?



the rare circumstance that she and Nevil Beauchamp have find a subject

of agreement, partially overcome the sentiment Cecilia entertain for

the foreign lady; and have now one idea in common with he, she

conceive the possibility that there might be more. there must be many,

for he love England, and she no less. she clung, however, to the topic

of Bevisham, prefer to dream of the many more, rather than run

risk. undoubtedly the town be of an ignoble aspect; and it be

decline in prosperity; and it be consequently over-populate. and

undoubtedly (so she be induce to coincide for the moment) a

Government, act to any extent like a supervise head, should aid

and direct the energy of town and port and trade, and not leave

everything everywhere to chance: school for the people, public

morality, should be the charge of Government. Cecilia have surrender

the lead to he, and be force to subscribe to an equivalent of

“undoubtedly” the Tories just as little as the Liberals have do these

good office. party against party, neither of they have a forethoughtful

head for the land at large. they wait for the Press to spur a great

imperial country to be but defensively armed, and they accept the

so-call volunteer, with a nominal one-month’s drill per annum, as a

guarantee of defence!



Beauchamp startle she, actually kindle her mind to an activity of

wonder and regret, with the statement of how much Government, act

with some degree of farsightedness, _might_ have win to pay the public

debt and remit taxation, by originally retain the line of railway,

and fasten on the valuable land adjoining station. hundred of

million of pound!



she drop a sigh at the prodigious amount, but inquire, “who have

calculate it?”



for though perfectly aware that this kind of conversation be a special

compliment pay to she by her friend Nevil, and dimly perceive that

it imply something beyond a compliment—in fact, that it be his

manner of probe she for sympathy, as other man would have conduct

the process preliminary to deadly flattery or to wooing, her wit

fence her heart about; the exercise of shrewdness be an instinct of

self-preservation. she have nothing but her poor wit, daily grow

fainter, to resist he with. and he seem to know it, and therefore

assail they, never try at the heart.



that vast army of figure might be but a phantom army conjure out of

the radical mist, might it not? she hint. and besides, we cannot

surely require a Government to speculate in the future, can we?



possibly not, as government go, Beauchamp say.



but what think you of a Government of landowner decree the

enclosure of million of acre of common land amongst themselves;

take the property of the people to add to their own! Say, be not that

plunder? public property, observe; decreed to they by their own

law-making, under the pretence that it be be reclaim for

cultivation, when in reality it have be but an addition to their

pleasure-ground: a flat robbery of pasture from the poor man’s cow and

goose, and his right of cut furze for fire. consider that!

Beauchamp’s eye flash democratic in recite this injury to the

object of his warm solicitude—the man, the cow, and the goose. but so

must he have look when front England’s enemy, and his aspect of

fervour subdue Cecilia. she confess her inability to form an

estimate of such conduct.



“be they do it still?” she ask.



“we owe it to Dr. Shrapnel foremost that there be now a watch over they

to stop they. but for he, Grancey Lespel would have enclose half of

Northeden Heath. as it be, he have filch bit here and there, and he

will have to put back his paling.”



however, now let Cecilia understand that we English, call ourselves

free, be under morally lawless rule. _government_ be what we require,

and our mean of get it must be through universal suffrage. at

present we have no government; only shift Party Ministries, which

be the tool of diver interest, wealthy faction, to the sacrifice

of the Commonwealth.



she listen, like Rosamund Culling overbear by Dr. Shrapnel, inwardly

pray that she might discover a man to reply to he.



“a Despotism, Nevil?”



he hope not, decline the despot, be English enough to stand against

the good of man in that character; but he cast it on Tory, Whig, and

Liberal, otherwise the Constitutionalists, if we be to come upon the

despot.



“they see we be close on universal suffrage; they’ve be bid each

in turn for ‘the people,’ and that have bring they to it, and now

they’re alarmed, and accuse one another of treason to the Constitution,

and they don’t accept the situation: and there’s a fear, that to carry

on their present system, they will be thwart the people or

corrupt they: and in that case we shall have our despot in some

shape or other, and we shall suffer.”



“Nevil,” say Cecilia, “I be out of my depth.”



“I’ll support you; I can swim for two,” say he.



“you be very self-confident, but I find I be not fit for battle; at

least not in the front rank.”



“nerve I, then: will you? try to comprehend once for all what the

battle be.”



“I be afraid I be too indifferent; I be too luxurious. that remind I:

you want to meet your uncle Everard and if you will sleep at Mount

Laurels to-night, the _Esperanza_ shall take you to France to-morrow

morning, and can wait to bring you back.”



as she speak she perceive a flush mount over Nevil’s face. soon it

be communicate to hers.



the strange secret of the blood electrify they both, and reveal the

burn undercurrent run between they from the heart of each. the

light that show how near they be to one another be kindle at the

barrier divide they. it remain as good as a secret, unchallenged

until they have separate, and after midnight Cecilia look through her

chamber window at the drive moon of a hurricane scud, and read

clearly his honourable reluctance to be waft over to his french love

by her assistance; and Beauchamp on board the tossing steamboat

perceive in her sympathetic reddening that she have divine he.



this auroral light eclipse the other event of the day. he drive into

a town royally decorate, and still hum with the ravishment of the

Tory entrance. he sail in the schooner to Mount Laurels, in the

society of Captain Baskelett and his friend, who, find he tame

than they expect, banter he in the cheerfullest fashion. he wait

for his uncle Everard several hour at Mount Laurels, peruse the

junior Tory’s address to the Electors, throughout which there be not

an idea—safe of address to canvass upon! peruse likewise, at

Captain Baskelett’s request, a broad sheet of an article introduce

the new candidate to Bevisham with the battle-axe Romfreys to back he,

in high burlesque of Timothy Turbot upon Beauchamp: and Cecil hope his

cousin would not object to his borrow a Romfrey or two for so

press an occasion. all very funny, and no doubt the presence of Mr.

Everard Romfrey would have heighten the fun from the fountain-head;

but he happen to be delay, and Beauchamp have to leave direction

behind he in the town, besides the discussion of a whole plan of

conduct with Dr. Shrapnel, so he be under the necessity of depart

without see his uncle, really to his regret. he leave word to that

effect.



take leave of Cecilia, he talk of his return “home” within three or

four day as a certainty.



she say: “canvass should not be neglect now.”



her hostility be confuse by what she have do to save he from

annoyance, while his behaviour to his cousin Cecil increase she

respect for he. she detect a pathetic meaning in his mention of the

word home; she muse on his having call she beautiful: whither be

she hurry? forgetful of her horror of his revolutionary idea,

forgetful of the elevation of her own, she thrill secretly on hear

it state by the jubilant young Tories at Mount Laurels, as a

characteristic of Beauchamp, that he be clever in parry political

thrust, and slip from the theme; he who with she give out

unguardedly the thought deepest in he. and the thoughts!—were they

not of generous origin? where so true a helpmate for he as the one to

whom his mind appeal? it could not be so with the Frenchwoman.

Cecilia divine a generous nature by generosity, and set herself to

believe that in honour he have not yet dare to speak to she from the

heart, not be at heart quite free. she be at the same time in she

remain of pride cool enough to examine and rebuke the weakness she

succumb to in now cling to he by that which yesterday she hardly

less than loathe, still deeply dislike.









CHAPTER XXIII.

TOURDESTELLE





on the part of Beauchamp, his conversation with Cecilia during the

drive into Bevisham open out for the first time in his life a

prospect of home; he have feel the word in speak it, and it signify

an end to the distraction produce by the sex, allegiance to one

beloved respected woman, and also a basis of operation against the

world. for she be evidently conquerable, and once match with he

would be the very woman to nerve and sustain he. do she not listen to

he? he like her resistance. that element of the barbarous which go

largely to form his emotional nature be overjoy in wrest such a

woman from the enemy, and subdue she personally. she be a prize. she

be a splendid prize, cut out from under the gun of the fort. he

render all that be due to his eminently good cause for its part in

so signal a success, but individual satisfaction be not diminish by

the thought that the individual’s discernment select the cause thus

beneficent to he.



Beauchamp’s meditation be divert by the sight of the coast of

France dash in rain-line across a weed-strew sea. the “three day”

grant he by Renée be over, and it scarcely trouble he that he

should be behind the time; he detest mystery, hold it to be a sign

of pretentious feebleness, often of imposture, it might be frivolity.

punctilious obedience to the mysterious brevity of the summon, and not

to chafe at it, appear to he as much as could be expect of a

struggle man. this be the state of the case with he, until he stand

on french earth, breathe french air, and chance to hear the tongue of

France twitter by a lady on the quay. the charm be instantaneous. he

remind himself that Renée, unlike her countrywoman, have no gift for

writing letter. they have never correspond since the hour of her
