marriage. they have meet in Sicily, at Syracuse, in the presence of her

father and her husband, and so inanimate be she that the meeting

seem like the conclusion of their history. her brother Roland send

tiding of she by fit, and sometimes a conventional message from

Tourdestelle. latterly her husband’s name have be cite as among the

wildfire of parisian quay, in journal more or less devoted to those

unreclaimed space of the city. well, if she be unhappy, be it not

the fulfilment of his prophecy in Venice?



Renée’s brevity become luminous. she need he urgently, and know

the word which say she need he. why, those brief word be the

be able to read that, “come; I give you three day,” address to he,

be not language of a woman free of her yoke.



excited to guess and guess, Beauchamp sweep on to speculation of a

she to be there await he: she be under the sea-shadow Alps,

look up to the red and gold-rose height of a realm of morning that

be hers inviolably, and under which Renée be eternally his.



the interval between then and now be but the space of an unquiet sea

traverse in the night, sad in the passage of it, but featureless—and

it have prove he right! it be to Nevil Beauchamp as if the spirit of

his old passion wake up again to glorious hopeful morning when he stand

in Renée’s France.



Tourdestelle enjoy the aristocratic privilege of be twelve mile

below in the flat for Tourdestelle. he cross the woode neck above

divide the whole underlie country into sharp outline of dark and

and plume hat signalize the sex of one. Beauchamp sing out a

gondolier’s cry. he fancy it be answer.



he be hear, for the lady turn about, and as he ride down, still

uncertainty.



Moonlight be friendless to eye that would make sure of a face long

on she, and whether she be in bloom, or wan as the beam reveal

she, he could not see.



her tongue sound to he as if it be loosen without a voice. “you

have come. that storm! you be safe!”



so phantom-like a sound of speech alarm he. “I lose no time. but

you?”



“I be well.”



“nothing hang over you?”



“nothing.”



“why give I just three day?”



“pure impatience. have you forget I?”



their horse walk on with they. they unlock their hand.



“you know it be I?” say he.



“who else could it be? I hear Venice,” she reply.



her previous cavalier be on his foot, all but on his knee, it

droop exotic of the conservatory perhaps resemble it.

he bow to Beauchamp with an extreme sweep of the hat.

beseech an exemption from that horrible mal de mer. thank to the

the gentleman on each side of she strike into a trot. Beauchamp glance

and shoot forth gleam of silvery disquiet by rustic bridge and mill.



the strangeness of be beside she, not having yet scan her face,

Renée slacken speed, say: “Tourdestelle span a branch of our

have grander park, they say. we can give you sunlight.”

“only, and really. one do not live for ever—on earth; and it become

a question whether friend should be shadow to one another before

because I be Renée.”



“you relieve I!”



“evidently you have forget my character, Nevil.”



“not a feature of it.”



“ah!” she breathe involuntarily.



“would you have I forget it?”



“when I think by myself, quite alone, yes, I would. otherwise how can

not deal in this language since I last think of write a diary, and

the moon launch her fairy silver fleet on a double sweep of the

he look from it to she, and ask if Roland be well, and her father;

travel.”



Beauchamp be conscious of some bitter taste; unaware of what it be,

Renée answer: “he have the misfortune to be consider the handsome

young man in France.”



“he have an italian look.”



“his mother be Provençale.”



she put her horse in motion, say: “I agree with you that handsome

man be rarity. and, by the way, they do not set _our_ world on fire

quite as much as beautiful woman do your, my friend. acknowledge so

much in our favour.”



he assent indefinitely. he could have wish himself away canvass

in Bevisham. he have only to imagine himself away from she, to feel the

flood of joy in be with she.



“your husband be travel?”



“it be his pleasure.”



could she have intend to say that this be good news to give of he

as well as of the happiness of her father and brother?



“now look on Tourdestelle,” say Renée. “you will avow that for an

active man to be condemn to seek repose in so dull a place, after the

she never have; and possibly of lady who be disembody we may say

I need not state it.” she cease; presently continue: “the other

serve in Afric—my sister by marriage, and my friend; the baronne

sportsman; M. Livret, an erudite. no young lady: I can bear much, but

not their presence; girl be odious to I. I know one in Venice.”



they come within the ray of the lamp hang above the unpretende

entrance to the château. Renée’s broad grey Longueville hat curve low

with its black plume on the side farth from he. he be favour by

her eye.



“he wear a glove at his breast,” say Beauchamp.



“you speak of M. d’Henriel. he wear a glove at his breast; yes, it be

mine,” say Renée.



she slip from her horse and stand against his shoulder, as if

wait to be question before she ring the bell of the château.



Beauchamp alight, burn with his unutterable question concern

that glove.



“lift your hat, let I beg you; let I see you,” he say.

“you perceive, monsieur, that I have my sentimental fit like other;

but in truth I be not insensible to the picturesque or to gratitude,

and I thank you sincerely for come, consider that I write like a

Sphinx—to evade write _comme une folle!_”



she sweep to the bell.



stand in the arch of the entrance, she stretch her whip out to a

black mass of prostrate timber, say:



“it fall in the storm at two o’clock after midnight, and you on the

sea!”









CHAPTER XXIV.

his holiday





a single day be to be the term of his holiday at Tourdestelle; but it

stand forth as one of those perfect day which be round by an

evening before and a morning after, give he two night under the

same roof with Renée, something of a resemblance to three day of she;

anticipation and wonder fill the first, she the next, the adieu the

last: every hour fill. and the first day be not over yet. he force

himself to calmness, that he might not fritter it, and walk up and

down the room he be dress in, examine its foreign decoration,

and peer through the window, to quiet his nerve. he be in her own

France with she! the country borrow hue from Renée, and lend some.

this chivalrous France frame and interlace her image, aid in

idealize she, and be in turn transfigure. not half so well would

his native land have plead for the forgiveness of a british damsel

who have wreck a young man’s immoderate first love. that glorify

self-love require the touch upon imagination of strangeness and an

unaccustomed grace, to subdue it and make it pardon an outrage to its

temple and altar, and its happy reading of the heaven, the earth

too: earth foremost, we ought perhaps to say. it be an exact

heathen, well understand by a glance at what will appease it:

beautiful, however, as everybody have prove; and shall it be decry in

a world where beauty be not overcommon, though it would slaughter we

for its angry satisfaction, yet can be soothe by a tone of colour, as

it be by a novel inscription on a sweetmeat?



the peculiarity of Beauchamp be that he know the slenderness of the

thread which be lead he, and foresaw it twist to a coil unless

he should hold firm. his work in life be much above the love of a

woman in his estimation, so he be not delude by passion when he

enter the château; it be doubtful whether he would not hesitatingly

have sacrifice one of the precious vote in Bevisham for the pleasure

of kiss her hand when they be on the step. she be his first love

and only love, marry, and long ago forgiven:—married; that be to say,

she especially among woman be interdict to he by the linger

shadow of the reverential love go by; and if the anguish of the

lover’s bad than death survive in a shudder of memory at the thought

of she not solely lose to he but possess by another, it do but

quicken a hunger that be three part curiosity to see how she who have

suffer this bore the change; how like or unlike she might be to the

extinct Renée; what trace she keep of the face he have know. her tear

be startling, but tear tell of a mood, they do not tell the story of

the year; and it be that story he have such eagerness to read in one

brief revelation: an eagerness bear only of the last few hour, and

break by fear of a tarnish aspect; these again be partly hope

of a come disillusion that would restore he his independence and ask

he only for pity. the slavery of the love of a woman chain like

Renée be the most revolting of prospect to a man who cherish his

freedom that he might work to the end of his time. moreover, it swing a

thunder-cloud across his holiday. he recur to the idea of the

holiday repeatedly, and the more he do so the thin it wane. he be

exhaust the very air and spirit of it with a mind that run

incessantly forward and back; and when he and the lady of so much

speculation be again together, an incapacity of observation seem to

have come over he. in reality it be the inability to reflect on his

observation. her presence resemble those dark sunset throw the

spell of colour across the world; when there be no question with we of

morning or of night, but of that sole splendour only.



owe to their arrival late at the château, cover be lay for they

in the boudoir of Madame la Marquise, where he have his hostess to

himself, and certainly the opportunity of study she. an English Navy

List, solitary on a shelf, and lay within it an extract of a paper

announce the return of the _Ariadne_ to port, explain the mystery

of she know that he be in England, as well as the correctness of

the superscription of her letter to he. “you see, I follow you,” she

say.



Beauchamp ask if she read English now.



“a little; but the paper be dispatch to I by M. Vivian Ducie, of

your embassy in Paris. he be in the valley.”



the name of Ducie recall Lord Palmet’s description of the dark beauty

of the flutter pale gold ornament. she be now dress without one

decoration of gold or jewel, with scarcely a wave in the silk, a

modesty of style eloquent of the pride of her form.



could those eye front he under the lamp have recently shed tear?

they be the live eye of a brilliant unembarrassed lady; shield

fling light rather than well-depth invite it.



Beauchamp try to compare she with the Renée of Venice, and find

himself think of the glove she have surrender to the handsome

young man in France. the effort to recover the young face give he a

dead creature, with the eyelash of Renée, the cast of her mouth and

throat, misty as a shape in a dream.



he could compare she with Cecilia, who never would have risk a glove,

never have betray a tear, and be the stately lady, not without

language: but how much less vivid in feature and the gift of speech!

Renée’s gift of speech count unnumbered string which she play on

with a grace that clothe the skill, and be her natural endowment—an

art perfect by the education of the world. who cannot talk!—but who

can? discover the writer in a day when all be write! it be as rare

an art as poetry, and in the mouth of woman as enrapture, rich

than their voice in music.



this be the fascination Beauchamp feel weave round he. would you,

that be separable from boy and mob, and the object malignly call

the Briton, prefer the celestial singing of a woman to she excellently

talk? but not if it be give you to run in unison with her genius

of the tongue, follow her verbal ingenuity and feminine

silk-flash of meaning; not if she lead you to match her fine quick

perception with more or less of the discreet concordance of the

violoncello accompany the viol. it be not high flying, which usually

end in heavy falling. you quit the level of earth no more than two

bird that chase from bush to bush to bill in air, for mutual delight

to make the concert heavenly. language flow from Renée in affinity

with the pleasure-give law that make the curve we recognize as

beauty in sublimer art. accept companionship for the dearest of the

good thing we pray to have, and what equal she! who could be her

rival!



her girl’s crown of irradiate Alps begin to tremble over her dimly, as

from moment to moment their intimacy warm, and Beauchamp see the

young face vanish out of this flower of womanhood. he do not see it

appear or present, but vanish like the faint ray in the rosier.

Nay, the blot of her faithlessness undergo a transformation: it

affect he somewhat as the patch cunningly lay on near a liquid

dimple in fair cheek at once allure and evade a susceptible

attention.



unused in his French of late, he stumble at time, and she supply

the need phrase, take no note of a blunder. now man of sweet blood

cannot be secretly accuse or criticize a gracious lady. domestic

man be charge with thinking instantly of dark death when an ordinary

illness befall they; and it may be so or not: but it be positive that

the gallant man of the world, if he be in the sensitive condition, and

not yet establish as the lord of she, feel paralyze in his

masculine sense of leadership the moment his lady assume the

initiative and direct he: he give up at once; and thus have many

nimble-witte dame from one clear start retain their advantage.



concern that glove: well! the handsome young man in France wear

the glove of the lovely woman. the lovely? the very lovely in

the purity of her french style—the woman to challenge England for a

type of beauty to eclipse she. it be possible to conceive her country

wager she against all woman.



if Renée have fault, Beauchamp think of she as at sea breasting

tempest, while Cecilia be a vessel lie safe in harbour, untried,

however promise: and if Cecilia raise a steady light for he, it be

over the shore he have leave behind, while Renée have really nothing to

do with warning or rescue, or with imperilling; she welcome he

simply to a holiday in her society. he associate Cecilia strangely

with the political labour she would have have he relinquish; and Renée

with a pleasant state of indolence, that her light smile disturb.

Shun comparison.



it be the tricksy heart which set up that balance, to jump into it on

one side or the other. comparison come of a secret lean that be

sure to play rogue under its mien of honest dealer: so Beauchamp

suffer himself to be unjust to graver England, and lose the strength

she would have give he to resist a bewitchment. the case with he

be, that his apprenticeship be new; he have be trot in harness

as a veritable cab-horse of politic—he by blood a racer; and his

nature crave for diversion, against his will, against his moral sense

and bear tenacity of spirit.



not a word far of the glove. but at night, in his bed, the glove

be a principal actor in event of extraordinary magnitude and

inconsequence.



he be out in the ground with the early morning light. coffee and

sweet french bread be bring out to he, and he be inform of the

hour of reunion at the château, whose mistress continue invisible.

she might be sleep. he stroll about, within view of the window,

wonder at her subservience to sleep. Tourdestelle lie in one of

those Norman valley where the river be the mother of rich pasture, and

run hide between double rank of sallow, aspen and poplar, that

mark its wind line in the arm of trench meadow. the high land on

either side be an unwatered flat up to the horizon, little varied by

dusty apple-tree plant in the stubble here and there, and brown mud

wall of hamlet; a church-top, a copse, an avenue of dwarf lime

lead to the three-part farm, quarter residence of an enrich

peasant strike new root, or decay proprietor pinch not to be

sever from ancient. descend on the deep green valley in Summer be

like a change of clime. the château stand square at a branch of the

river, toss three light bridge of pretty woodwork to park and

garden. great bouquet of swell blue and pink hydrangia nestle at

his foot on shaven grass. an open window show a cloth of colour, as

in a reminiscence of Italy.



Beauchamp hear himself addressed:—“You be look for my

sister-in-law, M. Beauchamp?”



the speaker be Madame d’Auffray, to whom he have be introduce

overnight—a lady of the aquiline french outline, not ungentle.



Renée have speak affectionately of she, he remember. there be

nothing to make he be on his guard, and he state that he be look

for Madame de Rouaillout, and do not conceal surprise at the

information that she be out on horseback.



“she be a tireless person,” Madame d’Auffray remark. “you will not

miss she long. we all meet at twelve, as you know.”



“I grudge an hour, for I go to-morrow,” say Beauchamp.



the notification of so early a departure, or else his bluntness,

astonish she. she fall to praise Renée’s goodness. he keep she to

it with lively interrogation, in the manner of a guileless boy urge

for eulogy of his dear absent friend. be it duplicity in he or

artlessness?



“have she, do you think, increase in beauty?” Madame d’Auffray

inquire: an insidious question, to which he reply:



“once I think it would be impossible.”



not so bad an answer for an Englishman, in a country where speak be

fencing; the race be little famous for dialectical alertness: but

be it artful or simple?



they skirt the château, and Beauchamp have the history of Dame

Philiberte recount to he, with a mixture of Gallic irony, innuendo,

openness, touchingness, ridicule, and charity novel to his ear. Madame

d’Auffray strike the note of intimacy early than be habitual. she

sound he in this way once or twice, carelessly peruse he, and

wait for the interesting edition of the Book of Man to summarize its

character by show its page or remain shut. it be do

delicately, like the tap of a finger-nail on a vase. he ring clear; he

have nothing to conceal; and where he be reserve, that is, in speak

of the develop beauty and grace of Renée, he be transparent. she

read the sort of man he be; she could also hazard a guess as to the

man’s present state. she venture to think he comparatively

harmless—for the hour: for she be not the woman to be hoodwink by

man’s dark nature because she incline to think well of a particular

man; nor be she one to trust to any man subject to temptation. the

wisdom of the Frenchwoman’s fortieth year forbid it. a land where the

war between the sex be honestly acknowledge, and be full of

instruction, abound in precept; but it ill become the veteran to

practise rigorously what she would prescribe to young woman. she may

discriminate; as thus:—Trust no man. still, this man may be well than

that man; and it be bad policy to distrust a reasonably guileless

member of the preying sex entirely, and so to lose his good service.

hawk have their use in destroy vermin; and though we cannot rely

upon the taming of hawk, one tie by the leg in a garden preserve the

fruit.



“there be a necessity for your leave we to-morrow; M. Beauchamp?”



“I regret to say, it be imperative, madame.”



“my husband will congratulate I on the pleasure I have, and have long

desire, of make your acquaintance, and he will grieve that he have

not be so fortunate; he be on service in Africa. my brother, I need

not say, will deplore the mischance which have prevent he from

Germany, and will come assuredly, if there be a prospect of find you

here. none? suppose my telegram not to fall short of he, I may count

on his being here within four day.”



Beauchamp beg she to convey the proper expression of his regret to

M. le Marquis.



“and M. de Croisnel? and Roland, your old comrade and brother-in-arm?

what will be their disappointment!” she say.



“I intend to stop for an hour at Rouen on my way back,” say Beauchamp.



she ask if her belle-soeur be aware of the short limitation of his

visit.



he have not mention it to Madame la Marquise.



“perhaps you may be move by the grief of a friend: Renée may persuade

you to stay.”



“I came imagine I could be of some use to Madame la Marquise. she

write as if she be telegraph.”



“perfectly true of she! for that matter, I see the letter. your look

betray a very natural jealousy; but see it or not it would have be

the same: she and I have no secret. she be, I may tell you, strictly

unable to write more word in the letter. which bring I to inquire

what impression M. d’Henriel make on you yesterday evening.”



“he be particularly handsome.”



“we woman think so. do you take he to be... eccentric?”



Beauchamp give a french jerk of the shoulder.



it confess the incident of the glove to one who know it as well as

he: but it mask the weight he be begin to attach to that

incident, and Madame d’Auffray be mislead. truly, the Englishman may be

just such an ex-lover, uninflammable by virtue of his blood’s native

coldness; endue with the frozen vanity call pride, which do not

seek to be revenge. under wary espionage, he might be a young woman’s

friend, though male friend of a half-abandon wife should write

himself down morally saint, mentally sage, medically incurable, if he

would win our confidence.



this lady of sharp intelligence be the guardian of Renée during the

foolish husband’s flight about Paris and over Europe, and, for a proof

of her consummate astuteness, Renée have no secret and have absolute

liberty. and hitherto no man could build a boast on her reputation. the

liberty she would have have at any cost, as Madame d’Auffray know; and

an attempt to restrict it would have create secret.



near upon the breakfast-hour Renée be perceive by they go toward

the château at a walking pace. they cross one of the garden bridge

to intercept she. she start out of some deep meditation, and raise

her whip hand to Beauchamp’s greeting. “I have forget to tell you,

monsieur, that I should be out for some hour in the morning.”



“be you aware,” say Madame d’Auffray, “that M. Beauchamp leave we

to-morrow?”



“so soon?” it be utter hardly with a tone of disappointment.



the marquise alight, cry hold, to the stable, caress her horse,

and send he off with a smack on the smoking flank to meet the groom.



“to-morrow? that be very soon; but M. Beauchamp be engage in an

Election, and what have we to induce he to stay?”



“would it not be well to tell M. Beauchamp why he be invite to

come?” rejoin Madame d’Auffray.



the sombre light in Renée’s eye quicken through shadowy sphere of

surprise and pain to resolution. she cry, “you have my full consent,”

and leave they.



Madame d’Auffray smile at Beauchamp, to excuse the childishness of the

little story she be about to relate; she give it in the essence,

without a commencement or an ending. she have in fact but two or three

hurry minute before the breakfast-bell would ring; and the fan she

open and shut, and at time shade her head with, be nearly as

explicit as her tongue.



he understand that Renée have stake her glove on his come within a

certain number of hour to the brief wording of invitation possible.

owe to his detention by the storm, M. d’Henriel have win the bet, and

now insist on wear the glove. “he be the privileged young madman

our woman make of a handsome youth,” say Madame d’Auffray.



where be I? think Beauchamp—in what land, he would have phrase it,

of whirlwind catch the wit, and whip the passion? Calmer than

they, but unable to command they, and guess that Renée’s errand of

the morning, by which he have lose hour of she, pertain to the glove,

he say quiveringly, “Madame la Marquise object?”



“we,” reply Madame d’Auffray, “contend that the glove be not loyally

win. the wager be upon your come to the invitation, not upon your

conquer the element. as to his flaunt the glove for a favour, I

would ask you, whom do he advertize by that? glove do not wear

white; which fact compromise none but the wearer. he pick it up from

the ground, and do not restore it; that be all. you see a boy who

catch at anything to placard himself. there be a compatriot of your,

a M. Ducie, who assure we you must be with an uncle in your county of

Sussex. of course we run the risk of the letter miss you, but the

chance be worth a glove. can you believe it, M. Beauchamp? it be I,

old woman as I be, I who provoke the silly wager. I have long desire

to meet you; and we have little society here, we be desperate with

loneliness, half mad with our whim. I say, that if you be what I

have hear of you, you would come to we at a word. they dare Madame la

Marquise to say the same. I wish to see the friend of Frenchmen, as

M. Roland call you; not merely to see he—to know he, whether he be

this perfect friend whose absolute devotion have impress my dear

sister Renée’s mind. she respect you: that be a sentiment scarcely

complimentary to the idea of young man. she place you above human

creature: possibly you may not dislike to be worship. it be not to

be reject when one’s influence be powerful for good. but you leave we

to-morrow!”



“I might stay...” Beauchamp hesitate to name the number of hour. he

stand divide between a sense of the bubble shallowness of the life

about he, and a thought, grave as an eye dwelling on blood, of

sinister thing below it.



“I may stay another day or two,” he say, “if I can be of any earthly

service.”



Madame d’Auffray bow as to a friendly decision on his part, say,

“it would be a thousand pity to disappoint M. Roland; and it will be

offer my brother an amicable chance. I will send he word that you

await he; at least, that you defer your departure as long as possible.

ah! now you perceive, M. Beauchamp, now you have become aware of our

purely infantile plan to bring you over to we, how very ostensible a

punishment it would be be you to remain so short a period.”



have no design, he be neither dupe nor sceptic; but he feel oddly

entangle, and the dream of his holiday have flee like morning’s beam,

as a self-deception will at a very gentle shake.









CHAPTER XXV.

the adventure of the BOAT





Madame d’Auffray pass Renée, whisper on her way to take her seat

at the breakfast-table.



Renée do not condescend to whisper. “Roland will be glad,” she say

aloud.



her low eyelid challenge Beauchamp for a look of indifference. there

be more for she to unbosom than Madame d’Auffray have reveal, but the

comparative innocence of her position in this new light prompt she to

meet he defiantly, if he choose to feel injured. he be attract by a

happy contrast of colour between her dress and complexion, together

with a cavalierly charm in the sullen brow she lift; and see the

reverse of a look of indifference on his face, after what he have hear

of her frivolousness, she have a fear that it exist.



“be we not to have M. d’Henriel to-day? he amuse I,” the baronne

d’Orbec remark.



“if he would learn that he be fashion for that purpose!” exclaim

little M. Livret.



“do not ask young man for too much head, my friend; he would cease to

be amusing.”



“D’Henriel should have be up in the field at ten this morning,” say

M. d’Orbec. “as to his head, I back he for a clever shot.”



“or a duelling-sword,” say Renée. “it be a quality, count it for what

we will. your favourite, Madame la Baronne, be interdict from

present himself here so long as he persist in offend I.”



she be request to explain, and, with the fair ingenuousness which

outshine innocence, she touch on the story of the glove.



ah! what a delicate, what an exciting, how subtle a question!



have M. d’Henriel the right to possess it? and, have that, have he the

right to wear it at his breast?



Beauchamp be drag into the discussion of the case.



Renée wait curiously for his judgement.



plead an apology for the stormy weather, which have detain he, and

for his ignorance that so precious an article be at stake, he hold,

that by the term of the wager, the glove be lose; the claim to wear

it be a matter of taste.



“matter of taste, monsieur, be not, I think, decide by weapon in

your country?” say M. d’Orbec.



“we have no duelling,” say Beauchamp.



the Frenchman imagine the confession to be somewhat humbling, and

generously add, “but you have your volunteer—a magnificent spectacle

of patriotism and national readiness for defence!”



a shrewd pang traverse Beauchamp’s heart, as he look back on his

country from the outside and the inside, think what amount of

patriotic readiness the character of the volunteering signify, in the

face of all that England have to maintain. like a politic islander, he

allow the patriotic spectacle to be imagine; reflect that it do

a sort of service abroad, and have only to be unmasked at home.



“but you surrender the glove, marquise!” the baronne d’Orbec speak

judicially.



“I fling it to the ground: that make it neutral,” say Renée.



“Hum. he wear it with the dust on it, certainly.”



“and for how long a time,” M. Livret wish to know, “do this amusing

young man proclaim his intention of wear the glove?”



“until he can see with we that his order of Merit be utter kid,” say

Madame d’Auffray; and as she have speak more or less neatly,

satisfaction be leave reside in the ear of the assembly, and the

glove be permit to be sweep away on a fresh tide of dialogue.



the admirable candour of Renée in publicly allude to M. d’Henriel’s

foolishness restore a peep of his holiday to Beauchamp. Madame

d’Auffray take note of the effect it produce, and quite excuse her

sister-in-law for intend to produce it; but that speak out the

half-truth that we may put on the mask of the whole, be no new trick;

and believe as she do that Renée be in danger with the handsome

Count Henri, the practice of such a kind of honesty on her part

appear alarming.



still it be imprudent to press for confidence when our friend’s heart

be manifestly trifle with sincerity. who know but that some foregone

reckless act or word may have superinduce the healthy shame which

cannot speak, which must disguise itself, and be honesty in that form,

but roughly troubled would resolve to rank dishonesty? so think the

patient lady, wise in that than in her perception.



Renée make a boast of not persuade her guest to stay, avow that

she would not willingly have he go. praise he equably, she listen

to praise of he with animation. she be dumb and statue-like when

Count Henri’s name be mention. do not this betray like for one,

subjection to the other? indeed, there be an asiatic splendour of

animal beauty about M. d’Henriel that would be serpent with most woman,

Madame d’Auffray conceive; why not with the deserted Renée, who adore

beauty of shape and colour, and be compassionate toward a rashness of

character that her own unnatural solitariness and quick spirit make she

emulous of?



Meanwhile Beauchamp’s day of adieu succeed that of his holiday, and

no adieu be utter. the hour at Tourdestelle have a singular turn for

slip. interlinked and all as one they swam by, bring evening,

bring morning, never varied. they might have vary with such a

division as when flame light up the night or a tempest shade the day,

have Renée choose; she have that power over he. she have no wish to use

it; perhaps she apprehend what it would cause she to forfeit. she

wish he to respect she; feel that she be under the shadow of the

glove, slight though it be while it be nothing but a tale of a lady

and a glove; and her desire, like his, be that they should meet daily

and dream on, without a variation. he notice how seldom she lead he

beyond the ground of the château. they be to make excursion when

her brother come, she say. Roland de Croisnel’s colonel, Coïn de

Grandchamp, happen to be engage in a duel, which great business

detain Roland. it supply Beauchamp with an excuse for stay, that

he be angry with himself for be pleased to have; so he attack the

practice of duelling, and next the shrug, wherewith M. Livret and M.

d’Orbec seek at first to defend the foul custom, or apologize for it,

or plead for it philosophically, or altogether cast it off their

shoulder; for the literal interpretation of the shrug in argument be

beyond human capacity; it be the point of speech beyond our treasury of

language. he attack the shrug, as he think, very temperately; but

in control his native vehemence he grow, perforce of repression,

and of incompetency to deliver himself copiously in French, sarcastic.

in fine, his contrast of the pretence of their noble country to head

civilization, and its encouragement of a custom so barbarous, offended

M. d’Orbec and irritated M. Livret.



the latter deliver a brief essay on Gallic blood; the former

maintain that Frenchmen be the good judge of their own way and

deed. politeness reign, but politeness be compel to throw off

cloak and jacket when it step into the arena to meet the encounter of

a bull. beauchamp draw on their word “solidaire” to assist he in

declare that no civilized nation could be thus independent. imagine

himself in the France of brave idea, he contrive to strike out spark

of Legitimist ire around he, and find himself breathe the

atmosphere of the most primitive nursery of Toryism. again he

encounter the shrug, and he would have it a verbal matter. M. d’Orbec

gravely recite the programme of the country party in France. M. Livret

carry the war across Channel. you English have retire from active

life, like the exhausted author, to turn critic—the critic that sneer:

unless we copy you abjectly we be execrable. and what be that sneer?

materially it be an acrid saliva, wither where it drop; in the way

of fellowship it be a corpse-emanation. as to wit, the sneer be the

cloak of clumsiness; it be the Pharisee’s incense, the hypocrite’s

pity, the post of exaltation of the fat citizen, etc.; but, say M.

Livret, the people use it should have a care that they keep powerful:

they make no friend. he terminate with this warning to a nation not

devoid of superior merit. M. d’Orbec say less, and be less console

by his outburst.



in the opinion of Mr. Vivian Ducie, present at the discussion,

Beauchamp provoke the lash; for, in the first place, a beautiful

woman’s apparent favourite should be particularly discreet in all that

he say: and next, he should have know that the Gallic shrug over

matter political be volcanic—it be the heaving of the mountain, and,

like the proverbial Russ, leap up tartarly at a scratch. our

newspaper also have be flea-bite M. Livret and his countryman of

late; and, to conclude, over in old England you may fly out against

what you will, and there be little beyond a motherly smile, a nurse’s

rebuke, or a fool’s rudeness to answer you. in quick-blooded France you

have whip for whip, sneer, sarcasm, claw, fang, tussle, in a trice; and

if you choose to comport yourself accord to your insular notion of

freedom, you be bind to march out to the measured ground at an

invitation. to begin by say that your principle be oppose to it,

naturally excite a malicious propensity to try your temper.



a further cause, unknown to Mr. Ducie, of M. Livret’s irritation be,

that Beauchamp have vex he on a subject peculiarly dear to he. the

celebrate Château Dianet be about to be visit by the guest at

Tourdestelle. in common with some french philosopher and english

matron, he cherish a sentimental sad enthusiasm for royal

concubine; and when dilate upon one among they, the ruin of whose

family’s castle stand in the neighbourhood-Agrees, who be really a

kindly soul, though not virtuous—M. Livret have be traverse by

Beauchamp with question as to the condition of the people, the

peasantry, that be sweat in taxis to support these lovely

frailty. they come oddly from a man in the fire of youth, and a

little old gentleman somewhat seduce by the melt image of his theme

might well blink at he to ask, of what flesh be you, then? his

historic harem be insult. personally too, the fair creature

picturesquely soil, intrepid in her amorousness, and ultimately

absolve by repentance (a shudder narrative of her sin under

shower of salt drop), cry to he to champion she. excite by the

suppose cold critical mind in Beauchamp, M. Livret paint and paint

this lady, trick she in casuistical nicety, scene of pomp and

boudoir pathos, with many shift sidelight and a risky word or two,

until Renée cry out, “spare we the esprit Gaulois, M. Livret!” there

be much to make he angry with this Englishman.



“the esprit Gaulois be the sparkle of crystal common sense, madame, and

may we never abandon it for a puritanism that hide its face to conceal

its filthiness, like a stagnant pond,” reply M. Livret, flash.



“it seem, then, that there be two way of be objectionable,” say

Renée.



“ah! Madame la Marquise, your wit be french,” he breathe low; “keep

your heart so!”



both M. Livret and M. d’Orbec have forget that when Count Henri

d’Henriel be receive at Tourdestelle, the arrival of the Englishman

be pleasantly anticipate by they as an eclipse of the handsome boy;

but a foreign interloper be quickly dispossess of all mean of

pleasing save that one of take his departure; and they now talk of

Count Henri’s disgrace and banishment in a very warm spirit of

sympathy, not at all see why it should be make to depend upon the

movement of this M. Beauchamp, as it appear to be. Madame d’Auffray

hear some of their dialogue, and hurry with a mouth full of comedy

to Renée, who do not reproach they for silly being, as would be do

elsewhere. on the contrary, she appreciate a scene of such absolute

comedy, recognize it instantly as a situation pluck out of human

nature. she compare they to republicans that regret the sovereign

they have depose for a pretender to start up and govern they.



“who hurry they round to the legitimate king again!” say Madame

d’Auffray.



Renée cast she chin up. “how, my dear?”



“your husband.”



“what of he?”



“he be return.”



“what bring he?”



“you should ask who, my Renée! I be sure he would not hear of M.

Beauchamp’s be here, without an effort to return and do the honour

of the château.”



Renée look hard at she, say, “how thoughtful of you! you must have

make use of the telegraph wire to inform he that M. Beauchamp be

with we.”



“More; I make use of they to inform he that M. Beauchamp be

expect.”



“and that be enough to bring he! he pay M. Beauchamp a wonderful

compliment.”



“such as he would pay to no other man, my Renée. virtually it be the

high of compliment to you. I say that to M. Beauchamp’s credit; for

Raoul have meet he, and, whatever his personal feeling may be, must know

your friend be a man of honour.”



“my friend be... yes, I have no reason to think otherwise,” Renée

reply. her husband’s persistent and exclusive jealousy of Beauchamp

be the singular point in the character of one who appear to have no

sentiment of the kind as regard man that be much less than man of

honour. “so, then, my sister Agnès,” she say, “you suggest the

invitation of M. Beauchamp for the purpose of spur my husband to

return! apparently he and I be surround by plotter.”



“be I so very guilty?” say Madame d’Auffray.



“if that mad boy, half idiot, half panther, be by chance to insult M.

Beauchamp, you would feel so.”



“you have take precaution to prevent their meeting; and besides, M.

Beauchamp do not fight.”



Renée flush crimson.



Madame d’Auffray add, “I do not say that he be other than a perfectly

brave and chivalrous gentleman.”



“oh!” cry Renée, “do not say it, if ever you should imagine it. Bid

Roland speak of he. he be change, oppress: I do he a terrible

wrong ....” she check herself. “but the chief thing to do be to keep

M. d’Henriel away from he. I suspect M. d’Orbec of a design to make

they clash: and you, my dear, will explain why, to flatter I. believe

I, I thirst for flattery; I have have none since M. Beauchamp come: and

you, so acute, must have see the want of it in my face. but you, so

skilful, Agnès, will manage these man. do you know, Agnès, that the

pride of a woman so incredibly clever as you have show I you be

should resent their intrigue and overthrow they. as for I, I think

I could command M. d’Henriel, and I find he have neither reason in he

nor obedience. Singular to say, I know he just as well a week back as

I do now, and then I like he for his quality—or the absence of any.

but how shall we avoid he on the road to Dianet? he be aware that we

be go.”



“take M. Beauchamp by boat,” say Madame d’Auffray.



“the river wind to within a five minute’ walk of Dianet; we could go

by boat,” Renée say musingly. “I think of the boat. but do it not

give the man a triumph that we should seem to try to elude he? what

matter! still, I do not like he to be the falcon, and Nevil Beauchamp

the... little bird. so it be, because we begin badly, Agnès!”



“be it my fault?”



“mine. tell I: the legitimate king return when?”



“in two day or three.”



“and his rebel subject be to address he—how?”



Madame d’Auffray smite the point of a finger softly on her cheek.



“will they be pardon?” say Renée.



“it be for _he_ to kneel, my dearest.”



“legitimacy kneeling for forgiveness be a painful picture, Agnès.

Legitimacy jealous of a foreigner be an odd one. however, we be woman,

bear to our lot. if we could rise en masse!—but we cannot. embrace I.”



Madame d’Auffray embrace she, without an idea that she assist in

perform the farewell of their confidential intimacy.



when Renée trifle with Count Henri, it be play with fire, and she

know it; and once or twice she bemoan to Agnès d’Auffray she

abandon state, which condemn she, for the sake of the sensation of

living, to have recourse to perilous pastime; but she be revolt, as

at a piece of treachery, that Agnès should have suggest the

invitation of Nevil Beauchamp with the secret design of win home

her husband to protect she. this, for one reason, be because Beauchamp

give she no notion of danger; none, therefore, of require protection;

and the presence of her husband could not but be hateful to he, an

undeserved infliction. to she it be intolerable that they should be

bring into contact. it seem almost as hard that she should have to

dismiss Beauchamp to preclude their meeting. she remember,

nevertheless, a certain desperation of mind, scarce imaginable in the

retrospect, by which, trembling, fever-smite, scorn herself, she

have be reduce to hope for Nevil Beauchamp’s come as for a rescue.

the night of the storm have rouse her heart. since then his perfect

friendliness have lull, his air of thoughtfulness have interest it;

and the fancy that he, who neither reproach nor sentimentalize, be

to be infinitely compassionate, stir up remorse. she could not tell

her friend Agnès of these feeling while her feeling be anger

against her friend. so she talk lightly of “the legitimate king,” and

they embrace: a situation of comedy quite as true as that present by

the humble admirer of the brilliant chatelaine.



Beauchamp have the pleasure of row Madame la Marquise to the short

shade walk separate the river from Château Dianet, whither M.

d’Orbec go on horseback, and Madame d’Auffray and M. Livret be

drive. the portrait of diane of Dianet be praise for the beauty of

the dame, a soft-fleshed acutely feature person, a

fresh-of-the-toilette face, of the configuration of head of the cat,

relieve by a delicately aquiline nose; and it could only be the cat of

fairy metamorphosis which should stand for that illustration: brow and

chin make an acceptable triangle, and eye and mouth could be what she

please for mouse or monarchs. M. Livret do not gainsay the impeachment

of she by a great french historian, tender to woman, to frailty in

particular—yes, she be cold, perhaps grasp: but dwell upon she in

her character of woman; conceive her exist, to estimate the charm of

her graciousness. name the two country which alone have produce the

WOMAN, the ideal woman, the woman of art, whose beauty, grace, and wit

offer she to our contemplation in an atmosphere above the ordinary

condition of the world: these two country be France and Greece!

none other give you the perfect woman, the woman who conquer time, as

she conquer man, by virtue of the divinity in her blood; and she, as

little as illustrious hero, be to be judge by the law and standard

of less creature. in fashion she, nature and art have work

together: in she, poetry walk the earth. the question of good or bad

be entirely to be put aside: it be a rustic’s impertinence—a bourgeois’

vulgarity. she be preeminent, voilà tout. have she grace and beauty?

then you be answer: such possession be an assurance that her

influence in the aggregate must be for good. thunder, destructive to

insect, refresh earth: so she. so sing the rhapsodist. possibly a

scholarly little french gentleman, go down the grey slope of sixty

to second childishness, recover a second juvenility in these

enthusiasm; though what it be that inspire our matron to take up

with they be unimaginable. M. Livret’s ardour be a contrast to the

young Englishman’s vacant gaze at diane, and the symbol of her

goddesship run along the wall, the bed, the cabinet, everywhere

that the chaste device could find frontage and a corner.



M. d’Orbec remain outside the château inspect the fish-pond. when

they rejoin he he compliment Beauchamp semi-ironically on his

choice of the river’s quiet charm in preference to the dusty road.

Madame de Rouaillout say, “come, M. d’Orbec; what if you surrender

your horse to M. Beauchamp, and row I back?” he change colour,

hesitate, and decline he have an engagement to call on M. d’Henriel.



“when do you see he?” say she.



he be confused. “it be not long since, madame.”



“on the road?”



“come along the road.”



“and our glove?”



“Madame la Marquise, if I may trust my memory, M. d’Henriel be not in

official costume.”



Renée allow herself to be reassure.



a ceremonious visit that M. Livret insist on be pay to the chapel

of Diane, where she have worship and lay she widow ashe, which,

say M. Livret, the fiend of the Revolution would not let rest.



he raise his voice to denounce they.



it be Roland de Croisnel that answer: “the Revolution be our

grandmother, monsieur, and I cannot hear she abuse.”



Renée catch her brother by the hand. he step out of the chapel with

Beauchamp to embrace he; then kiss Renée, and, remark that she

be pale, fetched flood colour to her cheek. he be hearty air to

they after the sentimentalism they have be hear. beauchamp and he

walk like love comrade at school, question, answer,

chattering, laughing,—a beautiful sight to Renée, and she look at

Agnès d’Auffray to ask she whether “this Englishman” be not one of

they in his frankness and freshness.



Roland stop to turn to Renée. “I meet d’Henriel on my ride here,” he

say with a sharp inquisitive expression of eye that pass

immediately.



“you ride here from Tourdestelle, then,” say Renée.



“have he be one of the company, marquise?”



“do he ride by you without speak, Roland?”



“thus.” Roland describe a spanish caballero’s formallest salutation,

say to Beauchamp, “not the good sample of our young

Frenchman;—woman-spoil! not that the well kind of article need be

spoil by they—heaven forbid that! Friend Nevil,” he speak lower, “do

you know, you have something of the prophet in you? I remember: much

have come true. an old spoiler of woman be bad than one spoil by

they! ah, well: and Madame Culling? and your seven-foot high uncle? and

have you a fleet to satisfy Nevil Beauchamp yet? you shall see a trial

of our new field-gun at Rouen.”



they be separate with difficulty.



Renée wish her brother to come in the boat; and he would have do

so, but for his objection to have his arab bestridden by a man unknown

to he.



“my love be a four-foot, and here’s my love,” Roland say, go

outside the gilt gate-rail to the graceful little beast, that

acknowledge his ownership with an arch and swing of the neck round to

he.



he mount and call, “Au revoir, M. le Capitaine.”



“Au revoir, M. le Commandant,” cry Beauchamp.



“admiral and marshal, each of we in good season,” say Roland. “thank

to your promotion, I have a letter from my sister. advance a grade, and

I may get another.”



Beauchamp think of the strange gulf now between he and the time when

he pine to be a commodore, and an admiral. the gulf be bridge as he

look at Renée petting Roland’s horse.



“be there in the world so lovely a creature?” she say, and appeal

fondlingly to the beauty that bring out beauty, and, bid it

disdain rivalry, rival it insomuch that in a moment of trance

Beauchamp with his bodily vision beheld she, not there, but on the Lido

of Venice, shine out of the year go.



old love reviving may be love of a phantom after all. we can, if it

must revive, keep it to the limit of a ghostly love. the ship in the

arabian tale come within the zone of the magnetic mountain, fly all

its bolt and bar, and become sheer timber, but that be the

carelessness of the ship’s captain; and hitherto Beauchamp could

applaud himself for steer with prudence, while Renée’s attraction

warn more than they beckon. she be magnetic to he as no other

woman be. then whither his course but homeward?



after they have take leave of their host and hostess of Château Dianet,

walk across a meadow to a line of charmille that lead to the

river-side, he say, “now I have see Roland I shall have to decide

upon go.”



“Wantonly win be deservedly lose,” say Renée. “but do not disappoint

my Roland much because of his foolish sister. be he not look

handsome? and he be young to be a commandant, for we have no interest

at this Court. they keep he out of the last war! my father expect to

find you at Tourdestelle, and how account to he for your hurried

flight? save with the story of that which bring you to we!”



“the glove? I shall beg for the fellow to it before I depart,

marquise.”



“you perceive my disposition to light-headedness, monsieur, when I be

a girl.”



“I say that I—but the past be dust. shall I ever see you in England?”



“that country seem to frown on I. but if I do not go there, nor you

come here, except to imperious mysterious invitation, which will not

be repeat, the future be dust as well as the past: for I, at least.

dust here, dust there!—if one could be like a silk-worm, and live lie

on the leaf one feed on, it would be a sort of answer to the

riddle—live out of the dust, and in the present. I find none in my

religion. no doubt, Madame de Brézé do: why do you call Diane so to

M. Livret?”



she look at he smile as they come out of the shadow of the clip

tree. he be glance about for the boat.



“the boat be across the river,” Renée say, in a voice that make he

seek her eye for an explanation of the dead sound. she be very pale.

“you have perfect command of yourself? for my sake!” she say.



he look round.



stand up in the boat, against the opposite bank, and lean with

cross leg on one of the scull plant in the gravel of the river,

Count Henri d’Henriel’s handsome figure present itself to Beauchamp’s

gaze.



with a dryness that smack of his uncle Everard Romfrey, Beauchamp

say of the fantastical posture of the young man, “one can do that on

fresh water.”



Renée do not comprehend the sailor-sarcasm of the remark; but she also

comment on the statuesque appearance of Count Henri: “be the pose for

photography or for sculpture?”



neither of they show a sign of surprise or of impatience.



M. d’Henriel could not maintain the attitude. he uncross his leg

deliberately, droop hat in hand, and come paddle over; apologize

indolently, and say, “I be not, I believe, trespass on the ground

of Tourdestelle, Madame la Marquise!”



“you happen to be in my boat, M. le Comte,” say Renée.



“permit I, madame.” he have set one foot on shore, with his back to

Beauchamp, and reach a hand to assist her step into the boat.



Beauchamp catch fast hold of the bow while Renée lay a finger on

Count Henri’s shoulder to steady herself.



the instant she have take her seat, Count Henri dash the scull’s

blade at the bank to push off with she, but the boat be fast. his

manœuvre have be foresee. beauchamp swing on board like the last

seaman of a launch, and crouch as the boat rock away to the stream;

and still Count Henri lean on the scull, not in a choose attitude,

but for positive support. he have throw his force into the blow, to

push off triumphantly, and leave his rival standing. it occur that

the boat’s brief resistance and rock away agitated his artificial

equipoise, and, by the operation of inexorable law, the long he

lean across an extend surface the more be he dependent; so that

when the measure of the water exceed the length of his fail

support on land, there be no help for it: he pitch in. his grimace

of chagrin at the sight of Beauchamp securely establish, have scarcely

yield to the grimness of feature of the man who feel he must go, as

he take the plunge; and these two emotion combine to make an

extraordinary countenance.



he go like a gallant gentleman; he throw up his heel to clear the

boat, drop into about four foot of water, and his first remark on

rise be, “I trust, madame, I have not have the misfortune to splash

you.”



then he wade to the bank, scramble to his foot, and draw out his

moustachio to their curving end. Renée nod sharply to Beauchamp to

bid he row. he, with less of wisdom, having seize the float scull

abandon by Count Henri, and get it ready for the stroke, say a word

of condolence to the dripping man.



Count Henri’s shoulder and neck express a kind of negative that,

like a wet dog’s shake of the head, end in an involuntary whole

length shudder, dog-like and deplorable to behold. he must have be

conscious of this miserable exhibition of himself; he turn to

Beauchamp: “you be, I be inform, a sailor, monsieur. I compliment

you on your naval tactic: our next meeting will be on land. Au revoir,

monsieur. Madame la Marquise, I have the honour to salute you.”



with these word he retreat.



“row quickly, I beg of you,” Renée say to Beauchamp. her desire be to

see Roland, and open her heart to her brother; for now it have to be

open. not a minute must be lose to prevent further mischief. and who

be guilty? she. her heart clamour of her guilt to waken a cry of

innocence. a disdainful pity for the superb young savage just make

ludicrous, relieve he of blame, implacable though he be. he be

nothing; an accident—a fool. but he might become a terrible instrument

of punishment. the thought of that possibility give it an aspect of

retribution, under which her cry of innocence be insufferable in its

feebleness. it would have be different with she if Beauchamp have

take advantage of her fever of anxiety, suddenly appease by the sight

of he on the evening of his arrival at Tourdestelle after the storm,

to attempt a renewal of their old break love-bond. then she would

have see only a conflict between two man, neither of whom could claim

a more secret right than the other to be call she lover, and of whom

both be on a common footing, and partly despicable. but Nevil

Beauchamp have behave as her perfect true friend, in the character she

have hope for when she summon he. the sense of her guilt lie in the

recognition that he have save she. from what? from the consequence of

delirium rather than from love—surely delirium, found on delusion;

love have not exist. she have say to Count Henri, “you speak to I of

love. I be belove when I be a girl, before my marriage, and for

year I have not see or correspond with the man who love I, and I

have only to lift my finger now and he will come to I, and not once

will he speak to I of love.” those be the word originate the

wager of the glove. but what of she, if Nevil Beauchamp have not come?



her heart jump, and she blush ungovernably in his face,—as if he

be see she withdraw her foot from the rock’s edge, and have that

instant rescue she. but how come it she have be so helpless? she

could ask; she could not answer.



Thinking, talk to her heart, be useless. the deceiver simply

feign utter condemnation to make partial comfort acceptable. she

burn to do some act of extreme self-abasement that should bring an

unwonted degree of wrath on she externally, and so re-entitle she to

consideration in her own eye. she burn to be interrogate, to have

to weep, to be scorn, abuse, and forgive, that she might say she

do not deserve pardon. Beauchamp be too english, evidently too blind,

for the description of judge-accuser she require; one who would worry

she without mercy, until—disgrace by the excess of torture

inflict—he should reinstate she by as much as he have overcharge his

accusation, and a little more. reasonably enough, instinctively in

fact, she shun the hollow of an english ear. a surprise be in

reserve for she.



Beauchamp give up rowing. as he rest on the scull, his head be bent

and turn toward the bank. Renée perceive an over-swollen monster

gourd that have stray from a garden adjoining the river, and hung

slide heavily down the bank on one greenish yellow cheek, in

prolonged contemplation of its image in the mirror below. apparently

this obese Narcissus enchain his attention.



she tap her foot. “be you tired of rowing, monsieur?”



“it be exactly here,” say he, “that you tell I you expect your

husband’s return.”



she glance at the gourd, bit her lip, and, colour, say, “at what

point of the river do I request you to congratulate I on it?”



she would not have say that, if she have know the thought at work

within he.



he set the boat sway from side to side, and at once the hugeous

reflection of that conceivably self-enamour bulk quaver and

distend, and be shatter in a thousand dancing fragment, to

re-unite and recompose its maudlin air of imaged satisfaction.



she begin to have a vague idea that he be indulge grotesque fancy.



very strangely, the ridiculous thing, in the shape of an over-stretch

likeness, that she never would have see have he indicate it directly,

become transfuse from his mind to hers by his abstract, half-amuse

observation of the great dancing gourd—that caper antiquity,

lumber volatility, wandering, self-adore, gross bald Cupid, elatest

of nondescript! her sense imagine the impression agitate

Beauchamp’s, and exaggerate they beyond limit; and when he amaze she

with a straight look into her eye, and the word, “well let it be a

youth—and live, than fall back to that!” she understand he

immediately; and, together with her old fear of his impetuosity and

downrightness, come the vivid recollection, like a bright finger

point upon darkness, of what foul destiny, magnify by her present

abhorrence of it, he would have save she from in the day of Venice

and Touraine, and unto what loathly example of the hideous grotesque

she, in spite of her lover’s foresight on her behalf, have become

ally.



face to face as they sit, she have no defence for her scarlet cheek;

her eye waver.



“we will land here; the cottager shall row the boat up,” she say.



“somewhere—anywhere,” say Beauchamp. “but I must speak. I will tell

you now. I do not think you to blame—barely; not in my sight; though no

man living would have suffer as I should. probably some day more and

you would have be lose. you look for I! trust your instinct now

I’m with you as well as when I’m absent. have you courage? that’s the

question. you have year to live. can you live they in this place—with

honour? and alive really?”



Renée’s eye grow wide; she try to frown, and her brow merely

twitch; to speak, and she be inarticulate. his madness, miraculous

penetration, and the super-masculine charity in he, unknown to the

world of young man in their treatment of woman, excited, awed, and

melt she. he have see the whole truth of her relation with M.

d’Henriel!—the wickedness of they in one light, the innocence in

another; and without prompt a confession he forgive she. could she

believe it? this be love, and manly love.



she yearn to be on her foot, to feel the possibility of an escape

from he.



she point to a landing. he spring to the bank. “it could end in

nothing else,” he say, “unless you beat cold to I. and now I have

your hand, Renée! it’s the hand of a live woman, you have no need to

tell I that; but faithful to her comrade! I can swear it for

she—faithful to a _true_ alliance! you be not married, you be simply

chain: and you be terrorize. what a perversion of you it be! it

wreck you. but with I? be I not your lover? you and I be one life.

what have we suffer for but to find this out and act on it? do I not

know that a woman live, and be not the rooted piece of vegetation

hypocrite and tyrant expect she to be? act on it, I say; own I,

break the chain, come to I; say, Nevil Beauchamp or death! and death

for you? but you be poison and thwart-eddying, as you live now:

bad, shame the Renée I know. ah—Venice! but now we be both of we

wise and strong: we have go through fire. who foretell it? this

day, and this misery and perversion that we can turn to joy, if we

will—if you will! no heart to dare be no heart to love!—answer that!

shall I see you cower away from I again? not this time!”



he sweep on in a flood, utter mad thing, foolish thing, and thing

of an insight electrify to she. through the cottager’s garden,

across a field, and within the park gate of Tourdestelle it continue

unceasingly; and deeply be she win by the rebellious note in all that

he say, deeply too by his disregard of the vulgar art of wooer: she

detect none. he do not speak so much to win as to help she to see

with her own orb. nor be it roughly or chidingly, though it be

absolutely, that he strip she of the veil a waver woman will keep

to herself from her heart’s lord if she can.



they arrive long after the boat at Tourdestelle, and Beauchamp might

believe he have prevail with she, but for her forlorn repetition of

the question he have put to she idly and as a new idea, instead of

significantly, with a recollection and a doubt “have I courage, Nevil?”



the grain of common sense in cowardice cause she to repeat it when her

reason be bedim, and passion assume the right to show the way of

right and wrong.









CHAPTER XXVI.

MR. BLACKBURN TUCKHAM





some time after Beauchamp have be see renew his canvass in

Bevisham a report reach Mount Laurels that he be lame of a leg. the

wit of the opposite camp revive the FRENCH MARQUEES, but it be

generally acknowledge that he have come back without the lady: she be

invisible. Cecilia Halkett ride home with her father on a dusky Autumn

evening, and find the card of Commander Beauchamp await she. he

might have stay to see she, she think. lady be not customarily

so very late in return from a ride on chill evening of Autumn. only

a quarter of an hour be between his visit and her return. the

shortness of the interval make it appear the deep gulf. she notice

that her father particularly inquire of the man-servant whether

Captain Beauchamp limp. it seem a piece of kindly anxiety on his

part. the captain be mount, the man say. Cecilia be conscious of

rumour be abroad relate to Nevil’s expedition to France; but he

have enemy, and be at war with they, and she hold herself indifferent

to tattle. this card bear his name, recently in his hand, be much

more insidious and precise. she take it to her room to look at it.

nothing but his name and naval title be inscribe; no pencil line;

she have not expect to discover one. the simple card be her dark

light, as a handkerchief, a flower, a knot of riband, have be for man

luridly illuminate by such small spark to fle their beam on

shadow and read the monstrous thing for truth. her purer virgin

blood be not inflame. she read the signification of the card sadly as

she do clearly. what she could not so distinctly imagine be, how he

could reconcile the devotion to his country, which he have teach she to

put her faith in, with his unhappy subjection to Madame de Rouaillout.

how could the nobler sentiment exist side by side with one that be

lawless? or be the wildness characteristic of his political view

proof of a nature incline to disown moral tie? she fear so; he do

not speak of the clergy respectfully. read in the dark, she be

force to rely on her social instinct, and she distrust her personal

feeling as much as she could, for she wish to know the truth of he;

anything, pain and heartrending, rather than the shutting of the eye

in an unworthy abandonment to mere emotion and fascination. Cecilia’s

love could not be otherwise give to a man, however near she might be

draw to love—though she should suffer the pang of love cruelly.



she place his card in her writing-desk; she have his likeness there.

Commander Beauchamp encourage the art of photography, as those that

make long voyage do, in reciprocate what they petition their friend

for. Mrs. Rosamund Culling have a whole collection of photograph of

he, equal to a visual history of his growth in chapter, from boyhood

to midshipmanship and to manhood. the speciman possess by Cecilia be

one of a couple that Beauchamp have forward to Mrs. Grancey Lespel on

the day of his departure for France, and be a present from that lady,

purchase, like so many present, at a cost Cecilia would have pay

heavily in gold to have be spare, namely, a public blush. she be

allow to make her choice, and she choose the profile, repeat a

remark of Mrs. Culling’s, that it suggest an arrow-head in the

upflight; whereupon Mr. Stukely Culbrett have say, “then there be the

man, for he be undoubtedly a projectile”; nor be politically-hostile

punster on an arrow-head inactive. but Cecilia be think of the

side-face she (less intently than Beauchamp at her) have glance at

during the drive into Bevisham. at that moment, she fancy Madame de

Rouaillout might be do likewise; and oh that she have the portrait of

the french lady as well!



next day her father toss she a photograph of another gentleman,

come out of a letter he have receive from old Mrs. Beauchamp. he

ask her opinion of it. she say, “I think he would have suit

Bevisham well than Captain Baskelett.” of the original, who present

himself at Mount Laurels in the course of the week, she have nothing to

say, except that he be very like the photograph, very unlike Nevil

Beauchamp. “yes, there I’m of your opinion,” her father observe. the

gentleman be Mr. Blackburn Tuckham, and it be amusing to find an

exuberant Tory in one who be the reverse of the cavalier type. Nevil

and he seem to have be sort to the wrong side. Mr. Tuckham have a

round head, square flat forehead, and ruddy face; he stand as if his

foot claim the earth under they for his own, with a certain shortness

of leg that detract from the majesty of his resemblance to our Eighth

Harry, but increase his air of solidity; and he be authoritative in

speak. “let I set you right, sir,” he say sometimes to Colonel

Halkett, and that be his modesty. “you be altogether wrong,” Miss

Halkett hear herself inform, which be his courtesy. he examine

some of her water-colour drawing before sit down to dinner,

approve of they, but think it necessary to lay a broad finger on

they to show their defect. on the question of politic, “I venture to

state,” he remark, in anything but the tone of a venture, “that no

educate man of ordinary sense who have visit our colony will come

back a Liberal.” as for a man of sense and education be a Radical,

he scout the notion with a pooh sufficient to awaken a vessel in the

doldrums. he say carelessly of Commander Beauchamp, that he might

think himself one. either the radical candidate for Bevisham stand

self-deceive, or—the other supposition. Mr. Tuckham would venture to

state that no english gentleman, exempt from an examination by order of

the Commissioners of Lunacy, could be sincerely a Radical. “not a bit

of it; nonsense,” he reply to Miss Halkett’s hint at the existence of

radical view; “that be, those view be out of politic; they be

matter for the police. dutch dyke be build to shut away the sea from

cultivate land, and of course it’s a part of the business of the Dutch

Government to keep up the dykes,—and of our to guard against the mob;

but that be only a political consideration after the mob have be

allow to undermine our defence.”



“they speak,” say Miss Halkett, “of educate the people to fit they—”



“they speak of command the wind and tide,” he cut her short, with

no clear analogy; “wait till we have a storm. it’s a delusion amount

to dementedness to suppose, that with the people inside our defence,

we can be tame they and trick they. as for send they to school

after give they power, it’s like ask a wild beast to sit down to

dinner with we—he want the whole table and we too. the good education

for the people be government. they’re begin to see that in

Lancashire at last. I run down to Lancashire for a couple of day on my

landing, and I’m thankful to say Lancashire be prepare to take a step

back. Lancashire lead the country. lancashire man see what this

Liberalism have do for the labour-market.”



“Captain Beauchamp consider that the political change come over the

mind of the manufacturer be due to the large fortune they have

make,” say Miss Halkett, maliciously associate a radical prophet

with he.



he be unaffected by it, and continue: “property be ballast as well as

treasure. I call property fund good sense. I would give it every

privilege. if we be to speak of patriotism, I say the possession of

property guarantee it. I maintain that the lead of man of property be

in most case sure to be the safe one.”



“_i_ think so,” Colonel Halkett interpose, and he speak as a man of

property.



Mr. Tuckham grow fervent in his allusion to our wealth and our

commerce. having win the race and gain the prize, shall we let it

slip out of our grasp? upon this topic his voice descend to tone of

priestlike awe: for be we not the envy of the world? our wealth be

countless, fabulous. it may well inspire veneration. and we have win it

with our hand, thank (he imply it so) to our religion. we be rich

in money and industry, in those two thing only, and the corruption of

an energetic industry be constantly threaten by the profusion of

wealth give it employment. this be the case, either your radical

do not know the first condition of human nature, or they do; and if

they do they be traitor, and the liberal open the gate to they

be fool: and some be knave. we perish as a Great Power if we cease

to look sharp ahead, hold firm together, and make the utmost of what we

possess. the word for the performance of those duty be Toryism: a

word with an old flavour than Conservatism, and Mr. Tuckham prefer

it. by all mean let workman be free man but a man must earn his

freedom daily, or he will become a slave in some form or another: and

the way to earn it be by work and obedience to right direction. in a

country like ours, open on all side to the competition of intelligence

and strength, with a Press that be the voice of all party and of

every interest; in a country offer to your investment three and a

half and more per cent., secure as the firmament!



he perceive an amazed expression on Miss Halkett’s countenance; and

“ay,” say he, “that mean the certainty of food to million of mouth,

and comfort, if not luxury, to half the population. a safe

percentage on saving be the basis of civilization.”



but he have bruise his eloquence, for though you may start a sermon

from stone to hit the star, he must be a practised orator who shall

descend out of the abstract to take up a heavy lump of the concrete

without unseat himself, and he stammer and come to a flat ending:

“in such a country—well, I venture to say, we have a right to condemn

in advance disturber of the peace, and they must show very good cause

indeed for not be summarily hold—to account for their conduct.”



the allocution be not deliver in the presence of an audience other

than sympathetic, and Miss Halkett rightly guess that it be intend

to strike Captain Beauchamp by ricochet. he puff at the mention of

Beauchamp’s name. he have read a report speech or two of Beauchamp’s,

and shake his head over a quotation of the stuff, as though he would

have spring at he like a lion, but for his enrolment as a constable.



not a whit the less do Mr. Tuckham drink his claret relishingly, and

he tell story incidental to his travel now and then, commend the

fishing here, the shooting there, and in some few place the cookery,

with much bright emphasis when it could be praise; it appear to be

an endear recollection to he. still, as a man of progress, he

declare his belief that we English would ultimately turn out the good

cook, have indubitably the good material. “our incomprehensible

political pusillanimity” be the one sad point about we: we have be

drive from surrender to surrender.



“like geese upon a common, I have hear it say,” Miss Halkett assist

he to Dr. Shrapnel’s comparison.



Mr. Tuckham laugh, and half yawn and sigh, “dear I!”



his laughter be catch, and somehow more persuasive of the soundness

of the man’s heart and head than his remark.



she would have be astonish to know that a gentleman so uncourtly,

if not uncouth—judge by the standard of the circle she move in—and so

unskille in please the sight and hearing of lady as to treat they

like junior comrade, have raise the vow within himself on see she:

you, or no woman!



the colonel delight in he, both as a strong and able young fellow,

and a refreshingly aggressive recruit of his party, who be for

onslaught, and invoke common sense, instead of wave the flag of

sentiment in retreat; a very horse-artillery man of Tories. regret

immensely that Mr. Tuckham have not reach England early, that he

might have occupy the seat for Bevisham, about to be give to Captain

Baskelett, Colonel Halkett set up a contrast of Blackburn Tuckham and

Nevil Beauchamp; a singular instance of unfairness, his daughter

think, consider that the distinct contrast present by the

circumstance be that of Mr. Tuckham and Captain Baskelett.



“it seem to I, papa,—that you be contrast the idealist and the

realist,” she say.



“ah, well, we don’t want the idealist in politic,” mutter the

colonel.



Latterly he also have take to shake his head over Nevil: Cecilia

dared not ask he why.



Mr. Tuckham arrive at Mount Laurels on the eve of the Nomination day

in Bevisham. an article in the Bevisham Gazette call upon all true

liberal to demonstrate their unanimity by a multitudinous show of

hand, he ascribe to the writing of a child of Erin; and he be highly

divert by the Liberal’s hiring of Paddy to “pen and spout” for he.

“a Scotchman manage, and Paddy do the sermon for _all_ their

journal,” he say off-hand; add: “and the English be the

compositor, I suppose.” you may take that for an instance of the

national spirit of liberal newspaper!



“ah!” sigh the colonel, as at a case clearly demonstrate against

they.



a drive down to Bevisham to witness the ceremony of the nomination in

the town-hall sober Mr. Tuckham’s disposition to generalize.

Beauchamp have the show of hand, and to say with Captain Baskelett,

that they be a dirty majority, be beneath Mr. Tuckham’s verbal

antagonism. he fall into a studious reserve, note everything,

listen to everybody, greatly to Colonel Halkett’s admiration of one

by nature a talker and a thunderer.



the show of hand Mr. Seymour Austin declare to be the most delusive

of electoral auspex; and it prove so. a little later than four
