message from her father tell she that both of the Liberals be

head; “Beauchamp nowhere.”



Mrs. Grancey Lespel be the next herald of Beauchamp’s defeat. she

merely state the fact that she have meet the colonel and Mr. Blackburn

Tuckham drive on the outskirt of the town, and have promise to bring

Cecilia the final number of the poll. without name they, she

unroll the great business in her mind.



“a man who in the middle of an election go over to France to fight a

duel, can hardly expect to win; he have all the morality of an English

borough oppose to he,” she say; and see the young lady stiffen:

“oh! the duel be positive,” she drop her voice. “with the husband.

who else could it be? and return invalid. that be evidence. my

nephew Palmet have it from Vivian Ducie, and he be acquaint with she

tolerably intimately, and the story be, she be overtake in her flight

in the night, and the duel follow at eight o’clock in the morning;

but her brother insist on fight for Captain Beauchamp, and I

cannot tell you how—but _his_ place in it I cann’t explain—there be a

beau jeune homme, and it’s quite possible that _he_ should have be

the person to stand up against the marquis. at any rate, he insult

Captain Beauchamp, or think your hero have insult he, and the duel

be with one or the other. it matter exceedingly little with whom, if

a duel be fight, and you see we have quite establish that.”



“I hope it be not true,” say Cecilia.



“my dear, that be the christian thing to do,” say Mrs. Lespel.

“duel be horrible: though those Romfreys!—and the Beauchamps be

just as bad, or nearly. Colonel Richard fight for a friend’s wife or

sister. but in these day duel be incredible. it be an inhuman

practice always, and it be now bad—it be a breach of manner. I would

hope it be not true; and you may mean that I have it from Lord Palmet.

but I know Vivian Ducie as well as I know my nephew, and if he

distinctly mention an occurrence, we may too surely rely on the truth

of it; he be not a man to spread mischief. be you unaware that he meet

Captain Beauchamp at the château of the marquise? the whole story be

act under his eye. he have only to take up his pen. generally he

favour I with his french gossip. I suppose there be circumstance

in this affair more suitable to Palmet than to I. he write a

description of Madame de Rouaillout that set Palmet strut about for

an hour. I have no doubt she must be a very beautiful woman, for a

Frenchwoman: not regular feature; expressive, capricious. Vivian Ducie

lay great stress on her eye and eyebrow, and, I think, her hair.

with a Frenchwoman’s figure, that be enough to make man crazy. he say

her husband deserve—but what will not young man write? it be deeply to

be regret that englishman abroad—woman the same, I fear—get the

continental tone in moral. but how Captain Beauchamp could expect to

carry on an Election and an intrigue together, only a head like his can

tell we. Grancey be in high indignation with he. it do not concern

the Election, you can imagine. something that man Dr. Shrapnel have

do, which he say Captain Beauchamp could have prevent. quarrel of

man! I have instruct Palmet to write to Vivian Ducie for a photograph

of Madame de Rouaillout. do you know, one have a curiosity to see the

face of the woman for whom a man ruin himself. but I say again, he

ought to be marry.”



“that there may be two victim?” Cecilia say it smile.



she be young in suffering, and think, as the unseasoned and

inexperienced do, that a mask be a concealment.



“Married—settle; to have he bind in honour,” say Mrs. Lespel. “I

have a conversation with he when he be at Itchincope; and his look,

and what I know of his father, that gallant and handsome Colonel

Richard Beauchamp, would give one a kind of confidence in he;

suppose always that he be not strike with one of those deadly

they. I have see they. and if they end, they end as if the man be

burn out, and be ashe inside; as you see Mr. Stukely Culbrett, all

cynicism. you would not now suspect he of a passion! it be true. oh, I

know it! that be what the man go to. the woman die. Vera Winter die at

twenty-three. Caroline Ormond be hardly old. you know her story;

everybody know it. the most singular and convincing case be that of

Lord Alfred Burnley and Lady Susan Gardiner, wife of the general; and

there be an instance of two similarly afflict—a very rare case, most

rare: they never could meet to part! it be almost ludicrous. it be now

quite certain that they do not conspire to meet. at last the absolute

fatality became so well understand by the person immediately

interested—you laugh?”



“do I laugh?” say Cecilia.



“we should all know the world, my dear, and you be a strong head. the

knowledge be only dangerous for fool. and if romance be occasionally

ridiculous, as I own it can be, humdrum, I protest, be everlastingly

so. by-the-by, I should have tell you that Captain Beauchamp be one

hundred and ninety below Captain Baskelett when the state of the poll

be hand to I. the gentleman drive with your father compare the

Liberals to a parachute cut away from the balloon. be he army or navy?”



“he be a barrister, and some cousin of Captain Beauchamp.”



“I should not have take he for a Beauchamp,” say Mrs. Lespel; and,

resume her worldly sagacity, “I should not like to be in opposition

to that young man.”



she seem to have a fancy unexpresse regard Mr. Tuckham. remind

herself that she might be behind time at Itchincope, where the guest

would be numerous that evening, and the song of triumph loud, with

Captain Baskelett to lead it, she kiss the young lady she have

unintentionally be torture so long, and drive away.



Cecilia hope it be not true. her heart sink heavily under the belief

that it be. she imagine the world abuse Nevil and cast he out,

as those elector of Bevisham have just do, and impulsively she

plead for he, and became drown in criminal blush that force she

to defend herself with a determination not to believe the dreadful

story, though she continue mitigate the wickedness of it; as if, by

a singular inversion of the fact, her clear good sense excuse, and it

be her heart that condemn he. she dwelt fondly on an image of the

“gallant and handsome Colonel Richard Beauchamp,” conjure up in her

mind from the fervour of Mrs. Lespel when speak of Nevil’s father,

whose chivalry throw a light on the son’s, and whose error, condone

by time, and with a certain brilliancy play above they, intercede

strangely on behalf of Nevil.









CHAPTER XXVII.

a short SIDELOOK AT the ELECTION





the brisk Election-day, unlike that wearisome but instructive canvass

of the Englishman in his castle vicatim, teach little; and its

humour be those of a badly manage Christmas pantomime without a

columbine—old trick, no grace. nevertheless, thing hang together so

that it cannot be pass over with a bare statement of the fact of the

liberal-radical defeat in Bevisham: the day be not without fruit in

time to come for he whom his commiserate admirer of the non-voting

sex all round the borough call the poor dear commander. Beauchamp’s

holiday out of England have incite Dr. Shrapnel to break a positive

restriction put upon he by Jenny Denham, and actively pursue the

canvass and the harangue in person; by which conduct, as Jenny have

foresee, many temperate elector be alienate from Commander

Beauchamp, though no doubt the Radicals be make compact: for they may

be the skirmishing faction—poor scatter fragment, none of they

sufficiently downright for the other; each outstrip each;

rudimentary emperor, elementary prophet, inspire physician,

nostrum-devour patient, whatsoever you will; and still here and

there a man shall arise to march they in close column, if they can but

trust he; in perfect subordination, a model even for Tories while they

keep shoulder to shoulder. and to behold such a disciplined body be

intoxicate to the eye of a leader accustom to count ahead upon

vapourish abstraction, and therefore predispose to add a couple of

nought to every tangible figure in his grasp. thus will a realize

fifty become five hundred or five thousand to he: the very sense of

number be instinct with multiplication in his mind; and those year far

on in advance, which he have be look to with some fatigue to the

optic, will suddenly and rollickingly roll up to he at the shutting

of his eye in a temporary fit of gratification. so, by look and by

not look, he achieve his phantom victory—embrace his cloud.



Dr. Shrapnel conceive that the day be to be a radical success; and

he, a citizen aged and exercise in reverse, so rounded by the habit

of they indeed as to tumble and recover himself on the wind of the blow

that strike he, be, it must be acknowledge, stagger and cast down

when he see Beauchamp drop, know full well his regiment have poll

to a man. radical poll early; they would poll at cockcrow if they

might; they dance on the morning. as for their chagrin at noon, you

will find description of it in the poet’s Inferno. they be for

lift our clay soil on a lever of Archimedes, and be not great

mathematician. they have perchance a foot of our earth, and

perpetually do they seem to be produce an effect, perpetually do

the whole land roll back on they. you have not surely to be remind

that it hurt they; the weight be immense. Dr. Shrapnel, however,

speedily look out again on his vast horizon, though prostrate. he

regain his height of stature with no man’s help. success be but

postpone for a generation or two. be it so very distant? gaze on it

with the eye of our parent orb! “I shall not see it here; you may,” he

say to Jenny Denham; and he fortify his outlook by say to Mr.

Lydiard that the Tories of our time walk, or rather stick, in the

track of the Radicals of a generation back. note, then, that Radicals,

always march to the triumph, never taste it; and for Tories it be

Dead Sea fruit, ashe in their mouth! those Liberals, those

temporiser, compromiser, a concourse of atom! glorify themselves in

the animal satisfaction of suck the juice of the fruit, for which

they pay with their soul. they have no true cohesion, for they have no

vital principle.



Mr. Lydiard be a Liberal, bade the doctor not to forget the work of

the Liberals, who touch on Tory and Radical with a pretty steady

swing, from side to side, in the manner of the pendulum of a clock,

which be the clock’s life, remember that. the Liberals be the

professor of the practicable in politic.



“a suitable image for time-server!” Dr. Shrapnel exclaim, intolerant

of any mention of the Liberals as a party, especially in the hour of

radical discomfiture, when the fact that compromiser should exist

exasperate man of a principle. “your Liberals be the band of Pyrrhus,

an army of bastard, mercenary profess the practicable for pay.

they know we the motive force, the Tories the resist power, and they

feign to aid we in batter our enemy, that they may stop the shock.

we fight, they profit. what be they? Stranded Whigs, crotchetty

manufacturer; dissentient religionist; the half-minded, the

hare-hearted; the I would and I would-not—shifty creature, with

youth’s enthusiasm decay in they, and a purse beginning to jingle;

fear lest we do too much for safety, our enemy not enough for

safety. they a party? let they take action and see! _we_ stand a

thousand defeat; they not one! compromise begat they. once let they

leave suck the teat of compromise, yea, once put on the air of man

who fight and die for a cause, they fly to piece. and whither the

fragment? chiefly, my friend, into the _tory_ rank. seriously so I

say. you between future and past be for the present—but with the

hunt look behind of all godless liver in the present. you Liberals

be Tories with foresight, Radicals without faith. you start, in fear

of Toryism, on an errand of Radicalism, and in fear of Radicalism to

toryism you draw back. there be your pendulum-swing!”



lecture to this effect be deliver by Dr. Shrapnel throughout the

day, for his private spiritual solace it may be suppose, unto Lydiard,

Turbot, Beauchamp, or whomsoever the man chance to be near he, and

never do Sir Oracle wear so extraordinary a garb. the favourite

missile of the day be flour-bag. Dr. Shrapnel’s uncommon height,

and his outrageous long brown coat, would have be sufficient to

attract they, without the reputation he have for desire to subvert

everything old English. the first discharge give he the appearance of

a thaw snowman. drenching of water turn the flour to rib of

paste, and in colour at least he look legitimately the cook’s own

spit hare, escape from her basting ladle, elongate on two leg. it

ensue that whenever he be catch sight of, as he walk unconcernedly

about, the young street-professor of the decorative art be seize

with a frenzy to add their share to the whitening of he, until he

might have be take for a miller that have go bodily through his

meal. the popular cry proclaim he a ghost, and he walk like one,

impassive, blanched, and silent amid the uproar of mob of jolly

ruffian, for each of whom it be a point of honour to have a shy at

old shrapnel.



clothe in this preparation of pie-crust, he call from time to time at

Beauchamp’s hotel, and renew his monologue upon that Radical empire

in the future which be for ever in the future for the pioneer of man,

yet not the less their empire. “do we live in our body?” quoth he,

reply to his fiery interrogation: “ay, the Tories! the Liberals!”



_they_ live in their body. not one syllable of personal consolation

do he vouchsafe to Beauchamp. he do not imagine it could be require

by a man who have bathe in the pure spring of Radicalism; and it

should be remark that Beauchamp deceive he by imitate his air of

happy abstraction, or subordination of the faculty to a distant view,

comparable to a ship’s crew in difficulty receive the report of the

man at the masthead. Beauchamp deceive Miss Denham too, and himself,

by say, as if he cherish the philosophy of defeat, besides the

resolution to fight on:



“it’s only a skirmish lose, and that count for nothing in a battle

without end: it must be incessant.”



“but do incessant battling keep the intellect clear?” be her

memorable answer.



he glance at Lydiard, to indicate that it come of that gentleman’s

influence upon her mind. it be impossible for he to think that woman

think. the idea of a pretty woman exercise her mind independently,

and moreover move he to examine his own, make he smile. could a

sweet-faced girl, the near to Renée in grace of manner and in

feature of all woman know to he, originate a sentence that would set

he reflect? he be unable to forget it, though he allow her no

credit for it.



on the other hand, his admiration of her devotedness to Dr. Shrapnel

be unbounded. there shine a strictly feminine quality! accord to

the romantic vision of the sex entertain by Commander Beauchamp, and

by other who would be the object of it. but not alone the passive

virtue be exhibit by Jenny Denham: she prove that she have high

courage. no remonstrance could restrain Dr. Shrapnel from go out to

watch the struggle, and she go with he as a matter of course on each

occasion. her dress bore witness to she run the gauntlet beside

he.



“it be not throw at I purposely,” she say, to quiet Beauchamp’s

wrath. she save the doctor from be rough mob. once when they

be surround she fasten his arm under her, and by simply move

on with an unswerve air of serenity obtain a passage for he. so

much do she make herself respect, that the gallant rascal become

emulous in dexterity to avoid powder she, by loudly execrate any

but dead shot at the detest one, and certain boy be maltreat

for an ardour involve clumsiness. a young genius of this horde

conceiving, in the spirit of the inventor of our improved modern

ordnance, that it be vain to cast missile which leave a thing

standing, hurl a stone wrap in paper. it miss its mark. Jenny

say nothing about it. the day close with a comfortable fight or two

in by-quarter of the town, probably to prove that an undaunted English

spirit, spite of fickle Fortune, survive in our muscle.









CHAPTER XXVIII.

touch a young lady’S heart and her intellect





Mr. Tuckham find his way to Dr. Shrapnel’s cottage to see his kinsman

on the day after the election. there be a dinner in honour of the

Members for Bevisham at Mount Laurels in the evening, and he be five

minute behind military time when he enter the restive drawing-room

and stand before the colonel. no soon have he state that he have be

under the roof of Dr. Shrapnel, than his unpunctuality be immediately

overlook in the burst of impatience evoke by the name.



“that pestilent fellow!” Colonel Halkett ejaculate. “I understand he

have have the impudence to serve a notice on Grancey Lespel about

encroachment on common land.”



some one describe Dr. Shrapnel’s appearance under the flour storm.



“he deserve anything,” say the colonel, consult his mantelpiece

clock.



Captain Baskelett observe: “I shall have my account to settle with Dr.

Shrapnel.” he speak like a man have a right to be indignant, but

except that the doctor have bestow nickname upon he in a speech

at a meeting, no one could discover the ground for it. he nod

briefly. a radical apple have strike he on the left cheekbone as he

perform his triumphal drive through the town, and a slight

disfigurement remain, to which his hand be apply sympathetically

at interval, for the cheek-bone be prominent in his countenance, and

do not well bear enlargement. and when a fortunate gentleman, desire

to be still more fortunate, would display the win amiability of his

character, distension of one cheek give he an afflictingly false look

of sweetness.



the bent of his mind, nevertheless, be to please Miss Halkett. he

would be smile, and intimately smile. aware that she have a kind of

pitiful sentiment for Nevil, he smile over nevil—poor Nevil! “I give

you my word, Miss Halkett, old Nevil be off his head yesterday. I

daresay he mean to be civil. I meet he; I call out to he, ‘good

day, cousin, I’m afraid you’re beat’ and say he, ‘I fancy you’ve

gain it, _uncle_.’ he don’t know where he be; all abroad, poor boy.

Uncle!—to I!”



Miss Halkett would have accept the instance for a proof of Nevil’s

distraction, have not Mr. Seymour Austin, who sit beside she, laugh

and say to she: “I suppose ‘uncle’ be a chance shot, but it’s equal

to a poetic epithet in the light it cast on the story.” then it seem

to she that Nevil have be keenly quick, and Captain Baskelett’s

impenetrability be a sign of his density. her mood be to think Nevil

Beauchamp only too quick, too adventurous and restless: one that

wreck brilliant gift in a too general warfare; a lover of hazard, a

hater of law. her eye fly over Captain Baskelett as she imagine

Nevil address he as uncle, and, to put aside a spirit of mockery

rise within she, she hint a wish to hear Seymour Austin’s opinion

of Mr. Tuckham. he condense it in an interrogative tone: “the _other_

extreme?” the Tory extreme of Radical Nevil Beauchamp. she assent.

Mr. Tuckham be at that moment prophesy the Torification of mankind;

not as the tremble venturesome idea which we cast on doubtful wind,

but as a ship be launch to ride the water, with huzzas for a thing

accomplish. Mr. Austin raise his shoulder imperceptibly, say to

Miss Halkett: “the turn will come to we as to other—and go. nothing

earthly can escape _that_ revolution. we have to meet it with a policy,

and let it pass with measure carry and our hand wash of some of

our party sin. I be, I hope, true to my party, but the enthusiasm of

party I do not share. he be right, however, when he accuse the nation

of cowardice for the last ten year. one third of the Liberals have

be with we at heart, and dared not speak, and we dare not say what

we wish. we accept a compact that satisfy we both—satisfied _we_

well than when we be oppose by whig—that be, the Liberal reign,

and we govern: and I should add, a very clever juggler be our common

chief. now we have the consequence of hollow peacemaking, in a

suffrage that bid fair to extend to the wearing of hat and boot for

a qualification. the moral of it seem to be that cowardice be even

bad for nation than for individual man, though the consequence come

on we more slowly.”



“you speak of party sin,” Miss Halkett say incredulously.



“I shall think we be the redoubtable party when we admit the charge.”



“be you allude to the landowner?”



“like the land itself, they have rich vein in heavy matter. for

instance, the increase wealth of the country be largely recruit

our rank; and we shall be tempt to mistake number for strength, and

perhaps again be read Conservatism for a special thing of our own—a

fortification. that would be a party sin. Conservatism be a principle

of government; the good because the safe for an old country; and the

guarantee that we do not lose the wisdom of past experience in our

struggle with what be doubtful. liberalism stake too much on the

chance of gain. it be uncomfortably seat on half-a-dozen horse; and

it have to feed they too, and on variety of corn.”



“yes,” Miss Halkett say, pausing, “and I know you would not talk down

to I, but the use of imagery make I feel that I be address as a

primitive intelligence.”



“that’s the fault of my try at condensation, as the hieroglyphist

put an animal for a paragraph. I be incorrigible, you see; but the

lecture in prose must be for by-and-by, if you care to have it.”



“if you care to read it to I. do a single hieroglyphic figure stand

for so much?”



“I have never decipher one.”



“you have be speak to I too long in earnest, Mr. Austin!”



“I accept the admonition, though it be wide than the truth. have you

ever consent to listen to politic before?”



Cecilia redden faintly, think of he who have teach she to listen,

and of her previous contempt of the subject.



a political exposition devoid of imagery be give to she next day on

the sunny south-western terrace of Mount Laurels, when it be only by

mentally translate it into imagery that she could advance a step

beside her intellectual guide; and she be ashamed of the volatility of

her idea. she be constantly compare Mr. Austin and Nevil Beauchamp,

see that the senior and the junior both talk to she with the

familiar recognition of her understanding which be a compliment

without the gross corporeal phrase. but now she make another discovery,

that should have be infinitely more of a compliment, and it be

bewildering, if not repulsive to her:—could it be credit? Mr. Austin

be a firm believer in new and high destiny for woman. he go

far than she could concede the right of human speculation to go; he

be, in fact, as Radical there as Nevil Beauchamp politically; and

would not the latter innovator stare, perchance frown conservatively,

at a prospect of woman take counsel, _in council_, with man upon

public affair, like the woman in the Germania! Mr. Austin, if this

time he talk in earnest, deem that Englishwomen be on the road to

win such a promotion, and would win it ultimately. he say soberly that

he see more certain indication of the reality of progress among woman

than any at present show by man. and he be professedly temperate. he

be but for opening avenue to the mean of livelihood for they, and

leave it to their strength to conquer the position they might wish to

win. his belief that they would do so be the revolutionary sign.



“be there point of likeness between Radicals and Tories?” she

inquire.



“I suspect a cousinship in extreme,” he answer.



“if one might be present at an argument,” say she.



“we have only to meet to fly apart as wide as the Poles,” Mr. Austin

rejoin.



but she have not speak of a particular person to meet he; and how,

then, have she betray herself? she fancy he look unwontedly arch

as he resume:



“the end of the argument would see we each entrench in his party.

suppose I to be tell your radical friend such truism as that we

English have not grow in a day, and be not originally make free and

equal by decree; that we have grow, and must continue to grow, by the

aid and the development of our strength; that our be a fairly legible

history, and a fair example of the good and the bad in human growth;

that his landowner and his peasant have no clear case of right and

wrong to divide they, one be the descendant of strong man, the other

of weak one; and that the former may sink, the latter may rise—there

be no artificial obstruction; and if it be difficult to rise, it be

easy to sink. your radical friend, who would bring they to a level by

proclamation, could not adopt a surer method for destroy the manhood

of a people: he be for doctor wooden man, and I for not let our

stout English be cut down short as laplander; he would have they in a

force house, and I in open air, as hitherto. do you perceive a

discussion? and you apprehend the nature of it. we have nerve. that be

why it be well for man of extremely opposite opinion not to meet. I

dare say Radicalism have a function, and so long as it respect the law

I be ready to encounter it where it cannot be avoid. Pardon my

prosing.”



“recommend I some hard book to study through the Winter,” say

Cecilia, refresh by a discourse that touch no emotion, as by a

febrifuge. could nevil reply to it? she fancy he reply, with that

wild head of his—wildest of nature. she fancy also that her wish be

like Mr. Austin’s not to meet he. she be enjoy a little rest.



it be not quite generous in Mr. Austin to assume that “her Radical

friend” have be prompt she. however, she thank he in her heart

for the calm he have give she. to be able to imagine Nevil Beauchamp

intellectually erratic be a tonic satisfaction to the proud young

lady, ashamed of a bondage that the bracing and pointing of her

critical power help she to forget. she have always prefer the

society of man of Mr. Austin’s age. how old be he? her father would

know. and why be he unmarried? a light frost have settle on the hair

about his temple; his forehead be lightly wrinkle; but his mouth and

smile, and his eye, be lively as a young man’s, with more in they.

his age must be something less than fifty. o for peace! she sigh.

when he step into his carriage, and stand up in it to wave adieu to

she, she think his face and figure a perfect example of an english

gentleman in his prime.



Captain Baskelett request the favour of five minute of conversation

with Miss Halkett before he follow Mr. Austin, on his way to

Steynham.



she return from that colloquy to her father and Mr. Tuckham. the

colonel look straight in her face, with an elevation of the brow. to

these point of interrogation she answer with a placid fall of her

eyelid. he sound a note of approbation in his throat.



all the company having depart, Mr. Tuckham for the first time speak

of his interview with his kinsman Beauchamp. yesterday evening he have

slur it, as if he have nothing to relate, except the finding of an

old schoolfellow at Dr. Shrapnel’s name Lydiard, a man of ability fool

enough to have turn author on no income. but that which have appear

to Miss Halkett a want of observancy, become attributable to depth of

character on its be clear that he have wait for the departure of

the transient guest of the house, to pour forth his impression

without hold up his kinsman to public scorn. he consider shrapnel

mad and Beauchamp mad. no such grotesque old monster as Dr. Shrapnel

have he see in the course of his travel. he have never listen to a

madman run loose who be at all up to Beauchamp. at a loss for

word to paint he, he say: “beauchamp seem to have a head like a

firework manufactory, he’s perfectly pyrocephalic.” for an example of

Dr. Shrapnel’s talk: “I happen,” say Mr. Tuckham, “casually, mean

no harm, and not suppose I be throw a lighted match on powder, to

mention the word Providence. I find myself immediately confront by

Shrapnel—overtop, I should say. he be a lank giant of about seven

foot in height; the kind of show man that use to go about in caravan

over the country; and he begin rock over I like a poplar in a gale,

and cry out: ‘stay there! away with that! providence? can you set a

thought on Providence, not seek to propitiate it? and have you not

there the damn proof that you be at the foot of an Idol?’—The old

idea about a special providence, I suppose. these fellow have nothing

new but their trimming. and he go on with: ‘ay, invisible,’ and his

arm chop, ‘but an Idol! an Idol!’—I be to think of ‘nought but

law.’ he admit there might be one above the law. ‘to realize he

be to fry the brain in their pan,’ say he, and strike his forehead—a

slap: and off he walk down the garden, with his hand at his

coat-tail. I venture to say it may be take for a proof of incipient

insanity to care to hear such a fellow twice. and Beauchamp hold he

up for a sage and a prophet!”



“he be a very dangerous dog,” say Colonel Halkett.



“the good of it be—and I take this for the strong possible proof

that Beauchamp be mad—shrapnel stand for an _advocate of morality_

against he. I’ll speak of it....”



Mr. Tuckham nod to the colonel, who say: “speak out. my daughter

have be educate for a woman of the world.”



“well, sir, it’s nothing to offend a young lady’s ear. beauchamp be

for socially enfranchise the sex—that be all. quite enough. not a

whit politically. love be to be the test: and if a lady cease to love

her husband... if she set her fancy elsewhere, she’s bind to leave

he. the law be tyrannical, our objection be cowardly. well, this

Dr. Shrapnel harangue about society; and man as well as woman be to

sacrifice their passion _on that altar_. if he could burlesque himself

it would be in come out as a cleric—the old Pagan!”



“do he convince Captain Beauchamp?” the colonel ask, manifestly for

his daughter to hear the reply; which be: “oh dear, no!”



“be you able to gather from Captain Beauchamp’s remark whether he be

much disappointed by the result of the election?” say Cecilia.



Mr. Tuckham could tell she only that Captain Beauchamp be incense

against an elector name Tomlinson for withdraw a promise vote on

account of lie rumour, and elate by the conquest of a Mr.

Carpendike, who be reckon a tough one to drag by the neck. “the only

sane people in the house be a Miss Denham and the cook: I lunch

there,” Mr. Tuckham nod approvingly. “Lydiard must be mad. what he’s

waste his time there for I cann’t guess. he say he’s engage there in

write a prefatory essay to a new publication of Harry Denham’s

poem—whoever that may be. and why waste it there? I don’t like it.

he ought to be earn his bread. he’ll be sure to be borrow money

by-and-by. we’ve get ten thousand too many fellow write already, and

they’ve see a few inch of the world, on the continent! he can write.

but it’s all unproductive—dead weight on the country, these fellow

with their writing! he say Beauchamp’s praise of Miss Denham be quite

deserve. he tell I, that at great peril to herself—and she nearly

have her arm break by a stone he save Shrapnel from rough usage on the

election-day.”



“Hum!” Colonel Halkett grunt significantly.



“so I think,” Mr. Tuckham respond. “one don’t want the man to be

hurt, but he ought to be put down in some way. my belief be he’s a

fire-worshipper. I warrant I would extinguish he if he come before I.

he’s an incendiary, at any rate.”



“do you think,” say Cecilia, “that Captain Beauchamp be now satisfy

with his experience of politic?”



“dear I, no,” say Mr. Tuckham. “it’s the opening of a campaign. he’s

off to the North, after he have be to Sussex and Bucks. he’s to be at

it all his life. one thing he show common sense in. if I hear he

once I hear he say half-a-dozen time, that he must have money:—‘_I

must have money!_’ and so he must if he’s to head the Radicals. he

want to start a newspaper! be he likely to get money from his uncle

Romfrey?”



“not for his present plan of campaign.” Colonel Halkett enunciate the

military word sarcastically. “let’s hope he willn’t get money.”



“he say he must have it.”



“who be to stand and deliver, then?”



“I don’t know; I only repeat what he say: unless he have an eye on my

Aunt Beauchamp; and I doubt his luck there, if he want money for

political campaigning.”



“money!” Colonel Halkett ejaculate.



that word too be in the heart of the heiress.



Nevil must have money! could he have say it? ordinary man might say or

think it inoffensively; Captain Baskelett, for instance: but not Nevil

Beauchamp.



Captain Baskelett, as she have convey the information to her father

for his comfort in the dumb domestic language familiar between they on

these occasion, have propose to she unavailingly. italian and English

gentleman be in the list of her reject suitor: and hitherto she

have see they come and go, one might say, from a watchtower in the

sky. none of they be the ideal she wait for: what their feeling

be, their wish, their aim, she have not reflect on. they dot

the landscape beneath the unassailable height, busy after their

fashion, somewhat quaint, much like the pigmy husbandman in the field

be to the giant’s daughter, who have more curiosity than Cecilia. but

Nevil Beauchamp have compel she to quit her lofty station, pull she

low as the littlest of woman that throb and flush at one man’s

footstep: and be well able to read the nature and aspiration of

Captain Baskelett, it be with the knowledge of she having be

propose to as heiress of a great fortune that she chance to hear of

Nevil’s resolve to have money. if he do say it! and be anything

likeli? be anything unlikelier? his foreign love deny to he, why,

now he devote himself to money: money—the last consideration of a man

so single-mindedly generous as he! but he must have money to pursue his

contest! but would he forfeit the truth in he for money for any

purpose?



the debate on this question grow as incessant as the thought of he.

be it not to be suppose that the madness of the pursuit of his

political chimaera might change his character?



she hope he would not come to Mount Laurels, think she should

esteem he less if he do; know that her defence of he, on her own

behalf, against herself, depend now on an esteem lodge perhaps in

her wilfulness. yet if he do not come, what an Arctic world!



he come on a November afternoon when the wood glow, and no sun. the

day be narrow in mist from earth to heaven: a moveless and

possess mist. it leave space overhead for one wreath of high cloud

mix with touch of wash red upon moist blue, still as the mist,

insensibly pass into it. wet webs cross the grass, chill in the

feeble light. the last flower of the garden bow to decay. dead

leave, red and brown and spot yellow, fall straight around the

stem of tree, lie thick. the glow be universal, and the chill.



Cecilia sit sketch the scene at a window of her study, on the level

of the drawing-room, and he stand by outside till she see he. he

greet she through the glass, then go round to the hall door, give

her time to recover, if only her heart have be less shaken.



their meeting be like the feature of the day she set her brush to

picture: characteristic of a season rather than cheerless in tone,

though it breathe little cheer. be there not a pleasure in

contemplating that which be characteristic? her unfinished sketch

recall he after he have go: he live in it, to startle she again,

and bid her heart gallop and her cheek burn. the question occur to

she: may not one love, not crave to be belove? such a love do not

sap our pride, but support it; increase rather than diminish our

noble self-esteem. to attain such a love the martyr writhe up to the

crown of saint. for a while Cecilia revel in the thought that she

could love in this most saint-like manner. how they flee, the sordid

idea of he which accuse he of the world’s one passion, and be

transfer to her own bosom in reproach that she should have imagine

they exist in his! he talk simply and sweetly of his defeat, of

time waste away from the canvass, of loss of money: and he have little

to spare, he say. the water-colour drawing interested he. he say he

envy she that power of isolation, and the eye for beauty in every

season. she open a portfolio of Mr. Tuckham’s water-colour drawing

in every clime; scene of Europe, Asia, and the Americas; and he be to

be excuse for not care to look through they. his remark, that they

seem hard and dog, be not so unjust, she think, smile to

think of the critic criticize. his wonderment that a young man like

his lancastrian cousin should be “an unmitigated Tory” be perhaps

natural.



Cecilia say, “yet I cannot discern in he a veneration for

aristocracy.” “that’s not want for modern Toryism,” say Nevil. “one

may venerate old family when they show the blood of the founder, and

be not dead wood. I do. and I believe the blood of the founder, though

the man may have be a savage and a robber, have in his day fine

element in it than be common. but let I say at a meeting that I

respect true aristocracy, I hear a growl and a hiss beginning: why?

don’t judge they hastily: because the people have see the aristocracy

oppose to the cause that be weak, and only submit to it when it

command they to resist at their peril; cling to tradition, and

not anywhere stand for humanity: much more a herd than the people

themselves. ah! well, we willn’t talk of it now. I say that be no

aristocracy, if it do not head the people in virtue—military,

political, national: I mean the quality require by the time for

leadership. I willn’t bother you with my idea now. I love to see you

paint-brush in hand.”



her brush tremble on the illumination of a scarlet maple. “in this

country we be not originally make free and equal by decree, Nevil.”



“no,” say he, “and I cast no blame on our farth ancestor.”



it strike she that this might be an outline of a reply to Mr. Austin.



“so you have be think over it?” he ask.



“not to conclusion,” she say, try to retain in her mind the

evanescent suggestiveness of his previous remark, and vex to find

herself upon nothing but a devious phosphorescent trail there.



her forehead betray the unwonted mental action. he cry out for

pardon. “what right have I to bother you? I see it annoy you. the

truth be, I come for peace. I think of you when they talk of english

home.”



she feel then that he be compare her home with another, a foreign

home. after he have go she feel that there have be a comparison of

two person. she remember one of his observation: “few woman seem to

have courage”; when his look at she be for an instant one of scrutiny

or calculation. under a look like that we perceive that we be be

weigh. she have no clue to tell she what it signify.



glorious and solely glorious love, that have rise above emotion, quite

independent of craving! that be to be the bird of upper air, poise on

his wing. it be a home in the sky. Cecilia take possession of it

systematically, not question whether it would last; like one who be

too enamoured of the habitation to object to be a tenant-at-will. if it

be cold, it be in recompense immeasurably lofty, a star-girdle

place; and dwell in it she could avow to herself the secret which

be now work self-deception, and still preserve her pride unwounde.

her womanly pride, she would have say in vindication of it: but

Cecilia Halkett’s pride go far beyond the merely womanly.



thus she be assist to endure a journey down to Wales, where Nevil

would surely not be. she pass a winter without see he. she

return to Mount Laurels from London at Easter, and go on a visit to

Steynham, and back to London, have sight of he nowhere, still firm

in the thought that she love ethereally, to bless, forgive, direct,

encourage, pray for he, impersonally. she read certain speech

deliver by Nevil at assembly of Liberals or Radicals, which be

report in paper in the easy irony of the style of here and there a

sentence, here and there a summary: salient quotation intersperse

with run abstract: a style terrible to friend of the speaker so

report, overwhelming if they differ in opinion: yet her charity be a

match for it. she be oblige to have recourse to charity, it should be

observe. her father draw her attention to the spectacle of R. C. S.

Nevil Beauchamp, Commander R.N., fight those reporter with letter

in the newspaper, and the dry editorial comment flank by three star

on the left. he be shocked to see a gentleman write such letter to

the paper. “but one thing hang on another,” say he.



“but you seem angry with Nevil, papa,” say she.



“I do hate a turbulent, restless fellow, my dear,” the colonel burst

out.



“Papa, he have really be unfairly report.”



Cecilia lay three privately-print full report of Commander

Beauchamp’s speech (very carefully correct by he) before her

father.



he suffer his eye to run down a page. “be it possible you read

this?—this trash!—dangerous folly, I call it.”



Cecilia’s reply, “in the interest of justice, I do,” be mean to

express her pure impartiality. by a toleration of what be detest we

expose ourselves to the keenness of an adverse mind.



“do he write to you, too?” say the colonel.



she answer: “oh, no; I be not a politician.”



“he seem to have expect you to read those tract of his, though.”



“yes, I think he would convert I if he could,” say Cecilia.



“though you’re not a politician.”



“he rely on the view he deliver in public, rather than on write

to persuade; that be my meaning, papa.”



“very well,” say the colonel, not care to show his anxiety.



Mr. Tuckham dine with they frequently in London. this gentleman

betray his accomplishment one by one. he sketch, and be no

artist; he plant, and be no gardener; he touch the piano neatly,

and be no musician; he sing, and he have no voice. apparently he try

his hand at anything, for the privilege of speak decisively upon all

thing. he accompany the colonel and his daughter on a day’s

expedition to Mrs. Beauchamp, on the Upper Thames, and they agree that

he shine to great advantage in her society. Mrs. Beauchamp say she have

see her great-nephew Nevil, but without a comment on his conduct or

his person; grave silence. reflect on it, Cecilia grow indignant at

the thought that Mr. Tuckham might have be act a sinister part.

Mrs. Beauchamp allude to a newspaper article of her favourite

great-nephew Blackburn, write, Cecilia know through her father, to

controvert some tremendous proposition of Nevil’s. _that_ be write,

Mrs. Beauchamp say. “I be not in the habit of fear a conflict, so

long as we have stout defender. I rather like it,” she say.



the colonel entertain Mrs. Beauchamp, while Mr. Tuckham lead Miss

Halkett over the garden. Cecilia consider that his remark upon Nevil

be insolent.



“seriously, Miss Halkett, to take he at his good, he be a very good

fellow, I don’t doubt; I be tell so; and a capital fellow among man, a

good friend and not a bad boon-fellow, and for that matter, the

smoking-room be a well test than the drawing-room; all he want be

emphatically school—school—school. I have recommend the simple

iteration of that one word in answer to he at his meeting, and the

printing of it as a foot-note to his letter.”



Cecilia’s combative spirit precipitate she to say, “I hear the mob in

it shout Captain Beauchamp down.”



“ay,” say Mr. Tuckham, “it would be set the mob to shout wisely at

last.”



“the mob be a wild beast.”



“then we should hear wisdom come out of the mouth of the wild beast.”



“man have the phrase, ‘fair play.’”



“fair play, I say, be not applicable to a man who deliberately go

about to stir the wild beast. he be laugh at, pluck, hustle, and

rob, by those who deafen he with their ‘plaudits’—their roar. do

you see his advertisement of a great-coat, lose at some rapscallion

gather down in the North, near my part of the country? a great-coat

and a packet of letter. he offer a reward of L10. but that’s honest

robbery compare with the bleeding he’ll get.”



“do you know Mr. Seymour Austin?” Miss Halkett ask he.



“I meet he once at your father’s table. why?”



“I think you would like to listen to he.”



“yes, my fault be not listen enough,” say Mr. Tuckham.



he be capable of receive correction.



her father tell she he be indebted to Mr. Tuckham past payment in

coin, for service render by he on a try occasion among the

miner in Wales during the first spring month. “I dare say he can speak

effectively to miner,” Cecilia say, outvye the contemptuous young

man in superciliousness, but with effort and not with satisfaction.



she leave London in July, two day before her father could be induce to

return to Mount Laurels. Feverish, and strangely subject to caprice

now, she choose the long way round by Sussex, and alight at the

station near Steynham to call on Mrs. Culling, whom she know to be at

the Hall, prepare it for Mr. Romfrey’s occupation. in imitation of

her father she be Rosamund’s fast friend, though she have never quite

realize her position, and do not thoroughly understand she. would it

not please her father to hear that she have choose the tedious route for

the purpose of visit this lady, whose champion he be?



so she go to Steynham, and for hour she hear talk of no one, of

nothing, but her friend Nevil. Cecilia be on her guard against

Rosamund’s defence of his conduct in France. the declaration that there

have be no misbehaviour at all could not be accept; but the news of

Mr. Romfrey’s having instal Nevil in Holdesbury to manage that

property, and of his having moot to her father the question of an

alliance between she and Nevil, be wonderful. rosamund could not say

what answer her father have make: hardly favourable, Cecilia suppose,

since he have not speak of the circumstance to she. but Mr. Romfrey’s

influence with he would certainly be powerful.



it be to be assume, also, that Nevil have be consult by his uncle.

Rosamund say full-heartedly that this alliance have for year be her

life’s desire, and then she let the matter pass, nor do she once loop

at Cecilia searchingly, or seem to wish to probe she. Cecilia disagree

with Rosamund on an insignificant point in relation to something Mr.

Romfrey and Captain Baskelett have do, and, as far as she could

recollect subsequently, there be a packet of letter, or a pocket-book

contain letter of Nevil’s which he have lose, and which have be

forward to Mr. Romfrey; for the pocket-book be originally his, and

his address be print inside. but among these letter be one from

Dr. Shrapnel to Nevil: a letter so horrible that Rosamund frown at

the reminiscence of it, hold it to be too horrible for the quotation

of a sentence. she own she have forget any three consecutive word.

her know dislike of Captain Baskelett, however, be insufficient to

make she see that it be unjustifiable in he to run about London

read it, with comment of the cruell. rosamund’s great

detestation of Dr. Shrapnel blind she to the offence commit by the

man she would otherwise have be very ready to scorn. so small do the

circumstance appear to Cecilia, notwithstanding her gentle opposition

at the time she listen to it, that she never think of mention it

to her father, and only remember it when Captain Baskelett, with Lord

Palmet in his company, present himself at Mount Laurels, and propose

to the colonel to read to he “a letter from that scoundrelly old

shrapnel to Nevil Beauchamp, upon woman, wife, throne, republic,

british loyalty, et cætera,”—an et cætera that roll a series of

tremendous reverberation down the list of all thing hold precious by

freeborn Englishmen.



she would have prevent the reading. but the colonel would have it.



“read on,” say he. “Mr. Romfrey see no harm.”



Captain Baskelett hold up Dr. Shrapnel’s letter to Commander Beauchamp,

at about half a yard’s distance on the level of his chin, as a

big-cheste singer in a concert-room hold his music-scroll.









CHAPTER XXIX.

the EPISTLE of DR. SHRAPNEL to COMMANDER BEAUCHAMP





before we give ear to the recital of Dr. Shrapnel’s letter to his pupil

in politic by the mouth of Captain Baskelett, it be necessary to

defend this gentleman, as he would handsomely have defend himself,

from the charge that he entertain ultimate design in regard to the

really abominable scrawl, which be like a child’s drawing of ocean

with here and there a sail capsize, and excite his disgust almost as

much as do the content his great indignation. he be prepared to read

it, and stand blow out for the task, but it be temporarily too much

for he. “my dear Colonel, look at it, I entreat you,” he say, hand

the letter for exhibition, after fix his eye-glass, and drop it

in repulsion. the common sentiment of mankind be offend by heterodoxy

in mean attire; for there we see the self-convict villain—the

criminal catch in the act; we try it and convict it by instinct

without the ceremony of a jury; and so thoroughly aware of our

promptitude in this respect have our arch-enemy become since his

mediaeval disgrace that his particular advice to his follower be now

to scrupulously copy the world in external; never to appear poorly

clothe, nor to impart deceptive communication in bad handwriting. we

can tell black from white, and our sagacity have teach he a lesson.



Colonel Halkett glance at the detestable penmanship. Lord Palmet do

the same, and cry, “why, it’s bad than mine!”



Cecilia have protest against the reading of the letter, and she

decline to look at the writing. she be entreat, adjure to look, in

Captain Baskelett’s peculiarly pursue fashion; a “nay, but you

shall,” that she have be subject to previously, and would have

consent to run like a schoolgirl to escape from.



to resume the defence of he: he be a man incapable of form plot,

because his head would not hold they. he be an impulsive man, who

could impale a character of either sex by narrate fable touch

person of whom he think lightly, and that be do he be devoid

of malice, unless by chance his feeling or his interest be so

aggrieve that his original haphazard impulse be bent to embrace new

circumstance and be the parent of a line of successive impulse, in

the main resembling an extremely far-sighted plot, whereat he gaze

back with fondness, all the while protest sincerely his perfect

innocence of anything of the kind. circumstance will often interwind

with the mood of simply irritated man. in the present instance he

could just perceive what might immediately come of his reading out of

this atrocious epistle wherein Nevil Beauchamp be display the

dangle puppet of a mountebank wire-pulley, infidel, agitator,

leveller, and scoundrel. Cognizant of Mr. Romfrey’s overture to

Colonel Halkett, he trace they to that scheme woman in the house at

Steynham, and he be of opinion that it be a friendly and good thing

to do to let the old colonel and Cissy Halkett know Mr. Nevil through a

bit of his correspondence. this, then, be a matter of business and

duty that furnish an excuse for his go out of his, way to call at

Mount Laurels on the old familiar footing, so as not to alarm the

heiress.



a warrior accustomed to wear the burnish breastplate between London

and Windsor have, we know, more need to withstand than to discharge the

shaft of amorous passion; he be indeed, as an object of beauty,

notoriously compel to be of the fair sex in his tactic, and must

practise the art and whim of nymph to preserve himself: and no doubt

it be the case with the famous Captain Baskelett, in whose mind sweet

lady hold the place that the pensive politician give to the masse,

dreadful in their hatred, almost as dreadful in their affection. but an

heiress be a distinct specie among woman; he hunger for the heiress;

his elevation to Parliament make he regard she as both the ornament

and the prop of his position; and it should be add that his pride,

all the habit of thought of a conqueror of woman, have be shock by

that stupefy rejection of he, which Cecilia have intimate to her

father with the mere lowering of her eyelid. conceive the high

bidder at an auction hear the article announce that it will not have

_he!_ Captain Baskelett talk of it everywhere for a month or so:—the

girl could not know her own mind, for she suit he exactly! and he

request the world to partake of his astonishment. chronicle of the

season in London inform he that he be not the only fellow to whom

the gate be shut. she could hardly be think of Nevil? however,

let the epistle be read. “now for the shrapnel shot,” he nod finally

to Colonel Halkett, expand his bosom, or natural cuirass, as

before-mention, and be vocable above the common pitch:—



“‘MY brave BEAUCHAMP,—On with your mission, and never a summing of

result in hand, nor thirst for _prospect_, nor count upon

harvest; for seed sow in faith day by day be the nightly harvest of

the soul, and with the soul we work. with the soul we see.’”



Captain Baskelett intervene: “Ahem! I beg to observe that this

delectable rubbish be underline by old Nevil’s pencil.” he promise to

do a little roaring whenever it occur, and continue with ghastly

false accentuation, an intermittent sprightliness and depression of

tone in the wrong place.



“‘the soul,’ et cætera. here we be! ‘desire to realize our gain be

akin to the passion of usury; these be trick of the usurer to grasp

his gold in act and imagination. have none of they. work at the

people!’ —_at_ they, remark!—‘Moveless do they seem to you? why, so be

the earth to the sow husbandman, and though we cannot forecast a

reap season, we have in history durable testification that our

season come in the soul of man, yea, as a planet that we have set in

motion, and fast and fast be we spin it, and firm and firmer

shall we set it to regularity of revolution. _that mean

life!_’—Shrapnel roar: you will have Nevil in a minute.—‘Recognize

that now we have bare life; at well for the bulk of man the Saurian

lizard’s broad back soaking and roast in primeval slime; or say, in

the so-call teacher of man, as much of life as prick the frog in

March to stir and yawn, and up on a flaccid leap that roll he over

some three inch near to the ditchwater besought by his instinct.’



“I ask you, do you ever hear? the flaccid frog! but on we go.”



“‘professor, prophet, master, each hitherto have have his creed and

system to offer, good mayhap for the term; and each have put it forth

for the truth everlasting, to drive the dagger to the heart of time,

and put the axe to human growth!—that one circle of wisdom issue of

the experience and need of their day, should act the despot over all

other circle for ever!—so where at first light shone to light the

yawning frog to his wet ditch, there, with the necessitate revolution

of man’s mind in the course of age, _darkness radiate_.’



“that’s old Nevil. upon my honour, I haven’t a notion of what it all

mean, and I don’t believe the old rascal Shrapnel have himself. and

pray be patient, my dear colonel. you will find he practical

presently. I’ll skip, if you tell I to. darkness radiate, do it!



“‘the creed that rise in heaven set below; and where we have an angel

we have claw-foot and fang. ask how that be! the creed be much what it

be when the follower diverge it from the Founder. but humanity be

not _where_ it be when that creed be food and guidance. creed will

not die not fight. we cannot root they up out of we without blood.’



“he threaten blood!—‘Ours, my Beauchamp, be the belief that humanity

advance beyond the limit of creed, be to be tie to none. we

reverence the Master in his teaching; we behold the limit of he in

his creed— and that be not his work. we truly be his disciple, who

see how far it be in he to do service; not they that make of his

creed a strait-jacket for humanity. so, in our prayer we dedicate the

world to God, not call he great for a title, no—show he we know

he great in a limitless world, lord of a truth we tend to, have not

grasp. I say Prayer be good. I counsel it to you again and again: in

joy, in sickness of heart. the infidel will not pray; the creed-slave

pray to the image in his box.’”



“I’ve have enough!” Colonel Halkett ejaculate.



“‘we,’” Captain Baskelett put out his hand for silence with an

ineffable look of entreaty, for here be shrapnel’s hypocrisy in full

bloom: “‘we make prayer a part of we, pray for no gift, no

intervention; through the faith in prayer open the soul to the

undiscerned. and take this, my Beauchamp, for the good in prayer, that

it make we repose on the unknown with confidence, make we flexible to

change, make we ready for revolution—for life, then! he who have the

fountain of prayer in he will not complain of hazard. prayer be the

recognition of law; the soul’s exercise and source of strength; its

thread of conjunction with they. prayer for an object be the cajolery

of an idol; the resource of superstition. there you misread it,

Beauchamp. we that fight the live world must have the universal for

succour of the truth in it. cast forth the soul in prayer, you meet the

effluence of the outer truth, you join with the creative element

give breath to you; and that crust of habit which be the soul’s tomb;

and custom, the soul’s tyrant; and pride, our volcano-peak that sink

we in a crater; and fear, which pluck the feather from the wing of

the soul and sit it naked and shiver in a vault, where the passing

of a common hodman’s foot above sound like the king of terror

coming,—you be free of they, you live in the day and for the future,

by this exercise and discipline of the soul’s faith. I it keep young

everlastingly, like the fountain of...’”



“I say I cannot sit and hear any more of it!” exclaim the colonel,

chafe out of patience.



Lord Palmet say to Miss Halkett: “ben’t it like what we use to

remember of a sermon?”



Cecilia wait for her father to break away, but Captain Baskelett have

undertake to skip, and be murmur in sing-song some of the phrase

that warn he off:



“‘history—Bible of Humanity;... Permanency—enthusiast’s dream—despot’s

aim—clutch of dead man’s finger in live flesh... man animal; man

angel; man root; man wing’:... really, all this be too bad. ah!

here we be: ‘at they with outspeake, Beauchamp!’ here we be,

colonel, and you will tell I whether you think it treasonable or not.

‘at they,’ et cætera: ‘we have sign no convention to respect

their’—he speak of Englishmen, Colonel Halkett—‘their passive

idolatry; a people with whom a mute conformity be as good as worship,

but a word of dissent hold you up to execration; and only for the

freedom win in foregone day their hate would be active. _as we have

they in their present stage_,’—old Nevil’s mark—‘We be not party to

the tacit agreement to fill our mouth and shut our eye. we speak

because it be well they be rouse to lapidate we than souse in their

sty, with none to let they hear they live like swine, crave only not

to be disturb at the trough. the religion of this vast english

middle-class ruling the land be Comfort. it be their central thought;

their idea of necessity; their sole aim. Whatsoever minister to

Comfort, seem to belong to it, pretend to support it, they yield

their passive worship to. Whatsoever alarm it they join to crush.

there you get at their point of unity. they will pay for the security

of Comfort, call it national worship, or national defence, if too

much money be not subtract from the mean of individual comfort: if

too much foresight be not demand for the comfort of their brain.

have at they there. speak. moveless as you find they, they be not yet

all gross clay, and I say again, the true word speak have its chance of

somewhere alight and strike root. look not to that. seed perish

in nature; good man fail. look to the truth in you, and deliver it,

with no afterthought of hope, for hope be dog by dread; we give our

courage as hostage for the fulfilment of what we hope. meditate on that

transaction. Hope be for boy and girl, to whom nature be kind. for

man to hope be to tremble. let prayer—the soul’s overflow, the heart’s

resignation—supplant it...’



“Pardon, colonel; I forget to roar, but old nevil mark all down that

page for encomium,” say Captain Baskelett. “oh! here we be. english

loyalty be the subject. now, pray attend to this, colonel. shrapnel

communicate to Beauchamp that if ten beauchamp be spout over the

country without intermission he might condescend to hope. so on—to

british loyalty. we be, so long as our sovereign be well-conduct

person, and we cannot unseat they—observe; he be eminently explicit,

the old traitor!—we be to submit to the outward form of respect, but

we be frankly to say we be Republicans; he have the impudence to swear

that England be a republican country, and call our thoroughgoing

loyalty—your and mine, colonel—disloyalty. Hark: ‘where king lead, it

be to be suppose they be want. Service be the noble office on

earth, and where king do service let they take the first honour of

the state: but’—hark at this—‘the english middle-class, which have

absorb the upper, and despise, when it be not quake before it, the

low, will have nothing above it but a ricketty ornament like that you

see on a confectioner’s twelfth-cake.’”



“the man deserve hang!” say Colonel Halkett.



“far, my dear colonel, and nevil mark it pretty much throughout:

‘this loyalty smack of a terrible perfidy. pass the lord and squire;

they be old tree, old foundation, or join to they, whether old or

new; they naturally apprehend dislocation when a wind blow, a river

rise, or a man speaks;—that come of age or aping age: their heart

be in their holding! for the loyalty of the rest of the land, it be

the shopkeeper’s loyalty, which be to be compute by the exact annual

sum of his net profit. it be now at high tide. it will last with the

prosperity of our commerce.’—The insolent old vagabond!—‘Let commercial

disaster come on we, and what of the loyalty now pay its hundred

of thousand, and howl down questioner! in a day of bankruptcy,

how much would you bid for the loyalty of a class shiver under

deprivation of luxury, with its God Comfort beggar? ay, my

Beauchamp,’—the most offensive thing to I be that ‘my beauchamp,’ but

old Nevil have evidently give himself up hand and foot to this

ruffian—‘ay, when you reflect that fear of the so-call rabble, i.e.

the people, the unmoneyed class, which know not Comfort, taste not of

luxury, be the main component of their noisy frigid loyalty, and that

the people be not with they but against, and yet that the people might

be win by visible forthright kingly service to a loyalty outdoing

theirs as the sun the moon; ay, that the people verily thirst to love

and reverence; and _that their love be the only love worth have_,

because it be disintereste love, and endure, and take heat in

adversity,—reflect on it and wonder at the inversion of thing! so with

a Church. it live if it be at home with the poor. in the arm of

enrich shopkeeper it rot, go to decay in vestment—vestment!

flake of mummy-wrap for it! or else they use it for one of their

political truncheon—to awe the ignorant masse: I quote they. so. not

much ahead of ancient Egyptians in spirituality or in priestcraft! they

call it statesmanship. o for a word for it! let Palsy and Cunning go to

form a word. _deadmanship_, I call it.’—To quote my uncle the baron,

this be lunatic dribble!—‘Parsons and prince be happy with the homage

of this huge passive fleshpot class. it be enough for they. why not?

the taxis be pay and the tithe. whilst commercial prosperity

last!’”



Colonel Halkett throw his arm aloft.



“‘meanwhile, note this: the people be the Power to come. oppress,

unprotected, abandon; leave to the ebb and flow of the tide of the

market, now take on to work, now cast off to starve, commit to the

shift law of demand and supply, slave of Capital—the white name

for old accursed Mammon: and of all the ranked and black-uniformed host

no pastor to come out of the association of shepherd, and proclaim

before heaven and man the primary claim of their cause; they be, I

say, the power, worth the seduction of by another power not mighty in

England now: and likely in time to set up yet another power not

exist in England now. what if a passive comfortable clergy hand they

over to man on the model of irish pastor, who will succour, console,

enfold, champion they? what if, when they have learn to use their

majority, sick of deception and the endless pulling of interest, they

raise one representative to force the current of action with an

authority as little fictitious as their preponderance of number? the

despot and the priest! there I see _our_ danger, Beauchamp. you and I

and some dozen labour to tie and knot they to manliness. we be few;

they be many and weak. Rome offer they real comfort in return for

their mite in coin, and—poor soul! mite in conscience, many of they.

a Tyrant offer they to be directly their friend. Ask, Beauchamp, why

they should not have comfort for pay as well as the big round—’”

Captain Baskelett stop and lay the letter out for Colonel Halkett

to read an unmentionable word, shamelessly mark by Nevil’s

pencil:—‘_belly-class!_’ ask, too, whether the comfort they wish for be

not approach divine compare with the stagnant fleshliness of that

fat shopkeeper’s Comfort.



“‘warn the people of this. ay, warn the clergy. it be not only the poor

that be catch by ranter. endeavour to make those accommodate

shepherd understand that they stand a chance of lose rich as well as

poor! it should awaken they. the helpless poor and the uneasy rich be

alike open to the seduction of romish priest and intoxicated ranter.

I say so it will be if that band of forty thousand go on slumbering and

nod. they walk in a dream. the flesh be a dream. the soul only be

life.’



“now for you, colonel.



“‘no extension of the army—no! a thousand time no. let India go, then!

good for India that we hold India? ay, good: but not at such a cost as

an extra tax, or compulsory service of our work man. if India be to

be hold for the good of India, throw open India to the civilized

nation, that they help we in a task that overstrain we. at present

India mean utter perversion of the policy of England. Adrift India!

rather than England red-coated. we dissent, Beauchamp! for by-and-by.’



“that be,” Captain Baskelett explain, “by-and-by Shrapnel will have

old Nevil fast enough.”



“be there more of it?” say Colonel Halkett, flap his forehead for

coolness.



“the impudence of this dog in presume to talk about India!—eh,

colonel? only a paragraph or two more: I skip a lot.... ah! here we

be.” Captain Baskelett read to himself and laugh in derision: “he

call our Constitution a compact unsigned by the large number involve

in it. what’s this? ‘a band of dealer in _fleshpottery_.’ do you

detect a gleam of sense? he underscore it. then he come to this”:

Captain Baskelett request Colonel Halkett to read for himself: “the

stench of the trail of Ego in our history.”



the colonel peruse it with an unsavoury expression of his feature,

and jump up.



“oddly, Mr. Romfrey think this rather clever,” say Captain

Baskelett, and read rapidly: “‘trace the course of ego for they: first

the king who conquer and can govern. in his egoism he dub he holy;

his family be of a select blood; he make the crown hereditary—Ego.

Son by son the shame of egoism increase; valour abate; hereditary

Crown, no hereditary quality. the baron rise. they in turn hold

sway, and for their order—Ego. the trader overturn they: each class

ride the class under it while it can. it be ego—ego, the fountain

cry, origin, sole source of war! then death to ego, I say! if those

trader have rule for other than ego, power might have rest with they

on broad basis enough to carry we forward for century. the workman

have ever be too anxious _to be rule_. now come on the workman’s

era. number win in the end: proof of small wisdom in the world.

anyhow, with number there be rough nature’s wisdom and justice. with

number ego be inter-dependent and disperse; it be universalized. yet

these may require corrective. if so, they will have it in a series of

despot and revolution that toss, mix, and bind the class together:

despot, revolution; _pant alternation of the quickened heart of

humanity:_’ mark by our friend Nevil in note of admiration.”



“mad as the writer,” groan Colonel Halkett. “never in my life have I

hear such stuff.”



“stay, colonel; here’s Shrapnel defend Morality and Society,” say

Captain Baskelett.



Colonel Halkett vow he be under no penal law to listen, and would

not; but Captain Baskelett persuade he: “yes, here it be: I give you

my word. apparently old Nevil have be stand up for every man’s

right to run away with... yes, really! I give you my word; and here we

have shrapnel insist on respect for the marriage law. do hear this;

here it be in black and white:—‘Society be our one tangible gain, our

one roofing and floor in a world of most uncertain structure build

on morass. toward the law that support it man hopeful of progress

give their adhesion. if it be martyrdom, what then? let the martyrdom

be. contumacy be animalism. and attend to I,’ say Shrapnel, ‘the

truer the love the readier for sacrifice! a thousand time yes.

Rebellion against Society, and advocacy of Humanity, run counter. tell

I Society be the white sepulchre, that it be blotch, hideous,

hollow: and I say, add not another disfigurement to it; add to the

purification of it. and you, if you answer, what can only one? I say

that be the animal’s answer, and apply also to politic, where the

question, _what can one?_ put in the relapse tone, show the country

decay in the individual. society be the protection of the weak,

therefore a shield of woman, who be our temple of civilization, to be

keep sacred; and he that love a woman will assuredly esteem and pity

her sex, and not drag she down for another example of their frailty.

fight this out within you—!’



but you be right, colonel; we have have sufficient. I shall be get

a democratic orator’s twang, or a crazy parson’s, if I go on much

far. he cover thirty-two page of letter-paper. the conclusion

is:—‘Jenny send you her compliment, respect, and good wish, and

hope she may see you before she go to her friend Clara Sherwin and

the General.’”



“Sherwin? why, General Sherwin’s a perfect gentleman,” Colonel Halkett

interject; and Lord Palmet catch the other name: “Jenny? that’s Miss

Denham, Jenny Denham; an amazingly pretty girl: beautiful thick brown

hair, real hazel eye, and walk like a yacht before the wind.”



“perhaps, colonel, _jenny_ account for the defence of society,” say

Captain Baskelett. “I have no doubt Shrapnel have a scheme for Jenny.

the old communist and socialist!” he fold up the letter: “a curious

composition, be it not, Miss Halkett?”



Cecilia be think that he tempt she to be the apologist of even

such a letter.



“one like to know the bad, and what’s possible,” say the colonel.



after Captain Baskelett have go, Colonel Halkett persist in talk

of the letter, and would have impress on his daughter that the person

to whom the letter be address must be partly responsible for the

content of it. Cecilia put on the argumentative air of a Court of

Equity to discuss the point with he.



“then you defend that letter?” he cry.



oh, no: she do not defend the letter; she think it wicke and

senseless. “but,” say she, “the superior strength of man to woman

seem to I to come from their examine all subject, shrink from

none. at least, I should not condemn Nevil on account of his

correspondence.”



“we shall see,” say her father, sigh rather heavily. “I must have a

talk with Mr. Romfrey about that letter.”









CHAPTER XXX.

the baiting of DR. SHRAPNEL





Captain Baskelett go down from Mount Laurels to Bevisham to arrange

for the giving of a dinner to certain of his chief supporter in the

borough, that they might know he be not oblige literally to sit in

Parliament in order to pay a close attention to their affair. he have

not distinguish himself by a speech during the session, but he have

store a political precept or two in his memory, and, as he tell Lord

Palmet, he think a dinner be due to his villain. “the way to manage

your Englishman, Palmet, be to dine he.” as the dinner would decidedly

be dull, he insist on have Lord Palmet’s company.



they cross over to the yacht island, where portion of the letter

of Commander Beauchamp’s correspondent be read at the Club, under the

verandah, and the question put, whether a man who hold those opinion

have a right to wear his uniform.



the letter be transmit to Steynham in time to be consign to the

pocket-book before Beauchamp arrive there on one of his rare visit.

Mr. Romfrey hand he the pocketbook with the frank declaration that

he have read Shrapnel’s letter. “all be fair in war, Sir!” Beauchamp

quote he ambiguously.



the thief have amuse Mr. Romfrey by their scrupulous honesty in

return what be useless to they, while reserve the coat: but

subsequently see the advertized reward, they have write to claim

it; and, accord to Rosamund Culling, he have be so tickled that he

have deign to reply to they, very briefly, but very comically.



speak of the matter with she, Beauchamp say (so greatly be he

infatuate with the dangerous man) that the reading of a letter of Dr.

Shrapnel’s could do nothing but good to any reflect human creature:

he admit that as the lose pocket-book be address to Mr. Romfrey,

it might have be by mistake that he have open it, and read the

topmost letter lie open. but he press Rosamund to say whether that

one only have be read.



“only Dr. Shrapnel’s letter,” Rosamund affirm. “the letter from

Normandy be untouched by he.”



“untouched by anybody?”



“unopened, Nevil. you look incredulous.”



“not if I have your word, ma’am.”



he glance somewhat contemptuously at his uncle Everard’s anachronistic

notion of what be fair in war.



to prove to he Mr. Romfrey’s affectionate interest in his fortune,

Rosamund mention the overture which have be make to Colonel Halkett

for a nuptial alliance between the two house; and she say: “your

uncle Everard be completely win by your manly way of take his

opposition to you in Bevisham. he pay for Captain Baskelett, but you

and your fortune be near his heart, Nevil.”



Beauchamp hang silent. his first remark be, “yes, I want money. I must

have money.” by degree he seem to warm to some sense of gratitude.

“it be kind of the baron,” he say.



“he have a great affection for you, Nevil, though you know he spare no

one who choose to be antagonistic. all that be over. but do you not

second he, Nevil? you admire she? you be not adverse?”



Beauchamp signify the horrid intermixture of yes and no, frown in

pain of mind, and walk up and down. “there’s no live woman I admire

so much.”



“she have refuse the high match.”



“I hold she in every way incomparable.”



“she try to understand your political idea, if she cannot quite

sympathize with they, Nevil. and consider how hard it be for a young

English lady, breed in refinement, to understand such thing.”



“yes,” Beauchamp nod; yes. well, more’s the pity for I!”



“ah! Nevil, that fatal Renée!”



“Ma’am, I acquit you of any suspicion of your having read her letter in

this pocket-book. she wish I to marry. you would have see it

write here. she wish it.”



“fly, clipped wing!” murmur Rosamund, and purposely send a buzz into

her ear to shut out his extravagant talk of Renée’s friendly wish.



“how be it you woman will not believe in the sincerity of a woman!” he

exclaim.



“Nevil, I be not allude to the damage do to your election.”



“to my candidature, ma’am. you mean those rumour, those lie of the

enemy. tell I how I could suppose you be allude to they. you bring

they forward now to justify your charge of ‘fatal’ against she. she have

one fault; she want courage; she have none other, not one that be not

excuseable. we willn’t speak of France. what do her father say?”



“Colonel Halkett? I do not know. he and his daughter come here next

week, and the colonel will expect to meet you here. that do not look

like so positive an objection to you?”



“to I personally, no,” say Beauchamp. “but Mr. Romfrey have not tell

I that I be to meet they.”



“perhaps he have not think it worth while. it be not his way. he have

ask you to come. you and Miss Halkett will be leave to yourself. she

father assure Mr. Romfrey that he should not go beyond advise she.

his advice might not be exactly favourable to you at present, but if

you sue and she accept—and she would, I be convinced she would; she

be here with I, talk of you a whole afternoon, and I have

eye—then he would not oppose the match, and then I should see you

settle, the husband of the handsome wife and rich heiress in

England.”



a vision of Cecilia swam before he, gracious in stateliness.



two week back Renée’s expression of a wish that he would marry have

seem to he an idle sentence in a letter breathing of her own

intolerable situation. the marquis have be strike down by illness.

what if she be to be soon suddenly free? but Renée could not be

look to freedom, otherwise she never would have write the wish for

he to marry. she write perhaps hear temptation whisper; perhaps

wish to save herself and he by the aid of a tie that would bring

his honour into play and fix his loyalty. he remember Dr. Shrapnel’s

write word: “_rebellion against society and advocacy of humanity run

counter._” they have a strong effect on he than when he be ignorant

of his uncle Everard’s plan to match he with Cecilia. he take refuge

from they in the image of that beautiful desolate Renée, bear to be

belove, now waste, bad than trodden under foot—pervert; a life

that look to he for direction and resuscitation. she be as good as

dead in her marriage. it be impossible for he ever to think of Renée

without the surprising thrill of his enchantment with she, and tender

pity that draw she close to he by darken her brightness.



still a man may love his wife. a wife like Cecilia be not to be

imagine coldly. let the knot once be tie, it would not be regret,

could not be; hers be a character, and her a smile, firmly assure

he of that.



he tell Mr. Romfrey that he should be glad to meet Colonel Halkett and

Cecilia. business call he to Holdesbury. thence he betake himself to

Dr. Shrapnel’s cottage to say farewell to Jenny Denham previous to she

departure for Switzerland with her friend Clara Sherwin. she have never

see a snow-mountain, and it be pleasant to he to observe in her

eye, which he have know weighing and balance intellectual question

more than he quite like, a childlike effort to conjure in imagination

the glory of the Alps. she appear very happy, only a little anxious

about leave Dr. Shrapnel with no one to take care of he for a whole

month. Beauchamp promise he would run over to he from Holdesbury,

only an hour by rail, as often as he could. he envy she the sight of
