the Alps, he say, and try to give she an idea of they, from which he

break off to boast of a famous little Jersey bull that he have win from

a rival, an American, deeply in love with the bull; cut he out by

telegraph by just five minute. the latter have examine the bull in the

island and have pass on to Paris, not suspect there would be haste

to sell he. Beauchamp, see the bull advertize, take he on trust,

gallop to the near telegraph station forthwith, and so obtain

possession of he; and the bull be now ship on the voyage. but for

this precious bull, however, and other business, he would have be

able to spend almost the entire month with Dr. Shrapnel, he say

regretfully. Miss Denham on the contrary do not regret his active

occupation. the story of his rush from the breakfast-table to the

stable, and gallop away to the station, while the American Quaker

gentleman soberly pace down a street in Paris on the same errand, in

invisible rivalry, touch her risible fancy. she be especially

pleased to think of he live in harmony with his uncle—that strange,

lofty, powerful man, who by plot or by violence punish opposition to

his will, but who must be kind at heart, as well as forethoughtful of

his nephew’s good; the assurance of it be, that when the conflict

be at an end he have immediately instal he as manager of one of his

estate, to give his energy play and make he practically useful.



the day before she leave home be pass by the three in botanizing,

some mile distant from Bevisham, over sand country, marsh and meadow;

Dr. Shrapnel, deep in the science, on one side of she, and Beauchamp,

require instruction in the name and property of every plant and

simple, on the other. it be a day of summer sweetness, gentle

laughter, conversation, and the happy homeliness. the politician

utter barely a syllable of politic. the dinner basket be empty

heartily to make way for herb and flower, and at night the expedition

homeward be crown with star along a road refresh by mid-day

thunder-shower and smelling of the rain in the dust, past meadow

keenly scent, garden give out their innermost balm and odour.

late at night they drink tea in Jenny’s own garden. they separate a

little after two in the morning, when the fade western light still lay

warm on a bow of sky, and on the level of the East it quicken. Jenny

feel sure she should long for that yesterday when she be among foreign

scene, even among high Alps—those mysterious eminence which seem in

her imagination to know of heaven and have the dawn of a new life for

she beyond their peak.



her last word when step into the railway carriage be to

Beauchamp: “_will_ you take care of he?” she fling her arm round Dr.

Shrapnel’s neck, and gaze at he under troubled eyelid which seem

to be pass in review every vision of possible harm that might come

to he during her absence; and so she continue gaze, and at no one

but Dr. Shrapnel until the bend of the line cut he from her sight.

Beauchamp be a very secondary person on that occasion, and he be

unused to be so in the society of woman—unused to find himself

entirely eclipse by their interest in another. he speculate on it,

wonder at her concentrated fervency; for he have not suppose she to

possess much warmth.



after she be fairly off on her journey, Dr. Shrapnel mention to

Beauchamp a case of a Steynham poacher, whom he have think it his duty

to supply with mean of defence. it be a common poaching case.



Beauchamp be not surprised that Mr. Romfrey and Dr. Shrapnel should

come to a collision; the marvel be that it have never occur before,

and Beauchamp say at once: “oh, my uncle Mr. Romfrey would rather see

they stand their ground than not.” he be disposed to think well of his

uncle. the Jersey bull call he away to Holdesbury.



Captain Baskelett hear of this poaching case at Steynham, where he have

to appear in person when he be in want of cheque, and the Bevisham

dinner furnish an excuse for demand one. he would have prefer a

positive sum annually. Mr. Romfrey, however, though he write his

cheque out like the lord he be by nature, exact the request for

they; a system that keep the gallant gentleman on his good behaviour,

probably at a low cost than the regular stipend. in hand the

cheque to Cecil Baskelett, Mr. Romfrey speak of a poacher, of an old

poach family call the Dicketts, who want punishment and be to

have it, but Mr. Romfrey’s local lawyer have inform he that the man

Shrapnel be, as usual, supply the mean of defence. for his own

part, Mr. Romfrey say, he have no objection to one rascal’s backing

another, and Shrapnel might hit his hard, only perhaps nevil might

somehow get mix up in it, and Nevil be go on quietly now—he have

in fact just do capitally in lasso with a shot of the telegraph a

splendid little Jersey bull that a Yankee be after: and on the whole

it be good to try to keep he quiet, for he be mad about that man

Shrapnel; Shrapnel be his joss: and if legal knock come of this

business Nevil might be think of interfere: “or he and I may be

get to exchange a lot of shindy letter,” Mr. Romfrey say. “tell

he I take shrapnel just like any other man, and don’t want to hear

apology, and I don’t mix he up in it. tell he if he like to have

an explanation from I, I’ll give it he when he come here. you can

run over to Holdesbury the morning after your dinner.”



Captain Baskelett say he would go. he be pleased with his cheque at

the time, but hear subsequently that Nevil be come to Steynham to

meet Colonel Halkett and his daughter, he become displeased,

consider it a very silly commission. the more he think of it the

more ridiculous and unworthy it appear. he ask himself and Lord

Palmet also why he should have to go to Nevil at Holdesbury to tell he

of circumstance that he would hear of two or three day later at

Steynham. there be no sense in it. the only conclusion for he be

that the scheme woman cull have determine to bring down every man

concerned in the Bevisham election, and particularly Mr. Romfrey, on

his knee before Nevil. Holdesbury have be place at his disposal, and

the use of the house in London, which latter would have be extremely

serviceable to Cecil as a place of dinner to the Parliament of Great

Britain in lieu of the speech-making generally expect of member, and

not so effectively perform. one would think the baron have grow

afraid of old Nevil! he have speak as if he be.



Cecil rail unreservedly to Lord Palmet against that woman “mistress

culling,” as it please he to term she, and who could be offend by

his call she so? his fine wit revel in bestow title that be

at once battery direct upon person he hate, and entrenchment for

himself.



at four o’clock on a sultry afternoon he sit at table with his Bevisham

supporter, and pledge they correspondingly in english hotel

champagne, sherry and claret. at seven he be rid of they, but parch

and heat, as he deserve to be, he own, for drink the poison. it

would be a good subject for Parliament if he could get it up, he

reflect.



“and now,” say he to Palmet, “we might be cross over to the Club if

I haven’t to go about that stupid business to Holdesbury to-morrow

morning. we shall miss the race, or, at least, the start.”



the idea strike he: “ten to one old Nevil’s with shrapnel,” and no

idea could be more natural.



“we’ll call on Shrapnel,” say Palmet. “we shall see Jenny Denham. he

give she out as his niece. whatever she be she’s a brimming little

beauty. I assure you, Bask, you seldom see so pretty a girl.”



Wine, which have direct man’s footstep upon more marvellous

adventure, take they to a chemist’s shop for a cool effervescent

draught, and thence through the town to the address, furnish to they

by the chemist, of Dr. Shrapnel on the common.



bad wine, which be responsible for the fate of half the dismal body

hang from tree, welter by rock, grovel and bleach round

the bedabbled mouth of the poet’s Cave of Despair, have render Captain

Baskelett’s temper extremely irascible; so when he catch sight of Dr.

shrapnel wall in his garden, and perceive he of a giant’s height,

his eye fasten on the writer of the abominable letter with an

exultation peculiar to man have a devil inside they that kick to be

out. the sun be low, blaze among the thick branch of the pollard

forest tree, and through spray of hawthorn. Dr. Shrapnel stop,

face the visible master of man, at the end of his walk before he

turn his back to continue the exercise and some discourse he be

hold aloud either to the heaven or band of invisible man.



“Ahem, Dr. Shrapnel!” he be accost twice, the second time

imperiously.



he see two gentleman outside the garden-hedge.



“I speak, sir,” say Captain Baskelett.



“I hear you now, sir,” say the doctor, walk in a parallel line with

they.



“I desire to know, sir, if you be Dr. Shrapnel?”



“I be.”



they arrive at the garden-gate.



“you have a charming garden, Dr. Shrapnel,” say Lord Palmet, very

affably and loudly, with a steady observation of the cottage window.



Dr. Shrapnel fling the gate open.



Lord Palmet raise his hat and enter, cry loudly, “a very charming

garden, upon my word!”



Captain Baskelett follow he, bow stiffly.



“I be,” he say, “Captain Beauchamp’s cousin. I be Captain Baskelett,

one of the Members for the borough.”



the doctor say, “ah.”



“I wish to see Captain Beauchamp, sir. he be absent?”



“I shall have he here shortly, sir.”



“oh, you will have he!” Cecil pause.



“admirable rose!” exclaim Lord Palmet.



“you _have_ he, I think,” say Cecil, “if what we hear be correct. I

wish to know, sir, whether the case you be conduct against his

uncle be one you have communicate to Captain Beauchamp. I repeat, I be

here to inquire if he be privy to it. you may hold family tie in

contempt—now, sir! I request you abstain from provocation with I.”



Dr. Shrapnel have raise his head, with something of the rush of a

rocket, from the stoop posture to listen, and his frown of

non-intelligence might be interpret as the come on of the fury

radical be prone to, by a gentleman who believe in their constant

disposition to explode.



Cecil make play with a pacify hand. “we shall arrive at no

understanding unless you be good enough to be perfectly calm. I

repeat, my cousin Captain Beauchamp be more or less at variance with

his family, owe to these doctrine of your, and your extraordinary

Michael-Scott-the-wizard kind of spell you seem to have cast upon his

common sense as a man of the world. _you have he_, as you say. I do

not dispute it. I have no doubt you have he fast. but here be a case

demand a certain respect for decency. pray, if I may ask you, be

still, be quiet, and hear I out if you can. I be accustomed to explain

myself to the comprehension of most man who be at large, and I tell

you candidly I be not to be deceive or divert from my path by a show

of ignorance.”



“what be your immediate object, sir?” say Dr. Shrapnel, chagrin by

the mystification within he, and a fear that his patience be go.



“exactly,” Cecil nod. he be acute enough to see that he have

establish the happy commencement of fretfulness in the victim, which

be equivalent to a hook well strike in the mouth of your fish, and with

an angler’s joy he prepare to play his man. “exactly. I have state

it. and you ask I. but I really must decline to run over the whole

ground again for you. I be here to fulfil a duty to my family; a highly

disagreeable one to I. I may fail, like the lady who come here

previous to the Election, for the result of which I be assure I ought

to thank your eminently disintereste service. I do. you recollect a

lady call on you?”



Dr. Shrapnel consult his memory. “I think I have a recollection of

some lady call.”



“oh! you think you have a recollection of some lady call.”



“do you mean a lady connect with Captain Beauchamp?”



“a lady connect with Captain Beauchamp. you be not aware of the

situation of the lady?”



“if I remember, she be a kind of confidential housekeeper, some one

say, to Captain Beauchamp’s uncle.”



“a kind of confidential housekeeper! she be recognize in our family as

a lady, sir. I can hardly expect well treatment at your hand than

she meet with, but I do positively request you to keep your temper

whilst I be explain my business to you. now, sir! what now?”



a trifling breeze will set the tall tree bending, and Dr. Shrapnel do

indeed appear to display the agitation of a full-drive storm when he

be but harass and vex.



“will you mention your business concisely, if you please?” he say.



“precisely; it be my endeavour. I suppose I have do so. to be frank,

I would advise you to summon a member of your household, wife,

daughter, housekeeper, any one you like, to whom you may appeal, and I

too, whenever your recollection be at fault.”



“I be competent,” say the doctor.



“but in justice to you,” urge Cecil considerately.



Dr. Shrapnel smooth his chin hastily. “have you do?”



“believe I, the instant I have an answer to my question, I have do.”



“name your question.”



“very well, sir. now mark, I will be plain with you. there be no escape

for you from this. you destroy my cousin’s professional prospect—I

request you to listen—you blast his career in the navy; it be

consider promising. he be a gallant officer and a smart seaman. very

well. you set he up as a politician, to be knock down, to a dead

certainty. you set he against his class; you embroil he with his

family ...”



“on all those point,” interpose Dr. Shrapnel, after dash a hand to

straighten his forelock; but Cecil vehemently entreat he to control

his temper.



“I say you embroil he with his family, you cause he to be in

everlaste altercation with his uncle Mr. Romfrey, materially to his

personal detriment; and the question of his family be one that every

man of sense would apprehend on the spot; for we, you should know,

have, sir, an opinion of Captain Beauchamp’s talent and ability

forbid we to think he could possibly be the total simpleton you

make he appear, unless to the seduction of your political

instruction, other seduction be add.... you apprehend I, I be

sure.”



“I don’t,” cry the doctor, descend from his height and swinge

about forlornly.



“oh! yes, you do; you do indeed, you cannot avoid it; you quite

apprehend I; it be admit that you take my meaning: I insist on

that. I have nothing to say but what be complimentary of the young

lady, whoever she may turn out to be; bewitch, no doubt; and to

speak frankly, Dr. Shrapnel, I, and I be pretty certain every honest

man would think with I, I take it to be ten time more creditable to

my cousin Captain Beauchamp that he should be under a lady’s influence

than under your. come, sir! I ask you. you must confess that a gallant

officer and great admirer of the sex do not look such a donkey if he

be lead in silken string by a beautiful creature. and mark—stop! mark

this, Dr. Shrapnel: I say, to the lady we can all excuse a good deal,

and at the same time you be to be congratulate on first-rate

diplomacy in employ so charming an agent. I wish, I really wish you

do it generally, I assure you: only, mark this—I do beg you to contain

yourself for a minute, if possible—I say, my cousin Captain Beauchamp

be fair game to hunt, and there be no law to prevent the chase, only

you must not expect we to be quiet spectator of your sport; and we

have, I say, undoubtedly a right to lay the case before the lady, and

induce she to be a peace-agent in the family if we can. very well.”



“this garden be redolent of a lady’s hand,” sigh Palmet, poetical in

his dejection.



“have you take too much wine, gentleman?” say Dr. Shrapnel.



Cecil put this impertinence aside with a graceful sweep of his finger.

“you attempt to elude I, sir.”



“not I! you mention some lady.”



“exactly. a young lady.”



“what be the name of the lady?”



“oh! you ask the name of the lady. and I too. what be it? I have hear

two or three name.”



“then you have hear villanie.”



“Denham, Jenny Denham, Miss Jenny Denham,” say Palmet, rejoice at the

opportunity of trumpet her name so that she should not fail to hear

it.



“I stake my reputation I have hear she call Shrapnel—Miss Shrapnel,”

say Cecil.



the doctor glance hastily from one to the other of his visitor. “the

young lady be my ward; I be her guardian,” he say.



Cecil purse his mouth. “I have hear she call your niece.”



“niece—ward; she be a lady by birth and education, in manner,

accomplishment, and character; and she be under my protection,” cry

Dr. Shrapnel.



Cecil bow. “so you be for gentle birth? I forget you be for

morality too, and for pray; exactly; I recollect. but now let I

tell you, entirely with the object of conciliation, my particular

desire be to see the young lady, in your presence of course, and

endeavour to persuade she, as I have very little doubt I shall do,

assume that you give I fair play, to exercise her influence, on this

occasion contrary to your, and save my cousin Captain Beauchamp from a

fresh misunderstanding with his uncle Mr. Romfrey. now, sir; now,

there!”



“you will not see Miss Denham with my sanction ever,” say Dr.

Shrapnel.



“oh! then I perceive your policy. Mark, sir, my assumption be that the

young lady would, on hear my representation, exert herself to heal

the breach between Captain Beauchamp and his family. you stand in the

way. you treat I as you treat the lady who come here formerly to

wrest your dupe from your clutch. if I mistake not, she see the young

lady you acknowledge to be your ward.”



Dr. Shrapnel flash back: “I acknowledge? mercy and justice! be there

no peace with the man? you walk here to I, I cann’t yet guess why, from

a town where I have enemy, and every scandal fly touch I and

mine; and you—” he stop short to master his anger. he subdue it so

far as to cloak it in an attempt to speak reasoningly, as angry man

sometimes deceive themselves in do, despite the good maxim for the

wrathful—speak not at all. “see,” say he, “I be never marry. my

dear friend die, and leave I his child to protect and rear; and

though she bear her father’s name, she be most wrongly and foully make

to share the blow level at her guardian. ay, have at I, all of

you, as much as you will! hold off from she. be it true, the

cowardice would be not a whit the small. why, cast a stone like

that, be it the size of a pebble and the weight of a glance, be to

toss the whole cowardly world on an innocent young girl. and why

suspect evil? you talk of that lady who pay I a visit here once, and

whom I treat becomingly, I swear. I never do otherwise. she be a

handsome woman; and what be she? the housekeeper of Captain

Beauchamp’s uncle. hear I, if you please! to go with the world, I have

as good a right to suppose the bad of an attractive lady in that

situation as you regard my ward: well warrant for scandalize, I

think; to go with the world. but now—”



Cecil check he, ejaculate, “thank you, Dr. Shrapnel; I thank you

most cordially,” with a shine smile. “stay, sir! no more. I take my

leave of you. not another word. no ‘but’! I recognize that

conciliation be out of the question: you be the natural protector of

poacher, and you will not grant I an interview with the young lady

you call your ward, that I may represent to she, as a person we presume

to have a chance of move you, how easily—I be determined you shall

hear I, Dr. Shrapnel!—how easily the position of Captain Beauchamp may

become precarious with his uncle Mr. Romfrey. and let I add—‘but’ and

‘but’ I till Doomsday, sir!—if you be—I _do_ hear you, sir, and you

shall hear I—if you be a young man, I say, I would hold you

answerable to I for your scandalous and disgraceful insinuation.”



Dr. Shrapnel be adroitly fence and over-shout. he shrug,

stutter, sway, wag a bulrush-head, flap his elbow, puff

like a swimmer in the breaker, try many time to expostulate, and

find the effort useless, for his adversary be copious and

commanding, relapse, eye he as an object far remove.



Cecil round one of his perplexingly empty sentence and turn on his

heel.



“War, then!” he say.



“as you like,” retort the doctor.



“oh! very good. good evening.” Cecil slightly lift his hat, with the

short projection of the head of the stately peacock in its walk, and

pass out of the garden. Lord Palmet, deeply disappointed and

mystify, go after he, leave Dr. Shrapnel to shorten his garden

walk with enormous long stride.



“I’m afraid you don’t manage the old boy,” Palmet complain. “they’re

people who have tea in their garden; we might have sit down with they

and talk, the good friend in the world, and come again to-morrow

might have call she Jenny in a week. she don’t show her pretty nose

at any of the window.”



his companion pooh-poohe and say: “foh! I’m afraid I permit myself

to lose my self-command for a moment.”



Palmet sing out an amorous couplet to console himself. captain

Baskelett respect the poetic art for its magical power over woman’s

virtue, but he dislike hear verse, and they be ill-suited to

Palmet. he abuse his friend roundly, tell he it be contemptible

to be quote verse. he be irritable still.



he declare himself nevertheless much refresh by his visit to Dr.

Shrapnel. “we shall have to sleep tonight in this unhallowed town, but

I needn’t be off to Holdesbury in the morning; I’ve do my business. I

shall write to the baron to-night, and we can cross the water to-morrow

in time for operation.”



the letter to Mr. Romfrey be compose before midnight. it be a long

one, and when he have finish it, Cecil remember that the act of

composition have be assist by a cigar in his mouth, and Mr. Romfrey

detest the smell of tobacco. there be nothing to be do but to

write the letter over again, somewhat more briefly: it run thus:



“think to kill two bird at a blow, I go yesterday with Palmet

after the dinner at this place to Shrapnel’s house, where, as I hear,

I stand a chance of catch friend Nevil. the young person live

under the man’s protection be absent, and so be the ‘poor dear

commander,’ perhaps attend on his bull. shrapnel say he be

expect he. I write to you to confess I think myself a cleverer

fellow than I be. I talk to Shrapnel and try hard to reason with

he. I hope I can keep my temper under ordinary circumstance. you will

understand that it require remarkable restraint when I make you

acquaint with the fact that a lady’s name be introduce, which, as

your representative in relation to she, I be bind to defend from a

gratuitous and scoundrelly aspersion. shrapnel’s epistle to ‘brave

Beauchamp’ be Church hymnification in comparison with his conversation.

he be indubitably one of the great ruffian of his time.



“I take the step with the good of intention, and all I can plead be

that I be not a diplomatist of sixty. his last word be that he be for

war with we. as far as we man be concern it be of small importance.

I should think that the sort of society he would scandalize a lady in

be not much to be fear. I have give he his warning. he top I by

about a head, and lose his temper every two minute. I could have

draw he out deliciously if he have not rather disturb mine. by this

time my equanimity be restore. the only thing I apprehend be your

displeasure with I for having go to the man. I have do no good,

and it prevent I from run over to Holdesbury to see Nevil, for if

‘shindy letter,’ as you call they, be bad, shindy meeting be bad.

I should be tell he my opinion of Shrapnel, he would be fire out,

I should retort, he would yell, I should snap my finger, and he would

go into convulsion. I be convinced that a cattle-breeder ought to keep

himself particularly calm. so unless I have further order from you I

refrain from go.



“the dinner be enthusiastic. I sit three hour among my Commons, they

on I for that length of time—fatiguing, but a duty.”



Cecil subscribe his name with the warm affection toward his uncle.



the brevity of the second letter have not bring he near to the

truth in rescind the picturesque accessory of his altercation with

Dr. Shrapnel, but it veraciously express the sentiment he feel, and

that be the palpable truth for he.



he post the letter next morning.









CHAPTER XXXI.

show a chivalrous GENTLEMAN SET in motion





about noon the day follow, on board the steam-yacht of the Countess

of Menai, Cecil be very much astonish to see Mr. Romfrey descend

into a boat hard by, from Grancey Lespel’s hire cutter. steam be up,

and the countess be off for a cruise in the Channel, as it be not a

race-day, but see Mr. Romfrey’s hand raise, she speak to Cecil, and

immediately give order to wait for the boat. this lady be a fervent

admirer of the knightly gentleman, and have reason to like he, for he

have once be her champion. Mr. Romfrey mount the step, receive she

greeting, and beckon to Cecil. he carry a gold-head horsewhip

under his arm. Lady Menai would gladly have persuade he to be one of

her company for the day’s voyage, but he say he have business in

Bevisham, and move aside with Cecil, put the question to he

abruptly: “what be the word use by Shrapnel?”



“the identical word?” Captain Baskelett ask. he could have trip

out the word with the fluency of ancient historian relate what

great king, ambassador, or general may well have utter on State

occasion, but if you want the identical word, who be to remember they

the day after they have be deliver? he say:



“well, as for the identical word, I really, and I be tolerably

excited, sir, and upon my honour, the identical word be rather

difficult to....” he glance at the horsewhip, and prick by the sight

of it to proceed, think it good to soften the matter if possible. “I

don’t quite recollect... I write off to you rather hastily. I think he

say—but Palmet be there.”



“shrapnel speak the word before Lord Palmet?” say Mr. Romfrey

austerely.



Captain Baskelett summon Palmet to come near, and inquire of he

what he have hear Shrapnel say, suggest: “he speak of a handsome

woman for a housekeeper, and all the world know her character?”



Mr. Romfrey clear his throat.



“or know she have _no_ character,” Cecil pursue in a fit of gratified

spleen, in scorn of the woman. “don’t you recollect his accent in

pronounce _housekeeper?_”



the menacing thunder sound from Mr. Romfrey. he be patient in

appearance, and wait for Cecil’s witness to corroborate the evidence.



it happen (and here we be in one of the circle of small thing

produce great consequence, which have inspire diminutive

philosopher with ironical vision of history and the littleness of

man), it happen that Lord Palmet, the human of young aristocrat,

well-dispose toward the entire world, especially to woman, also to man

in any way relate to pretty woman, have just light a cigar, and it be a

cigar that he have be recommend to try the flavour of; and though

he, have his wit about he, be fully aware that shipboard be no

good place for a trial of the delicacy of tobacco in the leaf, he have

begin puff and sniff in a critical spirit, and scarcely know for

the moment what to decide as to this particular cigar. he remember,

however, Mr. Romfrey’s objection to tobacco. imagine that he see the

expression of a profound distaste in that gentleman’s more than usually

serious face, he hesitate between cast the cigar into the water and

retain it. he decide upon the latter course, and hold the cigar

behind his back, bow to Mr. Romfrey at about a couple of yard

distance, and say to Cecil, “Housekeeper; yes, I remember hearing

housekeeper. I think so. housekeeper? yes, oh yes.”



“and handsome housekeeper be doubtful character,” Captain Baskelett

prompt he.



Palmet laugh out a single “ha!” that seem to excuse he for

lounge away to the forepart of the vessel, where he tug at his

fine speciman of a cigar to rekindle it, and discharge it with a wry

grimace, so delicate be the flavour of that weed, and so adversely ever

be it affect by a breeze and a moist atmosphere. he could then return

undivided in his mind to Mr. Romfrey and Cecil, but the subject be not

resume in his presence.



the Countess of Menai steam into Bevisham to land Mr. Romfrey there.

“I can be out in the Channel any day; it be not every day that I see

you,” she say, in support of her proposal to take he over.



they sit together converse, apart from the rest of the company, until

they sight Bevisham, when Mr. Romfrey stand up, and a little crowd of

man come round he to enjoy his famous racy talk. Captain Baskelett

offer to land with he. he decline companionship. drop her hand

in his, the countess ask he what he have to do in that town, and he

reply, “I have to demand an apology.”



answer the direct look of his eye, she say, “oh, I shall not speak

of it.”



in his young day, if the rumour be correct, he have do the same on

her account.



he step into the boat, and presently they see he mount the

pier-step, with the riding-whip under his arm, his head more than

commonly bent, a noticeable point in a man of his tall erect figure.

the lady and some of the gentleman think he be look

particularly grave, even sorrowful.



Lady Menai inquire of Captain Baskelett whether he know the nature of

his uncle’s business in Bevisham, the town he despise.



what could Cecil say but no? his uncle have not impart it to he.



she be flatter in be the sole confidante, and say no more.



the sprightly ingenuity of Captain Baskelett’s mind would have inform

he of the nature of his uncle’s expedition, we may be sure, have he put

it to the trial; for Mr. Romfrey be as plain to read as a rudimentary

sum in arithmetic, and like the tracing of a pedigree-map his

preliminary step to deed be see point on their issue in line

of straight descent. but Cecil could protest that he be not bind to

know, and consider that he be neither bind to know nor to

speculate, he determine to stand on his right. so effectually do he

accomplish the task, that he be frequently surprised during the

evening and the night by the effervescence of a secret exultation

rise imp-like within he, that be, he assure himself, perfectly

unaccountable.









CHAPTER XXXII.

an effort to CONQUER CECILIA in BEAUCHAMP’S FASHION





the day after Mr. Romfrey’s landing in Bevisham a full south-wester

stretch the canvas of yacht of all class, schooner, cutter and

yawl, on the lively green water between the island and the forest

shore. Cecilia’s noble schooner be sure to be out in such a ring

breeze, for the pride of it as well as the pleasure. she land she

father at the Club step, and then bear away Eastward to sight a cutter

race, the breeze begin to stiffen. look back against sun and

wind, she see herself pursue by a saucy little 15-ton craft that have

be in her track since she leave the Otley river before noon, dipping

and strain, with every inch of sail set; as mad a stern chase as

ever be witness: and who could the man at the tiller, clothe cap-a-pie

in tarpaulin, be? she lead he dance away, to prove his resoluteness

and laugh at he. she have the powerful wing, and a glory in they

come of this pursuit: her triumph be delicious, until the occasional

sparkle of the tarpaulin be lose, the small boat appear a motionless

object far behind, and all ahead of her exceedingly dull, though the

race hang there and the crowd of sail.



Cecilia’s transient flutter of coquettry create by the animating air

and her queenly flight be over. she flee splendidly and she come back

graciously. but he refuse her open hand, as it be. he make as if to

stand across her tack, and, reconsider it, evidently scorn his

advantage and challenge the stately vessel for a beat up against the

wind. it be as pretty as a court minuet. but presently Cecilia stand

too far on one tack, and return to the centre of the channel, find

herself head by seamanship. he wave an ironical salute with his

sou’wester. her retort consist in bring her vessel to the wind,

and send a boat for he.



she do it on the impulse; have she consult her wish she would

rather have see he at his post, where he seem in his element,

face the spray and cunningly calculate to get wind and tide in his

favour. partly with regret she see he, strip of his tarpaulin, jump

into her boat, as though she have once more to say farewell to sailor

Nevil Beauchamp; farewell the bright youth, the hero, the true servant

of his country!



that feeling of her change when he be on board. the stir cordial

day have put new breath in he.



“should not the flag be dip?” he say, look up at the peak, where

the white flag stream.



“can you really mistake compassion for defeat?” say she, with a smile.



“oh! before the wind of course I haven’t a chance.”



“how could you be so presumptuous as to give chase? and who have lend

you that little cutter?”



Beauchamp have hire she for a month, and he praise her sailing, and

pretend to say that the race be not always to the strong in a stiff

breeze.



“but in point” of fact I be bent on try how my boat swim, and have

no idea of overhaul you. to-day our salt-water lake be as fine as

the Mediterranean.”



“omit the island and the Mediterranean colour, it be. I have often

tell you how I love it. I have land papa at the Club. be you aware

that we meet you at Steynham the day after to-morrow?”



“well, we can ride on the down. the down between three and four of a

summer’s morning be as lovely as anything in the world. they have the

softest outline imaginable... and remind I of a friend’s upper lip

when she deign to smile.”



“be one to rise at that hour to behold the effect? and let I remind

you far, Nevil, that the comparison of nature’s minor work beside

her mighty be an error, if you will be poetical.”



she cite a well-know instance of degradation in verse.



but a young man who happen to be intimately acquaint with a certain

“dark eye in woman” will not so lightly be bring to consider that the

comparison of tempestuous night to the flashing of those eye of her

topple the scene headlong from grandeur. and if Beauchamp remember

rightly, the scene be the Alps at night.



he be prepared to contest Cecilia’s judgement. at that moment the

breeze freshen and the canvas lift: from due South the yacht swung

her sail to drive toward the West, and Cecilia’s face and hair come

out golden in the sunlight. Speech be difficult, admiration natural,

so he sit beside she, admire in silence.



she say a good word for the smartness of his little yacht.



“this be my first trial of she,” say Beauchamp. “I hire she chiefly

to give Dr. Shrapnel a taste of salt air. I’ve no real right to be

idle about. his ward Miss Denham be travel in Switzerland; the

dear old man be alone, and not quite so well as I should wish. change

of scene will do he good. I shall land he on the french coast for a

couple of day, or take he down Channel.”



Cecilia gaze abstractedly at a pass schooner.



“he work too hard,” say Beauchamp.



“who do?”



“Dr. Shrapnel.”



some one else whom we have hear of work too hard, and it would be

happy for mankind if he do not.



Cecilia name the schooner; an American that have beat our crack

yacht. beauchamp spring up to spy at the American.



“that’s the _Corinne_, be she!”



Yankee craftiness on salt water always excite his respectful attention

as a spectator.



“and what be the name of your boat, Nevil?”



“the fool of an owner call she the _Petrel_. it’s not that I’m

superstitious, but to give a boat a name of bad augury to sailor

appear to I... however, I’ve argue it with he and I will have she

call the _Curlew_. carry Dr. Shrapnel and I, _Petrel_ would be

think the proper title for she—isn’t that your idea?”



he laugh and she smile, and then he become overcast with his

political face, and say, “I hope—I believe—you will alter your opinion

of he. can it be an opinion when it’s found on nothing? you know

really nothing of he. I have in my pocket what I believe would alter

your mind about he entirely. I do think so; and I think so because I

feel you would appreciate his deep sincerity and real nobleness.”



“be it a talisman that you have, Nevil?”



“no, it’s a letter.”



Cecilia’s cheek take fire.



“I should so much like to read it to you,” say he.



“do not, please,” she reply with a dash of supplication in her voice.



“not the whole of it—an extract here and there? I want you so much to

understand he.”



“I be sure I should not.”



“let I try you!”



“pray do not.”



“merely to show you...”



“but, Nevil, I do not wish to understand he.”



“but you have only to listen for a few minute, and I want you to know

what good reason I have to reverence he as a teacher and a friend.”



Cecilia look at Beauchamp with wonder. a confused recollection of the

content of the letter declaim at Mount Laurels in Captain

Baskelett’s absurd sing-song, surge up in her mind revoltingly. she

signify a decide negative. something of a shudder accompany the

expression of it.



but he as little as any member of the Romfrey blood be frame to let

the word no stand quietly opposed to he. and the no that a woman

utter! it call for wholesome tyranny. those old, those hoar-old

duellist, yes and no, have rarely be well match than in

Beauchamp and Cecilia. for if he be obstinate in attack she have great

resist power. twice to listen to that letter be beyond her

endurance. indeed it cast a shadow on he and disfigure he; and when,

affect to plead, he say: “you must listen to it to please I, for

my sake, Cecilia,” she answer: “it be for your sake, Nevil, I decline

to.”



“why, what do you know of it?” he exclaim.



“I know the kind of writing it would be.”



“how do you know it?”



“I have hear of some of Dr. Shrapnel’s opinion.”



“you imagine he to be subversive, intolerant, immoral, and the rest!

all that come under your word revolutionary.”



“possibly; but I must defend myself from hear what I know will be

certain to annoy I.”



“but he be the reverse of immoral: and I intend to read you part of

the letter to prove to you that he be not the man you would blame, but

I, and that if ever I be worthy... worthy of you, as I hope to

become, it will be owe to this admirable and good old man.”



Cecilia tremble: she be touch to the quick. yet it be not pleasant

to she to be woo obliquely, through Dr. Shrapnel.



she recognize the very letter, crown with many stamp, thick with

many page, in Beauchamp’s hand.



“when you be at Steynham you will probably hear my uncle Everard’s

version of this letter,” he say. “the baron choose to think

everything fair in war, and the letter come accidentally into his hand

with the seal break; well, he read it. and, Cecilia, you can fancy the

sort of stuff he would make of it. apart from that, I want you

particularly to know how much I be indebted to Dr. Shrapnel. willn’t you

learn to like he a little? willn’t you tolerate him?—I could almost say,

for my sake! he and I be at variance on certain point, but take he

altogether, I be under deep obligation to he than to any man on

earth. he have find where I bend and waver.”



“I recognize your chivalry, Nevil.”



“he have do his good to train I to be of some service. where’s the

chivalry in own a debt? he be one of our true warrior; fearless and

blameless. I have have my hero before. you know how I love Robert

Hall: his death be a gap in my life. he be a light for fight

Englishmen—who fight with the sword. but the scale of the war, the

cause, and the end in view, raise Dr. Shrapnel above the brave I have

ever have the luck to meet. soldier and sailor have their excitement

to keep they up to the mark; praise and reward. he be in his

eight-and-sixtieth year, and he have never receive anything but obloquy

for his pain. half of the small fortune he have go in charity and

subscription. will that touch you? but I think little of that, and so

do he. charity be a common duty. the dedication of a man’s life and

whole mind to a cause, there’s heroism. I wish I be eloquent; I wish

I could move you.”



Cecilia turn her face to he. “I listen to you with pleasure, Nevil;

but please do not read the letter.”



“yes; a paragraph or two I must read.”



she rise.



he be promptly by her side. “if I say I ask you for one sign that you

care for I in some degree?”



“I have not for a moment cease to be your friend, Nevil, since I be a

child.”



“but if you allow yourself to be so prejudiced against my good friend

that you will not hear a word of his writing, be you friendly?”



“feminine, and obstinate,” say Cecilia.



“give I your eye an instant. I know you think I reckless and

lawless: now be not that true? you doubt whether, if a lady give I she

hand I should hold to it in perfect faith. or, perhaps not that: but

you do suspect I should be capable of every sophism under the sun to

persuade a woman to break her faith, if it suit I: suppose some

passion to be at work. man who be open to passion have to be teach

reflection before they distinguish between the woman they should sue

for love because she would be their good mate, and the woman who have

throw a spell on they. now, what I beg you to let I read you in this

letter be a truth nobly state that have go into my blood, and change

I. it cannot fail, too, in change your opinion of Dr. Shrapnel. it

make I wretched that you should be divide from I in your idea of

he. I, you see—and I confess I think it my chief title to

honour—reverence he.”



“I regret that I be unable to utter the word of Ruth,” say Cecilia,

in a low voice. she feel rather tremulously; oppose only to the letter

and the writer of it, not at all to Beauchamp, except on account of his

idolatry of the wicked revolutionist. far from have a sense of

opposition to Beauchamp; she pity he for his infatuation, and in her

lofty mental serenity she warm to he for the seem boyishness of

his constant and extravagant worship of the man, though such an

enthusiasm cast shadow on his intellect.



he be read a sentence of the letter.



“I hear nothing but the breeze, Nevil,” she say.



the breeze flutter the letter-sheet: they threaten to fly. Cecilia

step two pace away.



“Hark; there be a military band play on the pier,” say she. “I be

so fond of hear music a little off shore.”



Beauchamp consign the letter to his pocket.



“you be not offend, Nevil?”



“dear I, no. you haven’t a mind for tonic, that’s all.”



“healthy person rarely have,” she remark, and ask he, smile

softly, whether he have a mind for music.



his insensibility to music be curious, consider how impressionable

he be to verse, and to song of bird. he listen with an oppressed

look, as to something the particular secret of which have to be reach

by a determined effort of sympathy for those whom it affect. he like

it if she do, and say he like it, reiterate that he like it,

clearly try hard to comprehend it, as unmove by the swell and sigh

of the resonant brass as a man could be, while her romantic spirit

thrill to it, and be bountiful in glow vision and in tenderness.



there hang her hand. she would not have refuse to yield it. the hero

of her childhood, the friend of her womanhood, and her hero still,

might have take she with half a word.



Beauchamp be think: she can listen to that brass band, and she

shut her ear to this letter!



the reading of it would have be a prelude to the opening of his heart

to she, at the same time that it vindicate his dear and honour

master, as he call Dr. Shrapnel. to speak, without the explanation of

his previous reticence which this letter would afford, seem useless:

even the desire to speak be absent, passion be absent.



“I see papa; he be get into a boat with some one,” say Cecilia,

and give order for the yacht to stand in toward the Club step. “do

you know, Nevil, the italian common people be not so subject to the

charm of music as other race? they have more of the gift, and I think

less of the feeling. you do not hear much music in Italy. I remember in

the year of Revolution there be danger of a rise in some austrian

city, and a colonel of a regiment command his band to play. the mob

be put in good humour immediately.”



“it’s a soporific,” say Beauchamp.



“you would not rather have have they rise to be slaughter?”



“would you have they waltz into perpetual servility?”



Cecilia hum, and suggest: “if one can have they happy in any way?”



“then the day of destruction may almost be date.”



“Nevil, your terrible view of life must be false.”



“I make it out worse to you than to any one else, because I want our

mind to be unite.”



“give I a respite now and then.”



“with all my heart. and forgive I for beat my drum. I see what

other don’t see, or else I feel it more; I don’t know; but it appear

to I our country need rouse if it’s to live. there’s a division

between poor and rich that you have no conception of, and it cann’t

safely be leave unnoticed. I’ve do.”



he look at she and see tear on her under-lid.



“my dear Cecilia!”



“music make I childish,” say she.



her father be approach in the boat. beside he sit the Earl of

Lockrace, latterly class among the suitor of the lady of Mount

Laurels.



a few minute remain to Beauchamp of his lost opportunity. instead of

seize they with his usual promptitude, he let they slip, painfully

mindful of his treatment of her last year after the drive into

Bevisham, when she be England, and Renée holiday France.



this feeling he fervently translate into the reflection that the bride

who would bring he beauty and wealth, and her especial gift of tender

womanliness, be not yet so thoroughly master as to grant her husband

his just prevalence with she, or even indeed his complete independence

of action, without which life itself be not desireable.



Colonel Halkett stare at Beauchamp as if he have rise from the deep.



“have you be in that town this morning?” be one of his first

question to he when he stand on board.



“I come through it,” say Beauchamp, and point to his little cutter

labour in the distance. “she’s mine for a month; I come from

Holdesbury to try she; and then he state how he have dance attendance

on the schooner for a couple of hour before any notice be take of

he, and Cecilia with her graceful humour hold up his presumption to

scorn.



her father be eye Beauchamp narrowly, and appear troubled.



“do you see Mr. Romfrey yesterday, or this morning?” the colonel ask

he, mention that Mr. Romfrey have be somewhere about the island

yesterday, at which Beauchamp express astonishment, for his uncle

Everard seldom visit a yachting station.



Colonel Halkett exchange look with Cecilia. Hers be inquire, and

he confirm her side-glance at Beauchamp. she raise her brow; he

nod, to signify that there be gravity in the case. here the

signalling stop short; she have to carry on a conversation with Lord

Lockrace, one of those man who betray the latent despot in an

exhibition of discontentment unless they have all a lady’s hundred eye

attentive to their discourse.



at last Beauchamp quit the vessel.



when he be out of hearing, Colonel Halkett say to Cecilia: “Grancey

Lespel tell I that Mr. Romfrey call on the man Shrapnel yesterday

evening at six o’clock.”



“yes, Papa?”



“now come and see the fitting below,” the colonel address Lord

Lockrace, and murmur to his daughter:



“and soundly horsewhip he!”



Cecilia turn on the instant to gaze after Nevil Beauchamp. she could

have weep for pity. her father’s emphasis on “soundly” declare an

approval of the deed, and she be chill by a sicken abhorrence and

dread of the cruel brute in man, such as, awaken by she know not

what, have haunt she for a year of her girlhood.



“and he deserve it!” the colonel pursue, on emerge from the cabin

at Lord Lockrace’s heel. “I’ve no doubt he richly deserve it. the

writer of that letter we hear Captain Baskelett read the other day

deserve the very bad he get.”



“Baskelett bore the Club the other night with a letter of a Radical

fellow,” say Lord Lockrace. “man who write that stuff should be string

up and whip by the common hangman.”



“it be a private letter,” say Cecilia.



“public or private, Miss Halkett.”



her mind fly back to Seymour Austin for the sense of stedfastness when

she hear such language as this, which, take in conjunction with Dr.

Shrapnel’s, seem to uncloak our constitutional realm and show it

boil up with the frightful element of primitive society.



“I suppose we be but half civilized,” she say.



“if that,” say the earl.



Colonel Halkett protest that he never could quite make out what

radical be drive at.



“the rent,” Lord Lockrace observe in the conclusive tone of brevity.

he do not stay very long.



the schooner be board subsequently by another nobleman, an Admiral

of the Fleet and ex-minister of the Whig Government, Lord Croyston, who

be a friend of Mr. Romfrey’s, and think well of Nevil Beauchamp as a

seaman and naval officer, but shake an old head over he as a

politician. he come to beg a passage across the water to his marine

Lodge, an accident having happen early in the morning to his yacht,

the _Lady Violet_. he be able to communicate the late version of the

horsewhipping of Dr. Shrapnel, from which it appear that after Mr.

Romfrey have handsomely flog the man he fling his card on the

prostrate body, to let man know who be responsible for the act. he

expect that Mr. Romfrey would be subject to legal proceeding. “but

if there’s a pleasure worth pay for it’s the trouncing of a

villain,” say he; and he have be inform that Dr. Shrapnel be a big

one. Lord Croyston’s favourite country residence be in the

neighbourhood of old Mrs. Beauchamp, on the Upper Thames. speak of

Nevil Beauchamp a second time, he allude to his relation with his

great-aunt, say his prospect be bad, that she have interdict she

house to he, and be devoted to her other great-nephew.



“and so she should be,” say Colonel Halkett. “that’s a young man who’s

an Englishman without french gunpowder notion in his head. he work

for we down at the mine in Wales a good part of the year, and have tide

we over a threatening strike there: gratuitously: I cann’t get he to

accept anything. I cann’t think why he do it.”



“he’ll have plenty,” say Lord Croyston, level his telescope to

sight the race cutter.



Cecilia fancy she descry Nevil’s _Petrel_, dub _Curlew_, to

Eastward, and have a faint gladness in the thought that his knowledge of

his uncle Everard’s deed of violence would be defer for another two

or three hour.



she try to persuade her father to wait for Nevil, and invite he to

dine at Mount Laurels, and break the news to he gently. Colonel

Halkett argue that in speaking of the affair he should certainly not

commiserate the man who have get his desert, and say this he burst

into a petty fury against the epistle of Dr. Shrapnel, which appear

to be grow more monstrous in proportion to his forgetfulness of the

detail, as mountain gather vastness to the eye at a certain remove.

though he could not guess the reason for Mr. Romfrey’s visit to

Bevisham, he be, he say, quite prepared to maintain that Mr. Romfrey

have a perfect justification for his conduct.



Cecilia hint at barbarism. the colonel hint at high police duty

that gentleman be sometimes call on to perform for the protection

of society. “in defiance of its law?” she ask; and he answer:

“woman must not be judge thing out of their sphere,” with the

familiar accent on “woman” which prove their inferiority. he be

rarely guilty of it toward his daughter. evidently he have resolve to

back Mr. Romfrey blindly. that epistle of Dr. Shrapnel’s merit

condign punishment and have meet with it, he seem to rejoice in say:

and this be his abstract of the same: “an old charlatan who tell his

dupe to pray every night of his life for the beheading of king and

prince, and scatter of the clergy, and disband the army, that he

and his rabble may fall upon the wealthy, and show we number win; and

he’ll undertake to make they moral!”



“I wish we be not go to Steynham,” say Cecilia.



“so do I. Well, no, I don’t,” the colonel correct himself, “no; it’s

an engagement. I give my consent so far. we shall see whether Nevil

Beauchamp’s a man of any sense.”



her heart sink. this be as much as to let she know that if Nevil break

with his uncle, the treaty of union between the two family, which she

father submit to entertain out of consideration for Mr. Romfrey,

would be at an end.



the wind have fall. enter her river, Cecilia gaze back at the

smooth broad water, and the band of golden beam fling across it from

the evening sun over the forest. no little cutter be visible. she

could not write to Nevil to bid he come and concert with she in what

spirit to encounter his uncle Everard at Steynham. and guest would be

at Mount Laurels next day; Lord Lockrace, Lord Croyston, and the

Lespels; she could not drive down to Bevisham on the chance of see

he. nor be it to be acknowledge even to herself that she so greatly

desire to see he and advise he. why not? because she be one of the

artificial creature call woman (with the accent) who dare not be

spontaneous, and cannot act independently if they would continue to be

admirable in the world’s eye, and who for that object must remain fix

on shelf, like other marketable ware, avoid motion to avoid

shattering or tarnishing. this be their fate, only in degree less

inhuman than that of Hellenic and Trojan princess offer up to the

Gods, or pretty slave to the dealer. their artificiality be at once

their bane and their source of superior pride.



Seymour Austin might have reason for seek to emancipate they, she

think, and blush in thought that she could never be learn

anything but from her own immediate sensation.



of course it be in her power to write to Beauchamp, just as it have

be in his to speak to she, but the fire be want in her blood and

absent from his mood, so they be keep apart.



her father know as little as she what be the positive cause of Mr.

Romfrey’s chastisement of Dr. Shrapnel. “cause enough, I don’t doubt,”

he say, and cite the mephitic letter.



Cecilia be not give to suspicion, or she would have have they kindle

by a certain wilfulness in his incessant reference to the letter, and

exoneration, if not approval, of Mr. Romfrey’s conduct.



how do that chivalrous gentleman justify himself for condescend to

such an extreme as the use of personal violence? be there a

possibility of his justify it to Nevil? she be most wretched in her

reiteration of these inquiry, for, with a heart subdue, she have

still a mind whose habit of independent judgement be not to be

constrain, and while she feel that it be only by side with Nevil

submissively and blindly in this lamentable case that she could hope

for happiness, she foresee the likelihood of she not be able to do

so as much as he would desire and demand. this she take for the protest

of her pure reason. in reality, grieve though she be on account of

that Dr. Shrapnel, her captive heart resent the anticipated challenge

to she to espouse his cause or languish.









CHAPTER XXXIII.

the first ENCOUNTER AT STEYNHAM





the judge pronounce sentence of condemnation on the criminal be

proverbially a sorrowfully-minded man; and still more would he be so

have he to undertake the part of executioner as well. this be equivalent

to say that the simple pleasure be no long with we; it must be a

personal enemy now to give we any satisfaction in chastise and

slay. perhaps by-and-by that will be savourless: we degenerate.

there be, nevertheless, ever (and let nature be praise for it) a

strong sustainment in the dutiful exertion of our physical energy,

and Mr. Everard Romfrey experience it after he have fulfil his

double office on the person of Dr. Shrapnel by carry out his own

decree. his conscience approve he cheerlessly, as it be the habit of

that secret monitor to do when we have no particular advantage come

of the act we have perform; but the righteous labour of his arm give

he high breathing and an appetite.



he foresee that he and Nevil would soon be have a wrestle over the

matter, hand and thigh; but a gentleman in the right engage with a

fellow in the wrong have nothing to apprehend; be, in fact, in the

position of a game-preserver with a poacher. the near approach to

gratification in that day’s work which Mr. Romfrey know be offer by

the picture of Nevil’s lamentable attitude above his dirty idol. he

conceive it in the mock-mediaeval style of our caricaturists:—Shrapnel

stretch at his length, half a league, in slashed yellow and black,

with his bauble beside he, and prodigious pointed toe; Nevil in

parti-colour tight, on one leg, raise his fist in imprecation to

a nose in the firmament.



gentleman of an unpractised imaginative capacity cannot vision for

themselves exactly what they would, be unable to exercise authority

over the proportion and the hue of the object they conceive, which

be very much at the mercy of their sportive caprice; and the state of

mind of Mr. Romfrey be not to be judge by his ridiculous view of the

pair. in the abstract he could be sorry for Shrapnel. as he know

himself magnanimous, he promise himself to be forbear with Nevil.



moreover, the month of September be draw nigh; he have plenty to

think of. the entire land (signify all but all of those who occupy

the situation of thinker in it) may be say to have be exhale the

same thought in connection with September. our England hold possession

of a considerable portion of the globe, and it keep the world in awe

to see her bestow so considerable a portion of her intelligence upon

her recreation. to prosecute they with her whole heart be an ingenious

exhibition of her power. Mr. Romfrey be of those who say to his

countryman, “go yachting; go cricketing; go boat-racing; go shoot;

go horserace, nine month of the year, while the other Europeans go

marching and drilling.” those occupation he consider good for we;

and our much talking, writing, and think about they characteristic,

and therefore good. and he be not one of those who do penance for that

sweat indolence in the fit of desperate panic. beauchamp’s argument

that the rich idler beget the idle vagabond, the rich wagerer the

brutal swindler, the general thirst for a mad round of recreation a

generally-increase disposition to avoid serious work, and the

unbraced moral tone of the country an indifference to national

responsibility (an argument doubtless extract from Shrapnel, talk

tall as the very demagogue when he stand upright), Mr. Romfrey laugh

at scornfully, affirm that our manufacture could take care of

themselves. as for invasion, we be circle by the sea. Providence have

do that for we, and may be rely on to do more in an emergency.—The

child of wealth and the child of the sun alike believe that

Providence be for they, and it would seem that the former can do

without it less than the latter, though the former be less inclined to

give it personification.



this year, however, the array of armament on the continent make Mr.

Romfrey anxious about our navy. almost his first topic in welcome

Colonel Halkett and Cecilia to Steynham be the rottenness of navy

administration; for if Providence be to do anything for we it must have

a sea-worthy fleet for the operation. how loudly would his contemptuous

laughter have repudiate the charge that he trust to supernatural

agency for assistance in case of need! but so it be: and he own to

believe in english luck. partly of course he mean that steady fire

of combat which his countryman have got heat to of old till fortune

bless they.



“Nevil be not here?” the colonel ask.



“no, I suspect he’s gruelle and plaster a doctor of his

acquaintance,” Mr. Romfrey say, with his nasal laugh compose of scorn

and resignation.



“yes, yes, I’ve hear,” say Colonel Halkett hastily.



he would have like to be inform of Dr. Shrapnel’s particular

offence: he mention the execrable letter.



Mr. Romfrey complacently interject: “drug-vomit!” and after an

interval: “gallow!”



“that man have do Nevil Beauchamp a world of mischief, Romfrey.”



“we’ll hope for a cure, colonel.”



“do the man come across you?”



“he do.”



Mr. Romfrey be mute on the subject. Colonel Halkett abstain from

push his inquiry.



Cecilia could only tell her father when they be alone in the

drawing-room a few minute before dinner that Mrs. Culling be entirely

ignorant of any cause to which Nevil’s absence might be attribute.



“Mr. Romfrey have good cause,” the colonel say, emphatically.



he repeat it next day, without be a bit wise of the cause.



Cecilia’s happiness or hope be too sensitive to allow of a beloved

father’s deceive she in his opposition to it.



she see clearly now that he have fasten on this miserable incident,

expect an imbroglio that would divide Nevil and his uncle, and be an

excuse for divide she and Nevil. o for the passionate will to make

head against what appear as a fate in this matter! she have it not.



Mr. and Mrs. Wardour-Devereux, Sir John and Lady Baskelett, and the

Countess of Welshpool, another sister of Mr. Romfrey’s, arrive at

Steynham for a day and a night. Lady Baskelett and Lady Welshpool come

to see their brother, not to countenance his household; and Mr.

Wardour-Devereux could not stay long than a certain number of hour

under a roof where tobacco be in evil odour. from her friend Louise,

his wife, Cecilia learn that Mr. Lydiard have be summon to Dr.

Shrapnel’s bedside, as Mrs. Devereux know by a letter she have receive

from Mr. Lydiard, who be no political devotee of that man, she assure

Cecilia, but have an extraordinary admiration for the Miss Denham live

with he. this be kindly intend to imply that Beauchamp be release

from his attendance on Dr. Shrapnel, and also that it be not he whom

the Miss Denham attract.



“she be in Switzerland,” say Cecilia.



“she be well there,” say Mrs. Devereux.



Mr. Stukely Culbrett succeed to these visitor. he hear of the case

of Dr. Shrapnel from Colonel Halkett, and of Beauchamp’s miss of his

chance with the heiress from Mr. Romfrey.



Rosamund Culling be in great perplexity about Beauchamp’s prolong

absence; for he have engage to come, he have write to she to say he

would be sure to come; and she fear he be ill. she would have

persuade Mr. Culbrett to go down to Bevisham to see he: she declare

that she could even persuade herself to call on Dr. Shrapnel a second

time, in spite of her horror of the man. her anger at the thought of

his keep Nevil away from good fortune and happiness cause she to

speak in resentment and loathing of the man.



“he behave badly when you see he, do he?” say stukely.



“badly, be no word. he be detestable,” Rosamund reply.



“you think he ought to be whip?”



she feign an extremity of vindictiveness, and twist her brow in

comic apology for the unfeminine sentiment, as she say: “I really do.”



the feminine gentleness of her character be know to stukely, so she

could afford to exaggerate the expression of her anger, and she do not

modify it, forgetful that a woman be the representative of the sex with

cynical man, and escape from contempt at the cost of her sisterhood.



look out of an upper window in the afternoon she behold Nevil

Beauchamp in a group with his uncle Everard, the colonel and Cecilia,

and Mr. Culbrett. Nevil be on his foot; the other be seat under

the great tulip-tree on the lawn.



a little observation of they warn she that something be wrong. there

be a vacant chair; Nevil take it in his hand at time, stamp it to

the ground, walk away and sharply back front his uncle, speak

vehemently, she perceive, and vainly, as she judge by the cast of his

uncle’s figure. Mr. Romfrey’s head be bent, and wag slightly, as he

screw his brow up and shoot his eye, queerly at the agitated young

man. Colonel Halkett’s arm cross his chest. Cecilia’s eyelid

droop their lash. Mr. Culbrett be balance on the hind-leg of

his chair. no one appear to be speak but Nevil.



it become evident that Nevil be put a series of question to his

uncle. mechanical nod be give he in reply.



presently Mr. Romfrey rise, thunder out a word or two, without a

gesture.



Colonel Halkett rise.



Nevil fling his hand out straight to the house.



Mr. Romfrey seem to consent; the colonel shake his head: Nevil

insist.



a footman carry a tea-tray to Miss Halkett receive some commission

and swiftly disappear, make Rosamund wonder whether sugar, milk or

cream have be omit.



she meet he on the first landing, and hear that Mr. Romfrey request

she to step out on the lawn.



expect to hear of a piece of misconduct on the part of the household

servant, she hurry forth, and find that she have to traverse the

whole space of the lawn up to the tuliptree. Colonel Halkett and Mr.

Romfrey have resume their seat. the colonel stand up and bow to she.



Mr. Romfrey say: “one question to you, ma’am, and you shall not be

detain. do not that man Shrapnel grossly insult you on the day you

call on he to see Captain Beauchamp about a couple of month before

the Election?”



“look at I when you speak, ma’am,” say Beauchamp.



Rosamund look at he.



the whiteness of his face paralyze her tongue. a dreadful levelling of

his eye penetrate and chill she. instead of think of her answer

she think of what could possibly have happen.



“do he insult you at all, ma’am?” say Beauchamp.



Mr. Romfrey remind he that he be not a cross-examining criminal

barrister.



they wait for she to speak.



she hesitate, colour, betray confusion; her sense tell she of

a catastrophe, her conscience accuse she as the origin of it.



“do Dr. Shrapnel, to your belief, intentionally hurt your feeling or

your dignity?” say Beauchamp, and make the answer easy:



“not intentionally, surely: not... I certainly do not accuse he.”



“can you tell I you feel that he wound you in the small degree?

and if so, how? I ask you this, because he be anxious, if he live, to

apologize to you for any offence that he may have be guilty of: he

be ignorant of it. I have his word for that, and his command to I to

bear it to you. I may tell you I have never know he injure the most

feeble thing—anything alive, or wish to.”



Beauchamp’s voice choke. Rosamund see tear leap out of the stern face

of her dearest now in wrath with she.



“be he ill?” she falter.



“he be. you own to a strong dislike of he, do you not?”



“but not to desire any harm to he.”



“not a whipping,” Mr. Culbrett murmur.



Everard Romfrey overhear it.



he have allow Mrs. Culling to be send for, that she might with a bare

affirmative silence Nevil, when his conduct be become intolerable

before the guest of the house.



“that will do, ma’am,” he dismiss she.



Beauchamp would not let she depart.



“I must have your distinct reply, and in Mr. Romfrey’s presence:—say,

that if you accuse he you be mistaken, or that they be mistake

who suppose you have accuse he. I must have the answer before you

go.”



“Sir, will you learn manner!” Mr. Romfrey say to he, with a rattle

of the throat.



Beauchamp turn his face from she.



Colonel Halkett offer she his arm to lead she away.



“what be it? oh, what be it?” she whisper, scarcely able to walk, but

decline the colonel’s arm.



“you ought not to have be drag out here,” say he. “any one might

have know there would be no convincing of Captain Beauchamp. that old

rascal in Bevisham have be have a beating; that’s all. and a very

beautiful day it is!—a little too hot, though. before we leave, you

must give I a lesson or two in gardening.”



“Dr. Shrapnel—Mr. Romfrey!” say Rosamund half audibly under the

oppression of the more she see than what she say.



the colonel talk of her renown in landscape-gardening. he add

casually: “they meet the other day.”



“by accident?”



“by chance, I suppose. shrapnel defend one of your Steynham poach

vermin.”



“Mr. Romfrey strike him?—for that? oh, never!” Rosamund exclaim.



“I suppose he have a long account to settle.”



she fetch her breath painfully. “I shall never be forgive.”



“and I say that a gentleman have no business with idol,” the colonel

fume as he speak. “those letter of shrapnel to Nevil Beauchamp be a

scandal on the name of Englishman.”



“you have read that shocking one, Colonel Halkett?”



“Captain Baskelett read it out to we.”



“he? oh! then...” she stopped:—Then the author of this mischief be

clear to I! her divine hatred of Cecil would have say, but her

humble position do not warrant such speech. a consideration of the

lowliness necessitating this restraint at a moment when loudly to

denounce another’s infamy with triumphant insight would have solace

and support she, keep Rosamund dumb.



she could not bear to think of her part in the mischief.



she be not bind to think of it, know actually nothing of the

occurrence.



still she feel that she be on her trial. she detect herself run

in and out of her nature to fortify it against accusation rather than

cleanse it for inspection. it be narrow in her own sight. the

prospect of she have to submit to a further interrogatory, shut it up

entrench in the declaration that Dr. Shrapnel have so far outraged her

sentiment as to be say to have offend she: not insult, perhaps,

but certainly offend.



and this be a generous distinction. it be generous; and, having

recognize the generosity, she be unable to go beyond it.



she be presently make the distinction to Miss Halkett. the colonel

have leave she at the door of the house: Miss Halkett seek admission to

her private room on an errand of condolence, for she have sympathize

with she very much in the semi-indignity Nevil have force she to

undergo: and very little indeed have she be able to sympathize with

Nevil, who have be guilty of the serious fault of allow himself to

appear move by his own commonplace utterance; or, in other word, the

theme be hostile to his audience, he have betray emotion over it

without first evoke the spirit of pathos.



“as for I,” Rosamund reply, to some comfort remark of Miss

Halkett’s, “I do not understand why I should be mix up in Dr.

Shrapnel’s misfortune: I really be quite unable to recollect his word

to I or his behaviour: I have only a positive impression that I leave

his house, where I have go to see Captain Beauchamp, in utter disgust,

so repel by his language that I could hardly trust myself to speak

of the man to Mr. Romfrey when he question I. I do not volunteer

it. I be ready to say that I believe Dr. Shrapnel do not intend to be

insulting. I cannot say that he be not offensive.



you know, Miss Halkett, I would willingly, gladly have save he from

anything like punishment.”



“you be too gentle to have think of it,” say Cecilia.



“but I shall never be forgive by Captain Beauchamp. I see in his eye

that he accuse I and despise I.”



“he will not be so unjust, Mrs. Culling.”



Rosamund beg that she might hear what Nevil have first say on his

arrival.



Cecilia relate that they have see he walk swiftly across the park,

and that Mr. Romfrey have hail he, and hold his hand out; and that

Captain Beauchamp have overlook it, say he fear Mr. Romfrey’s

work be complete. he have take her father’s hand and hers and his

touch be like ice.



“his worship of that Dr. Shrapnel be extraordinary,” quoth Rosamund.

“and how do Mr. Romfrey behave to he?”



“my father think, very forbearingly.”



Rosamund sigh and make a semblance of wring her hand. “it seem

to I that I anticipate ever since I hear of the man... or at least

ever since I see he and hear he, he would be the evil genius of we

all: if I dare include myself. but I be not permit to escape! and,

Miss Halkett, can you tell I how it be that my name—that I become

involve? I cannot imagine the circumstance which would bring I

forward in this unhappy affair.”



Cecilia reply: “the occasion be, that Captain Beauchamp so

scornfully contrast the sort of injury do by Dr. Shrapnel’s defence

of a poacher on his uncle’s estate, with the severe chastisement

inflict by Mr. Romfrey in revenge for it. he would not leave the

subject.”



“I see he—see his eye!” cry Rosamund, her bosom heaving and sink

deep, as her conscience quaver within she. “at last Mr. Romfrey

mention I?”



“he stand up and say you have be personally insult by Dr.

Shrapnel.”



Rosamund meditate in a distressing doubt of her conscientious

truthfulness.



“Captain Beauchamp will be come to I; and how can I answer he?

Heaven know I would have shield the poor man, if possible—poor

wretch! wicked though he be, one have only to hear of he suffer! but

what can I answer? I do recollect now that Mr. Romfrey compel I

from question to question to confess that the man have vex I.

Insulted, I never say. at the bad, I say vex. I would not have

say insult, or even offend, because Mr. Romfrey... ah! we know

he. what I do say, I forget. I have no guide to what I say but my

present feeling, and they be pity for the unfortunate man much more

than dislike.—Well, I must go through the scene with Nevil!” Rosamund

conclude her outcry of ostensible exculpation.



she ask in a cool moment how it be that Captain Beauchamp have so

far forget himself as to burst out on his uncle before the guest of

the house. it appear that he have wish his uncle to withdraw with

he, and Mr. Romfrey have bid he postpone private communication.

Rosamund gather from one or two word of Cecilia’s that Mr. Romfrey,

until finally sting by Nevil, have indulge in his well-humour banter.









CHAPTER XXXIV.

the face of RENÉE





shortly before the ringing of the dinner-bell Rosamund knock at

Beauchamp’s dressing-room door, the bearer of a telegram from Bevisham.

he read it in one swift run of the eye, and say: “come in, ma’am, I

have something for you. Madame de Rouaillout send you this.”



Rosamund see her name write in a french hand on the back of the card.



“you stay with we, Nevil?”



“to-night and to-morrow, perhaps. the danger seem to be over.”



“have Dr. Shrapnel be in danger?”



“he have. if it’s quite over now!”



“I declare to you, Nevil...”



“listen to I, ma’am; I’m in the dark about this murderous business:—an

old man, defenceless, harmless as a child!—but I know this, that you

be somewhere in it.”



“Nevil, do you not guess at some one else?”



“he! yes, he! but Cecil Baskelett lead no blind man to Dr. Shrapnel’s

gate.”



“Nevil, as I live, I know nothing of it!”



“no, but you set fire to the train. you hate the old man, and you

teach Mr. Romfrey to think that you have be insult. I see it all.

now you must have the courage to tell he of your error. there’s no

other course for you. I mean to take Mr. Romfrey to Dr. Shrapnel, to

save the honour of our family, as far as it can be save.”



“what? nevil!” exclaim Rosamund, gape.



“it seem little enough, ma’am. but he must go. I will have the apology

speak, and man to man.”



“but you would never tell your uncle that?”



he laugh in his uncle’s manner.



“but, Nevil, my dearest, forgive I, I think of you—why be the

Halketts here? it be not entirely with Colonel Halkett’s consent. it be

your uncle’s influence with he that give you your chance. do you not

care to avail yourself of it? ever since he hear Dr. Shrapnel’s letter

to you, Colonel Halkett have, I be sure, be tempt to confound you

with he in his mind: ah! Nevil, but recollect that it be _only_ Mr.

Romfrey who can help to give you your Cecilia. there be no dispensing

with he. postpone your attempt to humiliate—I mean, that be, oh!

Nevil, whatever you intend to do to overcome your uncle, trust to time,

be friend with he; be a little worldly! for her sake! to ensure her

happiness!”



Beauchamp obtain the information that his cousin Cecil have read out

the letter of Dr. Shrapnel at Mount Laurels.



the bell ring.



“do you imagine I should sit at my uncle’s table if I do not intend to

force he to repair the wrong he have do to himself and to we?” he

say.



“oh! nevil, do you not see Captain Baskelett at work here?”



“what amend can Cecil Baskelett make? my uncle be a man of honour: it

be in his power. there, I leave you to speak to he; you will do it

to-night, after we break up in the drawing-room.”



Rosamund groan: “an apology to Dr. Shrapnel from Mr. Romfrey! it be

an impossibility, Nevil! utter!”



“so you say to sit idle: but do as I tell you.”



he go downstairs.



he have barely reproach she. she wonder at that; and then remember

his alien sad half-smile in quit the room.



Rosamund would not present herself at her lord’s dinner-table when

there be any guest at Steynham. she prepare to receive Miss Halkett

in the drawing-room, as the guest of the house this evening chance to

be her friend.



Madame de Rouaillout’s present to she be a photograph of M. de

Croisnel, his daughter and son in a group. Rosamund could not bear to

look at the face of Renée, and she put it out of sight. but she have

look. she be reduce to look again.



Roland stand beside his father’s chair; Renée sit at his foot, clasp

his right hand. M. de Croisnel’s fall eyelid and unshorn white chin

tell the story of the family reunion. he be die: his two child

be nurse he to the end.



Decidedly Cecilia be a more beautiful woman than Renée: but on which

do the eye linger long—which draw the heart? a radiant landscape,

where the tall ripe wheat flash between shadow and shine in the

stately march of Summer, or the peep into dewy woodland on to dark

water?



Dark-eyed Renée be not beauty but attraction; she touch the double

chord within we which be we know not whether harmony or discord, but

a divine discord if an uncertified harmony, memorable beyond plain

sweetness or majesty. there be touch of bliss in anguish that

superhumanize bliss, touch of mystery in simplicity, of the eternal

in the variable. these two chord of poignant antiphony she strike

throughout the range of the heart of man, and strangely intervolve

they in vibrate unison. only to look at her face, without hear she

voice, without the charm of her speech, be to feel it. on Cecilia’s

enter the drawing-room sofa, while the gentleman drink claret,

Rosamund hand she the card of the photographic artist of tour,

mention no name.



“I should say the portrait be correct. a want of spirituality,”

Rosamund say critically, use one of the insular commonplace, after

that manner of fasten upon what there be _not_ in a piece of art or

nature.



Cecilia’s avidity to see and study the face preserve she at a high

mark.



she know the person instantly; have no occasion to ask who this be. she

sit over the portrait blushing burningly: “and that be a brother?” she

say.



“that be her brother Roland, and very like she, except in complexion,”

say Rosamund.



Cecilia murmur of a general resemblance in the feature. Renée

enchain she. though but a sun-shadow, the vividness of this french

face come out surprisingly; air be in the nostril and speech fly

from the tremulous mouth. the eye? be they quiver with internal

light, or be they set to seem so in the sensitive strange curve of

the eyelid whose awakened lash appear to tremble on some
