incalculable good! and as far as his grief at the idea and some

reticence would let he venture, he sigh to think of the last Earl of

Romfrey having be see by he take the seat of his father.



Lord Romfrey shout “ha!” like a check peal of laughter, and glance

at his wife.









CHAPTER XLV.

a little plot against CECILIA





some day before Easter week Seymour Austin go to Mount Laurels for

rest, at an express invitation from Colonel Halkett. the working

barrister, who be also a work member of Parliament, be occasionally

remind that this mortal machine cannot adapt itself in perpetuity to

the long hour of labour by night in the House of Commons as well as by

day in the Courts, which would seem to have be arrange by a

compliant country for the purpose of aid his particular, and most

honourable, ambition to climb, while continue to fill his purse. Mr.

Austin break down early in the year. he attribute it to a cold. other

representative gentleman be on their back, of whom he could admit

that the protract nightwork have do they harm, with the reservation

that their constitution be originally unsound. but the House cannot

get on without lawyer, and lawyer must practise their profession, and

if they manage both to practise all day and sit half the night, other

should be able to do the simple late sitting; and we English be an

energetic people, we must toil or be beat: and besides, “night bring

counsel,” man be cool and wise by night. any amount of work can be

perform by careful feeder: it be the stomach that kill the

Englishman. brain be never the bad for activity; they subsist on

it.



these argument and citation, good and absurd, of a man more at home

in his harness than out of it, be address to the colonel to stop

his remonstrance and idle talk about burn the candle at both end.

to that illustration Mr. Austin reply that he do not burn it in the

middle.



“but you don’t want money, Austin.”



“no; but since I’ve have the habit of make it I have take to like

it.”



“but you’re not ambitious.”



“very little; but I should be sorry to be out of the tideway.”



“I call it a system of slaughter,” say the colonel; and Mr. Austin

say, “the world go in that way—love and slaughter.”



“not suicide though,” Colonel Halkett mutter.



“no, that’s only incidental.”



the casual word “love” lead Colonel Halkett to speak to Cecilia of an

old love-affair of Seymour Austin’s, in discuss the state of his

health with she. the lady be the daughter of a famous admiral,

handsome, and latterly of light fame. Mr. Austin have nothing to regret

in her having marry a man rich than himself.



“I wish he have marry a good woman,” say the colonel.



“he look unwell, papa.”



“he think you’re look unwell, my dear.”



“he think that of I?”



Cecilia prepare a radiant face for Mr. Austin.



she forget to keep it kindle, and he suspect she to be a victim of

one of the form of youthful melancholy, and lay stress on the benefit

to health of a change of scene.



“we have just return from Wales,” she say.



he remark that it be hardly a change to be within shot of our

newspaper.



the colour leave her cheek. she fancy her father have betray she to

the last man who should know her secret. Beauchamp and the newspaper

be roll together in her mind by the fever of apprehension waste

she ever since his declaration of Republicanism, and defence of it, and

an allusion to one must imply the other, she fear: fear, but far

from quailingly. she have come to think that she could read the man she

love, and detect a reasonableness in his extravagance. her father have

discover the impolicy of attack Beauchamp in her hearing. the

fever by which Cecilia be possess on her lover’s behalf, often

overcame discretion, set her judgement in a whirl, be like a delirium.

how it have happen she know not. she know only her wretched state; a

frenzy seize she whenever his name be utter, to excuse, account

for, all but glorify he publicly. and the immodesty of her conduct be

perceptible to she while she thus make her heart bare. she expose

herself once of late at Itchincope, and have try to school her tongue

before she go there. she feel that she should inevitably be see

through by Seymour Austin if he take the world’s view of Beauchamp, and

this to she be like a descent on the rapid to an end one shut eye

from.



he notice her perturbation, and speak of it to her father.



“yes, I’m very miserable about she,” the colonel confess. “girl

don’t see... they cann’t guess... they have no idea of the right kind of

man for they. a man like Blackburn Tuckham, now, a man a father could

leave his girl to, with confidence! he work for I like a slave; I

cann’t guess why. he don’t look as if he be attract. there’s a

man! but, no; harum-scarum fellow take their fancy.”



“be _she_ that kind of young lady?” say Mr. Austin.



“no one would have think so. she pretend to have opinion upon

politic now. it’s of no use to talk of it!”



but Beauchamp be fully indicate.



Mr. Austin propose to Cecilia that they should spend Easter week in

Rome.



her face light and clouded.



“I should like it,” she say, negatively.



“what’s the objection?”



“none, except that Mount Laurels in Spring have grow dear to I; and we

have engagement in London. I be not quick, I suppose, at new project.

I have order the yacht to be fit out for a cruise in the

Mediterranean early in the Summer. there be an objection, I be

sure—yes; papa have invite Mr. Tuckham here for Easter.”



“we could carry he with we.”



“yes, but I should wish to be entirely under your tutelage in Rome.”



“we would pair: your father and he; you and I.”



“we might do that. but Mr. Tuckham be like you, devote to work; and,

unlike you, careless of antiquity and Art.”



“he be a hard and serious worker, and therefore the good of companion

for a holiday. at present he be work for the colonel, who would

easily persuade he to give over, and come with we.”



“he certainly do love papa,” say Cecilia.



Mr. Austin dwell on that subject.



Cecilia perceive that she have praise Mr. Tuckham for his devotedness

to her father without recognize the beauty of nature in the young man

who could voluntarily take service under the elder he esteem, in

simple admiration of he. Mr. Austin scarcely say so much, or expect

she to see the half of it, but she wish to be extremely grateful, and

could only see at all by kindle altogether.



“he do himself injustice in his manner,” say Cecilia.



“that have become somewhat temper,” Mr. Austin assure she, and he

acknowledge what it have be with a smile that she reciprocate.



a rough man of rare quality civilize under various influence, and

half ludicrous, a little irritating, wholly estimable, have frequently

win the benign approbation of the sex. in addition, this rough man over

whom she smile be one of the few that never worry she concern

her hand. there be not a whisper of it in he. he simply love she

father.



Cecilia welcome he to Mount Laurels with grateful gladness. the

colonel have hasten Mr. Tuckham’s visit in view of the expedition to

Rome, and they discourse of it at the luncheon table. Mr. Tuckham let

fall that he have just see Beauchamp.



“do he thank you for his inheritance?” Colonel Halkett inquire.



“not he!” Tuckham reply jovially.



Cecilia’s eye, quick to flash, be drop.



the colonel say: “I suppose you tell he nothing of what you have do

for he?” and say Tuckham: “oh no: what anybody else would have do”;

and proceed to recount that he have call at Dr. Shrapnel’s on the

chance of an interview with his friend Lydiard, who use generally to

be hang about the cottage. “but now he’s free: his lunatic wife be

dead, and I’m happy to think I be mistake as to Miss Denham. man

practise literature should marry woman with money. the poor girl

change colour when I inform she he have be release for upwards of

three month. the old Radical’s not the thing in health. he’s anxious

about leave she alone in the world; he say so to I. beauchamp’s for

rig out a yacht to give he a sail. it seem that salt water do

he some good last year. they’re both of they rather the bad for a

row at one of their meeting in the North in support of that public

nuisance, the democrat and atheist Roughleigh. the radical doctor lose

a hat, and Beauchamp almost lose an eye. he would have be a Nelson of

politic, if he have be a monop, with an excuse for not see. it’s

a trifle to they; part of their education. they call themselves

student. Rome will be capital, Miss Halkett. you’re an italian

scholar, and I beg to be accept as a pupil.”



“I fear we have postpone the expedition too long,” say Cecilia. she

could have sink with languor.



“too long?” cry Colonel Halkett, mystify.



“until too late, I mean, papa. do you not think, Mr. Austin, that a

fortnight in Rome be too short a time?”



“not if we make it a month, my dear Cecilia.”



“be not our salt air well for you? the yacht shall be fit out.”



“I’m a poor sailor!”



“besides, a hasty excursion to Italy bring one’s anticipate regret

at the farewell too close to the pleasure of behold it, for the

enjoyment of that luxury of delight which I associate with the name of

Italy.”



“why, my dear child,” say her father, “you be all for go, the

other day.”



“I do not remember it,” say she. “one plan agreeable scheme. at

least we need not hurry from home so very soon after our return. we

have be travel incessantly. the cottage in Wales be not home. it

be hardly fair to Mount Laurels to quit it without observe the

change of the season in our flower and bird here. and we have

visitor come. of course, papa, I would not chain you to England. if

I be not well enough to accompany you, I can go to Louise for a few

week.”



be ever transparency so threadbare? Cecilia shrink from herself in

contemplate it when she be alone; and Colonel Halkett put the

question to Mr. Austin, say to he privately, with no far

reserve: “it’s that fellow Beauchamp in the neighbourhood; I’m not so

blind. he’ll be knock at my door, and I cann’t lock he out. Austin,

would you guess it be my girl speak? I never in my life have such an

example of intoxication before I. I’m perfectly miserable at the

sight. you know she; she be the proud girl live. her idea be

orderly and sound; she have a good intellect. now she more than half

defend he—a naval officer! good Lord!—for get up in a public room

to announce that he’s a Republican, and write heap of mad letter to

justify himself. he’s ruin in his profession: hopeless! he can never

get a ship: his career’s cut short, he’s a rudderless boat. a gentleman

drift to Bedlam, his uncle call he. I call his treatment of

Grancey Lespel anything but gentlemanly. this be the sort of fellow my

girl worship! what can I do? I cann’t interdict the house to he: it

would only make matter bad. thank God, the fellow hang fire

somehow, and don’t come to I. I expect it every day, either in a

letter or the man in person. and I declare to heaven I’d rather be

thread a Khyber Pass with my poor old friend who fall to a shot

there.”



“she certainly have another voice,” Mr. Austin assent gravely.



he do not look on Beauchamp as the good of possible husband for

Cecilia.



“let she see that you’re anxious, Austin,” say the colonel. “I’m her

old opponent in this affair. she love I, but she’s accustom to

think I prejudice: you she willn’t. you may have a good effect.”



“not by speak.”



“no, no; no assault: not a word, and not a word against he. Lay the

wind to catch a gossamer. I’ve have my experience of blow cold, and

try to run she down. he’s at Shrapnel’s. he’ll be up here to-day,

and I have an engagement in the town. don’t quit her side. let she

fancy you be interested in some discussion—Radicalism, if you like.”



Mr. Austin readily undertake to mount guard over she while her father

ride into Bevisham on business.



the enemy appear.



Cecilia see he, and could not step to meet he for trouble of heart.

it be bliss to know that he live and be near.



a transient coldness follow the fit of ecstasy enable she to swim

through the terrible first minute face to face with he.



he fold she round like a mist; but it grow a problem to understand

why Mr. Austin should be perpetually at hand, in the garden, in the

wood, in the drawing-room, wheresoever she waken up from one of her

trance to see thing as they be.



yet Beauchamp, with a daring and cunning at which her soul exult, and

her feminine nature tremble, as at the divinely terrible, have manage

to convey to she no less than if they have be alone together.



his parting word be: “I must have five minute with your father

to-morrow.”



how have she behave? what could be Seymour Austin’s idea of she?



she see the blind thing that she be, the senseless thing, the

shameless; and vulture-like in her scorn of herself, she alight on

that disgrace Cecilia and pick she to piece hungrily. it be clear:

Beauchamp have mean nothing beyond friendly civility: it be only her

abject greediness pecking at crumb. no! he love she. could a woman’s

heart be mistake? she melt and weep, thank he: she offer he

her remnant of pride, pitiful to behold.



and still she ask herself between-while whether it could be true of

an english lady of our day, that she, the fair stature under sun,

be ever knowingly twisted to this convulsion. she seem to look forth

from a bar window on flower, and field, and hill. Quietness exist

as a vision. be it impossible to embrace it? how pass into it? by

surrender herself to the flame, like a soul unto death! for why, if

they be overpower, attempt to resist they? it flatter she to

imagine that she have be resist they in their present burning might

ever since her lover step on the _Esperanza’s_ deck at the mouth of

Otley River. how foolish, see that they be fatal! a thrill of

satisfaction sweep she in reflect that her ability to reason be

thus active. and she be instantly reward for surrendering; pain

flee, to prove her reasoning good; the flame devour she gently they

care not to torture so long as they have she to themselves.



at night, candle in hand, on the corridor, her father tell she he have

come across Grancey Lespel in Bevisham, and hear what he have not quite

relish of the Countess of Romfrey. the glittering of Cecilia’s eye

frighten he. take she for the moment to know almost as much as he,

the colonel doubt the weight his communication would have on she; he

talk obscurely of a scandalous affair at Lord Romfrey’s house in

town, and Beauchamp and that Frenchwoman. “but,” say he, “Mrs. Grancey

will be here to-morrow.”



“so will Nevil, papa,” say Cecilia.



“ah! he’s come, yes; well!” the colonel puff. “well, I shall see

he, of course, but I... I can only say that if his oath’s worth

have, I ... and I think you too, my dear, if you... but it’s no use

anticipating. I shall stand out for your honour and happiness. there,

your cheek be flushed. go and sleep.”



some idle tale! Cecilia murmur to herself a dozen time, undisturbed

by the recurrence of it. Nevil be come to speak to her father

tomorrow! Adieu to doubt and division! happy to-morrow! and dear Mount

Laurels! the primrose be still fair in the wood: and soon the

cowslip would come, and the nightingale; she lie lapt in image of

everything innocently pleasing to Nevil. soon the _Esperanza_ would be

spread wing. she revel in a picture of the yacht on a tumble

Mediterranean Sea, meditate on the two speck near the tiller,—who

be blissful human creature, bl by heaven and in themselves—with

luxurious olympian benevolence.



for all that, she awake, start up in the first cold circle of

twilight, her heart in violent action. she have dream that the vessel

be wreck. “I do not think myself so cowardly,” she say aloud,

press her side and then, with the dream in her eye, she gasp: “it

would be together!”



strangely chill, she try to recover some fallen load. the bird of

the dawn twitter, chirp, dive aslant her window, flutter back.

instead of a fallen load, she fancy presently that it be an

expectation she be desire to realize: but what? what could be

expect at that hour? she quit her bed, and pace up and down the

room beneath a gold-star ceiling. her expectation, she resolve to

think, be of a splendid day of the young Spring at Mount Laurels—a day

to praise to Nevil.



she raise her window-blind at a window letting in sweet air, to gather

indication of promise weather. her lover stand on the grass-plot

among the flower-bed below, look up, as though it have be his

expectation to see she which have draw she to gaze out with an idea of

some expectation of her own. so visionary be his figure in the grey

solitariness of the moveless morning that she stare at the apparition,

scarce put faith in he as man, until he kiss his hand to she,

and have softly call her name.



Impulsively she wave a hand from her lip.



now there be no retreat for either of they!



she awake to this conviction after a flight of blush that burn she

thought to ashe as they sprang. thought bear blushing, all of the

crimson colour, a rose-garden, succeed, and correspond with their

speed her foot pace the room, both slender hand cross at her throat

under an uplifted chin, and the curve of her dark eyelash drop as

in a swoon.



“he love I!” the attestation of it have be visible. “no one but I!”

be that so evident?



her father pick up silly story of he—a man who make enemy

recklessly!



Cecilia be petrify by a gentle tap at her door. her father

call to she, and she throw on her dressing-gown, and open the door.



the colonel be in his riding-suit.



“I haven’t sleep a wink, and I find it’s the same with you,” he say,

pain she with his distressed kind eye. “I ought not to have hint

anything last night without proof. Austin’s as unhappy as I be.”



“at what, my dear papa, at what?” cry Cecilia.



“I ride over to Steynham this morning, and I shall bring you proof, my

poor child, proof. that foreign tangle of his...”



“you speak of Nevil, papa?”



“it’s a common scandal over London. that Frenchwoman be find at Lord

Romfrey’s house; Lady Romfrey cloak it. I believe the woman would

swear black’s white to make Nevil Beauchamp appear an angel; and he’s a

desperately cunning hand with woman. you doubt that.”



she have shudder slightly.



“you willn’t doubt if I bring you proof. till I come back from Steynham,

I ask you not to see he alone: not to go out to he.”



the colonel glance at her window.



Cecilia submit to the request, out of breath, consent to feel

like a tutor girl, that she might conceal her guilty knowledge of

what be to be see through the window.



“now I’m off,” say he, and kiss she.



“if you would accept Nevil’s word!” she murmur.



“not where woman be concern!”



he leave she with this remark, which find no jealous response in her

heart, yet range over certain disperse inflammable grain, like a

match apply to damp powder; again and again run in little leap

of harmless firm keep she alive to its existence, and surprise she

that it should not have be extinguish.



Beauchamp present himself rather late in the afternoon, when Mr.

Austin and Blackburn Tuckham be sip tea in Cecilia’s boudoir with

that lady, and a cousin of her sex, by whom she be lead to notice a

faint discoloration over one of his eye, that be, consider whence

it come, repulsive to compassion. a blow at a radical meeting! he speak

of Dr. Shrapnel to Tuckham, and assuredly could not complain that the

latter be unsympathetic in regard to the old man’s health, though when

he say, “poor old man! he fear he will die!” Tuckham rejoin: “he

have well make his peace.”



“he fear he will die, because of his leave Miss Denham unprotecte,”

say Beauchamp.



“well, she’s a good-look girl: he’ll be able to leave she something,

and he might easily get she married, I should think,” say Tuckham.



“he’s not satisfied with hand she to any kind of man.”



“if the choice be to be among Radicals and infidel, I don’t wonder. he

have come to one of the test.”



Cecilia hear Beauchamp speak of a newspaper. a great Radical

Journal, unmatched in sincerity, superior in ability, soon to be equal

in power, to the leader and exemplar of the lucre-Press, would some day

see the light.



“you’ll want money for that,” say Tuckham.



“I know,” say Beauchamp.



“be you prepared to stand forty or fifty thousand a year?”



“it need not be half so much.”



“count the libel, I rate the outlay rather low.”



“yes, lawyer, judge, and jury of tradesman, deal justice to a

radical print!”



Tuckham brush his hand over his mouth and aheme. “it’s to be a penny

journal?”



“yes, a penny. I’d make it a farthing—”



“pay to have it read?”



“Willingly.”



Tuckham do some mental arithmetic, quaintly, with rapidly blink

eyelid and open mouth. “you may count it at the cost of two pay

mine,” he say firmly. “that be, if it’s to be a consistently Radical

Journal, at law with everybody all round the year. and by the time it

have win a reputation, it will be undermine by a radicaller Radical

Journal. that’s how we’ve lower the country to this level. that’s an

Inferno of Circles, down to the ultimate mire. and what on earth be

you contend for?”



“Freedom of thought, for one thing.”



“we have quite enough free-thinking.”



“there’s not enough if there’s not perfect freedom.”



“dangerous!” quoth Mr. Austin.



“but it’s that danger which make man, sir; and it’s fear of the danger

that make our modern Englishman.”



“oh! oh!” cry Tuckham in the voice of a Parliamentary Opposition.

“well, you start your paper, we’ll assume it: what class of man will

you get to write?”



“I shall get good man for the hire.”



“you willn’t get the good man; you may catch a clever youngster or two,

and an old rogue of talent; you willn’t get man of weight. they’re

prejudiced, I dare say. the Journals which be commercial speculation

give we a guarantee that they mean to be respectable; they must, if

they wouldn’t collapse. that’s why the good man consent to write for

they.”



“money will do it,” say Beauchamp.



Mr. Austin disagree with that observation.



“some patriotic spirit, I may hope, sir.”



Mr. Austin shake his head. “we put different construction upon

patriotism.”



“besides—fiddle! nonsense!” exclaim Tuckham in the mild

interjection he could summon for a vent in society to his offended

common sense; “the well your man the bad your mark. you’re not

deal with an intelligent people.”



“there’s the old charge against the people.”



“but they’re not. you can madden, you cann’t elevate they by writing and

writing. defend we from the uneducated English! the common English be

doltish; except in the North, where you willn’t do much with they.

compare they with the Yankees for shrewdness, the Spaniards for

sobriety, the French for ingenuity, the Germans for enlightenment, the

Italians in the Arts; yes, the Russians for good-humour and

obedience—where be they? they’re only worth something when they’re

lead. they fight well; there’s good stuff in they.”



“I’ve hear all that before,” return Beauchamp, unruffled. “you don’t

know they. I mean to educate they by give they an interest in their

country. at present they have next to none. our governing class be

decidedly unintelligent, in my opinion brutish, for it’s indifferent.

my paper shall render your trader justice for what they do, and

justice for what they don’t do.”



“my trader, as you call they, be the sound foundation for a

civilized state that the world have yet see.”



“what be your paper to be call?” say Cecilia.



“the DAWN,” Beauchamp answer.



she blush fiery red, and turn the leave of a portfolio of

drawing.



“the DAWN!” ejaculate Tuckham. “the grey-eye, or the red?

extraordinary name for a paper, upon my word!”



“a paper that don’t devote half its column to the vice of the

rich—to money-getting, spending and betting—will be an extraordinary

paper.”



“I have it before I now!—two dose of flattery to one of the whip. no,

no; you haven’t hit the disease. we want union, not division. turn your

mind to be a moralist, instead of a politician.”



“the distinction shouldn’t exist!”



“only it do!”



Mrs. Grancey Lespel’s entrance divert their dialogue from a theme

wearisome to Cecilia, for Beauchamp shone but darkly in it, and Mr.

Austin do not join in it. Mrs. Grancey touch Beauchamp’s finger.

“still political?” she say. “you have be see about London with a

french officer in uniform.”



“it be M. le comte de Croisnel, a very old friend and comrade of

mine,” Beauchamp reply.



“why do those Frenchmen everlastingly wear their uniforms?—tell I!

don’t you think it detestable style?”



“he come over in a hurry.”



“now, don’t be huff. I know you, for defend your friend, Captain

Beauchamp! do he not come over with lady?”



“with relative, yes.”



“relative of course. but when british officer travel with lady,

relative or other, they prefer the simplicity of mufti, and so do I,

as a question of taste, I must say.”



“it be quite by misadventure that M. de Croisnel chance to come in

his uniform.”



“ah! I know you, for defend your friend, Captain Beauchamp. he be

in too great a hurry to change his uniform before he start, or en

route?”



“so it happen.”



Mrs. Grancey let a linger eye dwell maliciously on Beauchamp, who

say, to shift the burden of it: “the French be not so jealous of

military uniform as we be. M. de Croisnel lose his portmanteau.”



“ah! lose it! then of course he be excuseable, except to the naked eye.

dear I! you have have a bruise on your. be Monsieur votre ami in the

italian campaign?”



“no, poor fellow, he be not. he be not an Imperialist; he have to

remain in garrison.”



“he wear a multitude of medal, I have be tell. a cup of tea,

Cecilia. and how long do he stay in England with his relative?”



“two day.”



“only two day! a very short visit indeed—singularly short. somebody

inform I of their having be see at Romfrey Castle, which cannot

have be true.”



she turn her eye from Beauchamp silent to Cecilia’s hand on the

teapot. “half a cup,” she say mildly, to spare the poor hand its

betrayal of nervousness, and relapse from her air of mistress of the

situation to chatter to Mr. Austin.



Beauchamp continue silent. he take up a book, and presently a pencil

from his pocket, then talk of the book to Cecilia’s cousin; and

leave a paper-cutter between the leave, he look at Cecilia and

lay the book down.



she proceed to conduct Mrs. Grancey Lespel to her room.



“I do admire Captain Beauchamp’s cleverness; he be as good as a french

romance!” Mrs. Grancey exclaim on the stair. “he fib charmingly. I

could not help draw he out. two day! why, my dear, his french

party be a fortnight in the country. it be the marquise, you

know—the old affair; and one may say he’s a constant man.”



“I have not hear Captain Beauchamp’s cleverness much praise,” say

Cecilia. “this be your room, Mrs. Grancey.”



“stay with I a moment. it be the room I like. be we to have he at

dinner?”



Cecilia do not suppose that Captain Beauchamp would remain to dine.

feel herself in the clutch of a gossip, she would fain have go.



“I be just one bit glad of it, though I cann’t dislike he personally,”

say Mrs. Grancey, detain she and begin to whisper. “it be

really too bad. there be a french _party_ at the end, but there be

only _one_ at the commencement. the brother be get over for a curtain,

before the husband arrive in pursuit. they say the trick Captain

Beauchamp play his cousin Cecil, to get he out of the house when he

have make a discovery, be monstrous—fiendishly cunning. however, Lady

Romfrey, as that woman appear to be at last, cover it all. you know

she have one of those passion for Captain Beauchamp which completely

blind woman to right and wrong. he be her saint, let he sin ever so!

the story’s in everybody’s mouth. by the way, Palmet see she. he

describe her pale as marble, with dark long eye, the most innocent

look in the world, and a walk, the absurd fellow say, like a statue

set gliding. no doubt frenchwoman do walk well. he say her eye be

terrible traitor; I need not quote Palmet. the sort of eye that would

look fondly on a stone, you know. what her reputation be in France I

have only indistinctly hear. she have one in England by this time, I

can assure you. she find her match in Captain Beauchamp for boldness.

where any other couple would have see danger, _they_ see safety; and

they contrive to accomplish it, accord to those horrid talebearer.

you have plenty of time to dress, my dear; I have an immense deal to

talk about. there be half-a-dozen scandal in London already, and you

ought to know they, or you will be behind the tittle-tattle when you go

to town; and I remember, as a girl, I know nothing so excruciating as

to hear blank, dash, initial, and half word, without the key.

nothing make a girl look so silly and unpalatable. naturally, the

reason why Captain Beauchamp be more talk about than the rest be the

politic. your grand reformer should be careful. doubly heterodox will

not do! it make he interesting to woman, if you like, but he willn’t

soon hear the last of it, if he be for a public career. Grancey

literally crow at the story. and the wonderful part of it be, that

Captain Beauchamp refuse to be present at the earl’s first ceremonial

dinner in honour of his countess. now, that, we all think, be

particularly ungrateful: now, be it not?”



“if the countess—if ingratitude have anything to do with it,” say

Cecilia.



she escape to her room and dress impatiently.



her boudoir be empty: Beauchamp have depart. she recollect his look

at she, and turn over the leave of the book he have be hastily

scanning, and have condescend to approve of. on the two page where

the paper-cutter be fix she perceive small pencil dot under

certain word. read consecutively, with a participle termination strike

out to convey his meaning, they form the pathetically ungrammatical

line:



“hear: none: but: accuse: false.”



treble dot be under the word “to-morrow.” he have score the margin

of the sentence contain his dotted word, as if in admiration of

their peculiar wisdom.



she think it piteous that he should be reduce to such mean of

communication. the next instant Cecilia be shrink from the adept

intriguer—French-teach!



in the course of the evening her cousin remark:



“Captain Beauchamp must see merit in thing undiscoverable by my poor

faculty. I will show you a book he have mark.”



“do you see it? I be curious to examine it,” interpose Cecilia; “and

I be as much at a loss as you to understand what could have attract

he. one sentence...”



“about the sheikh in the stable, where he accuse the pretended

physician? yes, what be there in that?”



“where be the book?” say Mrs. Grancey.



“not here, I think.” Cecilia glance at the drawing-room book-table,

and then at Mr. Austin, the victim of an unhappy love in his youth, and

unhappy about she, as her father have say. Seymour Austin be not one

to spread the contagion of intrigue! she feel herself catch by it,

even melt to feel enamoured of herself in consequence, though not

love Beauchamp the more.



“this newspaper, if it’s not merely an airy project, will be

ruination,” say Tuckham. “the fact be, Beauchamp have no _bend_ in he.

he cann’t meet a man without try a wrestle, and as long as he keep

his stiffness, he believe he have win. I’ve hear an oculist say that

the eye that don’t blink end in blindness, and he who willn’t bend

break. it’s a pity, for he’s a fine fellow. a Radical daily Journal of

Shrapnel’s colour, to educate the people by give they an interest in

the country! Goodness, what a delusion! and what a waste of money!

he’ll not be able to carry it on a couple of year. and there go his

eighty thousand!”



Cecilia’s heart beat fast. she have no define cause for its excitement.



Colonel Halkett return to Mount Laurels close upon midnight, very

tired, cough and complain of the bitter blow East. his guest

shake hand with he, and go to bed.



“I think I’ll follow their example,” he say to Cecilia, after drink

a tumbler of mulled wine.



“have you nothing to tell I, dear papa?” say she, caress he

timidly.



“a confirmation of the whole story from Lord Romfrey in person—that’s

all. he say Beauchamp’s mad. I begin to believe it. you must use your

judgement. I suppose I must not expect you to consider I. you might

open your heart to Austin. as to my consent, know what I do, you

will have to tear it out of I. here’s a country perfectly content,

and that fellow at work dig up grievance to persuade the people

they’re oppress by we. why should I talk of it? he cann’t do much

harm; unless he have money—money! Romfrey say he mean to start a

furious paper. he’ll make a bonfire of himself. I cann’t stand by and

see you in it too. I may die; I may be spare the sight.”



Cecilia fling her arm round his neck. “oh! papa.”



“I don’t want to make he out bad than he be, my dear. I own to his

gallantry—in the french sense as well as the English, it seem! it’s

natural that Romfrey should excuse his wife. she’s another of the woman

who be crazy about Nevil Beauchamp. she speak to I of the ‘pleasant

visit of her french friend,’ and would have enlarge on it, but

Romfrey stop she. by the way, he propose Captain Baskelett for you,

and we’re to look for Baskelett’s come here, back by his uncle.

there’s no end to it; there never will be till you’re marry: and no

peace for I! I hope I shalln’t find myself with a cold to-morrow.”



the colonel cough, and perhaps exaggerate the premonitory symptom

of a cold.



“Italy, papa, would do you good,” say Cecilia.



“it might,” say he.



“if we go immediately, papa; to-morrow, early in the morning, before

there be a chance of any visitor come to the house.”



“from Bevisham?”



“from Steynham. I cannot endure a second persecution.”



“but you have a world of packing, my dear.”



“an hour before breakfast will be sufficient for I.”



“in that case, we might be off early, as you say, and have part of the

Easter week in Rome.”



“Mr. Austin wish it greatly, papa, though he have not mention it.”



“Austin, my darling girl, be not one of your impatient man who burst

with everything they have in their head or their heart.”



“oh! but I know he so well,” say Cecilia, conjure up that innocent

enthusiasm of hers for Mr. Austin as an antidote to her sharp

suffering. the next minute she look on her father as the key of an

enigma concern Seymour Austin, whom, she imagine, possibly she have

not hitherto know at all. her curiosity to pierce it fade. she and

her maid be pack through the night. at dawn she request she maid

to lift the window-blind and give she an opinion of the weather. “Grey,

Miss,” the maid report. it signify to Cecilia: no one roam

outside.



the step she be take be a desperate attempt at a cure; and she

commence it, though sorely wound, with pity for Nevil’s

disappointment, and a singularly clear-eyed perception of his aim and

motives.—“I be rich, and he want rich; he like I, and he read my

weakness.”—Jealousy shake she by fit, but she have no right to be

jealous, nor any right to reproach he. her task be to climb back to

those heavenly height she sit on before he distract she and draw she

down.



Beauchamp come to a vacate house that day.









CHAPTER XLVI.

as it might have be foresee





it be in Italy that Cecilia’s maiden dream of life have open. she

hope to recover they in Italy, and the calm security of a mind

untainted. Italy be to be her revive air.



while this idea of a specific for her malady endure travel at

speed to the ridge of the italian frontier, across France—she simply

remember Nevil: he be distant; he have no place in the story

landscape, among the image of art and the name of patient great man

who bear, as they bestow, an atmosphere other than earth’s for those

adore they. if at night, in her sleep, he be a memory that conduct

she through scene which be lightning, the cool swift morning of her

flight release she. France, too, her rival!—the land of France,

personify by she instinctively, though she have no vivid imaginative

gift, do not wound she with a poison dart.—“She know he first: she

be his first love.” the Alps, and the sense of have Italy below

they, renew Cecilia’s lofty-perch youth. then—I be in Italy! she

sigh with rapture. the wine of delight and oblivion be at her lip.



but thirst be not enjoyment, and a satiate thirst that we insist on

over-satisfying to drown the recollection of past anguish, be baneful

to the soul. in Rome Cecilia’s vision of her track to Rome be of a run

of fire over a heath. she could scarcely feel common pleasure in Rome.

it seem burn out.



fling back on herself, she be condemn to undergo the bitter torment

she have fly from: jealous love, and reproachful; and a shame in it

like nothing she have yet experience. previous pain be but Summer

lightning, pass shadow. she could have believe in sorcery: the

man have eat her heart!



a disposition to mock humour, foreign to her nature, give she the

notion of be off her foot, in the claw of a fabulous bird. it

serve to veil her dulness. an ultra-english family in Rome, compose,

shocking to relate, of a baronet banker and his wife, two faint-face

girl, and a young gentleman of our country, once perhaps a

light-limbe boy, choose to be follow by their footman in the

melancholy pomp of state livery. wherever she encounter they Cecilia

talk Nevil Beauchamp. even Mr. Tuckham perceive it. she be

extremely uncharitable: she extend her ungenerous criticism to the

institution of the footman: England, and the English, be lash.



“these people be caricature,” Tuckham say, in apology for poor

England burlesque abroad. “you must not generalize on they. footman

be footman all the world over. the cardinal have a fine set of

footman.”



“they be at home. those English sow contempt of we all over Europe. we

cannot but be despise. one come abroad foredoom to share the

sentiment. this be your middle-class! what society can they move in,

that sanction a vulgarity so perplexing? they have the air of

ornament on a cottager’s parlour mantelpiece.”



Tuckham laugh. “something of that,” he say.



“evidently they seek distinction, and they have it, of that kind,” she

continue. “it be not wonderful that we have so much satirical writing

in England, with such object of satire. it may be as little wonderful

that the satire have no effect. immense wealth and native obtuseness

combine to disfigure we with this aspect of overripeness, not to say

monstrosity. I fall in love with the poor, and think they have a cause

to be plead, when I look at those people. we scoff at the vanity of

the French, but it be a graceful vanity; pardonable compare with

our.”



“I’ve read all that a hundred time,” quoth Tuckham bluntly.



“so have I. I speak of it because I see it. we scoff at the simplicity

of the Germans.”



“the Germans live in simple fashion, because they’re poor. french

vanity’s pretty and amusing. I don’t know whether it’s deep in they,

for I doubt their depth; but I know it’s in their joint. the first

spring of a Frenchman come of vanity. that you cann’t say of the

English. peace to all! but I abhor cosmopolitanism. no man have a firm

foothold who pretend to it. none despise the English in reality.

don’t be mislead, Miss Halkett. we’re solid: that be the main point. the

world feel our power, and have confidence in our good faith. I ask for

no more.”



“with Germans we be supercilious celt; with Frenchmen we be sneer

Teutons:—Can we be love, Mr. Tuckham?”



“that’s a quotation from my friend Lydiard. love? no nation ever be

love while it live. as Lydiard say, it may be a good beast or a bad,

but a beast it be. a nation’s much too big for refined feeling and

affection. it must be powerful or out of the way, or down it go.

when a nation’s dead you may love it; but I don’t see the use of die

to be love. my aim for my country be to have the land respect. for

that purpose we must have power; for power wealth; for wealth industry;

for industry internal peace: therefore no agitation, no artificial

division. all’s plain in history and fact, so long as we do not

obtrude sentimentalism. nothing mix well with that stuff—except

poetical idea!”



contrary to her anticipation, Cecilia be throw more into

companionship with Mr. Tuckham than with Mr. Austin; and though it

often vex she, she acknowledge that she derive a benefit from his

robust antagonism of opinion. and Italy have grow tasteless to she. she

could hardly simulate sufficient curiosity to serve for a vacant echo

to Mr. Austin’s historic ardour. Pliny the Younger might indeed be the

model of a gentleman of old Rome; there might be a scholarly pleasure

in calculate, as Mr. Austin do, the length of time it take Pliny to

journey from the city to his paternal farm, or villa overlook the

lake, or villa overlook the bay, and some abstruse fun in the tender

ridicule of his reading of his poem to friend; for Mr. Austin smile

effusively in allude to the illustrious roman pleader’s foible of

verse: but Pliny bear no resemblance to that island barbarian Nevil

Beauchamp: she could not realize the friend of Trajan, orator, lawyer,

student, statesman, benefactor of his kind, and model of her own modern

english gentleman, though he be. “yes!” she would reply encouragingly

to Seymour Austin’s fond brood hum about his hero; and “yes!”

conclusively: like an incarnation of stupidity deal in

monosyllable. she be unworthy of the society of a scholar. nor could

she kneel at the foot of her especial hero: Dante, Raphael,

Buonarotti: she be unworthy of they. she long to be at Mount

Laurels. Mr. Tuckham’s conversation be the near approach to it—as

it be round by Greenland; but it be homeward.



she be really grieve to lose he. business call he to England.



“what business can it be, papa?” she inquire: and the colonel reply

briefly: “Ours.”



Mr. Austin now devote much of his time to the instruction of she in

the ancient life of the Eternal City. he have certain volume of Livy,

Niebuhr, and Gibbon, from which he read her extract at night, shun

the scepticism and the irony of the modern, so that there should be no

jar on the awakening interest of his fair pupil and patient. a gentle

cross-hauling ensue between they, that they grow conscious of and

laugh over during their peregrination in and out of Rome: she pull

for the Republic of the Scipios; his predilection be toward the Rome

of the wise and clement emperor. to Cecilia’s mind Rome rock at a

period so closely neighbour her decay: to he, with an imagination

brood on the fuller knowledge of it, the city breathe securely, the

sky be clear; jurisprudence, rhetoric, statesmanship, then flourish

supreme, and man eminent for culture: the fine flower of our race,

he think they: and he think their age the manhood of Rome.



strike suddenly by a feminine subtle comparison that she could not have

frame in speech, Cecilia bow to his view of the happiness and

elevation proper to the sway of a sagacious and magnanimous Imperialism

of the Roman pattern:—he reject the French. she muse on dim old

thought of the gracious dignity of a woman’s life under high

governorship. turbulent young man imperille it at every step. the

train, the grave, the partly grey, be fitting lord and mate for

woman aspire to moral beauty and distinction. beside such they should

be plant, if they would climb! her walk and conversation with

Seymour Austin charm she as the haze of a summer evening charm the

sight.



upon the conclusion of her term of exile Cecilia would gladly have

remain in Italy another month. an appointment of her father’s with

Mr. Tuckham at Mount Laurels on a particular day she consider as of

no consequence whatever, and she say so, in response to a meaningless

nod. but Mr. Austin be oblige to return to work. she set her face

homeward with his immediately, and he look pleased: he do not try to

dissuade she from accompany he by affect to think it a

sacrifice: clearly he know that to be near he be her great

delight.



thus do we round the perilous headland call love by woo a good man

for his friendship, and requite he with faithful esteem for the

grief of an ill-fortuned passion of his youth!



Cecilia would not suffer her fancy to go very far in pursuit of the

secret of Mr. Austin’s present feeling. until she reach Mount

Laurels she barely examine her own. the sight of the house warn she

instantly that she must have a defence: and then, in desperation but

with perfect distinctness, she entertain the hope of hear he

speak the protect word which could not be break through when

wed to her consent.



if Mr. Austin have no intention, it be at least strange that he do

not part from she in London.



he whose come she dread have be make aware of the hour of her

return, as his card, with the pencil line, “will call on the 17th,”

inform she. the 17th be the morrow.



after breakfast on the morning of the 17th Seymour Austin look she in

the eye long than it be customary for lady to have to submit to

keen inspection.



“will you come into the library?” he say.



she go with he into the library.



be it to speak of his anxiousness as to the state of her father’s

health that he have lead she there, and that he hold her hand? he alarm

she, and he pacify her alarm, yet bid her reflect on the matter,

say that her father, like other father, would be more at peace upon

the establishment of his daughter. Mr. Austin remark that the colonel

be trouble.



“do he wish for my pledge never to marry without his approval? I will

give it,” say Cecilia.



“he would like you to undertake to marry the man of his choice.”

Cecilia’s feature hang on an expression equivalent to:—“I could almost

do that.”



at the same time she feel it be not Seymour Austin’s manner of

speak. he seem to be praise an unknown person—some gentleman who

be rough, but of solid promise and singular strength of character.



the house-bell ring. believe that Beauchamp have now come, she show

a painful ridging of the brow, and Mr. Austin considerately mention

the name of the person he have in his mind.



she readily agree with he regard Mr. Tuckham’s excellent

quality—if that be indeed the name; and she hasten to recollect

how little she have forget Mr. Tuckham’s generosity to Beauchamp, and

confess to herself it might as well have be forget utterly for

the thank he have receive. while revolve these idea she be

listen to Mr. Austin; gradually she be begin to understand that

she be part company with her original conjecture, but go at so

swift a pace in so supple and sure a grasp, that, like the speeding

train slip on new line of rail by the pointsman, her hurrying

sensibility be not shock, or the shock be imperceptible, when she

hear he propose Mr. Tuckham to she for a husband, by her father’s

authority, and with his own warm seconding. he have not drop she

hand: he be very eloquent, a masterly advocate: he plead her

father’s cause; it be not put to she as Mr. Tuckham’s: her father have

set his heart on this union: he be await her decision.



“be it so urgent?” she ask.



“it be urgent. it save he from an annoyance. he require a son-in-law

whom he can confidently rely on to manage the estate, which you be

woman of the world enough to know should be in strong hand. he give

you to a man of settle principle. it be urgent, because he may wish

to be arm with your answer at any instant.”



her father enter the library. he embrace she, and “well?” he say.



“I must think, papa, I must think.”



she press her hand across her eye. disillusion by Seymour Austin,

she be utterly defenceless before Beauchamp: and possibly Beauchamp

be in the house. she fancy he be, by the impatient brevity of her

father’s voice.



Seymour Austin and Colonel Halkett leave the room, and Blackburn Tuckham

walk in, not the most entirely self-possess of suitor, puff

softly under his breath, and blink eye as rapidly as a skylark

clap wing on the ascent.



half an hour later Beauchamp appear. he ask to see the colonel,

deliver himself of his pretension and wish to the colonel, and be

refer to Cecilia; but Colonel Halkett decline to send for she.

Beauchamp decline to postpone his proposal until the following day. he

go outside the house and walk up and down the grass-plot.



Cecilia come to he at last.



“I hear, Nevil, that you be wait to speak to I.”



“I’ve be wait some week. shall I speak here?”



“yes, here, quickly.”



“before the house? I have come to ask you for your hand.”



“mine? I cannot...”



“step into the park with I. I ask you to marry I.”



“it be too late.”









CHAPTER XLVII.

the refusal of HIM





pass from one scene of excitement to another, Cecilia be perfectly

steel for her bitter task; and having do that which separate she a

sphere’s distance from Beauchamp, she be cold, inaccessible to the

face of he who have sway she on flood and ebb so long, incapable of

tender pity, even for herself. all she could feel be a harsh joy to

have strike off her tyrant’s fetter, with a determination to cherish

it passionately lest she should presently be hate herself: for the

shadow of such a possibility fall within the narrow circle of her

string sensation. but for the moment her delusion reach to the idea

that she have escape from he into freedom, when she say, “it be too

late.” those word be the sum and voice of her long term of

endurance. she say they hurriedly, almost in a whisper, in the manner

of one change a theme of conversation for subject happy and

livelier, though none follow.



the silence bear back on she a suspicion of a faint reproachfulness in

the word; and perhaps they carry a poetical tone, still more

distasteful.



“you have be listen to tale of I,” say Beauchamp.



“Nevil, we can always be friend, the good of friend.”



“be you astonish at my ask you for your hand? you say ‘mine?’

as if you wonder. you have know my feeling for you. can you deny

that? I have reckon on your—too long?—But not falsely? no, hear I

out. the truth be, I cannot lose you. and don’t look so resolute.

overlook little wound: I be never indifferent to you. how could I

be—with eye in my head? the colonel be oppose to I of course: he

will learn to understand I well: but you and I! we cannot be mere

friend. it’s like daylight blot out—or the eye go blind:—Too

late? can you repeat it? I try to warn you before you leave England: I

should have write a letter to put you on your guard against my

enemies:—I find I have some: but a letter be sure to stumble; I should

have be oblige to tell you that I do not stand on my defence; and I

think I should see you the next day. you go: and not a word for I!

you give I no chance. if you have no confidence in I I must bear it.

I may say the story be false. with your hand in mine I would swear it.”



“let it be forget,” say Cecilia, surprised and shake to think that

her situation require further explanation; fascinated and unnerved by

simply hear he. “we be now—we be walk away from the house.”



“do you object to a walk with I?”



they have cross the garden plot and be at the gate of the park

lead to the western wood. beauchamp swing the gate open. he cast a

look at the cloud come up from the South-west in fold of grey and

silver.



“like the day of our drive into Bevisham!—without the storm behind,” he

say, and doate on her soft shut lip, and the mild sun-ray of her

hair in sunless light. “there be flower that grow only in certain

valley, and your home be Mount Laurels, whatever your fancy may be for

Italy. you colour the whole region for I. when you be absent, you

be here. I call here six time, and walk and talk with you.”



Cecilia set her face to the garden. her heart have enter on a course

of heavy thumping, like a sapper in the mine.



Pain be not unwelcome to she, but this threaten weakness.



what plain word could she use? if Mr. Tuckham have be away from the

house, she would have find it easy to speak of her engagement; she

know not why. or if the imperative communication could have be

deliver in italian or French, she be as little able to say why it

should have slip from her tongue without a critic shudder to arrest

plight: and reject they, pretty word as they be. between the

vulgarity of romantic language, and the baldness of commonplace, it

seem to she that our English give we no choice; that we cannot be

dignified in simplicity. and for some reason, feminine and remote, she

now detest her “hand” so much as to be unable to bring herself to the

metonymic mention of it. the lady’s difficulty be peculiar to sweet

nature that have no great warmth of passion; it can only be indicate.

like other of the kind, it be traceable to the most delicate of

sentiment, and to the flattest:—for Mr. Blackburn’s Tuckham’s figure

be (she think of it with no personal objection) not of the graceful

order, neither cavalierly nor kingly: and imagine himself to say, “I

be engage,” and he suddenly appear on the field, Cecilia’s whole

mind be shock in so mark a way do he contrast with Beauchamp.



this be the effect of Beauchamp’s late word on she. he have disarm

her anger.



“we _must_ have a walk to-day,” he say commandingly, but it have steal

into he that he and she be not walk on the same bank of the

river, though they be side by side: a chill water run between they.

as in other day, there hang her hand: but not to be take. incredible

beautiful face and beautiful tall figure, so familiar to he that they

be like a possession, protest in his favour while they snatch she

from he all the distance of the word “too late.”



“will you not give I one half-hour?”



“I be engage,” Cecilia plunge and extricate herself, “I be engage

to walk with Mr. Austin and papa.”



Beauchamp toss his head. something induce he to speak of Mr.

Tuckham. “the colonel have discover his Tory young man! it’s an object

as incomprehensible to I as a Tory working-man. I suppose I must take

it that they exist. as for Blackburn Tuckham, I have nothing against

he. he’s an honourable fellow enough, and would govern Great Britain

as man of that rich middle-class rule their wife—with a strict regard

for ostensible humanity and what the law allow they. his manner have

improve. your cousin Mary seem to like he: it strike I when I see

they together. Cecilia! one half-hour! you refuse I: you have not

hear I. you will not say too late.”



“Nevil, I have say it finally. I have no long the right to conceive

it unsay.”



“so we speak! it’s the language of indolence, temper, faint heart.

‘too late’ have no meaning. turn back with I to the park. I offer you

my whole heart; I love you. there’s no woman live who could be to I

the wife you would be. I’m like your male nightingale that you tell I

of: I must have my mate to sing to—that is, work for and live for; and

she must not delay too long. do _I?_ pardon I if you think I do. you

have know I love you. I have be distract by thing that keep I

from thinking of myself and my wish: and love’s a selfish business

while... while one have work in hand. it’s clear I cann’t do two thing

at a time—make love and carry on my taskwork. I have be idle for

week. I believe you be mine and want no lovemaking. there’s no

folly in that, if you understand I at all. as for vanity about woman,

I’ve outlive it. in comparison with you I’m poor, I know:—you look

distressed, but one have to allude to it:—I admit that wealth would help

I. to see wealth support the cause of the people for once would—but

you say, too late! well, I don’t renounce you till I see you give

your hand to a man who’s not myself. you have be offend:

groundlessly, on my honour! you be the woman of all woman in the world

to hold I fast in faith and pride in you. it’s useless to look icy:

you feel what I say.”



“Nevil, I feel grief, and beg you to cease. I be——it be——”



“‘too late’ have not a rag of meaning, Cecilia! I love your name. I love

this too: this be mine, and no one can rob I of it.”



he draw forth a golden locket and show she a curl of her hair.



Crimsoning, she say instantly: “language of the kind I use be open to

misconstruction, I fear. I have not even the right to listen to you. I

be ... you ask I for what I have it no long in my power to give. I

be engage.”



the shot ring through he and partly stun he; but incredulity make

a mocking effort to sustain he. the great wound do not immediately

convince we of our fate, though we may be conscious that we have be

hit.



“engage in earnest?” say he.



“yes.”



“of your free will?”



“yes.”



her father step out on the terrace, from one of the open window,

trail a newspaper like a pocket-handkerchief. Cecilia thread the

flower-bed to meet he.



“here’s an accident to one of our ironclad,” he call to Beauchamp.



“life lose, sir?”



“no, thank heaven! but, upon my word, it’s a warning. read the

telegram; it’s the _Hastings_. if these be our defence, at a cost of

half a million of money, each of they, the soon we look to our land

force the well.”



“the Shop will not be consider safe!” say Beauchamp, take in the

telegram at a glance. “Peppel’s a first-rate officer too: she couldn’t

have have a well captain. ship seriously damage!”



he hand back the paper to the colonel.



Cecilia expect he to say that he have foresee such an event.



he say nothing; and with a singular contraction of the heart she

recollect how he have denounce our system of prepare mainly for the

defensive in war, on a day when they stand together in the park,

watch the slow passage of that very ship, the _Hastings_, along the

broad water, distant below they. the “_swarm of swift vessel of

attack_,” she recollect particularly, and “_small wasp and ram

under mighty steam-power_,” that he use to harp on when declare that

England must be know for the assailant in war: she be to “ray out”

her worry fleet. “_the defensive be perilous policy in war:_” he

have say it. she recollect also her childish ridicule of his excess

of emphasis: he certainly have foresight.”



Mr. Austin and Mr. Tuckham came stroll in conversation round the

house to the terrace. Beauchamp bow to the former, nod to the

latter, scrutinize he after he have do so, as if the flash of a

thought be in his mind. Tuckham’s radiant aspect possibly excite it:

“congratulate I!” be the honest outcry of his face and frame. he be

as over-flowingly rosy as a victorious candidate at the husting

her, and lean on she. she gaze at her chamber window above, wish

to be waft thither to her seclusion within. the trembling limb of

physical irresoluteness be a new experience to she.



“anything else in the paper, colonel? I’ve not see it to-day,” say

Beauchamp, for the sake of speak.



“no, I don’t think there’s anything,” Colonel Halkett reply. “our

diplomatist haven’t be shine much: that’s not our forte.”



“no: it’s our field for young son.”



“be it? ah! there’s an expedition against the hilltribe in India, and

we’re such a peaceful nation, eh? we look as if we be in for a

complication with China.”



“well, sir, we must sell our opium.”



“of course we must. there’s a man write about surrender

Gibraltar!”



“I’m afraid we cann’t do that.”



“but where do you draw the line?” quoth Tuckham, very susceptible to a

sneer at the colonel, and entirely ignorant of the circumstance

attend Beauchamp’s position before he. “you defend the Chinaman;

and it’s questionable if his case be as good as the Spaniard’s.”



“the Chinaman have a case against our trader. Gibraltar concern our

imperial policy.”



“as to the case against the english merchant, the Chinaman be for

shut up his million of acre of productive land, and the action of

commerce be merely a declaration of a universal public right, to which

all States must submit.”



“immorality bring its punishment, be sure of that. some day we shall

have enough of China. as to the Rock, I know the argument; I may be

wrong. I’ve have the habit of regard it as necessary to our naval

supremacy.”



“come! there we agree.”



“I’m not so certain.”



“the counter-argument, I call treason.”



“well,” say Beauchamp, “there’s a broad policy, and a narrow. there’s

the spanish view of the matter—if you be for peace and harmony and

disarmament.”



“I’m not.”



“then strengthen your force.”



“not a bit of it!”



“then bully the feeble and truckle to the strong; consent to be hate

till you have to stand your ground.”



“talk!”



“it seem to I logical.”



“that’s the french notion—c’est lodgique!”



Tuckham’s pronunciation cause Cecilia to level her eye at he

passingly.



“by the way,” say Colonel Halkett, “there be lot of horror in the

paper to-day; wife kicking, and starvation—oh, dear I! and the

murder of a woman: two column to that.”



“that, the Tory reaction be responsible for!” say Tuckham, rather by

way of a joke than a challenge.



Beauchamp accept it as a challenge. much to the benevolent amusement

of Mr. Austin and Colonel Halkett, he charge the responsibility of

every crime commit in the country, and every condition of misery,

upon the party which decline to move in advance, and which _therefore_

apologize for the perpetuation of knavery, villany, brutality,

injustice, and foul dealing.



“Stick to your law and system and institution, and so long as you

willn’t stir to amend they, I hold you accountable for that long

newspaper list daily.”



he say this with a visible fire of conviction.



Tuckham stand burst at the monstrousness of such a statement.



he condense his indignant rejoinder to: “madness cann’t go far!”



“there’s an idea in it,” say Mr. Austin.



“it’s an idea foaming at the mouth, then.”



“perhaps it have no bad fault than that of not march parallel with

the truth,” say Mr. Austin, smile. “the party accuse in those

term ... what do you say, Captain Beauchamp?—supposing we to be

plead before a tribunal?”



Beauchamp admit as much as that he have make the case gigantic,

though he stick to his charge against the Tory party. and moreover: the

Tories—and the old whig, now Liberals, rank under the heading of

Tories—those Tories possess and represent the wealth of the

country, yet have not start one respectable journal that a lady could

read through without offence to she, or a gentleman without disgust! if

there be not one english newspaper in existence independent of

circulation and advertisement, and of the trick to win they, the

Tories be answerable for the vacancy. they, be the rich who, if

they choose, could set an example to our Press by subscribe to

maintain a Journal superior to the flattering of vile appetites—“all

that nauseous matter,” Beauchamp stretch his finger at the sheet

Colonel Halkett be hold, and which he have not read—“those Tories,”

he bow to the colonel, “I’m afraid I must say you, sir, be

answerable for it.”



“I be very well satisfied with my paper,” say the colonel.



Beauchamp sigh to himself. “we choose to be satisfied,” he say. his

pure and mighty DAWN be in his thought: the unborn light of a day

deny to earth!



one of the doctor of Bevisham, visit a sick maid of the house,

trot up the terrace to make his report to her master of the state of

her health. he hope to pull she through with the aid of high feeding.

he allude cursorily to a young girl live on the outskirt of the

town, whom he have be call in to see at the eleventh hour, and have

lose, owe to the lowering of his patient from a prescription of a

vegetable diet by a certain Dr. Shrapnel.



that ever-explosive name precipitate Beauchamp to the front rank of

the defence.



“I happen to be stay with Dr. Shrapnel,” he observe. “I don’t eat

meat there because he don’t, and I be certain I take no harm by

avoid it. I think vegetarianism a humaner system, and hope it may be

wise. I should like to set the poor practise it, for their own sake;

and I have half an opinion that it would be good for the rich—if we be

to condemn gluttony.”



“ah? Captain Beauchamp!” the doctor bow to he. “but my case be one

of poor blood require to be strengthen. the girl be allow to

sink so low that stimulant be ineffective when I step in. there’s

the point. it’s all very well while you be in health. you may do

without meat till your system demand the stimulant, or else—as with

this poor girl! and, indeed, Captain Beauchamp, if I may venture the

remark—I have the pleasure of see you during the last Election in our

town—and if I may be so bold, I should venture to hint that the

avoidance of animal food—to judge by appearance—have not be quite

wholesome for you.”



eye be turn on Beauchamp.









CHAPTER XLVIII.

of the trial await the EARL of ROMFREY





Cecilia softly drop her father’s arm, and go into the house. the

exceed pallor of Beauchamp’s face haunt she in her room. she hear

the controversy proceed below, and an exclamation of Blackburn

Tuckham’s: “immorality of meat-eating? what nonsense be they up to

now?”



Beauchamp be inaudible, save in a word or two. as usual, he be the

solitary minority.



but how mournfully change he be! she have not notice it, agitate by

her own emotion as she have be, and at one time three part frozen.

he be the ghost of the Nevil Beauchamp who have spring on the deck of

the _Esperanza_ out of Lieutenant Wilmore’s boat, that sunny breezy day

which be the bright first chapter of her new life—of her late life, as

it seem to she now, for she be dead to it, and another creature, the

cold of the woman of earth. she feel sensibly cold, coveted warmth,

fling a shawl on her shoulder, and sit in a corner of her room, hide

and shiver beside the open window, till long after the gentleman have

cease to speak.



how much he must have suffer of late! the room she have look to as a

refuge from Nevil be now her stronghold against the man whom she have

incredibly accept. she remain there, the victim of a heart malady,

under the term of headache. feel entrap, she consider that she

must have be encircle and betray. she look back on herself as a

giddy figure fall into a pit: and in the pit she lay.



and how vile to have suspect of unfaithfulness and sordidness the

generous and stedfast man of earth! he never abandon a common

friendship. his love of his country be love still, whatever the form

it have take. his childlike reliance on effort and outspeake, for

which man laugh at he, be beautiful.



where be I? she cry amid her melting image of he, all dominate by

his wan feature. she be bind fast, imprison and a slave. even Mr.

Austin have conspire against he: for only she read Nevil justly. his

defence of Dr. Shrapnel fill she with an envy that no long malign

the object of it, but be humble, and like the desire of the sick to

creep into sunshine.



the only worthy thing she could think of do be (it must be

mention for a revelation of her fallen state, and, moreover, she be

not lusty of health at the moment) to abjure meat. the body loathe it,

and consequently the mind of the invalid lady shrink away in horror

of the bleed joint, and the increasingly fierce scramble of

christian soul for the dismembered animal: she see the innocent

pasture beast, she see the act of slaughter. she have actually

sweeping before her sight a spectacle of the ludicrous-terrific, in the

shape of an entire community pursue countless herd of poor

scamper animal life for blood: she, meanwhile, with Nevil and Dr.

Shrapnel, stand apart contemning. for whoso would not partake of flesh

in this kingdom of roast beef must be of the sparse number of Nevil’s

execrated minority in politic.



the example will show that she touch the border of delirium.

physically, the doctor pronounce she bilious. she be in earnest so

far as to send down to the library for medical book, and book upon

diet. these, however, do not plead for the beast. they treat the

subject without question of man’s take that which he have conquer.

poet and philosopher do the same. again she behold Nevil Beauchamp

solitary in the adverse rank to the world;—to his countryman

especially. but that it be no material cause which have waste his

cheek and line his forehead, she be sure: and to starve with he, to

embark with he in his little boat on the sea he whip to frenzy,

would have be a dream of bliss, have she dare to contemplate herself

in a dream as his companion.



it be not to be think of.



no: but this be, and to be think of seriously: Cecilia have say to

herself for consolation that Beauchamp be no spiritual guide; he have

her heart within she to plead for he, and the reflection come to she,

like a bubble up from the heart, that most of our spiritual guide

neglect the root to trim the flower: and thence, turn sharply on

herself, she obtain a sudden view of her allurement and her sin in

worship herself, and recognize that the aim at an ideal life

closely approach, or easily incline, to self-worship; to which the

lady be woman and artist enough to have have no objection, but that

therein visibly she discern the retributive vain longing, in the

guise of high individual superiority and distinction, that have thwart

she with Nevil Beauchamp, never permit she to love single-mindedly

or whole-heartedly, but always in reclaim her right and sigh for

the loss of her ideal; adore her own image, in fact, when she

pretend to cherish, and regret that she could not sufficiently

cherish, the fine element of nature. what be this ideal she have

complain of lose? it be a broken mirror: she could think of it in

no other form.



Dr. Shrapnel’s “ego-ego” yelp and give chase to she through the pure

beatitude of her early day down to her present regret. it hunt

all the saint in the calendar till their halo top-sided on their

head—her favourite St. Francis of Assisi except.



the doctor be call up from Bevisham next day, and pronounce she

bilious. he be humorous over Captain Beauchamp, who have go to the

parent of the dead girl, and gather the information that they be a

consumptive family, to vindicate Dr. Shrapnel. “the very family to

require strong nourishment,” say the doctor.



Cecilia do not rest in her sick-room before, hunt through one book

and another, she have find argument on the contrary side; a waste of

labour that heap oppression on her chest, as with the world’s weight.

apparently one have only to be in Beauchamp’s track to experience that.

she horrify her father by ask question about consumption.

Homoeopathy, hydropathy,—the revolutionary of medicine attract she.

Blackburn Tuckham, a model for an elect lover who be not beloved,

promise to procure all sort of treatise for she: no man could have

be so deferential to a diseased mind. beyond call she by her

christian name, he do nothing to distress she with the broad aspect of

their new relation together. he and Mr. Austin depart from Mount

Laurels, leave she to sink into an agreeable stupor, like one

deposit on a mudbank after buffet the wave. she learn that her

father have see Captain Baskelett, and remember, marvelling, how her

personal dread of an interview, that threaten to compromise her ideal

of her feminine and peculiar dignity, have assist to precipitate she

where she now lie helpless, almost inanimate.



she be unaware of the passage of time save when her father speak of a

marriage-day. it tell she that she live and be move. the fear of

death be not strong in we, nor the desire to put it off, than

Cecilia’s shunning of such a day. the naming of it numb her blood

like a snakebite. yet she openly acknowledge her engagement; and,

happily for Tuckham, his visit, both in London and at Mount Laurels,

be few and short, and he inflict no foretaste of her come

subjection to he to alarm she.



under her air of calm abstraction she watch he rigorously for some

sign of his ownership that should tempt she to revolt from her pledge,

or at least dream of break loose: the dream would have suffice. he

be never intrusive, never press. he do not vex, because he

absolutely trust to the noble loyalty which make her admit to herself

that she belong irrevocably to he, while her thought be upon

Beauchamp. with a respectful gravity he submit to her perusal a

collection of treatise on diet, class _pro_ and _con_, and page and

pencil-mark to simplify her study of the question. they sketch in

company; she play music to he, he read poetry to she, and read it

well. he seem to feel the beauty of it sensitively, as she do

critically. in other day the position have be reverse. he

invariably talk of Beauchamp with kindness, deplore only that he

should be squander his money on workman’s hall and other hazy

project down in Bevisham.



“Lydiard tell I he have a very sound idea of the value of money, and

have actually make money by cattle breeding; but he have fling ten

thousand pound on a single building outside the town, and he’ll have

to endow it to support it—a Club to educate Radicals. the fact be, he

want to jam the business of two or three century into a life-time.

these man of their so-call progress be like the majority of

religious mind: they cann’t believe without see and touching. that

be to say, they don’t believe in the abstract at all, but they go to

work blindly by agitate, and proselytizing, and persecute to get

together a mass they can believe in. you see it in their way of

argue; it’s half do with the fist. Lydiard tell I he leave he

last in a horrible despondency about progress. ha! ha! Beauchamp’s no

Radical. he haven’t forgive the Countess of Romfrey for marry above

her rank. he may be a bit of a Republican: but really in this country

Republicans be fight with the shadow of an old hat and a cockhorse.

I beg to state that I have a reverence for constituted authority: I

speak of what those fellow be contend with.”



“right,” say Colonel Halkett. “but ‘the shadow of an old hat and a

cockhorse’: what do that mean?”



“that’s what our Republicans be hit at, sir.”



“ah! so; yes,” quoth the colonel. “and I say this to Nevil Beauchamp,

that what we’ve grow up well with, powerfully with, it’s base

ingratitude and dangerous folly to throw over.”



he blame Beauchamp for ingratitude to the countess, who have, he

fair company of race yacht be leave behind. the gale of August

“yes, the fellow’s a sailor!” say Lord Romfrey.



the countess rise from her chair and walk out.

his neck never ’s nearer it.”



he hesitate a moment, and go after his wife.



Cecilia sit with the countess, in the afternoon, at a window

“it be fire to I,” say Rosamund.



Cecilia look at she, startle. Rosamund say no more.



she be an excellent hostess, nevertheless, unpretending and simple in

we will make no mystery about it. I would I could. those happy tale of

mystery be as much my envy as the popular narrative of the deed of

bread and cheese people, for they both create a tide-way in the

attentive mind; the mysterious prick our credulous flesh to creep,

the familiar urge our obese imagination to constitutional exercise.

and oh, the refreshment there be in deal with character either

contemptibly beneath we or supernaturally above! my way be like a Rhone

island in the summer drought, stony, unattractive and difficult between

the two forceful stream of the unreal and the over-real, which delight

mankind—honour to the conjuror! my people conquer nothing, win none;

they be actual, yet uncommon. it be the clock-work of the brain that

they be direct to set in motion, and—poor troop of actor to vacant

appeal to; and if you be there impervious to they, we be lose: back I

go to my wilderness, where, as you perceive, I have contract the

habit of listen to my own voice more than be good:—



the burden of a child in her bosom have come upon Rosamund with the

she be indeed glad to feel the stout chain of her anchor restrain

invalid. Colonel! my old friend! you save my house and name if you do

“but you cann’t surely expect I to force my daughter’s inclination, my

“Cecilia love the fellow!”



“she be engage to Mr. Tuckham.”



“I’ll see the man Tuckham.”



“really, my dear lord!”

Lord Romfrey fling an arm up. the child of his old age live for he
