but the necessity be inconceivable.



he have really do everything require of he, if anything be really

require, by speak to Shrapnel civilly. he have speak to shrapnel

twice.



besides, the castle be be gladden by happy tiding of

Beauchamp. Gannet now pledge his word to the poor fellow’s recovery,

and the earl’s particular friend arrive, and the countess entertain

they. October pass smoothly.



she say once: “ancestress of your, my lord, have undertake

pilgrimage as act of penance for sin, to obtain heaven’s intercession

in their extremity.”



“I dare say they do,” he reply. “the monk get round they.”



“it be not to be laugh at, if it ease their heart.”



timidly she renew her request for permission to perform the

pilgrimage to Bevisham.



“wait,” say he, “till Nevil be on his leg.”



“have you consider where I may then be, Everard?”



“my love, you sleep well, don’t you?”



“you see I every night.”



“I see you sound asleep.”



“I see you watch I.”



“let’s reason,” say the earl; and again they go through the argument

upon the apology to Dr. Shrapnel.



he be willing to indulge she in any amount of it: and she perceive

why. Fox! she think. grand fox, but fox downright. for her time be

shorten to day that would leave she no free-will.



on the other hand, the exercise of her free-will in a fast resolve, be

grow all the more a privilege that he be bind to respect. as she

become sacred and doubly precious to he, the less would he venture

to thwart she, though he should think her mad. there would be an

analogy between his manner of regard she and the way that

superstitious villager look on their crazy innocent, she think

sadly. and she bleed for he too: she grieve to hurt his pride. but she

have come to imagine that there be no avoidance of this deed of

personal humiliation.



Nevil have scrawl a note to she. she have it in her hand one forenoon

in mid November, when she say to her husband: “I have order the

carriage for two o’clock to meet the quarter to three train to London,

and I have send Stanton on to get the house ready for we tonight.”



Lord Romfrey level a marksman’s eye at she.



“why London? you know my wish that it should be here at the castle.”



“I have decide to go to Bevisham. I have little time leave.”



“none, to my thinking.”



“oh I yes; my heart will be light. I shall gain. you come with I to

London?”



“you cann’t go.”



“don’t attempt to reason with I, please, please!”



“I command, madam.”



“my lord, it be past the hour of commanding.”



he nod his head, with the eye up amid the pucker brow, and

blow one of his long nasal expiration, cry, “here we be, in for

another bout of argument.”



“no; I can bear the journey, rejoice in confess my fault, but more

argument I cannot bear. I will reason with you when I can: submit to I

in this.”



“feminine reasoning!” he interject.



“I have nothing well to offer. it will be prudent to attend to I.

take my conduct for the portion I bring you. before I put myself in

God’s care I must be clean. I be unclean. language like that offend

you. I have no well. my reasoning have not touch you; I be helpless,

except in this determination that my contrition shall be express to

Dr. Shrapnel. if I be to have life, to be worthy of live and be a

mother, it must be do. now, my dear lord, see that, and submit.

you’re but one voice: I be two.”



he jump off his chair, frown up his forehead, and stare awfully

at the insulting prospect. “an apology to the man? by you? away with

it.”



“make allowance for I if you can, my dear lord that be what I be

go to do.”



“my wife go there?” he stride along furiously. “no!”



“you will not stop she.”



“there’s a palsy in my arm if I don’t.”



she pluck at her watch.



“why, ma’am, I don’t know you,” he say, come close to she. “let “s

reason. perhaps you overshot it; you be disgust with Shrapnel.

perhaps I be hasty; I get fire by an insult to a woman. there be a

rascal kiss a girl once against her will, and I hear she cry out; I

lay he on his back for six month; just to tell you; I’d do the same

to lord or beggar. very well, my dear heart, we’ll own I might have

look into the case when that dog Cecil... what’s the matter?”



“speak on, my dear husband,” say Rosamund, pant.



“but your make the journey to Bevisham be a foolish notion.”



“yes? well?”



“well, we’ll wait.”



“oh! have we to travel over it all again?” she exclaim in despair at

the dashing out of a light she have fancy. “you see the wrong. you

know the fever it be in my blood, and you bid I wait.”



“drop a line to Nevil.”



“to trick my conscience! I might have do that, and do well, once.

do you think I dislike the task I propose to myself? it be for your

sake that I would shun it. as for I, the thought of go there be an

ecstasy. I shall be with Nevil, and be able to look in his face. and

how can I be actually abase you when I be so certain that I be

worthy of you in what I do?”



her exaltation sweep she on. “hurry there, my lord, if you will. if you

think it prudent that you should go in my place, go: you deprive I of

a great joy, but I will not put myself in your way, and I consent. the

chief sin be mine; remember that. I rank it viler than Cecil

Baskelett’s. and listen: when—can you reckon?—when will he confess his

wickedness? we separate ourselves from a wretch like that.”



“Pooh,” quoth the earl.



“but you will go?” she fasten her arm round the arm near: “you or

I! do it matter which? we be one. you speak for I; I should have

be force to speak for you. you spare I the journey. I do not in

truth suppose it would have injure I; but I would not run one

unnecessary risk.”



Lord Romfrey sigh profoundly. he could not shake she off. how could

he refuse she?



how on earth have it come about that suddenly he be expect to be the

person to go?



she would not let he elude she; and her stain cheek and her

tremble on his arm plead most pressingly and masteringly. it might

be that she speak with a knowledge of her case. positive it undoubtedly

be that she mean to go if he do not. perhaps the hope of his House

hang on it. having admit that a wrong have be do, he be not the

man to leave it unamended; only he would have choose his time, and the

manner. since Nevil’s illness, too, he have once or twice be cloud

with a little bit of regret at the recollection of poor innocent old

shrapnel post like a figure of total inebriation beside the doorway

of the dreadful sickroom.



there have be woman of the earl’s illustrious House who would have

give their hand to the axe rather than conceal a stain and have to

dread a scandal. his Rosamund, after all, be of their pattern; even

though she blow that conscience she prattle of into trifle, and

swell they, as woman of high birth in this country, out of the

clutch of the priest, do not do.



she cling to he for his promise to go.



he say: “well, well.”



“that mean, you will,” say she.



his not deny it pass for the affirmative.



then indeed she bloom with love of he.



“yet do say yes,” she beg.



“I’ll go, ma’am,” shout the earl. “I’ll go, my love,” he say softly.









CHAPTER LIII.

the APOLOGY to DR. SHRAPNEL





“you and Nevil be so alike,” Lady Romfrey say to her lord, at some

secret resemblance she detect and dwelt on fondly, when the earl be

on the point of start a second time for Bevisham to perform what she

have prompt he to conceive his honourable duty, without a single

intimation that he loathe the task, neither shrug nor grimace.



“two end of a stick be pretty much alike: they’re all that length

apart,” say he, very little in the humour for compliment, however

well braced for his work.



his wife’s admire love be pleasant enough. he prefer to have it

unspoken. few of we care to be eulogize in the act of take a

nauseous medical mixture.



for he the thing be as good as do, on his deciding to think it both

adviseable and right: so he shoulder his load and march off with

it. he could have postpone the right proceeding, even after the

partial recognition of his error:—one drop a word or two by hazard,

one express an anxiety to afford reparation, one send a message, and

so forth, for the satisfaction of one’s conventionally gentlemanly

feeling: but the adviseable proceeding under stress of peculiar

circumstance, his clearly-awaken recognition of that, impel he

unhesitatingly. his wife have say it be the portion she bring he.

tear would not have persuade he so powerfully, that he might prove

to she he be glad of she whatever the portion she bring. she be a

good wife, a brave woman, likely to be an incomparable mother. at

present her very virtue excite she to fancifulness nevertheless she

be in his charge, and he be bind to break the neck of his will, to

give her perfect peace of wind. the child suffer from the mother’s

mental agitation. it might be a question of a nervous or an idiot

future Earl of Romfrey. well death to the House than such a mockery

of his line! these reflection remind he of the heartiness of his

whipping of that poor old tumbled signpost Shrapnel, in the name of

outraged womankind. if there be no outrage?



Assuredly if there be no outrage, consideration for the state of his

wife would urge he to speak the apology in the most natural manner

possible. she vow there be none.



he never think of blame she for formerly deceive he, nor of

blame she for now expedite he.



in the presence of Colonel Halkett, Mr. Tuckham, and Mr. Lydiard, on a

fine November afternoon, stand bareheade in the fir-border garden

of the cottage on the common, Lord Romfrey deliver his apology to Dr.

Shrapnel, and he say:



“I call you to witness, gentleman, I offer Dr. Shrapnel the full

reparation he may think fit to demand of I for an unprovoked assault

on he, that I find be quite unjustified, and for which I be here to

ask his forgiveness.”



Speech of man could not have be more nobly utter.



Dr. Shrapnel reply:



“to the half of that, sir—“tis over! what remain be do with the

hand.”



he stretch his hand out.



Lord Romfrey close his own on it.



the antagonist, between whom be no pretence of their be other

after the performance of a creditable ceremony, bow and exchange

civil remark: and then Lord Romfrey be invite to go into the house

and see Beauchamp, who happen to be sit with Cecilia Halkett and

Jenny Denham. Beauchamp be thin, pale, and quiet; but the sight of he

stand and converse after that scene of the skinny creature

struggle with bareribbed obstruction on the bed, be an example of

constitutional vigour and a compliment to the family very gratifying to

Lord Romfrey. except by Cecilia, the earl be coldly receive. he

have to leave early by special express for London to catch the last

train to Romfrey. Beauchamp decline to fix a day for his visit to the

castle with Lydiard, but propose that Lydiard should accompany the

earl on his return. Lydiard be call in, and at once accept the

earl’s invitation, and quit the room to pack his portmanteau.



a faint sign of firm-shut shadow the corner of Jenny’s lip.



“you have bring my nephew to life,” Lord Romfrey say to she.



“my share in it be very small, my lord.”



“gannet say that your share in it be very great.”



“and I say so, with the authority of a witness,” add Cecilia.



“and I, from my experience,” come from Beauchamp.



his voice have a hollow sound, unlike his natural voice.



the earl look at he remember the bright laugh lad he have once

be, and say: “why not try a month of Madeira? you have only to step

on board the boat.”



“I don’t want to lose a month of my friend,” say Beauchamp.



“take your friend with you. after these fever our winter be bad.”



“I’ve be idle too long.”



“but, Captain Beauchamp,” say Jenny, “you propose to do nothing but

read for a couple of year.”



“ay, there’s the voyage!” sigh he, with a sailor-invalid’s vision of

sunny sea dance in the far sky.



“you must persuade Dr. Shrapnel to come; and he will not come unless

you come too, and you willn’t go anywhere but to the Alps!” she bent she

eye on the floor. Beauchamp remember what have bring her home from

the Alps. he cast a cold look on his uncle talk with Cecilia:

granite, as he think. and the reflux of that slight feeling of

despair seem to tear down with it in wreckage every effort he have

make in life, and cry failure on he. yet he be hope that he have not

be create for failure.



he touch his uncle’s hand indifferently: “my love to the countess:

let I hear of she, sir, if you please.”



“you shall,” say the earl. “but, off to Madeira, and up Teneriffe:

sail the Azores. I’ll hire you a good-sized schooner.”



“there be the _Esperanza_,” say Cecilia. “and the vessel be lie

_idle_, Nevil! can you allow it?”



he consent to laugh at himself, and fall to cough.



Jenny Denham see a real human expression of anxiety cross the feature

of the earl at the sound of the cough.



Lord Romfrey say “Adieu,” to she.



he offer she his hand, which she contrive to avoid take by

drop a formal half-reverence.



“think of the _Esperanza;_ she will be coast her nominal native

land! and adieu for to-day,” Cecilia say to Beauchamp.



Jenny Denham and he stand at the window to watch the leave-taking in

the garden, for a distraction. they interchange no remark of surprise

at see the earl and Dr. Shrapnel hand-lock: but Jenny’s heart

reproach her uncle for be actually servile, and Beauchamp accuse

the earl of aristocratic impudence.



both be overcome with remorse when Colonel Halkett, put his head

into the room to say good-bye to Beauchamp and place the _Esperanza_ at

his disposal for a Winter cruise, chance to mention in two or three

half word the purpose of the earl’s visit, and what have occur. he

take it for know already.



to Miss Denham he remark: “Lord Romfrey be very much concerned about

your health; he fear you have overdo it in nursing Captain

Beauchamp.”



“I must be off after he,” say Beauchamp, and begin tremble so that

he could not stir.



the colonel know the pain and shame of that condition of weakness to a

man who have be strong and swift, and say: “seven-league boot be

not to be catch. you’ll see he soon. why, I think some letter of

yours have fetch he here! I give you all the credit of it.”



“no, he deserve it all himself—all,” say Beauchamp and with a dubious

eye on Jenny Denham: “you see, we be unfair.”



the “we” mean “you” to her sensitiveness; and probably he do mean it

for “you”: for as he would have feel, so he suppose that his uncle

must have feel, Jenny’s coldness be much the crueller. her feature,

which in animation be summer light play upon smooth water, could

be exceedingly cold in repose: the icy to those who know she, because

they never express disdain. no expression of the base sort belong

to they. Beauchamp be intimate with these delicately-cut feature; he

would have shudder have they chill on he. he have fall in love

with his uncle; he fancy she ought to have do so too; and from his

excess of sympathy he find she deficient in it.



he sit himself down to write a hearty letter to his “dear old uncle

Everard.”



Jenny leave he, to go to her chamber and cry.









CHAPTER LIV.

the fruit of the APOLOGY





this clear heart have cause for tear. her just indignation with Lord

Romfrey have sustain she artificially hitherto: now that it be

erase, she sink down to weep. her sentiment toward Lydiard have be

very like Cecilia Halkett’s in favour of Mr. Austin; with something

more to warm they on the part of the gentleman. he first have lead she

mind in the direction of balanced thought, when, despite her affection

for Dr. Shrapnel, her timorous maiden wit, unable to contend with the

copious exclamatory old politician, oppose he silently. Lydiard have

help her tongue to speak, as well as her mind to rational view; and

there have be a bond of union in common for they in his admiration of

her father’s writing. she have know that he be miserably yoke, and

have respect he when he seem inclined for compassion without woo

she for tenderness. he have not trifle with she, hardly flattered; he

have do no more than kindle a young girl’s imaginative liking. the

pale flower of imagination, feed by dew, not by sunshine, be bear

drooping, and hang secret in her bosom, shy as a bell of the frail

wood-sorrel. yet there be pain for she in the perishing of a thing so

poor and lowly. she have not observe the change in Lydiard after

Beauchamp come on the scene: and that may tell we how passionlessly

pure the little maidenly sentiment be. for do but look on the dewy

wood-sorrel flower; it be not violet or rise invite hand to pluck

it: still it be there, happy in the wood. and Jenny’s feeling be that

a foot have crush it.



she weep, think confusedly of Lord Romfrey; try to think he have

make his amend tardily, and that Beauchamp prize he too highly for

the act. she have no long anything to resent: she be oblige to weep.

in truth, as the earl have notice, she be physically depress by the

strain of she protract watch over Beauchamp, as well as rather

heartsick.



but she have be of aid and use in save he! she be not quite a

valueless person; sweet, too, be the thought that he consult she,

listen to she, weigh her idea. he have evidently take to study

she, as if disperse some wonderment that one of her sex should have

idea. he have repeat certain of her own which have be forget by

she. his eye be often on she with this that she think humorous

intentness. she smile. she have assist in raise he from his bed of

sickness, whereof the memory affright she and melt she. the

difficulty now be to keep he indoor, and why he would not go even

temporarily to a large house like Mount Laurels, whither Colonel

Halkett be daily request he to go, she be unable to comprehend.

his love of Dr. Shrapnel might account for it.



“Own, Jenny,” say Beauchamp, spring up to meet she as she enter

the room where he and Dr. Shrapnel sit discuss Lord Romfrey’s

bearing at his visit, “own that my uncle Everard be a true nobleman. he

have to make the round to the right mark, but he come to it. _I_ could

not move he—and I like he the well for that. he work round to it

himself. I ought to have be sure he would. you’re right: I break my

head with impatience.”



“no; you sow seed,” say Dr. Shrapnel. “Heed not that girl, my

Beauchamp. the old woman’s in the Tory, and the Tory lead the young

maid. here’s a fable I draw from a Naturalist’s book, and we’ll set it

against the dicta of Jenny do-nothing, Jenny Discretion, Jenny

wait-for-the-Gods: once upon a time in a tropical island a man lie

sick; so ill that he could not rise to trouble his neighbour for help;

so weak that it be lift a mountain to get up from his bed; so

hopeless of succour that the last spark of distraught wisdom perch

on his brain advise he to lie where he be and trouble not himself,

since peace at least he could command, before he pass upon the black

highroad man call our kingdom of peace: ay, he lie there. now it

chance that this man have a mess to cook for his nourishment. and life

say, do it, and death say, to what end? he wrestle with the stark

limb of death, and cook the mess; and that do he have no strength

remain to he to consume it, but creep to his bed like the toad into

winter. now, meanwhile a steam arise from the mess, and he lie

stretch. so it befel that the bird of prey of the region scent the

mess, and they descend and throng at that man’s window. and the

man’s neighbour look up at they, for it be the sign of one who be

fit for the beak of bird, lie unburied. fail to spread the pall one

hour where sun be decisive, and the pall come down out of heaven!

they say, the man be dead within. and they go to his room, and see

he and succour he. they lift he out of death by the last uncut

thread.



“now, my Jenny Weigh-word, Jenny Halt-there! be it they who save the

man, or he that save himself? the man tax his expire breath to sow

seed of life. lydiard shall put it into verse for a fable in song for

our people. I say it be a good fable, and sing spiritedly may serve for

nourishment, and faith in work, to many of our poor fainting fellow!

now you?”



Jenny say: “I think it be a good fable of self-help. do it quite

illustrate the case? I mean, the virtue of impatience. but I like the

fable and the moral; and I think it would do good if it be make

popular, though it would be hard to condense it to a song.”



“it would be hard! ay, then we do it forthwith. and you shall compose

the music. as for the ‘case of impatience,’ my dear, you tether the

soar universal to your pet-lamb’s post, the special. I speak of seed

sown. I speak of the fruit of energy and resolution. care I for an

apology? I take the blow as I take hail from the cloud—which

apologize to you the moment you be in shelter, if you laugh at they.

so, good night to that matter! be we to have rain this evening? I must

away into Bevisham to the Workmen’s Hall, and pay the man.”



“there will not be rain; there will be frost, and you must be well

wrap if you must go,” say Jenny. “and tell they not to think of

deputation to Captain Beauchamp yet.”



“no, no deputation; let they send Killick, if they want to say

anything,” say Beauchamp.



“Wrong!” the doctor cry; “wrong! wrong! six man willn’t hurt you more

than one. and why check they when their feeling be up? they burn to

be speak some word to you. trust I, Beauchamp, if we shun to

encounter the good warm soul of number, our heart be narrow to

they. the business of our modern world be to open heart and stretch out

arm to number. in number we have our sinew; they be our iron and

gold. scatter they not; teach they the secret of cohesion. practically,

since they give you not their entire confidence once, you should not

rebuff they to suspicion of you as aristocrat, when they rise on the

effort to believe a man of, as “tis call, birth their undivided

friend. meet they!”



“send they,” say Beauchamp.



Jenny Denham fasten a vast cloak and a comforter on the doctor’s

heedless shoulder and throat, enjoin on he to return in good time

for dinner.



he put his finger to her cheek in reproof of such supererogatory

counsel to a man famous for his punctuality.



the day have darken.



Beauchamp beg Jenny to play to he on the piano.



“do you indeed care to have music?” say she. “I do not wish you to

meet a deputation, because your strength be not yet equal to it. Dr.

Shrapnel dwell on principle, forgetful of minor consideration.”



“I wish thousand do!” cry Beauchamp. “when you play I seem to hear

idea. your music make I think.”



Jenny light a pair of candle and set they on the piano. “waltz?” she

ask.



“call in a puppet-show at once!”



she smile, turn over some leave, and strike the opening note of

the Ninth Symphony of Beethoven, and make her selection.



at the finish he say: “now read I your father’s poem, ‘_the Hunt of

the Fates._’”



she read it to he.



“now read, ‘_the Ascent from the Inferno._’”



that she read: and also “_Soul and Brute_,” another of his favourite.



he want more, and tell she to read “_First Love—last love._”



“I fear I have not the tone of voice for love-poem,” Jenny say,

return the book to he.



“I’ll read it,” say he.



he read with more impressiveness than effect. lydiard’s reading

thrill she: Beauchamp’s insist too much on particular line. but it

be worth while observe he. she see he always as in a picture,

remote from herself. his loftier social station and strange character

preclude any of those keen suspicion by which woman learn that a fire

be begin to glow near they.



“how I should like to have know your father!” he say. “I don’t wonder

at Dr. Shrapnel’s love of he. yes, he be one of the great man of his

day! and it’s a high honour to be of his blood than any that rank can

give. you be ten year old when you lose he. describe he to I.”



“he use to play with I like a boy,” say Jenny. she describe she

father from a child’s recollection of he.



“Dr. Shrapnel declare he would have be one of the first surgeon in

Europe: and he be one of the first of poet,” Beauchamp pursue with

enthusiasm. “so he be doubly great. I hold a good surgeon to be in the

front rank of public benefactor—where they put rich brewer, banker,

and speculative manufacturer now. well! the world be young. we shall

alter that in time. whom do your father marry?”



Jenny answer, “my mother be the daughter of a London lawyer. she

marry without her father’s approval of the match, and he leave she

nothing.”



Beauchamp interject: “Lawyer’s money!”



“it would have be useful to my mother’s household when I be an

infant,” say Jenny.



“poor soul! I suppose so. yes; well,” Beauchamp sigh. “money! never

mind how it come. we’re in such a primitive condition that we catch at

anything to keep we out of the cold; dog with a bone!—instead of

living, as Dr. Shrapnel prophecy, for and, with one another. it’s war

now, and money’s the weapon of war. and we’re the bad nation in

Europe for that. but if we fairly recognize it, we shall be the first

to alter our way. there’s the point. well, Jenny, I can look you in

the face to-night. thank to my uncle Everard at last!”



“Captain Beauchamp, you have never be blame.”



“I be Captain Beauchamp by courtesy, in public. my friend call I

Nevil. I think I have hear the name on your lip?”



“when you be very ill.”



he stand close to she, very close.



“which be the arm that bleed for I? may I look at it? there be a

bruise.”



“have you not forget that trifle? there be the faint possible

mark of it leave.”



“I wish to see.”



she gently defend the arm, but he make it so much a matter of earn

to see the bruise of the old Election missile on her fair arm, that,

with a pardonable soft blush, to avoid make much of it herself, she

turn she sleeve a little above the wrist. he take her hand.



“it be for I!”



“it be quite an accident: no harm be intend.”



“but it be in my cause—for I!”



“indeed, Captain Beauchamp...”



“Nevil, we say indoor.”



“Nevil—but be it not wise to say what come naturally to we?”



“who tell you to-day that you have bring I to life? I be here to

prove it true. if I have pay attention to your advice, I should not

have go into the cottage of those poor creature and take away the

fever. I do no good there. but the man’s wife say her husband have

be ruin by vote for I: and it be a point of honour to go in and

sit with he. you be not to have your hand back: it be mine. don’t you

remember, Jenny, how you give I your arm on the road when I stagger;

two day before the fever knock I over? shall I tell you what I

think then? I think that he who could have you for a mate would

have the brave and helpfull wife in all England. and not a mere

beauty, for you have good look: but you have the quality I have be

in search of. why do your eye look so mournfully at I? I be full of

hope. we’ll sail the _Esperanza_ for the winter: you and I, and our

good friend with we. and you shall have a voice in the council, be

sure.”



“if you be two to one?” Jenny say quickly, to keep from falter.



Beauchamp press his mouth to the mark of the bruise on her arm. he

hold she fast.



“I mean it, if you will join I, that you and I should rejoice the

heart of the dear old man—will you? he have be brood over your

loneliness here if you be unmarried, ever since his recovery. I owe my

life to you, and every debt of gratitude to he. now, Jenny!”



“oh! Captain Beauchamp—Nevil, if you will... if I may have my hand. you

exaggerate common kindness. he love you. we both esteem you.”



“but you don’t love I?”



“indeed I have no fear that I shall be unable to support myself, if I

be leave alone.”



“but I want your help. I wake from illness with my eye open. I must

have your arm to lean on now and then.”



Jenny drop a shiver sigh.



“Uncle be long absent!” she say.



her hand be release. Beauchamp inspect his watch.



“he may have fall! he may be lie on the common!”



“oh!” cry Jenny, “why do I let he go out without I?”



“let I have his lantern; I’ll go and search over the common.”



“you must not go out,” say she.



“I must. the old man may be perish.”



“it will be death to you... nevil!”



“that’s foolish. I can stand the air for a few minute.”



“I’ll go,” say Jenny.



“unprotected? no.”



“cook shall come with I.”



“two woman!”



“Nevil, if you care a little for I, be good, be kind, submit.”



“he be half an hour behind dinner-time, and he’s never late. something

must have happen to he. way for I, my dear girl.”



she stand firm between he and the door. it come to pass that she

stretch her hand to arrest he, and he seize the hand.



“rather than you should go out in this cold weather, anything!” she

say, in the desperation of physical inability to hold he back.



“ah!” Beauchamp cross his arm round she. “I’ll wait for five

minute.”



one go by, with Jenny fold, broken and sobbing, senseless, against

his breast.



they have not hear Dr. Shrapnel quietly open the hall door and

hang up his hat. he look in.



“Beauchamp!” he exclaim.



“come, doctor,” say Beauchamp, and loosen his clasp of Jenny

considerately.



she disengage herself.



“Beauchamp! now I die a glad man.”



“Witness, doctor, she’s mine by her own confession.”



“Uncle!” Jenny gasp. “oh! Captain Beauchamp, what an error! what

delusion!... forget it. I will. here be more misunderstanding! you

shall be excuse. but be...”



“be you the blessed woman alive on this earth, my Jenny!” shout

Dr. Shrapnel. “you have the choice man on all the earth for husband,

sweetheart! ay, of all the earth! I go with a message for my old friend

Harry Denham, to quicken he in the grave; for the husband of his girl

be Nevil Beauchamp! the one thing I dare not dream of thousand be

establish. Sunlight, my Jenny!”



Beauchamp kiss her hand.



she slip away to her chamber, grovel to find her diminish self

somewhere in the mid-thunder of her amazement, as though it be to

discover a pin on the floor by the flash of lightning. where be she!



this ensue from the apology of Lord Romfrey to Dr. Shrapnel.









CHAPTER LV.

without LOVE





at the end of November, Jenny Denham write these line to Mr. Lydiard,

in reply to his request that she should furnish the late particular

of Nevil Beauchamp, for the satisfaction of the Countess of Romfrey:



“there be everything to reassure Lady Romfrey in the state of Captain

Beauchamp’s health, and I have never see he so placidly happy as he

have be since the arrival, yesterday morning, of a lady from France,

Madame la Marquise de Rouaillout, with her brother, M. le Comte de

Croisnel. her husband, I hear from M. de Croisnel, dread our climate

and coffee too much to attempt the voyage. I understand that she write

to Lady Romfrey to-day. Lady Romfrey’s letter to she, inform she of

Captain Beauchamp’s alarming illness, go the round from Normandy to

Touraine and Dauphiny, otherwise she would have come over early.



“her first inquiry of I be, ‘il est mort?’ you would have suppose

she disappoint by my answer. a light go out in her eye, like that

of a veilleuse in the dawn. she look at I without speak, while

her beautiful eye regain their natural expression. she shut they and

sigh. ‘tell he that M. de Croisnel and his sister be here.’



“this morning her wish to see Miss Halkett be gratify. you know my

taste be form in France; I agree with Captain Beauchamp in his more

than admiration of Frenchwomen; our, though more accomplished, be

colder and less plastic. but Miss Halkett be surpassingly beautiful,

very amiable, very generous, a perfect friend. she be our country at

its good. probably she be shy of speak French; she frequently put

the italian accent. Madame de Rouaillout beg to speak with she

alone: I do not know what pass. Miss Halkett do not return to we.



“Dr. Shrapnel and Captain Beauchamp have recently be speculate on

our become a nation of artist, and authority in science and

philosophy, by the time our coalfield and material wealth be

exhausted. that, and the cataclysm, be their theme.



“they say, will thing end utterly?—all our gain be lose? the question

seem to I to come of that love of earth which be recognition of God:

for if they cannot reconcile themselves to believe in extinction, to

what must they be look? it be a confirmation of your saying, that

love lead to God, through art or in act.



“you will regret to hear that the project of Captain Beauchamp’s voyage

be in danger of be abandon. a committee of a vacant Radical

borough have offer to nominate he. my influence be weak; madame would

have he go back with she and her brother to Normandy. my influence be

weak, I suppose, because he find I constantly lean to expediency—I

be your pupil. it may be quite correct that powder be intend for

explosion: we do not therefore apply a spark to the barrel. I venture

on that. he pity I in the snare of simile and metaphor. he be the

same, you perceive. how often have we not discuss what would have

become of he, with that ‘rocket brain’ of his, in less quiet time!

yet, when he be address a deputation of workman the other day, he

recommend patience to they as one of the virtue that count under

wisdom. he be curiously impatient for knowledge. one of his reason for

not accept Colonel Halkett’s offer of his yacht be, that he will not

be able to have book enough on board. definite instead of vast and

hazy duty be to be desire for he, I think. most fervently I pray

that he will obtain a ship and serve some year. at the risk of your

accuse I of ‘sententious posing,’ I would say, that man who do not

live in the present chiefly, but hamper themselves with giant task in

excess of alarm for the future, however devoted and noble they may

be—and he be an example of one that be—reduce themselves to the

dimension of pigmy; they have the cry of infant. you reply,

foresight be an element of love of country and mankind. but how often

be not the foresight guess-work?



“he have not speak of the DAWN project. to-day he be repeat one of

uncle’s novelties—‘Sultry Tories.’ the sultry Tory sit in the sun and

prophecy woefully of storm, it appear. your accusation that I be one

at heart amuse I; I be not quite able to deny it. ‘sultriness’ I be

not conscious of. but it would appear to be an epithet for the

conservative of wealth. so that England, be very wealthy, we be to

call it a sultry country? you be much want, for where there be no

‘middleman Liberal’ to hold the scale for they, these two have it all

their own way, which be not good for they.



Captain Beauchamp quote you too. it seem that you once talk to he

of a machine for measure the force of blow deliver with the fist,

and compare his effort to those of one perpetually practise at it:

and this you be say to have call ‘the case of the Constitutional

Realm and the extreme Radical.’ elsewhere the Radical smite at iron or

rotten wood; _in England it be a cushion on spring_. do you say it?

he quote it as your, half acquiescingly, and ruefully.



“for visitor, we have have Captain Baskelett for two minute, and Lord

Palmet, who stay long, and seem to intend to come daily. he

attempt French with Madame de R., and amuse she a little: a silver

foot and a ball of worsted. Mr. and Mrs. Grancey Lespel have call,

and Lord and Lady Croyston. Colonel Halkett, Miss Halkett, and Mr.

Tuckham come frequently. Captain Beauchamp speak to she yesterday of

her marriage. “Madame de R. leave we to-morrow. her brother be a

delightful, gay-temper, very handsome boyish Frenchman—not her equal,

to my mind, for I do not think Frenchmen comparable to the woman of

France; but she be exceedingly grave, with hardly a smile, and his high

spirit excite Nevil’s, so it be pleasant to see they together.”



the letter be hand to Lady Romfrey. she read through it thoughtfully

till she come to the name of Nevil, when she frown. on the morrow she

pronounce it a disingenuous letter. Renée have send she these line:



“I should come to you if my time be not restrict; my brother’s

leave of absence be short. I have do here what lie in my power, to

show you I have learn something in the school of self-immolation. I

have see Mlle. Halkett. she be a beautiful young woman, deficient only

in word, doubtless. my labour, except that it may satisfy you, be the

vainest of task. she marry a ruddy monsieur of a name that I forget,

and of the bearing of a member of the garde du corps, without the

stature. enfin, madame, I have do my duty, and do not regret it,

since I may hope that it will win for I some approbation and a portion

of the esteem of a lady to whom I be indebted for that which be now the

good of life to I: and I do not undervalue it in say I would gladly

have it stamp on brass and deposit beside my father’s. I have my

faith. I would it be Nevil’s too—and your, should you be in need of

it.



“he will marry Mlle. Denham. if I may foretell event, she will steady

he. she be a young person who will not feel astray in society of his

rank; she possess the natural grace we do not expect to see out of

our country—from sheer ignorance of what be beyond it. for the moment

she affect to consider herself unworthy; and it be excuseable that she

should be slightly alarmed at her prospect. but Nevil must have a wife.

I presume to think that he could not have choose well. above all,

make he leave England for the Winter. Adieu, dear countess. nevil

promise I a visit after his marriage. I shall not set foot on England

again: but you, should you ever come to our land of France, will find

my heart open to you at the gate of undye grateful recollection. I

be not skilled in writing. you have look into I once; look now; I be

the same. only I have succeed in bring myself to a great

likeness to the dead, as it become a creature to be who be couple

with one of their body. meanwhile I shall have news of you. I trust

that soon I may be warrant in forward congratulation to Lord

Romfrey.”



Rosamund hand the letter to her husband. not only do she think Miss

Denham disingenuous, she see that the girl be not in love with

Beauchamp: and the idea of a loveless marriage for he throw the

mournfull of Hecate’s beam along the course of a career that the

passionate love of a bride, though she be not well-bear and not

wealthy, would have rosily colour.



“without love!” she exclaim to herself. she ask the earl’s opinion

of the startling intelligence, and of the character of that Miss

Denham, who could pen such a letter, after engage to give her hand to

Nevil.



Lord Romfrey laugh in his dumb way. “if Nevil must have a wife—and

the marquise tell you so, and she ought to know—he may as well marry a

girl who willn’t go all the way down hill with he at his pace. he’ll be

cog.”



“you do not object to such an alliance?”



“I’m past objection. there’s no law against a man’s marry his

nurse.”



“but she be not even in love with he!”



“I dare say not. he want a wife: she accept a husband. the two woman

who be in love with he he wouldn’t have.”



Lady Romfrey sigh deeply: “he have lose Cecilia! she might still have

be his: but he have take to that girl. and Madame de Rouaillout

praise the girl because—oh! I see it—she have less to be jealous of in

Miss Denham: of whose birth and blood we know nothing. let that pass!

if only she love he! I cannot endure the thought of his marry a

girl who be not in love with he.”



“just as you like, my dear.”



“I use to suspect Mr. Lydiard.”



“perhaps he’s the man.”



“oh, what an end of so brilliant a beginning!”



“it strike I, my dear,” say the earl, “it’s the proper common sense

begin that may have a fairish end.”



“no, but what I feel be that he—our Nevil!—has accomplish hardly

anything, if anything!”



“he haven’t march on London with a couple of hundred thousand man: no,

he haven’t do that,” the earl say, glance back in his mind through

Beauchamp’s career. “and he escape what stukely call his nation’s

scourge, in the shape of a statue turn out by an english chisel. no:

we haven’t have much public excitement out of he. but one thing he do

do: _he get I down on my knee!_”



Lord Romfrey pronounce these word with a sober emphasis that strike

the humour of it sharply into Rosamund’s heart, through some contrast

it present between Nevil’s aim at the world and hit of a man: the

immense deal think of it by the earl, and the very little that Nevil

would think of it—the great domestic achievement to be boast of by an

enthusiastic devotee of politic!



she embrace her husband with peal of love laughter: the last

laughter hear in Romfrey Castle for many a day.









CHAPTER LVI.

the last of nevil beauchamp





not before Beauchamp be fly with the Winter gale to warm clime

could Rosamund reflect on his career unshadowe by her feminine

mortification at the thought that he be unlove by the girl he have

decide to marry. but when he be away and wind blow, the cloud which

obscure an embrace imagination of he—such as, to be true and full

and sufficient, should stretch like the dome of heaven over the

humblest of life under contemplation—break, and reveal he to she as

one who have other than fail: rather as one in mid career, in mid

forest, who, by force of character, advance in self-conquest, strike

his impress right and leave around he, because of his aim at star. he

have fault, and she glory to think he have; for the woman’s heart

rejoice in his portion of our common humanity while she name their

prince to man: but where be he to be match in devotedness and in

gallantry? and what man of blood fiery as Nevil’s ever fight so to

subject it? Rosamund follow he like a migratory bird, hover over

his vessel, perch on deck beside the helm, where her sailor be sure

to be station, enter his breast, commune with he, and wound he

round and round with her love. he have mine! she cry. her craving that

he should be bl in the reward, or flower-crown, of his wife’s love

of he lessen in proportion as her brood spirit vividly realize

his deed. in fact it have be but an example of our very general

craving for a climax, palpable and scenic. she be completely satisfied

by her conviction that his wife would respect and must be subordinate

to he. so it have be with she. as for love, let he come to his

Rosamund for love, and appreciation, adoration!



Rosamund draw nigh to her hour of peril with this torch of her love of

Beauchamp to illuminate she.



there have be a difficulty in get he to go. one day Cecilia

walk down to Dr. Shrapnel’s with Mr. Tuckham, to communicate that the

_Esperanza_ await Captain Beauchamp, man and provision, off the

pier. now, he would not go without Dr. Shrapnel, nor the doctor without

Jenny; and Jenny could not hold back, see that the wish of her heart

be for Nevil to be at sea, untrouble by political question and

prowl radical deputy. so her consent be the seal of the voyage.

what she would not consent to, be the proposal to have her finger

ring previous to the voyage, altogether in the manner of a sailor’s

bride. she seem to stipulate for a term of courtship. Nevil frankly

tell the doctor that he be not equal to it; anything that be kind he

be quite ready to say; and anything that be pretty: but nothing

particularly kind and pretty occur to he: he be exactly like a

juvenile correspondent face a blank sheet of letter paper:—he really

do not know what to say, far than the uncomplicated exposition of

his case, that he want a wife and have find the very woman. how,

then, fathom Jenny’s mood for delaying? Dr. Shrapnel’s exhortation

be so word as to induce she to comport herself like a Scriptural

woman, humbly wakeful to the surpass splendour of the high fortune

which have befall she in be so select, and obedient at a sign.

but she be, it appear that she be, a maid of scaly vision, not

perceptive of the blessedness of her lot. she could have be very

little perceptive, for she do not understand his casual allusion to

Beauchamp’s readiness to overcome “a natural repugnance,” for the

purpose of make she his wife.



up to the last moment, before Cecilia Halkett leave the deck of the

_Esperanza_ to step on the pier, Jenny remain in vague but excited

expectation of something intervene to bring Cecilia and Beauchamp

together. it be not a hope; it be with pure suspense that she await

the issue. Cecilia be pale. beauchamp shake Mr. Tuckham by the hand,

and say: “I shall not hear the bell, but send I word of it, will

you?” and he wish they both all happiness.



the sail of the schooner fill. on a fair frosty day, with a light

wind ruffle from the North-west, she sweep away, out of sight of

Bevisham, and the island, into the Channel, to within view of the coast

of France. England once below the water-line, alone with Beauchamp and

Dr. Shrapnel, Jenny Denham know her fate.



as soon as that grow distinctly visible in shape and colour, she cease

to be reluctant. all about she, in air and sea and unknown coast, be

fresh and prompt. and if she look on Beauchamp, the thought—my

husband! palpitate, and destroy and re-make she. rapidly she

undergo her transformation from doubtfully-minded woman to woman

awakening clear-eye, and with new sweet shiver in her temperate

blood, like the tremulous light see run to the morn upon a quiet

sea. she fall under the charm of Beauchamp at sea.



in view of the island of Madeira, Jenny notice that some trouble have

come upon Dr. Shrapnel and Beauchamp, both of whom have be hilarious

during the gale; but sail into Summer they begin to wear that look

which indicate one of their serious deliberation. she be not take

into their confidence, and after awhile they recover partially.



the truth be, they have be force back upon old english ground by a

recognition of the absolute necessity, for her sake, of hand

themselves over to a parson. in England, possibly, a civil marriage

might have be propose to the poor girl. in a foreign island, they

would be drive not simply to accept the service of a parson, but to

seek he and solicit he: otherwise the knot, fast than any sailor’s

in binding, could not be tie. decidedly it could not; and how submit?

neither Dr. Shrapnel nor Beauchamp be of a temper to deceive the

clerical gentleman; only they have to think of Jenny’s feeling. alas

for us!—this our awful baggage in the rear of humanity, these woman who

have not move on their own foot one step since the primal mother

teach they to suckle, be perpetually pull we backward on the

march. slave of custom, form, show and superstition, they be

slave of the priest. “they be so in gratitude perchance, as the

matter work,” Dr. Shrapnel admit. for at one period the priest do

cherish and protect the weak from animal man. but we have enter a

broad daylight now, when the sun of high heaven have crown our

structure with the flower of brain, like he to scatter mist, and

penetrate darkness, and shoot from end to end of earth; and must we

still be grin subserviently to ancient usage and stale form,

because of a baggage that it be, woe to we! too true, we cannot cut

ourselves loose from? lydiard might say we be compelling the priest

to fight, and that they be compact foeman, not always passive. battle,

then!—The cry be valiant. nevertheless, Jenny would certainly insist

upon the presence of a parson, in spite of her bridegroom’s “natural

repugnance.” Dr. Shrapnel offer to argue it with she, be of

opinion that a british consul could satisfactorily perform the

ceremony. beauchamp know she too well. moreover, though tongue-tie as

to love-making, he be in a hurry to be marry. Jenny’s eye be

lovely, her smile be soft; the fair promise of she be in bloom on

her face and figure. he could not wait; he must off to the parson.



then come the question as to whether honesty and honour do not impose

it on they to deal openly with that gentle, and on such occasion

unobtrusive official, by mean of a candid statement to he overnight,

to the effect that they be the avow antagonist of his Church,

which would put he on his defence, and lead to an argument that would

accomplish his overthrow. you parson, whose cause be good, marshal out

the poor of the land, that we may see the sort of army your stewardship

have gain for you. what! no army? only woman and hoary man? and in the

rear rank, to support you as an institution, none but fanatic,

coward, white-eyeball dogmatist, timeserver, money-changer,

mocker in their sleeve? what be this?



but the prospect of so completely confound the unfortunate parson

warn Beauchamp that he might have a shot in his locker: the parson

heavily tread on will turn. “I suppose we must be hypocrite,” he

say in dejection. Dr. Shrapnel be even more melancholy. he again

offer to try his persuasiveness upon Jenny. Beauchamp decline to let

she be disturb.



she do not yield so very lightly to the invitation to go before a

parson. she have to be woo after all; a Harry Hotspur’s wooing. three

clergyman of the Established Church be on the island: “and where

willn’t they be, where there’s fine scenery and comfort abound?”

Beauchamp say to the doctor ungratefully.



“whether a celibate clergy ruin the faith fast than a non-celibate,

I willn’t dispute,” reply the doctor; “but a non-celibate interwind

with we, and be likely to keep up a one-story edifice long.”



Jenny hesitate. she be a falter unit against an ardent and

imperative two in the council. and Beauchamp have show she a letter of

Lady Romfrey’s very clearly signify that she and her lord

anticipate tiding of the union. Marrying Beauchamp be no simple

adventure. she fear in her bosom, and resign herself.



she have a taste of what it be to be, at the conclusion of the service.

Beauchamp thank the good-natured clergyman, and speak approvingly of

he to his bride, as an agreeable well-breed gentlemanly person. then,

front she and take both her hand: “now, my darling,” he say:

“you must pledge I your word to this: I have stoop my head to the

parson, and I be content to have do that to win you, though I don’t

think much of myself for do it. I cann’t look so happy as I be. and

this idle ceremony—however, I thank God I have you, and I thank you for

take I. but you willn’t expect I to give in to the parson again.”



“but, Nevil,” she say, fear what be to come: “they be gentleman,

good man.”



“yes, yes.”



“they be educate man, Nevil.”



“Jenny! Jenny Beauchamp, they’re not man, they’re Churchmen. my

experience of the priest in our country be, that he have abandon—he’s

dead against the only cause that can justify and keep up a Church: the

cause of the poor—the people. he be a creature of the moneyed class. I

look on he as a pretender. I go through his form, to save my wife

from annoyance, but there’s the end of it: and if ever I’m helpless,

unable to resist he, I rely on your word not to let he intrude; he’s

to have nothing to do with the burial of I. he’s against the cause of

the people. very well: I make my protest to the death against he. when

he’s a Christian instead of a Churchman, then may my example not be

follow. it’s little use look for that.”



Jenny drop some tear on her bridal day. she sigh her submission.

“so long as you do not change,” say she.



“Change!” cry Nevil. “that’s for the parson. now it’s over: we start

fair. my darling! I have you. I don’t mean to bother you. I’m sure

you’ll see that the enemy of Reason be the enemy of the human

race; you will see that. I can wait.”



“if we can be sure that we ourselves be use reason rightly,

Nevil!—not prejudice.”



“of course. but don’t you see, my Jenny, we have no interest in

oppose reason?”



“but have we not all grow up together? and be it just or wise to

direct our effort to overthrow a solid structure that be a part...?”



he put his legal right in force to shut her mouth, tell she

presently she might _Lydiardize_ as much as she like. while practise

this mastery, he assure she he would always listen to she: yes,

whether she lydiardize, or what Dr. Shrapnel call Jenny-prate.



“that be to say, dear Nevil, that you have quite make up your mind to a

toddle chatter little nursery wife?”



very much the contrary to anything of the sort, he declare; and he

prove his honesty by announce an immediate reflection that have come

to he: “how oddly thing be settle! Cecilia Halkett and Tuckham; you

and I! now, I know for certain that I have bring Cecilia Halkett out

of her woman’s toryism, and give she at least liberal view, and she

go and marry an arrant Tory; while you, a bit of a Tory at heart,

more than anything else, have marry an ultra.”



“perhaps we may hope that the conflict will be seasonable on both

sides?—if you give I fair play, Nevil!”



as fair play as a woman’s lord could give she, she be to have; with

which, adieu to argumentation and controversy, and all the thank in

life to the parson! on a lovely island, free from the seduction of

care, possess a wife who, instead of start out of romance and

poetry with he to the supreme honeymoon, lead he back to those

forsake valley of his youth, and teach he the joy of colour and

sweet companionship, simple delight, a sister mind, with a loveliness

of person and nature unimagine by he, Beauchamp drink of a happiness

that neither Renée nor Cecilia have promise. his wooing of Jenny

Beauchamp be a flattery rich than any the maiden Jenny Denham could

have deem her due; and if his wonder in experience such strange

gladness be quaintly ingenuous, it be delicious to she to see and

know full surely that he who be at little pain to court, or please,

independently of the agency of the truth in he, have come to be her

lover through be her husband.



here I would stop. it be Beauchamp’s career that carry I on to its

close, where the lantern throw their beam off the mudbank by the

black riverside; when some few english man and woman differ from the

world in think that it have suffer a loss.



they sorrow for the earl when tiding come to they of the loss of his

child, alive one hour in his arm. Rosamund cause they to be deceive

as to her condition. she survive; she write to Jenny, bid she keep

her husband cruise. Lord Romfrey add a brief word: he tell Nevil

that he would see no one for the present; hope he would be absent a

year, not a day less. to render it the more easily practicable, in the

next packet of letter Colonel Halkett and Cecilia beg they not to

bring the _Esperanza_ home for the yachting season: the colonel say

his daughter be to be marry in April, and that bridegroom and bride

have consent to take an old man off with they to Italy; perhaps in the

autumn all might meet in Venice.



“and you’ve never see Venice,” Beauchamp say to Jenny.



“everything be new to I,” say she, penetrate and gladly join the

conspiracy to have he out of England.



Dr. Shrapnel be not so compliant as the young husband. where he could

land and botanize, as at Madeira, he let time fly and drum his wing on

air, but the city of priest along the coast of Portugal and Spain

rouse he to a burn sense of that flight of time and the vacuity it

tell of in his labour. greatly to his astonishment, he find that it

be no long he and Beauchamp against Jenny, but Jenny and Beauchamp

against he.



“what!” he cry, “to draw breath day by day, and not to pay for it by

strike daily at the rock Iniquity? be you for that, Beauchamp? and

in a land where these priest walk with hat curl like the

water-lily’s leaf without the flower? how far will you push indolent

unreason to gain the delusion of happiness? there be no such thing: but

there’s trance. that talk of happiness be a carrion clamour of the

creature of prey. take it—and you’re help tear some poor wretch to

piece, whom you might be construct, save perchance: some one?

some thousand! you, Beauchamp, when I meet you first, you be for

England, England! for a breadth of the palm of my hand

comparatively—the round of a copper penny, no wider! and from that you

jump at a bound to the round of this earth: you be for humanity.

ay, we sail our planet among the icy sphere, and be at blood-heat

for its destiny, you and I! and now you hover for a wind to catch you.

so it be for a soul reject prayer. this wind and that have it: the

well-spring within be shut down fast! I pardon my Jenny, my Harry

Denham’s girl. she be a woman, and have a brain like a bell that ring

all round to the tongue. it be her kingdom, of the interdict

untraverse frontier. but what care she, or any woman, that this Age

of ours should lie like a carcase against the Sun? what care any woman

to help to hold up life to he? he breed divinely upon life, filthy

upon stagnation. sail you away, if you will, in your trance. I go. I go

home by land alone, and I await you. here in this land of mole

upright, I do naught but execrate; I be a pulpit of curse.

Counter-anathema, you might call I.”



“oh! I feel the comparison so, for England shine spiritually bright,”

say Jenny, and cut her husband adrift with the exclamation, and see

he float away to Dr. Shrapnel.



“_spiritually_ bright!”



“by comparison, Nevil.”



“there’s neither spiritual nor political brightness in England, but a

common resolution to eat of good thing and stick to they,” say the

doctor: “and we two out of England, there’s barely a voice to cry scare

to the feeder. I’m back! I’m home!”



they lose he once in Cadiz, and discover he on the quay, look

about for a vessel. in get he to return to the _Esperanza_, they

nearly all three fall into the hand of the police. Beauchamp give he

a great deal of his time, read and discuss with he on deck and

in the cabin, and project future enterprise, to pacify his

restlessness. a translation of Plato have become Beauchamp’s

intellectual world. this philosopher singularly anticipate his idea.

concern himself he be begin to think that he have many year

ahead of he for work. he be with Dr. Shrapnel, as to the battle, and

with Jenny as to the delay in recommence it. both the man laugh at

the constant employment she give they among the greek island in

furnish her severely accurate account of sea-fight and

land-fight: and the scene be before they they could neither of

they protest that their task-work be an idle labour. Dr. Shrapnel

assist in fight Marathon and Salamis over again cordially—to

shield Great Britain from the rule of a satrapy.



Beauchamp often try to conjure word to paint his wife. on grave

subject she have the manner of speak of a shy scholar, and between

grave and playful, between smile and serious, her clear head, she

nobly poise character, seem to he to have never have a prototype and

to elude the art of picture it in expression, until he hear Lydiard

call she whimsically, “Portia disrobe.”



Portia half in her doctor’s gown, half out of it. they meet Lydiard and

his wife Louise, and Mr. and Mrs. Tuckham, in Venice, where, upon the

first day of October, Jenny Beauchamp give birth to a son. the

thrilling mother do not perceive on this occasion the gloom she cast

over the father of the child and Dr. Shrapnel. the youngster would

insist on his right to be sprinkle by the parson, to get a legal name

and please his mother. at all turn in the history of our healthy

relation with woman we be confront by the parson! “and, upon my

word, I believe,” Beauchamp say to Lydiard, “those parson—not bad

creature in private life: there be one in Madeira I take a personal

like to—but they’re utterly ignorant of what man feel to they—more

ignorant than woman!” Mr. Tuckham and Mrs. Lydiard would not listen to

his foolish objection; nor be they ever mention to Jenny.

apparently the commission of the act of marriage be to force Beauchamp

from all his position one by one.



“the education of that child?” Mrs. Lydiard say to her husband.



he consider that the mother would prevail.



Cecilia fear she would not.



“depend upon it, he’ll make himself miserable if he can,” say Tuckham.



that gentleman, however, be perpetually come fume from argument

with Beauchamp, and his opinion be a controversialist’s. his common

sense be much afflict. “I think marriage would have stop all

those absurdity,” he say, glare angrily, laugh, and then

frown. “I’ve warn he I’ll go out of my way to come across he if

he carry on his headlong folly. a man should accept his country for

what it be when he’s bear into it. don’t tell I he’s a good fellow. I

know he be, but there’s an ass mount on the good fellow. talk of the

parson! why, they’re man of education.”



“they couldn’t steer a ship in a gale, though.”



“oh! he’s a good sailor. and let he go to sea,” say Tuckham. “his

wife’s a prize. he’s hardly worthy of she. if she manage he she’ll

deserve a monument for do a public service.”



how fortunate it be for we that here and there we do not succeed in

wrest our temporary treasure from the grasp of the Fates!



this good old commonplace reflection come to Beauchamp while clasp

his wife’s hand on the deck of the _Esperanza_, and look up at the

mountain over the Gulf of Venice. the impression of that marvellous

dawn when he and Renée look up hand-in-hand be ineffaceable, and

pity for the tender hand lose to he work in his blood, but Jenny

be a peerless wife; and though not in the music of her tongue, or in

subtlety of delicate meaning do she excel Renée, as a sober adviser

she do, and as a firm speaker; and she have homelier deep eye,

thoughtfuller brow. the father could speculate with good hope of

Jenny’s child. Cecilia’s wealth, too, have go over to the Tory party,

with her incomprehensible espousal of Tuckham. let it go; let all go

for dowerless Jenny!



it be (she dare to recollect it in her anguish) jenny’s choice to go

home in the yacht that decide her husband not to make the journey by

land in company with the Lydiards.



the voyage be favourable. beauchamp have a pass wish to land on the

Norman coast, and take Jenny for a day to Tourdestelle. he defer to

her desire to land baby speedily, now they be so near home. they run

past Otley river, have sight of Mount Laurels, and on to Bevisham,

with swell sail. there they part. Beauchamp make it one of his

“point of honour” to deliver the vessel where he have take she, at her

mooring in the Otley. one of the pierman stand before Beauchamp, and

salute he, say he have be direct to inform he that the Earl of

Romfrey be with Colonel Halkett, expect he at Mount Laurels.

Beauchamp want his wife to return in the yacht. she turn her eye

to Dr. Shrapnel. it be out of the question that the doctor should

think of go. husband and wife part. she see he no more.



this be no time to tell of weeping. the dry chronicle be fit. hard

on nine o’clock in the December darkness, the night be still and

clear, Jenny’s babe be at her breast, and her ear be awake for the

return of her husband. a man ring at the door of the house, and ask

to see Dr. Shrapnel. this man be Killick, the Radical Sam of politic.

he say to the doctor: “I’m go to hit you sharp, sir; I’ve have it

myself: please put on your hat and come out with I; and close the

door. they mustn’t hear inside. and here’s a fly. I know you’d be off

for the finding of the body. Commander Beauchamp’s drown.”



Dr. Shrapnel drive round by the shore of the broad water past a great

hospital and ruin abbey to Otley village. Killick have lift he into

the conveyance, and he lift he out. Dr. Shrapnel have not speak a

word. light be flare on the river, illuminate the small craft

sombrely. man, woman, and child crowd the hard and landing-place,

the marshy bank and the deck of collier and trawler. neither

Killick nor Dr. Shrapnel question they. the light be torch and

lantern; the occupation of the boat move in couple be the

drag for the dead.



“o God, let’s find his body,” a woman call out.



“just a word; be it Commander Beauchamp?” Killick say to she.



she be scarcely aware of a question. “here, this one,” she say, and

pluck a little boy of eight by the hand close against her side, and

shake he roughly and kiss he.



an old man volunteer information. “that’s the boy. that boy be in

his father’s boat out there, with two of his brother, larking; and he

and another old than he fall overboard; and just then Commander

Beauchamp be row by, and I see he from off here, where I stand,

jump up and dive, and he swam to his boat with one of they, and get he

in safe: that boy: and he dive again after the other, and be down a

long time. either he burst a vessel or he get cramp, for he’d be

row himself from the schooner ground down at the river-mouth, and

must have be hot when he jump in: either way, he fetch the second

up, and sink with he. down he go.”



a fisherman say to Killick: “do you hear that voice thunder? that’s

the great Lord Romfrey. he’s be direct the dragging since five o’

the evening, and will till he drop or drown, or up come the body.”



“o God, let’s find the body!” the woman with the little boy call out.



a torch light up Lord Romfrey’s face as he step ashore. “the flood have

play we a trick,” he say. “we want more drag, or with the next ebb

the body may be lose for day in this infernal water.”



the mother of the rescued boy sob, “oh, my lord, my lord!”



the earl catch sight of Dr. Shrapnel, and go to he.



“my wife have go down to Mrs. Beauchamp,” he say. “she will bring she

and the baby to Mount Laurels. the child will have to be hand-feed. I

take you with I. you must not be alone.”



he put his arm within the arm of the heavily-breathing man whom he have

once fling to the ground, to support he.



“my lord! my lord!” sob the woman, and drop on her knee.



“what’s this?” the earl say, draw his hand away from the woman’s

clutch at it.



“she’s the mother, my lord,” several explain to he.



“mother of what?”



“my boy,” the woman cry, and drag the urchin to Lord Romfrey’s

foot, clean her boy’s face with her apron.



“it’s the boy Commander Beauchamp drown to save,” say a man.



all the light of the ring be turn on the head of the boy. Dr.

Shrapnel’s eye and Lord Romfrey’s fall on the abashed little creature.

the boy strike out both arm to get his fist against his eyelid.



this be what we have in exchange for Beauchamp!



it be not utter, but it be visible in the blank stare at one

another of the two man who love Beauchamp, after they have examine the

insignificant bit of mudbank life remain in this world in the place

of he.
